NVIDIA 失市场份额？还是被低估？

As far as the NVIDIA quarter, they went to a new reporting structure, Data Center and Edge, and then within AI, they have hyperscalers, and then I think they call it AI clouds, industrial, and enterprise. What I believe is if we were to make a true apples-to-apples comparison — and Broadcom, there's a narrative that NVIDIA is losing share to the TPU. And Broadcom guided for 143% year over year growth in their AI semiconductor revenue in the quarter that they will report that's comparable to the one NVIDIA just reported.

I think if you were to — and I just, I so wish they had reported slightly differently. I wish they'd done hyperscalers, AI clouds, and then industrial and enterprise. Because I think the segment that is comparable is the sum of hyperscalers plus AI clouds stripping out China, because Broadcom just did not have the China business that NVIDIA did. And I think on that basis, in other words, within the Western AI world, within data centers that need being built, whether they're being built by CoreWeave, XAI, Amazon, Google, NVIDIA's AI business is growing faster than Broadcom's. And faster than a lot of other companies that are, you know, seen as part of this ASIC share gain story.

And, you know, I think Jensen has become, you can hear it, increasingly frustrated, rightfully so, at two things. I would say, what is the performance of the stock to — He's been vocal about that. Like, what is going on here? Yes. We're putting up record numbers and we're getting no, like, credit. Yeah, I get it. And just how could there be a share loss narrative if I am gaining share? And it is indisputably true that he is growing faster than hyperscaler capex, even without these adjustments.

Yeah. I think the other thing that's so frustrating to him is these other ASICs are not being submitted for benchmarks. They're not in the SemiAnalysis Inference Max. They're not in MLPerf. I think the reason they're not being submitted is they will lose. And you can't fight shadows. And until we see a clean benchmark of whether it's GB300s versus TPU V7s or, you know, versus — versus Inferentia, yeah. Yeah, versus Trainium. We're not going to know. And that's why a lot of these other chips, I think Trainium's in a great spot, aren't being submitted. But nonetheless, NVIDIA's doing well.

Once you become the largest company in the world, you kind of — my observation would be at stair step patterns where you kind of the multiple compresses, compresses, compresses because people are skeptical of the size. Then you have a re-rating. And then you re-rate. New floors established at a higher rate. Yeah.

I think there was one other really important thing in the NVIDIA quarter. It's they said if they thought their CPU business was going to be $20 billion this year. Yeah, yeah, yeah. That's extraordinary. It means overnight for one of the world's largest CPU manufacturers.

And I think that is a testament to NVIDIA has a unique position. They're the only company that works with every lab. And so that puts them in the best position to architect their chips. They call it co-design for where the models are going. And I think that $20 billion CPU figure is pretty extraordinary.

This is the thing, like at the end of this Grok transaction last year, my kind of prevailing thought on this is we're going to move to these domain specific architectures. I thought that was like a fait accompli. We're just now waiting for which models. But the reality is that that DSA market evolution is actually happening inside of Nvidia. That's what's so insane to me. That was my takeaway from the quarter as well, which is like, holy shit, these guys actually have domain specific architectures because they're doing these design programs with every — this is why back to sort of the, you know, when he does DC to DC with Elon and Colossus 3 or whatever, it's just it's another game changer for everybody. He doesn't make a Moon trip. He makes nine.

The true lifespan of a GPU is more like two years, and therefore, the profits of all these businesses are overstated. The reality is—

So he's like, I don't know what anybody's talking about here. Like, this is just not informed analysis was his point, like the game on the field and people are betting with their dollars with him. And generally, everything else, I think you should not speculate and you should generally avoid, not just because it's an up market, but in every market. I've learned this the hard way. We've all kind of gone through this. As I get older, it's just not worth it. The vicissitudes of the market don't give me anywhere near the sugar high it used to on the way up. And it makes me feel horrible on the way down. So how I manage myself is I have a few companies that I really believe in, I have extremely concentrated large holdings and those large for me doesn't mean large for everybody else. And then otherwise, I just kind of stick to my eating and keep my head down. It's a much more rational way to behave.

One: Rates going up is very concerning.

Two: What is happening with AI right now with Anthropic growing faster than any country, any company in history at massive scale. And certainly any country. Absolutely unprecedented. Yes, they're actually, yeah, they're now the size of, you know, yeah, yeah, they're, we pull up where they rank. They're bigger than a hundred different countries for sure. Exactly. And come out really fast and now profitable, which I think really changes the moment. Which is bizarre. Yeah. How did that happen?

So those things can both be true. And I think we should all remember the tech bubble happened with the 10 year and the 30 year much higher than they are today. And the NVIDIA of the tech bubble is Cisco and it traded a hundred times forward earnings. And I think NVIDIA is probably at a low teens multiple of kind of low to mid teens multiple of real earnings. That'd be a buy side consensus, not an Atreides number.

Three: And then the third thing that I think is true is, Estrecho de Ormuz being closed, while it's terrible for everyone, it is relatively the best for America. Because we are self-sufficient in energy, we are self-sufficient in food, we have become a massive exporter of oil. We're now the world's largest, not only oil and gas producer, but oil and gas exporter.

And, you know, those three things can be true. What do they mean? I think it's probably hard for me to see with America being the most advantaged by what is going on. We're still the best currency. Yes. We're still the best economy. We still have the best public market companies. We have the best AI. And we have the best private market companies. Yeah. And we are one of the greatest producers of oil in the world. So we're in good shape despite this international chaos.

I don't think a dollar crisis is around the world. Now listen, if the Bundesbank was still in charge and you had the Deutsche Mark and there was a currency with better fundamentals, we would be at very high risk, probably. But just because we're the best house and what is globally a bad neighborhood of high debt levels, and we have AI in our corner, and we have energy self-sufficiency, and every day the Strait of Hormuz is closed, I think is relatively good for the re-industrialization of America.

Like, I think, you know, you have to — you're saying it's a forcing function — makes us, just like COVID did, be more resilient and more self-reliant. Yes.

And electricity is a base input to every manufacturing or industrial process, essentially all of them. And what we make electricity with in America overwhelmingly is natural gas. And you can look it up in G1. It is down this year. The input cost for electricity in the rest of the world — you know, lots of different things, but LNG is a very important one, and it's up 100, 200%. And so the Strait of Hormuz is absolutely bad for everyone, but relatively good for America and relatively good for Trump's policy goals. And that's why I think he's in no hurry. Every day the Strait is closed, and for whatever, he does seem like a relative thinker. Every day the Strait of Hormuz is closed is relatively good America. It's terrible for Europe. It is terrible for Asia. Japan and China need that oil. Philippines needs it. India needs it. Yeah. Yeah.

And so all these things can be true. But the one thing I do just want to say is rates going up and inflation going up is never good. But we have to hold what's happening with AI where the fundamentals are getting a lot stronger in our mind.

AI 的季节性？

And the one thing I would just add is AI has been seasonal. The market's seasonal. You know, it often, you know, sell in May, go away. And AI fundamentals also appear a little seasonal. In the past, that's been, you know, because college students, they use a lot less ChatGPT and Claude in the summer. And generally people maybe work a little less hard when the weather's nice. Now with the agentic AI, will the fundamentals still be seasonal? We will see.

Oh, that's a really interesting point, right? We see that e-commerce, apps, subscriptions. As investors in a lot of these companies, we would always have these board meetings, Chamath. Oh, Q3. Yeah, people are out gallivanting and they're not playing Candy Crush or buying Comm or whatever. But, oh, hey, Uber and DoorDash went up. People are traveling, et cetera.

五、特朗普与习近平峰会：表演还是实质？

OK, final story of the week. We had a 48-hour jaunt by a bunch of tech CEOs and the president to hang out with Xi. A lot of high fives, a lot of handshakes, a lot of great vibes. But coming out of it, we haven't seen anything definitive, Freeberg, in terms of policy. This was supposed to be some big breakthrough. It would have downstream effects on tariffs, on us selling chips to China. We did see a little movement there. And the Strait of Hormuz, we would become, you know, wonder twins with Xi and Trump reopening it. But nothing really definitive other than some soybeans being sold and some H100s, A200s, getting sold to Baidu and some of the top folks. So what do, and maybe some planes got sold too. So other than a little BD, a little business development, yes. Freeberg, what's the outcome here? Or was it just a bit performative in your mind?

There was a question. Would the administration leave China with a grand deal that made everyone feel like there's a long range view on a partnership? And I don't think that that's happened. There were a few announcements, obviously, around an intention to continue to work together in a cooperative way and find a path to partnership and intention to establish additional trade deals. And there were some purchases of aircraft and some agricultural product commitments. But fundamentally, the grand deal, the big deal that I would say reduces, de-escalates tension probably didn't manifest as some had hoped it would.

And I don't think it's any surprise that Putin is with Xi today. And this is also performative that following the US visit, there is now a relationship bonding moment happening between China and Russia. So the story continues. There is no happy ending and there is no rainbow colored chapter three in this book. It's gonna continue to be a dramatic arc as this rising power continues to challenge the United States. And I think this story continues.

Were you expecting a happy ending?

I can't answer that question. I mean, to the story of the China visit. I'm not talking about any other things going on in your life, but it did seem like some planes and soybeans got sold, some H100s perhaps, but I mean, it wasn't like there was some grand deal that occurred, but it's nice to see them together, right? I mean, that is nice.

I think it was successful. I think there's what you see on the surface and then there's what happens behind closed doors. And without speculating too much, I think that it was a useful and productive trip. I think the biggest thing that they probably got alignment on is just geopolitically the tic-tac-toe of what has to happen next. And I think that there's some amount of agreement there. I'm just guessing.

Yeah. And so that guess, if I was going to unpack it, hey, we have Venezuela, we have Iran and Taiwan. You have lots of needs around this geopolitical chessboard.

Here's what I would say just very generally. I think that there's a way to divide up the game board in a way that helps them and helps us.

卖芯片给中国的辩论

Gavin, any thoughts on specifically Nvidia being able to sell more chips into China, material for the company? Good for America? I mean, it's obviously a pretty debatable issue.

I think I disagree with Chamath on this. I think selling deprecated NVIDIA GPUs to China lowers the odds of them developing their own alternative ecosystem, which would be a lot power hungrier because you use optical... You bring in optical a lot earlier for scale up fabrics. I think there's sound arguments that this stabilizing for the world and is the best, highest probability path for keeping America ahead in AI and kind of keeping control of AI. And that's almost a shame we've had to have this debate because now people like me have said this many times in China. If they didn't understand it, they probably do really understand it. By the way, that's not to say we shouldn't have had the debate. But that is what I believe, you know, reasonable minds can disagree.

Wait, where do you think we disagree? I'm not sure. I agree with you. Oh, good. I'm glad we agree. I was going to jump in here. No, no, no. I'm like sell everything to them. No, what I was just saying is that there was what you see on the surface of what they can speak to the press. But I think the most important thing was the negotiation of, hey, listen, we're going to do these things, you do these other things. And that's never going to get put out in a multi-memoed press release. That's my point. That's all I'm saying.

I would just say, listen, American China talking is only good. We want to avoid the trap that is being discussed and China talks about a lot. They're very aware of it. They brought it up. Xi brought it up by name. Talking is an integral step of avoiding the Thucydides trap.

特朗普的"独裁者社交"超能力

I have to say, it's having a nice resolution. From my perspective, the greatest superpower Trump has is his ability to bond with dictators, monarchs, royal families, Gulf monarchies. He's just great at it. They see eye to eye. They vibe. He has no problem going to see them. He has no problem inviting them to UFC fights. This is like if Xi comes to the United States and he's sitting courtside with Dana White, that's when we know things are going to be OK. I do think he's probably given him the green light on like, hey, Taiwan's yours. Just let's not have it during my administration. Maybe like we do a 30 year deal or a 20 year handoff deal. I wouldn't be surprised if something like that happens. Or a 100 year deal or 200 year deal.

But the one thing I would just say that I'm sure was communicated is, hey, wars consume a vast amount of oil. You buy your oil from Iran, Venezuela and Russia. Russia alone can supply a fraction of what you need. And now it should be clear to the world. Two of the three are off the chessboard. Yeah, if you do — two or three are off the chessboard, if you do something we don't like, Venezuelan oil, gone for you. American oil, gone. Brazilian oil, gone. And we'll say to all of our good friends in the GCC, we're so sorry, but we have to close the Strait of Hormuz again. So Iran, all of Middle Eastern oil, gone for China. Now you just have Russia and good luck fighting a war with just Russian oil against us, Japan, South Korea, Australia, UK, France, Germany, the world. Good luck.

I mean, who knows about Europe? But for sure, Japan would be there. For sure, Japan would be there. Oh, and Australia, for sure. Korea, for sure. Yeah.

And so I think it's going to be a more stable world on the other side of Iran, however it resolves. And I think that's nothing but good. I think that's a good insight. I think it's a great insight.

收尾

All right, listen, we missed you, Sacks. Come back soon. And Gavin Baker. Shout out to bestie Gavin. Thank you. Yeah, you're so great. Thanks for coming. We appreciate it. Hey, and your father-in-law, what's his name again? Jeff Painter. Jeff Painter. We love you. Thank you so much for all the kind words. We'd love to invite you to come to Liquidity or the Summit. I know you're a big fan of the show. Wanted to give you a shout out here on the show.

Thanks. I used your fandom of the show to leverage Gavin, who was like, I can't make it today. And I was like, tell your father-in-law, we're going to get him backstage VIP tickets to the next two events if you show up today. And if you don't, I'm going to back channel it to him. And all of a sudden, Gavin made it to the show. Love you, boys.

A little bit of pressure. That's my way of the, that's my hormone straight. Yeah, I'm always there for my father-in-law. Absolutely. All right, everybody. We'll see you next time. Bye-bye. Thanks, guys. Take care.

We should all just get a room. Thank you, Georgie, because they're all just useless. It's like this sexual tension, but they just need to release them out. Wet your feet. Wet your feet. We need to get merch. I'm going all over.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反常识的观察：**Anthropic 在向 SpaceX 付租金，而非 AWS**
每月 12.5 亿美元、三年 450 亿美元——这个数字背后藏着一个被几乎所有评论员忽视的范式转移：**云计算的霸主不再由"数据中心运营经验"决定，而由"建造速度"决定**。SpaceX 三个数据中心的建设周期是 122→91→66 天，这条曲线本质上和 Falcon 9 的复用次数曲线是同一条曲线——**一个造火箭的学习率被直接迁移到了造算力的学习率**。
这意味着什么？**SpaceX 不是在跨界进入云计算，而是云计算本身被重新定义为"重工业制造问题"。** 当 AWS/Azure/GCP 还在用 IT 思维迭代时，Elon 用航天工业的"制造良率"思维降维打击。这条 90 天合同终止条款看起来是 Anthropic 的退路，但反过来看——**这是 SpaceX 在告诉市场：我有信心你 90 天后还是离不开我**。
## 二、跨领域连接：**Copernicus、核扩散与 AI 的反人类中心论**
Friedberg 那段被埋没的洞见值得再放大一倍：他把 AI 的社会抗拒类比为*

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Did population size speed up human evolution? – David Reich
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的核心：当人口足够大，"进化"就变成了一个时间问题，而非人口问题

大多数人——包括很多受过训练的人——会本能地认为"人口越多，进化越快"。这条转录最锋利的地方在于它反向拆解了这个直觉：Reich 指出，一旦人口跨过某个阈值（大约一百万），进化速度的瓶颈就从"突变够不够"切换成了"时间够不够长"。这是一个相变（phase transition）式的洞见——系统不是平滑加速的，而是在某个临界点之后，主导变量被悄悄替换了。

真正让人意外的观察藏在这里：现代人类其实早已进入了"突变饱和"状态。每代约 2400 亿个新点突变，砸向只有 30 亿碱基的基因组——意味着基因组上每一个可能发生的突变，每代都被独立地"重写"约 100 次。换句话说，从原材料供给的角度看，人类基因组每一代都被"穷举"了一遍。进化不再缺少素材，它缺少的是让素材被筛选出来的世代数。这把"创新"和"扩散"两件事彻底分开了——而我们通常把它们混为一谈。

跨领域连接：这其实是一条"创新经济学"和"组织扩张"的隐藏定律

这里有个出人意料的跨领域映射。Reich 描述的两种机制——"突变限制"（mutation-limited）与"漂变淹没"（drift-swamped）——几乎一字不差地对应着组织和市场中的创新动力学。

  • 小公司/小社区像"小人口"：好点子（有利突变）出现得稀疏，要等很多"代"才冒出一个；而且即使冒出来，也容易被随机噪声（人事变动、运气、内部政治＝遗传漂变）淹没——除非这个点子的"选择系数"足够强（≥1%，即明显的竞争优势）。
  • 大公司/大市场像"大人口"：任何能被想到的点子都已经被想到了 100 遍（突变不再是瓶颈），于是胜出的不再是"谁先想到"，而是"哪个微弱优势有足够长的时间复利累积"。

由此推出一个反常识结论：在大型系统里，微小但持久的优势（0.01% 级别）依然几乎不可能胜出——不是因为没人尝试，而是时间尺度上需要数十万"代"。这解释了为什么成熟行业里"颠覆"往往来自强烈的、可测量的差异（半个百分点以上的真实优势），而不是来自精妙的微优化。微优化在大系统里被时间稀释掉了。

隐藏的系统性真相：人类把"自然选择的引擎"换挡了，却没人宣布

这条转录埋着一个没被点破的系统性真相。Reich 否定了"青铜时代人口变大解锁了进化"这个假说，但他否定的方式恰恰揭示了一件更深的事：人类文明的真正转折点，不是人口规模，而是"基因流"（gene flow）和时间深度的组合。

把镜头拉远——你会发现一个奇妙的对称。在生物层面，人类已经"突变饱和"，进化的瓶颈是时间；而在文化与技术层面，人类恰恰相反，正处在"突变爆炸但时间被极度压缩"的状态：想法的产生速度史无前例，世代周期（一个 idea 的迭代周期）从几十年缩短到几个月。于是文化进化进入了基因进化从未到达过的区域——突变不限制，漂变也因为传播极快而被部分克服。

这带来一个"啊哈时刻"：我们常说"人类已经停止生物进化、改由文化进化接管"。但 Reich 的框架给了这句话一个精确的机制解释——不是我们"选择"了文化进化，而是生物进化撞上了时间这堵墙（数十万年才能筛出弱信号），而文化进化恰好把时间这个变量给"破解"了。人类没有超越进化，人类只是找到了一个世代周期可以被人为缩短的新基质。这才是青铜时代之后真正改变的东西。

（见上方 insights 标签）

📝 转录文本

当人口很小时：突变是限制因素

When the populations are relatively small, only 10,000 people, you might have to wait dozens or hundreds of generations for that mutation to arise.

But when the populations are large, there's not mutation limiting anymore. Every mutation that can occur does occur.

There's 8 billion people in the world. There are maybe 30 new mutations every generation. So that's like — what is it? — it's like 240 billion new point mutations every generation. There's only 3 billion DNA bases in the genome. So every mutation that can occur does occur about 100 times every generation. And we're not mutation limited anymore.

And so it's not like you have to — that the mutations can arise again. They do arise again. But when the population's only 10,000, you have to wait dozens or hundreds of generations sometimes for the new mutation to occur.

青铜时代假说：人口规模是关键吗？

And so how likely is it that the thing that changes in the Bronze Age is just that the human population was big enough? So you — 3000 BC — you go to, I think, a population of 50 million-ish people. The population is big enough that, and the gene flow between different areas is high enough, such that things which don't have an overwhelming selection coefficient, which aren't overwhelmingly favored by evolution, are finally visible — visible to selection.

I think that's not likely to be true, but it's an extremely interesting thing to think about.

So I think already when population sizes are on the order of a million or so, every mutation that can occur does occur within a few generations. And so that's well before the Bronze Age. If you take the population even of a place like Europe, but also of other places — or maybe it's at the dawn of the Bronze Age or the farming period.

小人口中自然选择真的失效吗？

So the question you ask is: maybe when the population is small, natural selection doesn't work effectively. So a common thing that people think about with natural selection — and that is true — is that in small populations selection doesn't work effectively.

And that's because mutations bop around in frequency from generation to generation a lot in a small population, just randomly. So if you have a population of size 1,000, mutations will pop around by a frequency of 1 over 1,000 every generation. And if the selection coefficient is less than that, it will be drowned in the random popping around of frequencies due to genetic drift.

But that is already, for a population of 1,000, a 0.1% selection coefficient — that is very weak. We're talking about 1% effects, and that's much, very strong. It will work very well even in a population of a size of 1,000 or 10,000.

真正的瓶颈：选择系数的时间尺度

If you are talking about mutations of the type that will start rising only in large populations, but not small populations, those are selection coefficients that are on the scale of 1 over 10,000 or 1 over 100,000. And those ones will take 10,000 or 100,000 generations to rise in frequency, which is hundreds of thousands or millions of years. So that's not going to do anything over the time scale we're talking about. There's just a time scale issue.

So we're talking about strong, measurable selection coefficients on the order of half a percent or more in this study, and all of those are going to work in small populations or large populations. It's not going to be affected by the population size.

结论：阈值之后，时间跨度才是主导因素

Interesting, but you're saying more generally: once you hit a given threshold of population, the dominant factor is time span, and not population size.

Correct.

Okay, interesting. It's very interesting.

And it's actually not widely understood.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

大多数人——包括很多受过训练的人——会本能地认为"人口越多，进化越快"。这条转录最锋利的地方在于它**反向拆解了这个直觉**：Reich 指出，一旦人口跨过某个阈值（大约一百万），进化速度的瓶颈就从"突变够不够"切换成了"时间够不够长"。这是一个相变（phase transition）式的洞见——系统不是平滑加速的，而是在某个临界点之后，**主导变量被悄悄替换了**。
真正让人意外的观察藏在这里：现代人类其实早已**进入了"突变饱和"状态**。每代约 2400 亿个新点突变，砸向只有 30 亿碱基的基因组——意味着基因组上**每一个可能发生的突变，每代都被独立地"重写"约 100 次**。换句话说，从原材料供给的角度看，人类基因组每一代都被"穷举"了一遍。进化不再缺少素材，它缺少的是让素材被筛选出来的世代数。这把"创新"和"扩散"两件事彻底分开了——而我们通常把它们混为一谈。

item.title: [Business of Sport] Does County Cricket Have a Future, ‘Small’ Clubs Are Outsmarting The Elite & Chelsea’s Alonso Move
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

这期节目其实只在讲一件事：谁有资格"等"

表面上这是四个互不相干的话题——切尔西换帅、利物浦内乱、兰开夏郡板球内战、欧洲小球会逆袭。但如果把它们叠在一起看，会发现一个被主持人自己都没点破的隐藏主线：整期节目从头到尾都在讨论"资本的耐心半径"。

切尔西在车库里囤积五六十个年轻球员、却不敢真正让他们踢球，因为球迷不允许"输球的赛季"；兰开夏郡的会员要的是立刻赢约克郡；而布莱顿、布伦特福德、博多/格林特之所以能用六分之一的预算赢球，唯一的、也是最被低估的优势，是他们的球迷不期待夺冠，于是管理层获得了"难看一阵子"的许可证。Charlie 把它说成"necessity is the mother of invention"，但更准确的说法是：小球会买到的不是球员，而是时间。Gidney 那句"placation is the point"才是全场最锋利的一刀——大俱乐部花的钱里有一大块根本不是买竞技力，而是买"球迷别在停车场堵我"的赎金。所以这期真正的命题不是"钱能不能买来成功"，而是"你被允许等多久"。

一个会让你意外的观察：Gidney 是被自己的成功开除的

通常我们以为高管下台是因为失败。但兰开夏郡的故事是个罕见的反例——Gidney 恰恰是因为太成功，才暴露了自己。 他被聘来时任务只有一个：别让俱乐部破产。他把 900 万收入做到 3500 万、盖了两座酒店、夺回了英格兰测试赛资格——干得无可挑剔。可正因为财务危机这块"遮羞布"被他亲手扯掉了，会员们才腾出眼睛看见了真正在意的东西：球队烂。换句话说，他解决问题的能力太强，反而让大家看清了他解决的根本不是大家想解决的那个问题。这在管理学里叫古德哈特定律的恶性版本——当一个代理指标(收入、酒店、董事会多样性比例)被当作目标全力优化，它最终会与真正的目标(板球赢球)彻底脱钩，而且优化得越彻底，脱钩越刺眼。Sarah Storey 把"性别平权"写进辞职声明里当政绩，恰恰是这个机制的活标本：她在用一套漂亮的代理指标给自己打分，而股东(会员)手里拿的是另一张完全不同的成绩单。

跨领域连接：这不是体育新闻，这是三场不同形态的"资本结构战争"

把体育术语剥掉，你会看到三个经典的金融/治理模型在同一期节目里同台演出：

  • 兰开夏郡 = 一场股东维权(proxy fight)。 Atherton、David Lloyd 这些名宿不是"积怨的老会员"，他们是手握声誉资本的激进股东；董事会临时把电子投票改成"必须墨水亲笔签名"、把股东大会安排在银行假日——这是教科书级的董事会防御性壕沟战术(entrenchment)，和上市公司里用毒丸、错列董事会拖延维权者一模一样。主持人说这像"北朝鲜级别的治理"，但它其实只是华尔街的日常。
  • 约克郡 = 一家公司的 IPO 抉择。 互助制(mutualized)结构意味着"literally uninvestable"——这跟一家创业公司必须决定要不要从合伙制转成有限公司、引入外部股权一字不差。Headingley 的翻修是一笔无法靠"向会员每人摊派一万英镑"完成的资本支出，于是 Sanjay Patel 面对的根本不是板球问题，而是资本结构问题。
  • 切尔西的"manager vs head coach" = 纯粹的符号学。 Savage 那句"there is no difference"是对的：头衔不改变任何内部权责，它只改变感知。这与兰开夏郡用酒店和多样性董事会"marking their own homework"是同一种动作——用符号替代实质。

而最漂亮的跨领域连接藏在最后：Charlie 解释了为什么曼城、阿森纳这些豪门明明买得起、却学不会布莱顿的模式——因为豪门的球迷结构强制要求"即战力"，逼着他们只能买已成型(因而已被充分定价)的球员。这正是克莱顿·克里斯坦森《创新者的窘境》的完美翻版：在位者不是看不见颠覆式创新，而是其客户/股东结构在结构上禁止他们采纳它。 切尔西囤年轻球员却不敢让他们踢，等于柯达发明了数码相机却不敢卖——颠覆的钥匙就握在手里，但拿钥匙的那只手被绑住了。小球会的"贫穷"不是劣势，约束本身就是它们唯一买不到、也无法被复制的护城河。

Cold Open

That little toe rag Mo Salah has been out on his social media. "Show some respect. Show some respect, Charles." Honestly, that little toe rag — one of the greatest players in history.

We know that Spurs will be having to fight for their life in that last game of the season, because tonight Chelsea are going to decimate them. I'll be editing that in or out come the result tonight.

The good thing they did manage to spin — a nice little bit of PR for once for the club — was the Xabi Alonso hire, which I thought is well above our station currently.

There is a broader issue across English county cricket that's been far more historical. What I'm talking about is a disagreement. A furious row. A furious row between Lancashire County Cricket — a civil war — between Lancashire County Cricket and its members, a rebel group.

What this guy has done in 13 years is going to have positive repercussions for Lancashire County Cricket Club. So why has he just had to announce his retirement?

Some of these stories across Europe this year are absolutely incredible — of small teams outperforming some of the bigger ones, even with budgets that you would think, and certainly most fans would think, you wouldn't be able to achieve that.

How can I put this without running foul of the cancellation police?

Opening Banter — A Red-Letter Day

Good afternoon, Charlie. Afternoon, Charlie. How's things? You okay? — I'm very well. Yeah, very well.

It's a bit of a sort of red-letter day for you, isn't it? Because this evening, you are attending yet another football match. Chelsea–Spurs. Chelsea–Spurs.

Now, this is something that you've been looking forward to, I think, pretty much since Chelsea's own season died, which was probably in about 1895. It feels that long ago. Ever since then, you've been looking forward to this moment when you potentially have the opportunity to really put the squeeze on your nearest and dearest.

I will remind the listeners that have been with us since the start of the journey: there was a time about six weeks into us doing this where you thought that we were a curse, because Chelsea kept winning. And you were saying it's the breakdown curse — Chelsea cannot stop. So it has shifted very quickly.

But tonight is a massive game. Obviously, when you hear this — I'm sorry, it's Tuesday, you're going to be hearing this on Friday — you're going to know the outcome. We know that Spurs will be having to fight for their life in that last game of the season, because tonight Chelsea are going to decimate them. You'll be laughing at me. I'll be editing that in or out come the result tonight.

But it's very exciting, because… I still think, in all seriousness, I thought West Ham were appalling, by the way, against Newcastle at the weekend. And that was their huge chance. They could really have turned that screw and put the pressure on tonight.

Tottenham have a huge chance tonight. They only need a draw, really, and that will make them safe. So if Spurs can get a point, then great. But I do think if they don't get a point and Chelsea do win tonight, and it is a shootout on that last day of the season — Everton is a very difficult game. Not that Leeds for West Ham isn't, but from a Spurs perspective…

Well, I mean… Everton are a better side than Leeds. So, you know, Leeds have got nothing to play for. Everton have European football still to play for. Maybe not — maybe they've just dropped out the back end of that. The loss against Sunderland could have worked. Yeah, I think that's probably right.

Anyhow, a big night tonight. I shall look forward to hearing all about it. And I know that my various Chelsea friends — it's almost like this is the ultimate free-hit game, where all the pressure is on Spurs and Chelsea get to be the party poopers.

Well, you know, it was a nice warm-up at the weekend for the FA Cup final. So, you know…

I love the way you're getting quite good, this PR thing, how you can keep on spinning terrible results as being like good warm-ups. Yeah, I know. It was tragic.

You know, the worst thing, just finally on this — when I realised where the club's at at the moment, I went into the Gents after the game. And I've been to a number of FA Cup finals recently, or actually Cup finals where we've lost mostly to Liverpool, and the fury — you can sense it, right? Everyone's mad in the Gents. Well, that's another one gone. "Could have been worse." Which actually was the biggest dagger. It was respectable. A respectable defeat.

The Xabi Alonso Hire — Manager or Head Coach?

A bit of excitement out of this — and we'll touch on a couple of managers — because the good thing they did manage to spin, a nice little bit of PR for once for the club, was the Xabi Alonso hire, which I thought is well above our station currently. And I'm very interested to see how that pans out.

Yeah. Well, I mean, there's no doubt that in terms of the out-of-work managers, head coaches available to Chelsea, this was probably the most impressive appointment that they could make.

Now you'll be aware, and listeners and viewers will also be aware, that I am generally speaking a sceptic about the idea of manager as miracle worker. I'm a believer that, as part of the overall jigsaw, a really good head coach or manager — and we're going to come on to the moment about what the difference is between those two things — can make 5% difference. Maybe in an absolutely exceptional situation, 10% difference. But what they cannot do on a sustained basis is turn around a shit show, right? A shit show is a shit show, and you can blame your manager all you want, but fundamentally it's going to be about sorting out the shit show. Man United have shown that relatively well in the last decade. That's right.

I'm afraid my concern for Chelsea is that the fundamental underlying structural problems with the club — I do not believe that having a different head coach, however perceptually better he might be (and there's an interesting argument to be had: is he miles better than Maresca, given where Maresca's about to go? Probably not, right?)…

So for me, it's the kind of thing which is really great for getting the fans off your back. The fans go: "Hey, we've heard of this guy. He was a great player. He's had a bit of success as a manager one season. He's been at Real Madrid." That's good brand recognition, sort of brand-name recognition type of stuff. Tick, tick. So we feel big about ourselves because we're hiring the guy who was at Real Madrid until earlier this year.

"Oh, and they've made him manager." Because you just slipped in there and you said head coach. The head coach doesn't change anything. But Charlie — he's the manager. So I'm sitting here going, this is the first manager Chelsea have hired since 2013, with José Mourinho. He was the last Chelsea coach to hold the manager title, not head coach. So I think this is a piece of genius from the ownership, don't you?

Well, the difference between head coach and manager is just absolute nonsense. It's not often that I agree with Robbie Savage, but I was in the car the other day — Saturday or Sunday — and he was on 606. And of course he is currently a head coach or manager, right? And somebody phoned in saying it's amazing, it's fantastic, it's transformational that Alonso is going to be a manager, not a head coach. And you'd hear Savage in the background just laughing, saying, "There is no difference. There is no difference."

Zero difference — because the only difference you have between different clubs is internal processes and responsibilities, right? You can have a head coach who has a very prominent position within the recruitment setup according to their contract, according to internal processes, and you can have a manager who has no say over the recruitment whatsoever. It is entirely perception-driven — by fans on the one hand, and then from a status perspective by head coaches or managers on the other hand. They like the sound of it better. It sounds a bit more important. But ultimately, there are managers who report to technical directors, and there are head coaches who report directly into owners. You have to look at what the internal structure is to understand whether actually any significant difference has been made.

Chelsea's Real Problem — Five Sporting Directors

So, when you remember — I think we talked about it on a previous show — we were talking about the Chelsea ownership post-Rosinia saying they were going to review and reflect and do things slightly differently, look to change some of the mistakes that they'd maybe made previously. You can see this is an easy win, where they're probably turning around internally patting themselves on the back going, "Well, that'll get them off our backs for a bit."

Yeah, so I think there are two things. At the time I said, you know, is this just "we're not going to keep on hiring inexperienced managers"? I mean, leaving aside the fact that Xabi Alonso is actually quite an inexperienced manager — but leaving that to one side — a very well-known former super-elite player versus a more experimental type of young head coach like Rosinia. That's clearly a decision which they've made, and I think that was an appropriate decision, understanding the challenges that face the bosses of super-elite clubs these days. To have been somebody who has neither experienced the dugout at the very top level nor even experienced the pitch at the very top level — I think that's a jump too far.

If you come from a non-super-elite playing background, then you need to really go and make your bones on the management side. By the time Jürgen Klopp becomes manager of Liverpool, he's a very experienced manager by that point, right? Even though he didn't have much of a playing career, he's a very, very experienced manager by that point. Yeah — José Mourinho won the Champions League with Porto. And so on and so forth. Arsène Wenger, the same.

So I think that bit of it is sound. And I'm not saying, by the way, that I have any problem with Alonso being called manager. I don't. Manager, head coach, whatever. You're just saying it's not going to change. That itself does not change anything.

What I need to understand is: do you still have five sporting directors? — Yes. — You do? — Yeah. — You sure? — Yeah. — So can you explain to me what each of them does? Exactly.

Which is where you'd say: from what I'm understanding, if there was any real change, what you would have seen is a structural change above, so that the processes in place previously were clearly not going to be in place. 100%.

What I would have wanted to have seen is the following. First of all, the ownership making a statement: "We have been far too involved and far too visibly involved with the playing side of this football club. We are now going to step back into a supervisory role, and we're going to appoint senior people to take up these command-and-control positions, and they are going to be accountable to us and the shareholders, and they may be accountable to the fans." That's the first thing I'd want to have seen.

I then want to have seen those five sporting directors rationalised to one. One sporting director. Now, if you need to have three or four senior people who work into that sporting director, that's fine. But you need to have one person who's accountable to the ownership — but also clarity that the head coach or the manager, whatever you call him, is in partnership to and reports into that person. Very clearly that person.

Why is this important? I'll tell you why. It's the conversation that happens before a match and after a match. It's the conversation that happens in pre-season and in post-season. These are analytical discussions, highly technical discussions, where effectively the head coach / manager needs to be held to account in such a way that he feels on a level with the person who is making the assessments about his work. He needs to look across the table at somebody and go, "Fundamentally, I might like it or I don't like it, but this guy knows what he's doing."

Because sometimes — I've just observed this in a functional way, at clubs I've worked at — two or three days before the game there'll be a little chat between the technical director and the head coach. "What are you looking to do this Saturday?" "Well, I think I'm going to make a couple of tweaks in the system. I'm thinking I'm going to probably bring this player in from the cold," etc. A 15-, 20-minute conversation. "Are you sure? Can you walk us through your thought process on that?" And 80 to 90% of the time, the head coach comes away — "Yeah" — and that's then what happens. But 10 to 15% of the time, the technical director is able to challenge in such a way that maybe that tweak doesn't happen, or maybe there's some other compensation made.

So that professional relationship — between the most senior football person in the club, who is not the manager, together with the manager — that is the most important relationship in the entire organisation. And you cannot have five of them.

So until Chelsea implement a functional system, and until the owners get their hands and feet out of the dressing room, then my suspicion is whoever the head coach or the manager is going to be is not going to make a very substantial difference.

Liverpool and Mo Salah

Yeah. Liverpool — they've also been in the news recently. That little toe rag Mo Salah has been out on his social media. "Show some respect. Show some respect, Charles." Honestly, that little toe rag — one of the greatest players in history. Honestly — he used to be employed by your club. I know, and I'm just jealous.

But no — Mo Salah, again, causing some problems for Liverpool. For the club — well, for Slot, should we say. On his way out, he's doing his best to burn the building down.

It was interesting. He put a post out after the game against Aston Villa, expressing his dissatisfaction with how the club is at the moment, but particularly with how they're playing football. And I think the most notable phrase he used was "heavy metal football" in his post. He knew what he was doing. Because he knew exactly what he was doing, right?

And "heavy metal football", for those of you that don't know, is very specifically related to Jürgen Klopp's style of play. So — what do you think he was saying? He was saying we need to get back to the days where Jürgen Klopp was managing this club.

What he's basically saying is, I think — Arne Slot's a heap of shit. Yeah. Right. That was it. Forget it. Mo Salah doesn't give a damn about Jürgen Klopp. He's far too selfish to think about that. He's using that. "Show some respect for one of the greatest players." Selfish is good. In sport — elite sport — selfish is good in many ways. So he doesn't give a damn about Jürgen Klopp. He is using that very knowingly as a tool, to get a dig in at Arne Slot and the Liverpool hierarchy on his way out the door.

Yeah. So Liverpool just look like a bit of a mess, don't they? Well, dare I say it, Charles — this was the subject of a breakdown two months ago, when we noted that there was a pattern of behaviour, events, stuff over a fairly protracted period of time: whilst short-term results had papered over some of the cracks that were starting to emerge, the cracks were very much there. And what happens in the pressure-cooker situation of a football club is that once there are cracks, those cracks just start to widen.

I'm just being really interested to see what happens this summer at Liverpool. Obviously there's the very obvious, back-page question of: do they keep Slot? And going back to Chelsea, that's one change they could make. But I think there are bigger problems here. I think they do — I think they keep him. You think they keep him, right? Maybe so.

But what is the current purpose of that club? When is the ownership going to speak to what their plan is, what their objectives are? It was very clear for the first 10 years of their ownership: the club hasn't won anything for a long time, we've come here to win the Premier League and win the Champions League. And they did it. Amazing.

And what we said in that segment we did on Liverpool a couple of months ago was: since those things have been achieved — first of all, we heard the club was going to go up for sale, then it wasn't; then we heard they were going to move to a multi-club model, then they weren't; first we heard Michael Edwards was leaving the club, then he came back; then we heard Ian Graham was leaving the club, and then there was no senior data person there. There's just a lack of clarity about what's really going on at Liverpool on a strategic level.

And in the absence of that kind of obvious plan, I'm afraid, when the cat's away, the mice start to play. There needs to be somebody who steps forward and gives some obvious vocal leadership into this situation. If Slot is to stay, somebody needs to be there to visually and vocally support him and explain what place he fills within the future strategy.

Aaron Rai and the PGA Championship

Quick nod to Aaron Rai this weekend — the winner of the PGA Championship. I thought that was amazing. The first Englishman in 100 years to win the competition, taking him a nice, cool $3.69 million.

Think of all the really storied British players during the last 30, 40 years. So it's very specifically English, this was. Of course, Rory's won it, right? But even just think about really well-known English players over that period who have neither won, nor in many cases even come close to winning, the US PGA. For somebody to come along — not Justin Rose, not Nick Faldo, not Lee Westwood, none of these guys — somebody who even quite a few people who follow golf a bit wouldn't probably have known a huge amount about… it's an amazing story.

He's from Wolverhampton. He plays with two gloves, interestingly. If you've seen it, he plays with two gloves because it was so cold when he was growing up that it kept him warm. He also has iron covers — the story behind that is that his family really couldn't afford to buy more clubs, so it made him look after his clubs a lot better. It shows you the amazing places that some of these stories can come from. And golf, again, with that kind of elitist mentality quite often shifting around — you get some amazing stories still in it.

Jannik Sinner and the Problem of Dominance

The other thing — we were just talking last week, we spoke a lot about tennis. And Jannik Sinner did something very few — well, only one other person, Novak Djokovic, has ever done — the weekend, which is he's won the career Golden Masters. That means he has won the full set of Masters 1000, ATP 1000 titles: Cincinnati, Indian Wells, Madrid, Rome, Monte Carlo, Shanghai, Paris, Miami, and the Canadian.

So that's a hell of an achievement for a guy at 24 years old. Novak Djokovic did it when he was 31. Sinner's won 36 of his 38 matches this year, which is quite frightening.

And part of our conversation last week did touch very slightly on this. But the top two — Alcaraz and Sinner — have created such a separation of superiority that, with Carlos Alcaraz now out injured, there is a danger for the sport that this just becomes an absolute procession over the next few months of Jannik Sinner holding a trophy.

I feel very harsh in saying that's a bad thing, because he's just such an extraordinary athlete. But from the perspective of a fan, you want to see some more competition.

And I just had a look at a couple of things. Firstly, he's actually only beaten Alcaraz once in winning those Masters titles. The majority of the other players he's beaten are very good players — and I would hate to disrespect them — but they wouldn't be what we would class as the higher echelons of the game. I looked at Novak Djokovic when he did it. You looked at who he had beaten in the finals of those competitions: it was Federer, Nadal, Del Potro, Andy Murray, Stan Wawrinka, Marin Čilić, I think, was in there.

So you can only ever beat what's in front of you. But I do feel that tennis — and this particularly showed it — has a slight issue at the moment with two absolute runaway talents and the rest of the field who, if they don't step up their game, could harm the appeal of the global sport, if we're not getting the competitions that we really want to see.

Interesting. Do you know the last time that a top player went on an unbelievable hot streak on tour? John McEnroe, 1984 — won, I think, 83 out of his 85 matches. Oh, I saw that, yeah. 83 out of 85 matches. And that was in an era where it was very competitive. That's amazing. That was against people like Jimmy Connors, and these types of guys — Ivan Lendl. You forget he was that good, don't you? Well, sorry — I forget he was that good, because it was before my time. So you've known him subsequently as a personality, as opposed to remembering the tennis genius that it was.

Now, the slight thing — again, this is so difficult to visit on Jannik Sinner, because what can he be other than fundamentally an outstanding tennis player who also seems like a pretty nice guy? What's wrong with that? Absolutely fine. The slight problem is: if you have a very, very dominant player in a sport who also is not long on the personality side, that's when you do have a bit of a challenge.

In other words, when McEnroe was winning 83 matches out of 85, he was such box office himself. People wanted him to be in every final — because even if it was him winning all the time, in the process of winning, he would chew out the umpires and the linesmen. He'd be a terribly bad sport, he would win gracelessly, but then afterwards would say sorry and all this type of stuff. He himself was absolute box office.

And the problem with Jannik Sinner is that he ain't. And that's not his fault. For him to become the global star that he could maybe become, he unfortunately does need Alcaraz. He needs to have the yin and the yang. He needs to have somebody who, when he does beat, the sporting public goes, "Wow — do you know what, you're a quiet guy, but that was fantastic."

It'll be really interesting to see if anyone can up their game. But fair play to him. Well done. It's an incredible achievement. And these two are going to dominate tennis for the next few years. It's going to be brilliant to see.

Listener Comments — Strikes, Posh Blokes and "The Drunk Down"

On the tennis topic — I was looking at some of the comments and questions that we have. And we do get a huge number of comments and questions. I think at some point, Charlie and I are considering doing a separate Q&A show, effectively, to deal with the huge numbers of comments and questions we get. Because if we did too many of them in a normal segment, we'd end up not actually covering anything in detail.

But we did discuss last week the topic of the top tennis players potentially going on strike. I've got it as the top of mine as well. Go on. What did someone say to you? Well — they didn't say it to me. They said it about me. I'm very hurt. Do you want me to read it out?

The background, the context, is that I was sceptical about whether a strike by the top tennis players earning 20 million bucks a year was really appropriate. And Franzen 1016 turned around and said: "Posh guy against strike action. Shocker." One of my all-time favourite comments, Franzen 1016. Thank you for that.

Charlie then decided to get on the phone to me and actually push back against this statement, because of a past life that he may have had, that you may all find very hard to believe. The truth is, Franzen — if that is indeed your name — probably unlike most of us on this show, and certainly many of our listeners, I was actually for a long time a trade-union shop steward who passed the last major strike battles on Fleet Street in the newspaper industry. So I am very much not a posh bloke who's against strike action. I have actually had the privilege of being a trade-union leader.

You're a picket-line guy. Well, do you know what — picket line was only a few days away when the Telegraph caved. I was working the Telegraph group at the time, and before the strike actually happened, the management called us in and we had a sensible discussion.

Brilliant. Well, there you go, Franzen. Actually — to think about it — instead of "there you go, Franzen", I think what you actually should be saying is "there you go, Jannik." Charlie's shown you the way. Just hold your nerve long enough and the big guys will bend. No, I completely agree.

There were a couple of others that caught my eye recently. Alistair Wood commented last week with a brilliant addition. He said he's "fully on board for a spin-off show called The Drunk Down" — referencing the couple of beers that may have snuck into the studio. We're recording this in the afternoon, not the evening, hence why it's back on the waters. But we did get a lot of love for it.

Yeah — and there's a little bit of me that feels that we haven't been entirely true to ourselves, Charles. What we said last week — because it's not 10 a.m., is it? It's not 10 a.m. It's 3. It's halfway through the afternoon, which I know for some people who work in respectable careers might be a little bit early to get on the beers. But referring to my previous journalistic career — if you hadn't had a pint by this time of the day, you weren't a proper journalist, I can tell you, let alone a serious podcast these days.

And what we actually said last week was that if it worked, then we would keep on drinking during podcasts. The show last week did our record numbers. It did. And yet we have not followed through. We have shown that sort of lack of consistency and lack of clarity in leadership that we so criticise in other sporting leaders. I just don't remember saying it. I probably — you know, I've had one too many. That's because it was me that said it. Anyway — "The Drunk Down". I like it.

And the final one I'll bring up, which I enjoyed greatly for one reason in particular — it said: "I wonder if you two should try a radio phone-in format. I'm awful with names, but the guy on the left can go on monologues to rival any LBC presenter." Now, he's watching it, right — so that's our camera angle. So the guy on the left is you.

My favourite bit about that is "I'm awful with names." The best thing about this show is you only need to remember one name. So, Thomas Johnson, who sent that comment in — it is the Charlies. It is Charlie on the left. Charlie Jr., if you want to refer to me, is how it's generally phrased.

Reading through some of these, it's brilliant to see you guys send it in. Honestly, in all seriousness, you contribute amazingly to it, and we love it. And we will absolutely look to speak more to you and answer more of the comments — because there was even a call for us to cover kabaddi the other day. Well, you say "even" — back in the 1990s, kabaddi was the coming sport. It used to be on Channel 4. So I'm super looking forward to getting back into that.

County Cricket — The Lancashire Civil War

Well, Charlie, thank you for the show today. No — I joke, because we actually have a great topic to get into now. And one that I've been keen to do — we've kind of moved around it a little bit in the past as we've talked about cricket in this country, in particular in England — but it's of particular relevance at the moment, because there is both a broader issue across English county cricket that's been far more historical, and also a very kind of real-time example where we can bring some of the information out and give you some proper context.

What I'm talking about is a disagreement — I don't really know, again you'll probably say I should put a stronger word on there — a disagreement between… A furious row. A furious row between Lancashire County Cricket — a civil war — between Lancashire County Cricket and its members, or a rebel group.

Well, you see — you've just made a false distinction there. You've said there's a row between Lancashire County Cricket Club and its members. But of course, Lancashire County Cricket Club is its members, because it is a mutualised entity, which means that it is member-owned. And effectively, it's a row between the members of Lancashire County Cricket Club and the executives and non-executives who are employed and engaged to act on their behalf.

And that makes it a proper civil war. That's not like an owner of some club and his paid executives in some fight against the fan base. This is the owners of the club fighting against the people who, in theory, they appoint. Quite an interesting situation.

So — Lancashire County Cricket Club. Let's just put a bit of context around this. There are three, maybe four, giants of English county cricket.

That is Surrey — who are the biggest county cricket club by revenue and by success in their history. They own the Oval, have a great catchment area, they're right in the centre of London, and so on. And they have been very successful in the last 10 years or so, as well as having big revenues, as well as updating the Oval. Big success story. Surrey County Cricket Club — very successful, just because it's going to feed in: very successful on non-cricket. Absolutely.

Warwickshire County Cricket Club plays at Edgbaston — a real bear pit for England cricket, probably one of the grounds with the biggest atmosphere. It's a really rowdy, loud place. Again, they make good money. Again, generally speaking, Warwickshire have been more or less successful in the last 10 to 15 years.

Then you have Yorkshire, who play at Headingley. And Headingley is a historic Test cricket ground of England, which is currently not being used very much by England because it's in bad nick. We'll come back on to that later on, after discussing Lancashire.

And then you've got Lancashire. Lancashire play at Old Trafford. And Old Trafford is another of the great historic Test venues of English Test cricket. So you've got Warwickshire at Birmingham, you've got Surrey in London — albeit they share London with Middlesex, but they're much the bigger club within London — and then you've got Lancashire in Manchester, which would be the third biggest city in the country. And then you've got Yorkshire at Leeds, which would be one of the next contenders for the biggest cities in the country.

So where do we start this? Lancashire County Cricket Club — huge county cricket history, very successful in the past, particularly in one-day cricket; responsible for a lot of the most famous England cricketers — Andrew Flintoff, Mike Atherton, Jimmy Anderson. These are quite recent superstars of English cricket. And also the home of a lot of very famous overseas players who have come to play here — people like Clive Lloyd, the West Indian great. This is a big, big club. If you go to Old Trafford, you're very aware: wow, this is a big place. This is a serious, serious place.

But around 2010, it effectively was facing bankruptcy. Old Trafford had lost Test-match status, because it was deemed as a facility not to be up to holding Test matches. It was crumbling, basically. And as a result of that — because when you're not hosting international cricket, when you are one of these big county cricket clubs, that places a lot of pressure on your finances, because you're still financing a big arena but you're not having the international matches that help you really do that.

Whereas if you're a smaller county cricket club, your operating costs are much lower. You don't get the England matches to bring that extra revenue in, but you might at least be able to just operate in a rather smaller type of way.

It's very important. I spoke to the chief executive of Surrey, Steve Elworthy, and he outlined that an international cricket match for Surrey is worth five to six million. And Surrey's total revenues are 55 to 60 million. Right. So one single Oval Test match is 10% of Surrey's total revenue — and they will quite often have two. So if you think about Surrey's revenue of 55 million quid, 10 million quid of that coming from a couple of Test matches — but they also have one-day internationals and so on — you might end up getting towards almost half of that annual total revenue coming from hosting England matches. It's big business.

And that's where Lancashire — Old Trafford losing the status to host it, where revenue is still good but not at 55 to 60 million — is a big problem for them, and therefore required something to happen to change that.

Daniel Gidney's 13 Years

Right. So in 2012, in a terrible situation, they appoint a man called Daniel Gidney. And Daniel Gidney had previously been running the Ricoh Arena, which is Coventry City's stadium. But at the time, Coventry had lost control around that time of their own stadium, and it was being run as a general leisure facility. They had a casino. They had a hotel. They also have a pitch.

Wasps played there. Wasps were playing there. And Coventry were sometimes playing there, sometimes not, depending on the deal that was between Coventry City and the Ricoh Arena. But the Ricoh Arena was a significant-size standalone venue without operating its own sports club. So Daniel Gidney was operating the Ricoh Arena as a sports-business executive, with no responsibility for playing matters.

And then prior to that, he ran hospital catering services for Sodexo — again, large-scale venues with lots of varying catering needs, etc. So these are quite complex jobs, where you operate under quite a lot of pressure and where the margins are thin.

So Daniel Gidney arrives at Lancashire County Cricket Club having made a good job of what are difficult jobs — operating significant-size leisure venues. Lancashire have lost all of their England contracts, have a crumbling venue, not very well-run club, going bankrupt, etc. So Daniel Gidney comes in — this is an important part of the story — Daniel Gidney comes into Lancashire Cricket Club with the club facing bankruptcy, in the abyss, in absolute, total catastrophe. With the club very much acting as a county cricket club — mutualised, owned and run by its members, but effectively, in appointing a chief executive like him, they delegated authority, passed the parcel for turning the club's finances around.

And it's hard to say that Daniel Gidney has not done a very, very, very good job of the job that he was appointed to do. So, 13 years later, that £9 million has turned into £35 million a year of revenue. They've regained their England status. Old Trafford has been effectively redeveloped — there are two hotels there, a conference centre, and the whole thing is now not quite on the same level as the Oval, but getting there. It's a very serious, great venue. I was there last summer for the England–India Test match. It was great. What a great venue.

But the problem is — when you have a brilliant thing happening at a cricket club, at a sports team, and really the standout success that you have is the opening of a Hilton hotel in your tenure, you can see where there's going to be probably some tension.

Right. So as a sports executive and a sports investor, I just want to give the beleaguered Gidney a round of applause. What this guy has done in 13 years is going to have positive repercussions for Lancashire County Cricket Club for 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40 years.

So why has he just had to announce his retirement? Because the membership are very upset with the state of the cricket team. And most importantly, with the traditional form of cricket — the four-day county cricket system — and fans of Lancashire. Oh, and they've been crap at one-day as well.

Right — so it's not that they're just very upset. It goes well beyond that. They've submitted a vote of no confidence — in the board. This is not just in Gidney. This is in the board as a whole.

So these rebels aren't just some dusty members. This includes the aforementioned legends. People like David Lloyd and Mike Atherton — the elite of the England cricket establishment. These are the people who played for England, coached England, who hold the biggest media jobs in the country. These are the most high-profile cricket people that there are in the whole world, who also happen to be legends and members of Lancashire County Cricket Club, and have submitted a vote of no confidence, backed by thousands of the members, in the board.

That has effectively, in some way, shape or form, made Gidney's position untenable, and he has announced his retirement. But the board is still in place, and the board is trying to resist this. You can't even call it a takeover, because they'd be taking over their own club. How do you describe it? It's like taking back control of their own club.

Now, what's the complaint? Charlie mentioned it, really — which is that during those 13 years of business regeneration, Lancashire County Cricket Club have been, frankly speaking, crap at cricket. During that time, they have won one trophy — it was a one-day trophy of some kind — and they have been relegated from the first division of the championship.

So if you think about Lancashire's status within the game as being one of the big three or four clubs, that's kind of equivalent to Tottenham going down, right? So Tottenham have gone down. And on top of that, all the former Tottenham legends have basically said, "We don't think that there is anybody at this club who understands anything about football." That's just what Tottenham fans say. But the difference is: whereas Tottenham fans do not own Tottenham, Lancashire County Cricket Club members do own Lancashire.

But the board they have is resisting this, and have basically started tweaking the rules around how such a no-confidence motion might be treated. There was no wet signature, wasn't there? Right — so first of all, historically, they have allowed electronic voting. Now they've said you actually pretty much have to turn up with an ink and quill and literally sort of do this. And secondly, they decided to hold the AGM on a bank holiday Monday. So this is like North Korean levels of governance, right? Genius. It is a form of genius, you're right.

Diversity vs. Cricket — Marking Your Own Homework

So this is civil war. Now, there's something which I found interesting about this. Having just applauded Daniel Gidney for his business acumen — and what he's done, from very difficult circumstances, to turn around the financial fortunes of Lancashire County Cricket Club — there's another element to his legacy which he is very proud of, and which the chairman of Lancashire County Cricket Club, Dame Sarah Storey, the multi-gold-medal-winning Paralympian, is also very proud of.

So this is a quote from Dame Sarah Storey on Daniel Gidney's resignation: "In addition [to turning the business around], Daniel has driven gender equity at both board and executive level, creating one of the most diverse leadership teams in UK elite sport. Beyond the financials, he has also been a passionate and influential advocate for gender equity."

So this is regarded by the board as being a key part of Daniel Gidney's strength — what he's done. Basically, really focused on women's cricket, on pushing girls' cricket, and on getting lots of people onto the board who either come from different communities, or who are female, or whatever it might be.

What the rebel leaders — including the former England legends — are saying is: you don't have anyone on your board who understands anything about cricket. And the result of that is that we're crap at cricket. And the point of our club, because we're a non-profit-making organisation, is not to open hotels. The point of our club is to be good at cricket.

So this is where the whole thing sort of really runs up against each other. And my experience of elite sport — having seen it through Formula One and horse racing and football — is that, how can I put this without running foul of the cancellation police? I think I've probably already been cancelled. Charlie, I think you've been cancelled quite a few times. Long cancelled. That's why I have to come on a podcast. I should be on the BBC. I have to come on a podcast and say… because basically I've been cancelled. Someone did actually say in the comments last week that you were obviously very misunderstood on Sunderland 'Til I Die, and he's delighted that you've come across in this manner. I don't know what he thought I'd come across like on Sunderland 'Til I Die. Anyhow, there we go.

Now — elite sport, and I'm just going to put this blandly, has no space for these bromides. Unfortunately, elite sport is brutal. You cannot afford to be box-ticking when it comes to elite sport.

Can you run a successful county cricket club that encourages girls' sport? Absolutely, yes. Can you stuff your board full of people who are there to tick various diverse boxes? No. No, you can't — because if your competitors are stuffing their boards with people who are experts in elite cricket and you do not, then you are going to fail at cricket, and then you're going to have problems.

What's happened here is that the board of Lancashire County Cricket Club have been marking their own homework. They've been saying, "We've built two hotels, and we've welcomed onto our board lots of people from diverse backgrounds." And the members of Lancashire County Cricket Club are going, "We don't care about the extra hotel, the second hotel. We don't care about how diverse your board is. We want to beat Yorkshire." And we are members of this cricket club because we want to be part of something which is successful in sporting terms.

But they also want to exist. And that's the balance, right? That's the friction you were talking about.

Well — I'm going to come on to that with Yorkshire. But can I just jump in there? Because on your point, Daniel Gidney's response to a lot of the attempts to remove him and the board have been interesting. And I have a "Belgian football" quote that he put out.

He said: "ECB Chairman Richard Thompson said we want cricket to be the most inclusive sport — which is a big thing to say. Let's look at a vote of no confidence in our board. We've got some amazing people, we've got a number of women, and we've got John Abrahams and Naveen Singh. And this feels a bit like 'I'm an old, white, entitled man that wants to get on the board of the club, and they don't like the fact that in our county partnership agreements we have to have 40% women on our board and 22% representatives of ethnic minority.' They acknowledge that it is not legally binding, but they want to put pressure on the board to resign. Effectively, that is bullying and intimidatory behaviour."

Of course he has to resign after that, because he's basically abusing his own employers — which is not viable. That's when they're going for the board.

Yeah. My point — we have fans that want it all, and we're all guilty of it. There is an element here of, as a chief executive of a sports club, as you know — any decision that you make, you have to appreciate the fans need to come on that with you in some way. Because the majority of the time, if you're coming in to really shake something up, it's probably not going to be in a way that the fans are used to experiencing their club or their sport.

So if you are going to try and save a club from bankruptcy, and it's not going to be done by improving how they're playing on the pitch, you need to make sure that that membership is absolutely integral to how you bring that club forward over the next 10 years, whatever it may be — even if some of it is completely pointless posturing, even if some of it doesn't have any influence on the outcome. This is a hearts-and-minds game at the top level as much as it is a delivery game. Totally agree.

Yorkshire and the Demutualisation Question

So yesterday I was listening to the radio in the car, and they were interviewing the chief executive of Yorkshire County Cricket Club, a guy called Sanjay Patel. I'd never heard Sanjay Patel speak before, but I was very, very impressed.

Why? Because Yorkshire County Cricket Club is in the middle of a controversy, or a contretemps. It's not a civil war — it's not like that. But the board of Yorkshire County Cricket Club wants to demutualise Yorkshire County Cricket Club. That is, to take it away from being a member-owned organisation, into being a private limited company — because that then gives it a structure which means it can attract external capital to come into the club to do what needs to be done with that club. Chief of which is developing Headingley.

Because if you remember what we said — Old Trafford had lost its Test-match status 10, 15 years ago. Headingley has not officially lost its Test-match status, but it's not due to hold a Test match for the next two or three years, which is pretty dramatic for Headingley — and disastrous financially, as we discussed earlier on.

And I'd say, as a fan, I'm devastated by that, because I think Headingley is an amazing place to have a Test match. And Leeds is a great place to have a Test match. The history of the place is unbelievable. And the atmosphere — we were saying about Edgbaston earlier on — you know, the Western Terrace at Headingley is serious atmosphere.

So there are many amongst the membership base who are in favour of this. And there are also many, as you might imagine, amongst the Yorkshire County Cricket Club membership base who are against this, because they don't like the idea of private money coming into the club where it might, quote, expect a return — because that's what investment is. That's the key, right?

So Sanjay Patel was being quizzed in some detail, and quite forcefully, by the radio interviewer, who was putting the points very eloquently — actually — of the people who do not like this move. His riposte, which was delivered very calmly and in a very balanced way, was:

"If we want Yorkshire County Cricket Club to be amongst the elite performers in cricket, and if we want to make sure that we offer young people from Yorkshire the opportunity to become what they need to be able to become, Headingley has to be able to become a world-class venue. The amount of capital that is required to do that is not achievable from within our membership base. We can't simply put out a capital call to our members saying 'by the way, we need £10,000 from each of you.' Therefore, the money has to come from external sources. We can't achieve it simply through bank lending, and therefore it needs to be private capital. Private capital can't come into a charitable situation except as a donation. If we could achieve a donation, that would be lovely — but no one's come forward and offered a donation. What we do know is that there are people with private capital who would be interested in taking an equity stake in Yorkshire County Cricket Club in return for putting money in — not to then control the cricket club, because the structures we're looking at putting in place would not enable that, but to make sure that if Yorkshire County Cricket Club did become very much more successful, and the commercial limited-company bit of that therefore was perceived to become more valuable, that ultimately, of course, they could potentially sell their stake and get their investment back — maybe even including a bit of a profit, maybe including a bit of a loss. But without that, how can we actually bring in the money to redevelop Headingley and get England cricket matches coming back to Leeds? Well, you can't."

So the answer is: you can't. He could so easily have gone for the Yorkshire County Cricket Club members, as Daniel Gidney did. He kept on resisting it. He kept on saying: "If it is decided by the members that they want to stay fully mutualised, that is their right. It's their club. It's their right. It's my job as chief executive to lay the facts out in front of them as to what the implications of that would be. They would have to accept that there will not be England matches here. They would have to accept that, as a county cricket club, we would not be able to compete financially at the top of the county championship against the other big boys of English cricket. If everyone says that they are okay with that, then I too am okay with that. But I want to make sure that they fully understand the implications of it."

I thought he handled it absolutely brilliantly. But he kept coming back to it. The reason why I brought it up is that Sanjay Patel kept coming back to being successful at cricket. This was a continuing thread in the conversation — "we want Yorkshire Cricket Club to be successful at cricket."

And this, I think, is the thing that has provoked the massive divide at Lancashire. If you read the quotes from Daniel Gidney and Sarah Storey, the chief executive and chairman of Lancashire County Cricket Club — not just now, but over a period of time — their own priorities don't appear to be the same as the people who ultimately employ them. And that doesn't matter which business you're in, Charlie — that's a problem.

So that's what I meant by communication. If Gidney's great in one thing, and he's smashing it out of the park, it doesn't really matter if he's not bringing the fans along with him. But would you not need, then, a chairman who did represent…? You would. And the problem here is they've doubled down on two people who come from exactly the same point of view. And Sarah Storey and Daniel Gidney's point of view is not the same as the members' point of view.

So for context, Yorkshire have a chairman called Colin Graves. Indeed. Who used to be the ECB chairman. Mr Cricket. He's brilliant. Mr Cricket. And actually, I think he'll be leading the meeting with the shareholders very soon to decide on this.

And the demutualisation piece is an interesting one right now, because English cricket has just had significant investment. People will know about the Hundred. Interestingly, Yorkshire were the only team to sell 100% of their franchise. So they had gone from the Northern Superchargers to Sunrisers Leeds — one of the IPL groups. And they sold — it was about £100 million. And this money was very carefully segmented in certain ways, so that clubs couldn't just use it to offset their running losses, basically. It had to be used for projects.

So when you're looking at this kind of double thing of demutualisation plus selling the stake, having a very specific purpose to go and create long-term value, bring Test matches back — that's an investable model. So if I was looking at demutualisation right now, I'd go, as an outside investor, I could actually attract something. I'm making my county cricket team very investable, which they haven't been for a very long time.

Well, that's right. They have been literally uninvestable. Well — uninvestable. As a mutualised business, you are literally uninvestable.

The Finances of County Cricket

To be really clear — as we were going through this, I just took a slightly broader look at some of the numbers. Leonard Curtis last year did a very comprehensive financial study conducted on English domestic cricket.

The total revenue of all 18 counties was £306 million. To put that into context, Aston Villa's last year was £378 million. So all 18 counties made £306 million. Eight of the 18 counties ran at a loss in their financial report that year. I actually thought it would be more than that. But without ECB support and Hundred income, it's very questionable whether some of the counties would survive.

And that came through when looking at the percentage of revenue that was reliant on ECB funding. So Surrey, for example — we've mentioned 55-ish million in revenue — 6% of that revenue is from ECB funding. You look at Northamptonshire at the bottom end: 71% of their revenue is ECB funding.

Yeah — so you're looking at ECB funding basically being about £3 million per club. But that £3 million at Surrey is 5%, whereas the £3 million at Northampton, whose total revenues are £5.5 million… What's that? About 70%. A revenue ratio of 12 to 1, really, between Surrey there.

You've got the big three — Surrey, Lancashire and Warwickshire — right at the top, generated 44% of all county cricket revenue between them. And going back to the Yorkshire point: it will be a dagger through the heart of Yorkshiremen to hear the big three being described as Warwickshire, Lancashire and Surrey. Which is Sanjay Patel's point — that the two big historic power bases of English cricket have been Yorkshire and Surrey. Now, Warwickshire and Lancashire — absolutely in the next tiny little tier. But Yorkshire and Surrey are the ones who won the most county championships, the ones who provided most English legends, and have had two of the stadiums that have always been Test venues — always the most famous Test venues.

And this is a challenge, though, to the members and the fans as well, right? We've talked about this a few times. No club, no sports team in this day and age has a right to survive. You have to support it.

And the numbers — interestingly, the ECB actually stopped reporting county championship figures after COVID. But what they have been able to see is that in 2018, it was around 600,000, and that dropped to about 545,000 in '21, and in '22 the guess is around 410,000, and it's gone to 390,000 since then. So you've had a 35% decline in county championship games.

They've also switched the calendar, which is not helpful to county championship cricket. It used to be played at various moments throughout the summer, but the advent of the Hundred and the prevalence of T20 cricket has meant that county championship matches have been shunted to the first part of the season and the back end of the season, when really county championship cricket is the type of thing that needs to be watched on a nice sunny day. And it's Monday to Thursday. Because you want to be there all day.

Yeah — but also, Charlie, like, T20 Blast matches regularly exceed 15,000; the Hundred has been touching 20,000.
