That's a fun question. How global is it? You know, is it very US-centric, or —

So, US is a large part, but we have currently — we have Asia, Europe and EU, and the US. Quite global.

Yeah.

It's quite global, we have it all. It's interesting that — I talked to you about this — our number one city by user —

Is Singapore.

Oh, wow.

Which is an interesting one, right? Not by revenue, just by individual headcount. Just, like, an interesting [thing].

Singapore is weirdly high in the adoption charts of AI, for the population. It's like a, you know, 7, 8 million population. And it keeps showing up.

No, it's quite interesting. We were quite shocked, and I was like, oh, this is interesting.

There's a reason I'm doing [things] in Singapore. I mean, so we're there, we're going to be there as well.

And it's interesting that Japan is in the top — or, like, Tokyo is in the top — which, in all the tech cycles, it has never been. It has never been. So it's quite interesting that —

I think the Japanese just love AI.

Yeah. It's that, and then [there] is Brazil.

Yeah.

But Brazil's always been in [it]. Even when I look — if you look at, like, GitHub's data, and us historically with Code Anywhere, it was always like, US, Western Europe, and then you have, like, India, Brazil, China, that would be there. But, like, Singapore was not in [it], specifically — Japan was never in the sort of top. So it's [interesting].

So, actually — but that helps you to distribute your load through all time, you know.

The Spiky Workload Problem

Yeah. So the interesting thing is, like — we have those kinds of loads. But if you look at the researcher loads, they're quite different. So what they are is, like — if you give them concurrency of 10,000, or 50,000, or 100,000 CPUs, whatever it may be — when they fire off a run, it just [goes] 100%, and then just runs, runs, runs, and it stops. The usage pattern is squares, basically, right? And it's also not follow-the-sun, because people will fire it off at midnight before they go to sleep, but then wake up [and] — so it's very unpredictable, you don't know where that is.

So the shapes of the usage are quite different than we have had before. And also, what's interesting is — when it's sort of a follow-the-sun, even if you have a high-growth company, you can sort of predict your usage patterns and have enough capacity for that, because it sort of grows in a way you can project.

When you have companies doing sort of, like, evals and RL, they're super spiky. So they're going to come in — it's like, "we're going to use nothing," then, "can we have 100,000?", right? And then go back down, and then 100,000, and go back down. So it's very, very different.

So, do you want to lock them into commits so that —

Yeah, we do. So we have to lock them into some sort of commits to have that capacity, because we have to have, basically — we have to have the capacity for peak.

Yeah.

Right. And so, right now, Daytona's mean utilization is 15%. Fifteen.

Oh my God.

So it's very low — because it's very spiky. But it's very spiky [and yet] we get up to 90%.

Yeah.

So we have these things. And so what we're looking at right now as a company is, similar to Cloudflare, where you can, like, geo-move things around. But that works really well for, basically, the background agent — where [it's] follow-the-sun. But this — it's a very different shape. Obviously, with scale you figure these things out. But that's an interesting new problem that we have as a compute provider in the agent space.

The Compute Conference

And when we were doing the conference recently — and so we talked to, like, Nikita from Neon, and [I should bring it up] — [Parag] from Parallel and whatnot — everyone has the same problem, whereas, like, usage is super spiky. And this is something that has not happened before — that you have these types of, like — it was always [that] the amplitudes were not this high. So it's quite [an] interesting use case and problem to solve.

Yeah. I don't know if we're going to bring this up again, but let's just talk about the conference. You had, like, a thousand-something people at the Warriors game — at the, sorry, where is it, what's the —

Chase Center.

Chase Center. I went — it was very impressive. Obviously, you know how to throw a conference. What did you learn? You pulled together all these impressive names — and what were you looking for?

My thesis behind the Compute Conference was: let's bring together people that are building infrastructure for AI agents. Because when I think of what we're building, it is — the agent is the primary user. What are the ergonomics and usage patterns of agents?

And what I found — this was a theory, it wasn't proven — is that we all have these problems, as I touched on. And, as I was talking on stage, it was like: we all have the same underlining infra problems — which is this spiky workload, unpredictable workloads that we've never had before in human compute or human infrastructure.

And it's, again — it's the same when I was talking to Parag, or when I was talking to Lin, Nikita. Lin especially — I was talking to her the other day as well. It is a very interesting type of problem to solve.

[I'll] touch on Cloudflare, because there's a lot of talk about that recently — is how they solve that. Which is: they have a bunch of geos, and basically, as users work in different places, and depending on your tier, they can move you around the geos. And so that's how they get to the higher utilization. But you can sort of predict these. And it's — if it's something — you'll rarely get a spike that is 10 orders of magnitude. Like, you'll get a spike like, let's say one of your customers has an exponential curve — what is that? I mean, I'm using cloud as an example — 10%, 20%, whatever. I don't have this data, I'm just assessing — it's surely not 10x, right? It's surely not something there.

And so, how do you go out and solve this problem? And we're all solving this in different ways.

So she also has the same thing.

So, yeah. I know specifically that, like, Neon had that issue as well — like, how are we solving these spiky loads and things like that. 'Cause we talked about [it]. And so the interesting thing for me to actually internalize was: yes, everyone that's building for agents-first is going through this, and we're all solving similar problems.

Let me double-click on this. Okay, so, for example, Neon — I happen to know that they're very sort of S3-oriented. Like, they're just, like, fully bet on S3, and you get to benefit from S3's distribution and infrastructure. So I would imagine that Neon doesn't have to care. Whereas Lin maybe has to care a bit more, because obviously she's doing GPU inference. And — for listeners, we did an episode with her one and a half years ago. And you have to care, but like —

So Parag cares for sure. And Parag is co — [CEO of] Parallel, former CTO of Twitter. They are the search [company]. You and I know — the listeners don't know.

We can put it down on the screen.

Put it up on the screen, so people can look it up if they too [are interested]. And yes — but they still have CPU and RAM allocation that you have to have and [keep] running. And so, CPU, RAM — you have to allocate that and have that ready.

Just-in-Time Compute

And so there's basically two ways to do it. One is, you either over-provision, and you can handle the bursts. Or two, you basically have — I don't know if this is a term — "just-in-time compute" — which is, like, as your usage comes in, you can fire off requests for VMs or bare metals at other cloud providers, and then get them up and running.

So this is, if you go above 100% — like, your overflow —

Your overflow — like, spillage or whatever.

You probably lose money on it, but it doesn't matter, right?

Not — well, you might not. That is a more cost-effective way to do it, but it's a slower way to do it. Because basically, what you have to do is, you have to, like, queue your requests, spin up these just-in-time compute, get it all ready, provision it, and then get your workload there.

And so, if the time isn't important that much, that's fine, and you can do that. But if your customer — and especially for, let's say, the RL training runs — the reason why a lot of people come to us is because GPUs are more expensive than CPUs, right? So you want your GPU running at — what? — 100% the entire time.

And so, when you're running runs on CPUs, when the CPU cycle is, like, down and spinning up the next one, you want that to be instantaneous, so that your GPU doesn't go down. And if you then have to, like, go out and provision machines, you're essentially telling the GPU that it has to wait, and that's incurring [a] cost. So there's things that you have to try to solve for.

RL Workloads: From Zero to 50%

Yeah. Let's talk about the different workload. You said that — what was it, a few months ago — you had zero RL workload, and now it's 50%.

It will be, this [month], 50%.

Let's talk about how different it is. Like, I imagine, for example, a lot less dynamic code generation of, like, arbitrary code — like, here it's probably all the same code, you're just doing parallel runs or something.

Yeah. So you'll have multiple — depends on, like — for each run, you'll have a snapshot. And for the most part, they actually do use our declarative image builder, which is like, "oh, the agent wants these dependencies, these env vars."

Declarative image builder.

Yeah. And so we build it on the fly, and then we propagate that snapshot, and you can spin up as many sandboxes as you want against that snapshot. And then, if you have to do changes, the model can — or, like, it could be automated — it's like, "oh, now, for the next run, we need to install these things, or remove these things," or whatever, to get a task done. And then it goes off and runs that.

So, yes, that is something that — it seems that they prefer.

Competing With Managed Kubernetes

The number one reason I found — or, should I say, let's take a step back — what we are competing against in that environment is essentially managed Kubernetes. So, EKS, GKS, whatever. That is what the vast majority run on.

And anyone that has tried Daytona versus GKS, EKS, is like, "I'm never going back." That has always been [the case]. There's a few reasons.

One is the ergonomics. So, if you're using Kubernetes to spin that up, you have to essentially manage the interface interactions with that. Daytona — although it's a compute provider — it's more akin to a Twilio and Stripe from a consumption perspective than it is an AWS. Like, you have an API and SDK, it's quite easy and seamless to get these things up and running. That's one.

The other is the speed to which we spin up — which we mentioned earlier — which is much, much faster. And the scale to which we can go to.

We haven't got into features, but an interesting feature is that it's very hard to OOM — or, out-of-memory — our sandboxes, because we can dynamically, on the fly, resize. Which is, like, impossible on almost any other thing. There are some technologies that enable that, but it's, like, a very hard thing.

And so we actually saw this when — the Terminal-Bench team brought us [in], actually. So thank you, Alex and the team — they brought us into this whole space.

It is very, very, very rare that, you know, a framework would just say, "guys, just use Daytona."

Yeah, I think it says it somewhere.

Yeah, I was like, what is this?

There's multiple [places] there, but they also mention a few other places. And so, Daytona specifically —

[Just] jumping on themes here — I don't know where it says Daytona, so —

Right, I don't know. There's a very, very strong recommendation, which is, like, very unusual.

We do not pay them for this.

Yes, I know.

They just like you.

Yeah, they like us.

And also — a thing — so Daytona has multiple isolation sets underneath. The customer doesn't have to know what they are. But basically, we have Docker, which is a container that's hardened with Sysbox — so it's Docker's isolation that is security-equivalent to a VM, but it's still a container. And that is the default.

And they — especially in these training workflows — really like that as an interface, to be able to use just a basic Docker container. And we enable Docker-in-Docker, which, for these RL runs — if you need to do a Docker Compose or Kubernetes — you can spin up a K3s inside of these things. Which unlocks a huge amount of workloads that you can do, that you cannot do on other providers.

So, just on that part, [it] is much more interesting. And so we went through that, we showed them that we could do that, and they enjoyed that quite a bit. They being the people — Harbor people.

Do you know — are they a company yet?

I do not know.

What's Coming Online: Computer Use

All right. Yeah, it's super obvious that, you know, there's a lot of excitement and success around these things. Tell us more. Like, this is an exploding workload — Harbor adopted you, which helps speed things along — but what are you learning as this new workload comes online?

Sure. There's a couple of things that we learned, which we [chatted] about in the beginning, and this has led our story.

As we mentioned, we talked to a lot of customers along the way, and we add more features and more tool sets as we talk to customers. And I think it's [that] the ecosystem is so small — or, the models get smarter — [that] when we see one user come with a request, we know it goes on a roadmap if, like, three to five customers come with the same request in that week.

It's, like, very bizarre, and it happens so many times. Which is, like —

Because they're all friends.

They're all friends, they're all in the same group chat.

Yeah, probably. Yeah. 'Cause they're like, "Oh, can you do this?" [And we're] like, "Okay, this is interesting, we'll put it on a feature request." And then the next one's like, "Oh, can you do this?" It's all the same, right?

And so, what we try to do — and I personally try to do — I try to be on as many quote-unquote "sales calls" [as] I can. I'm in every Slack channel. We literally have about a thousand Slack Connect channels, something like that.

There's so many interesting things you find out when you have all [these] Slack channels. You can also see where people transfer between companies — you see, leave Slack channel, enter Slack channel. It's an interesting thing.

Also — just, I digress — I feel that Slack Connect is literally [what] LinkedIn [should be]. You have a list —

LinkedIn charges you to, you know, use your own connections, but Slack doesn't, right? Slack is like, do it for free. It's more lock-in. It's great.

Yeah, it's amazing.

You're going to pay Slack for life.

Exactly. You're there for life.

So, that's interesting. And so, one of the things — the newer things — we talked about earlier is, we made a big bet and put a lot of investment on computer use. That has not seen publicly the light of day [yet].

Is there a thing I can pull up?

There is "computer use" there. It's right up a bit.

Yeah, okay, cool.

Computer Use and the $10 Trillion Opportunity

And what we have — what we talked about, and what we've seen publicly — there's this theme now about, like, the "human emulator." And Elon, from xAI, has talked about this publicly.

And if you think about the models today, they're actually quite sophisticated, and they can do a lot of work, but they still don't have access to all the tools. Like, I'm a strong believer that the most efficient way for an agent to work is essentially headless, or through, you know, terminal or whatnot.

But if we look at knowledge work in general — there's about 100 million knowledge workers in the US, about a billion in the world. And the salaries of them aggregate to 10 trillion in the US, 50 trillion worldwide, something like that.

And if we look at the five most important sectors of that — so, like, healthcare, and government, and financial services and whatnot — that's about 56% of that. So let's say it's about half of that. So, in the US, it's about 25 trillion.

And most of that work is actually still locked into legacy apps inside of Windows — which is not going anywhere for a very, very long time. Like, people just won't invest in that.

How much of it? Our assumption is the following. If, like, in the RPA market — which is a similar market, but not the same — 25% of, like, these white-collar workers' work is automated. If an agent is more sophisticated, can go through more runs, figure stuff out — let's say it's, like, 40%. And so, if you take 40% of that, you get to essentially, like, 10 trillion dollars.

A year.

That's a "T."

That is a "T." So that's the "T" of the models, right? That's not essentially ours, but you get to that size.

And to be able to do that, you essentially have to give agents these computers with the legacy [apps]. So, computer use — either Mac, or Windows, or Linux. Linux, we also obviously have, and others have. But Windows specifically is something very, very new. And the only option right now is an EC2 with Windows, or on Azure. Both of them take anywhere from 3 to 5 minutes to spin up.

We've created an actual sandbox. So it's a second instead of milliseconds, but you have, like, point-in-time snapshots, you have forking, you have all the things that you have from a sandbox — but [it] essentially enables you to, hopefully, unlock all this value.

And so that's been our big push and bet. [It's how] we've sort of kept our ear to the ground — what is sort of the next things in the market.

RPA's Resurgence

Yeah. Knowledge work, and building, and sort of RPA — the next wave of RPA. I got very excited about RPA kind of during COVID times. UiPath was IPO-ing, and it was, like, a very hard [name] — isn't it Eastern European?

It is — Romanian.

Romanian. It might be the only Romanian big unicorn.

Okay.

I think there's a stage being set for the resurgence of RPA, because everyone understands that no one wants to deal with these shitty apps, and no one's going to rewrite them — that you just have to do, like, a remote operation and programmatic operation of them.

But — my own setup was basically the following. So, I was doing a board deck recently — last month, whatever — and I'm like, okay, let's just do [it] automated. So, like, all our data is in, like, ClickHouse and PostHog and QuickBooks — like, where everyone else's is. And I'm basically like, I connected that all to, like, my Claude Code — like, "go off, here's integrations, go do that."

It pulled out the first report, which was great — it connects to Brex and all these things, pulled out [data], which was great. And I say, okay, now pull out this, this, this.

And I kept getting, like, really well, McKinsey-style designed reports, but the data said partial data — like, all the missing data, partial data — like, it can't access all the things.

And I got so frustrated. And so I got, you know, my Mac Mini virtual sandbox, with OpenCUA. I gave it its own account in our company, and then I went to all these services and created a read-only account. So, literally like an intern in your company.

And so I would say, "now go and do this report," and it'll get [it]. Or, like, "I can't, via the MCP or the API, get all the information" — I'm like, "go log in," and it will log in to the website, then go in, export the data — it'll export the data and do the thing, end-to-end.

So, even for things that have, today, APIs — not all of it is exposed. And [for me] to get value — I get immense value right now, but it has to be computer usage, unfortunately. And so I spend a bunch of tokens just on that, but I get the job done.

And so, if even a startup like ours, and using all the hottest tools, still needs a computer agent — what hope does, you know, Goldman have to have a headless [setup], right?

Yeah. Why isn't Microsoft doing this? Well — I'm pretty sure, like, Satya had a post yesterday: "Every agent needs a computer."

I see. So they have launched something.

Yeah, they have Microsoft Power Automate. I'm sure, like, you know, they're going to have their version of that. And you're going to try to do yours.

Mac OS Sandboxes

And I always know there's always demand for Mac, but I know it's, like, tricky to host Mac OS sandboxes.

So, we will have Mac OS sandboxes fairly soon. The problem with Mac OS sandboxes is — I'm deep in this — Mac OS has this problem.

It's a licensing thing.

Licensing thing. So, one — you're allowed to run only two parallel VMs per machine. So that's one.

Two — you can only license to a different user every 24 hours. So if you come in, and theoretically, if I want to charge you per second — I charge you 1 second, I have to have it idle for the rest of the day. Like, I can't have anyone else doing that. So the pricing will be different, in the sense that we would have to charge for 24 hours.

And that's not even the most difficult thing. But the thing above that is, from a security perspective — they enable you to do memory snapshots, pause, resume — but only on the same physical machine.

And so, what you can do in, like, Windows world or Linux world is that I can move, in the background, your snapshot from one to the other, and manage load. Right here, if you want to do that, you essentially have to have your snapshot [on] your physical machine. You can't break it up, you can't move things around [in] that.

And all of that — that part is, like, from a security standpoint — I understand the security aspect of that — but it disables you from doing these agentic, like, really scalable agentic workloads.

You need to do a vibe-coded clean-room implementation of Mac OS, that you can then [move around].

It's, like, "Clean OS" or something, I don't know. I guess so. I know, because, like, Linux was originally, like, a clean-room rewrite of Unix, something like that, right? Like, same thing to Mac OS — someone needs to do it. Someone will do that.

And so we'll have some long-running agents for a few days to figure this stuff out. But yeah — so, definitely we're really close to offering something, because people do want it. But the pricing will be different, and the feature set will be sort of stringent.

Yeah, nobody's going to use this. I mean, like — the labs will, because they want to —

They have to. But the point is, with the RL part — if you do RL on Mac OS, then the next iteration of the model comes out, it will be able to use these tools significantly [better]. Then you actually need to run those somewhere. So you're going to have to have that later on.

And — if anyone at Apple is listening — I very much feel that they are shooting [themselves] in the foot, [given] the scale of the revenue of compute or licensing they could get, if it would just enable a concurrency model similar to what you can get on a Windows.

Yeah. And I'm sure they've heard this before, they just don't care.

Yeah. And maybe they'll change their mind with the new CEO.

Yeah, we'll see. High hopes.

High hopes.

B2B and B2B2C

Okay. But, I mean, it's very clear the market opportunity is huge in Windows, and you can go for a long time on just Windows. But your customers are going to want both.

Yeah.

It is interesting to me that this is the sort of "god application" of agents, right? Like — how big was OpenCUA for you guys? Was there, like, a significant bump?

Not for us. So we're kind of positioned differently. Whereas, although it's completely PLG, and we have individual developers that use it — most of the users that use Daytona are sort of a B2B2C. It's either B2B, or B2B2C.

So, like, in the researcher world, [it]'s B2B — so you're selling to labs and neo-labs and things like that. But on the long-running agents, it's mostly — from a scale-revenue perspective — it's mostly B2B2C, where you have an app-layer agent that uses you.

Yeah. Like a Manus, Lovable type [company].

Yeah.

B2B2C is basically what I've been calling an "agent lab." Which is kind of like — you're not a model lab, but you're making a very, very good wrapper, that is a platform that other people can sign up [for], so they don't have to code those things. It sounds like a much better market than the direct OpenCUA market.

So, I've done multiple things. So the Code Anywhere part of our career path — on the calendar — was very much an end-user developer product. And so that is great. You can get a lot of developer love — and I feel that we do, as a company, have a bunch of developer love, but it's a different type, whereas it's people building these things.

Again, it's more akin to a Twilio, because you don't really run [it] as a person. You wouldn't run Twilio. I don't know how many people remember — it was like "Ask Your Developer," a billboard and whatnot. And people really loved Twilio, but they only used it inside of, like, "oh, I'm building this app or service for [a] thing."

And so we're very much directionally [like] that. And you also know that I used to work for a competitor [of] Twilio, so it's kind of ingrained, I guess, in my [thinking].

People don't know — Infobip is that big.

Yeah, it's like —

Because they're all American, they're like, "whatever is in Europe doesn't matter to me." But, like, it's the same size or bigger.

No, it's about half the size.

Half the size — but it's still huge, multiple billions a year.

Exactly.

These are, like, really interesting and large revenue-generating, very sticky businesses. Whereas when your focus is the end developer, it is a very hard sell, because they're very price-sensitive, very price-conscious. And it's very hard to scale — your cap is the number of people that are willing to spin up [and] first want to spin that up, and then spin up multiple of these.

Whereas if you're in the enterprise one — like, we know everyone's talking about, like, how many tokens they're spending — I'm spending, like — a lot of companies today are like, "this is our [company] spend, [spend] as much as you can." Like, basically, that is where we're going.

And so if you think about that paradigm, where you're selling to companies that say, "spend as much as you can to generate, you know, productivity," versus, "oh, I'm a single person, I have this much budget, and I'm doing this thing because it's fun, or it's helping me out" — it is a different go-to-market strategy.

Yeah.

I think it's, like, sort of all the things come together. And so there's so many things that impact that. To your point, like, OpenCUA wasn't huge for us. But, like, having the agent SDK from Anthropic — Claude Code — was very interesting.

The reason why it was interesting is that a lot of — let's call them "app" — I don't know what to call them — app-layer agent companies, essentially [say], "oh, I can create this new app, this new agent, all I need — I just use Claude Code, and throw it into a sandbox, and then I have my interface to the human."

And so that enabled so many more companies to actually offer this, and then they would pull on [a] sandbox. So that was interesting.

And, to your point, like, MCP versus the CLI — I mean, the MCP is an interface against an API, whereas the CLI is, like, you can actually go do things. The difference between integrations and actually running scripts, or data, or analysis against the thing. So being able to use a CLI very, very well enables the agent to do more things. And because [of] that, people will invoke a sandbox, they'll run [it] in the CLI, and it'll do analysis on that data, and then give you an actual result — versus, you know, just pulling data from an API.

It's a layer of indirection, basically. It's the same thing as agentic search versus RAG. Which — you just win whenever people put more agents into the workflow.

So, like, it doesn't really matter.

But I'm just kind of teasing out, like, what else have people heard about, that's sort of "oh yeah, this is another sandbox use case." Am I missing any big ones?

So, the thing — which is the computer-use stuff, which I think is probably the most interesting one — and, to your point, we've talked to so many people over the last year [who said], like, "oh, like, why do you need a sandbox? Why do you need this?" And, to your point, it's like, "oh, I need [a] sandbox for this, I need [a] sandbox for that" — "oh yeah, I need it for every single thing."

The Infinite Market

And so, basically — and it sounds like a broken record — it's like, you use a laptop every single day, right? And you are [just] one [person] — it's just you.

But now imagine [scaling] — and, by the way, the laptop, the computer, the PC market — the PC market is about equal to the cloud market. So it's about 50… 180 billion a year, something like that. It's about roughly [equal] — the three cloud hyperscalers is about equal to, like, Apple, HP, Lenovo, whatever. Well, it's a little bit less, but it's sort of like that.

And now imagine — and that's just, like — so, how big is the durable market? How many people are there in the world now? What's the latest [number]?

It's, [call it,] 8 billion.

8 billion. And so, let's say you can have two computers — like, you have one personal and one business, whatever — like, so it's double that. And so that's 16 billion.

How many agents are going to be running in two years, and 10 years, and 100 years? And for every single task, they will need one of these. And so — because that market is essentially quote-unquote "infinite" — you will get to the point [where] — and Dylan Patel was at the conference, talking about [it], from SemiAnalysis — that talks usually about GPUs — [he] was also talking about how CPUs will now be a bottleneck. Because it will be the constraint — you won't be able to grow, or we won't be able to have enough of these, because there won't be enough CPUs to basically do that.

Yeah. Well, I actually had a really good podcast with Doug O'Laughlin, [who] was [a] president at SemiAnalysis, where they've basically been [saying], like, yeah, it's been a GPU shortage first, but then it's cascaded down to memory, and now to CPUs.

Yeah.

And — I mean, what's next? Sorry — networking.

Yeah.

Networking actually has been a shortage for a while, if you're looking at, like, just GPU networking. But yeah — I mean, it's really crazy, the amount of computer use that's going on.

Open Source

Yeah, cool. I guess other questions are — the one very big part is the open-source-ness, which you didn't have to do — your competitors don't do [it]. I guess a lot of people are worried about keeping their projects open source, because some competitor can just fork it. I don't know if there's any reflections on just being an open-source company.

Yeah, there's a bunch. So, the original product that we did was open source. Doing that was actually very good for us.

There's basically a saying — what's the saying? — like, companies that are doing really well measure themselves against, you know, free cash flow. [Then] that are kind of okay, it's EBITDA. Then, you know, it goes all the way down — stars. Yeah, so you go all the way down [to] GitHub stars.

And so [the] original one was GitHub stars — that's what we talked about. We're [at] the point [where] we talk about revenue — so we've gone up the stack on that.

And profit.

Yeah, we haven't [done that yet] — we'll get there, we'll get there.

But basically, at that point, we did stars and GitHub. And what it was useful [for] — and the original variation that we did it — we would split the core into its own repo, and it was Apache 2.0 — so very permissive — and then we basically would bundle that, on the enterprise side, with a proprietary repo. So it was, like, open core, but it didn't [fill out] the repository. The repository was very clean.

When we did the pivot, we didn't have time to rethink this, and we wanted to — we had this open-source community, it felt a shame not to do that — and so, but we still did want to add some restrictions.

So, in the new sandbox product, we did add an AGPLv3. Which, you know, it's kind of a shortcut way to do that, where you are open source — and it is true open source, in the sense [that] enterprise can use it if it wants — but you essentially can't make a competitor without open-sourcing your stuff.

It's one of, like, three approaches. Like, there's, like, BSL and some of the other sort of Elastic license.

Yeah, there's some others there. So, pure open-source believers [will] agree that this is not full open source, and I totally respect that — that is absolutely true.

But we did leave that. And Daytona, in its essence — everything outside of what's under a feature flag today, which is, like, the Windows stuff, GPU stuff, and whatever it is — is open source. It is there. So everything is there — like, our own scheduler, everything is there.

So, I've had some competitors say, like, "you guys are actually open-source open-source — like, you can actually see that." And people do like that, and it has helped a bit. But it's actually more helped in the consumption of our cloud product than actually transferring people over.

The reason is, you can actually send the repository to your agent when you're integrating Daytona, and it just has more context.

This is why this is happening.

You could equivalently just have docs that you can — to be fair. And so it actually doesn't really help the growth significantly today. We've had this kind of conversation with, like, investors and other people — like, "how do you convert people from open source?"

The open-source business conversation is so all over the place, right? And — I'll just, like, for listeners who maybe haven't thought this through — a lot of people say, "oh, it's a free tier." Right? Like, "oh, if you run it yourself — but when you get serious, call us."

And then, [for] me personally, because of my Temporal experience — it actually is the way that it's the GTM into some of the largest companies, where we wouldn't pass their review process — maybe because we're too young of a company, or, like, there's parts of the stack that we haven't [completed], that just doesn't work with them. But because it's open source, then they adopt it, and then later [we] figure it out. That's the low end and the high end. I don't know if it [resonates] —

No, no, no, absolutely. And that has been, historically, the thing that we have found in this AI transition.

As [we haven't] talked about [it]: Daytona's customers are everything from, you know, the single developer, the YC startup, to — people say "Fortune 500" — I'll say "Fortune 5," like, the biggest companies of the world — and big labs. [As] you told me about — you know, we're going to keep them anonymous — [these are] enormous companies.

And because the market pull is so strong, we're able to circumvent these processes. I'm not saying [we skip them] — so we passed security audits, we passed all these things — but, as you [know], to your [point], like, Temporal — way back, in the day, in our old version of Daytona — like, it took us months, and usually at the end, they would churn off, because [it's] just like, "oh, you're too small of a company, like, we don't trust you enough."

Whereas today, we've had these large companies push us — like, they would push us through. Like, usually, when you would go through procurement to become a vendor of large companies, it would take you, like, 2, 3 months — we could have done [it in] 5 days now.

And this is not saying that maybe we're great — it's more, I think, a sign of the market, where it is today. And so, when you think about that, the open source is something that we, from a go-to-market perspective, don't think about that much. Because everything that we've created right now has been PLG through the cloud product — people signing up and just pulling us inwards.

Replacing GitHub

This is a personal interest, and I don't know if you have an answer. But do you have problems with GitHub?

I do, a little bit. A little bit.

Yeah, tell me — 'cause, you know, I'm thinking about, like — okay, what would it take to replace GitHub?

So there's a lot — I've thought about this, and I've tweeted about this, and I've actually invested personally in some [things].

Is it Entire?

No. Okay — yeah. So, I — and I've met Thomas virtually, and we've talked.

So I really think — and this was my reason for that — because we have a bunch of background long-running agents, and for a time, most of them were coding agents — like, everyone was building up a competitor to Lovable, or Devin or whatnot.

What we saw from our customers was that they were all trying to figure out how to do versioning. Everyone is doing [it] in different ways. There were some really weird ways where people were doing that.

And the reason was that GitHub, as is, was an overhead — like, it wasn't fast enough [for] what they needed. It didn't solve the problem that they needed. And, to be fair, like, GitHub is, [for] post[-]your[-laptop] — the inner loop — is post-your-laptop.

Yeah. GitHub is the point at which the outer loop starts.

Exactly. [But] people started using that for sandboxes, which is inner loop — which is usually, you know, on your laptop. And so that is not what it's made for.

And then we had everything from [different] people. Actually, the most interesting one is — we had one customer that would literally take the entire codebase inside the sandbox, and every [interval] — I forgot what the time sequence was — they would just dump it all into a JSON, and then push that to S3.

Yeah.

And that's it.

Make your own Git.

There's not even diffs. It's just the whole thing every single time. Because it was super fast. And then they would go back and search and find, you know, sort of what the file was, and write and whatnot. Because [it's a] text file there, JSON — they're very small, so the network cost is very low, and they didn't care, and they just did it that way.

And I'm like, if people are doing this, that means there needs to be a new solution to this problem, right? And so, for me, it's quite interesting to look at who is building these types of new things, agent-first.

I think Git, as is, still exists in the future — maybe even GitHub exists — but there will be a whole new service.

Yeah, exactly. The Git is, like, the deploy artifact to kick off CI/CD, but then there's a layer before that that is, like, the agent collaboration layer.

And so I think there's something to be said there.

The CI Bottleneck

But on the other side, like, there's issues with [CI] — another interesting thing is just, like, CI right now. So the amount of PRs being created is insane right now. In general — even for you guys, right? Everyone's creating a bunch of PRs, like, everyone — and then all that has to go through CI, and then that's the bottleneck.

Like, everyone is [hitting] — not just Actions — not just Actions, but, like, go to any CI provider, you will not be able to [keep up] if you have a high throughput of PRs. There's one company we're talking to, they do a thousand PRs a day. Which means, like — and they're just waiting, they have just a queue on that.

What do they use? Like, Buildkite, or —

I don't know what.

Circle? You know, technically your tech can be used for CI.

That was the conversation.

Oh, okay.

That was the conversation.

Is that a serious conversation?

So, we'll see how that goes. We've had quite a [few] conversations around that. We are not a CI provider by any means, right?

But what's missing?

Essentially, you could use a Daytona sandbox instead of whatever you use for, you know, your GitHub runners, essentially.

Yeah. The only thing I would say is, like — maybe CI machines are supposed to be very cheap. Maybe it's, like, the low end, because it's supposed to be, like, you know, non-blocking, or something — like, a background job. Like, the urgency is not that important for CI.

Performance, though.

Yeah.

Performance. Yeah.

Yeah. Okay, that's interesting.

What Sells Customers

Before we leave Daytona and go into, like, sort of broader, like, founder takes and whatnot — when startups evaluate you — so, you have all these names, and you have more that you can't even name — they see all your wall of competitors. And you have differentiation versus many of these. But, like, what sells them?

The thing that we found that sells people the most — this is more maybe a "day two" thing, instead of a "day one" thing — and we've seen this again and again.

So we have a bunch of case studies, and we have a bunch of them still coming out. They're all done by a third party — so we don't do the case studies. And it's actually interesting to watch those [recorded]. Because it's a third party, people are actually more open, and they will tell you, "oh, we use this competitor," or "we like this competitor more," or whatever.

And the number one thing that people come back to us for is that we have an insane responsiveness.

In terms of your team.

In terms of the team. Insane responsiveness has been, by far, the [main thing]. Now, we can talk about, like, features, and breadth of product, and concurrency, and CPUs, and all those things — but I feel that — so, if all other things are equal, that is very much a differentiator I found.

And is that entirely Slack, or Slack plus email?

There's email there as well, there's calls. But the vast majority is, like, on Slack. So it's Slack — like, we have had customers [say], "hey, we have a problem, can you get on [a] huddle?" Like, we will get on that huddle, like, in 5 minutes, literally. I've done this multiple times.

Wait, okay — so how big are you?

25 today.

How do you do this kind of support, like this?

We're insane. We don't sleep. "007." Have you heard the new thing?

"007." I mean, like — I've met your team, they're very impressive, they're very dedicated. But, like, also — how do you get a team to do that?

So there's —

I have Slack exhaustion, you know.

Yeah, we all have Slack exhaustion. We're very, very tired.

The thing that's unique — I don't know [if it's] unique about us — but unique, I would say, [about] any successful serial founder, is that you're able to pull in people that you've worked with before. And so you can't do that as a first-time founder. Like, I couldn't have done that.

[Of] the 25 people in Daytona, I think about 13 of them, we have worked with seven years-plus. So it's, like, high-trust, high-throughput — high — we know what we're signing off to do. And especially, these people worked with us when we were starting, and we were actually hustling — you know, hungry-for-food hustling-type level. And so those are the people that work with us.

Now, the new segment that has come is — almost everyone is sort of, you know, one degree of separation. So it's, like, someone that someone has known, and so they sort of come into this org. And we've had people that have, like, not fit into [the] org as well. It's just, like, it's that type of culture, where there is a high expectation of, like, being online, replying for these things.

And I do that first. You will — if you ask any engineer, they're like, "you never sleep," like, about me. And so then I do that as an — I don't do [it as] an example, that's just how I'm wired. My wife doesn't appreciate that, I tell you.

My wife doesn't appreciate [it]. I told her about 996. She said, "I wish."

It's like, these Chinese people are slacking.

Yeah.

So, like, that is something there. And so I think every company has their own culture, and that's something very, very deep. And it's something that's come up again and again — and every single day, we're reminded about that. And I didn't go out thinking that that is how I'm going to build it — it's just how I built these things.

Founder Life and Family

I'll transition a little bit on the founder side. Like, I'm very impressed by you, in general, [in terms] of, like, your sort of balance. You have a young family — two kids.

Yeah. No — two kids now.

Yeah, two kids now. I think a lot of people I meet, they're like, "Well, I'm starting a family, I can't be a founder and all that." What's your advice to those people?

Every single [day] — so, my family, they're here right now. But they're usually [in Croatia] — I fly between Croatia and here, like, a lot. Our team is in Croatia — a part of our team — and growing is here now in San Francisco. And so I spend a lot of time away from [my] family. And that is hard. Like, that is a sacrifice that you have to [make].

But, going in — like, people say, like, "on your deathbed, you're going to miss some of those things." And probably [that] might be true. But the thing — going into this, I already said, like, I know that this is going to hurt, and everything has to hurt.

By the way, I'm very much of a feeling that everything has to hurt. Going to the gym hurts. Losing weight hurts. Like, everything has to hurt, right? It does. Like, no [pain, no] gain. It is literal. But you actually have to enjoy the pain. And, just like — if you don't enjoy the pain, it's not for you. And so you get accustomed to that pain.

And so — love the kids, especially — I'm a [father of a] daughter and a son, [the] daughter's the eldest — like, love her, and do miss her when she's not here. But it's, like — that's what I signed up for, and there is a plan and target of what I'm trying to achieve.

And now, hopefully, with my wife, which does support me, we can get ourselves together more, so it doesn't [hurt as much] there. But she takes a large portion of that. And so, if you have a partner on the other side that is okay with that, then you can do that. But even if they do, you have to be okay with not being there, right?

Yeah. This is my vision for you. This meme.

Yeah, yeah, yeah.

So that's your kids in the future.

Yeah, yeah. I think so. Yeah. But we have to teach them that they're [not] —

Not — because Dad, you know, built the computes. Dad made sandboxes.

Dad made sandboxes.

And built the spiritual successor to serverless and Kubernetes, [but] for agents.

Hot Takes: Reselling Tokens

Any other sort of hot topics, trends? You have a lot of hot takes. Actually, you are best known for — you were sort of in, sort of, hustle-culture mode, right? And someone quoted you and said, "I haven't even heard of you, bro — just log off and take the Christmas off." And then your response was —

My response was, like, "that's why."

Yeah.

Yeah.

So, I mean, like, I think that's, like, very typical of you. I don't have it here, I can't bring it up. But I think that's very typical of the culture. But, like — I think you have a lot of, like, interesting hot takes like that. Any other sort of takes on the startup ecosystem?

Oh, the startup ecosystem. And this was the recent one — which is, I think — and this [is] general, like, business — I feel that — it didn't come off, I think, well, on Twitter, some people [always] misread it — which is: the market is adding [a] premium to SaaS vendors that are reselling tokens.

Yes.

And I think that's incorrect.

Why?

Why I think that's incorrect is that — if you look [at it] — one, your pricing depends on what the price is, if it's public market, or if it's private, or whatever. You're saying — the person that's reading that — that the re-acceleration of revenue is equal to the old revenue. Which it's not — not even close.

Because, one — you had, on SaaS, you had typical SaaS margins, whatever it was, [with] stickiness and all these things. Now, what you're doing is, you are saying, "here is my agent," and I have — whatever the margin is — it's way worse, right? And now you're using Anthropic, or, you know, [OpenAI's] model. And then we, as a community, are saying, "now that is re-acceleration."

And so, one, I think that's wrong, because — first, it's not the same, the makeup is not the same.

The other thing is — and [I] go back to, like, what I mentioned earlier — like, the QuickBooks [example], and how I set up OpenCUA and whatever. I don't want your agent, essentially. Because what happens — right now, we have a problem that, [as I] historically [mentioned] — you have data siloed, in, again, ClickHouse, QuickBooks — it's all siloed.

And now you're giving me an agent that'll give me the data, but it's still siloed, right? And so now I have to, like, take that data and then get another agent.

Just expose the data —

Just expose it. And one thing I have to [say] — and so I'm like, just expose everything, and charge me for that. So, charge me for consumption of API. So you'll have your old seat-based pricing for humans, [and] charge me for [the] number of agents.

The number of agents will skyrocket, and essentially, you'll have more usage. And charge for more, if your product has value. So, like — there's arguments — some of them do have value. It's database, not database, we can get into that — but some of them really do.

And I was actually shocked that the first person to do this was Benioff. Salesforce — there was a tweet, I think, 3 days ago, where [he] said every product in Salesforce has been exposed via API. Everything. And I'm like — now I understand why this person has built this. This is insane. Kudos to him. Amazing. It's like, "thank you" — I don't know if you listened to me or someone else — like, thank you for this world.

And so, if you can get real acceleration against that, against consumption of API — that is actual revenue, and that is actual real acceleration. And that is where value will come from. And I think that there will be a cold shower, when people understand, like, no one's actually going to use and pay for these agents and tokens. And that wasn't actually really acceleration — but it'll drop back down.

Yeah. I mean, look — like, obviously, I think [you're] generally correct, and I agree, I think. But people are going to try to become an AI company.

No, no, absolutely. And I have nothing against that. And — this is no, to be very clear — this is not a downer on anyone that's building this thing. Everyone has to get to, like — get to the revenues, get to multiples, get valuations, do what you have to, [to] get to the next step. Absolutely agree.

But we, as a community, are now, like, saying, "oh, this is, like, the magical way to get out." This is not, like — that is not what is happening, right?

No, I think there was, like, this kitchen-appliance company that put out some AI nonsense recently.

But it was also the sneaker — what was [it] called — Allbirds.

Allbirds is pivoting to GPU. That's fine. It's like, you know — "I have some money left, I'm just going to do some lottery tickets."

GPUs and Data Centers

Would you go into offering GPUs?

Oh yeah, we will. But not for inference. Like, essentially, what we think about is, like, the GPU sandbox. So, if you think of, like — if you have a GPU in your computer — that is what you [have]: a GPU in the sandbox.

So there are workloads that do need GPUs. Again, I always go back to 3D rendering, 'cause it's the easiest one to comprehend. But, like, if you want to do any sort of RL on, like, CAD or something like that, you will need a GPU in the sandbox. And so that's coming now as well.

How about own data centers?

Own data centers. So, we run on colocation providers, bare-metal machines. Data centers — we technically can run on that, or our own data center. Like, that's how we architected it today.

[But] from a gross-profit-margin perspective, [it] doesn't make sense for us to get in that. You have to raise a large amount of capital, a large amount of risk, for, like, single-digit percentage points. So today, that doesn't make sense. But we are fundamentally architected so that we can do that if we want.

Yeah. I mean, you're a large customer of these guys now. Do you see any opportunity?

We will see. Yeah. We will see.

I see a lot of people, like, trying to do the bare-metal thing. We talked to Railway the other day, and they're also doing a very similar strategy. They — I think they're building out something, or they have their own sort of data centers now.

Yeah, they're majority their own data centers. I — but I do think, like — I mean, they still use Equinix and all those things. So I think it's just interesting that, you know, this model basically hasn't changed. It's basically a real-estate model. They manage the facilities, and then you do everything else.

I wonder how it can be changed for the future. Because, I mean, you know, like, the AI wave is the opportunity to reinvent everything.

Where Does This Go

Yeah. Anything else?

Cool, I think that's about it. I didn't have any other topics. I think this is, like, a [good], best and comprehensive [discussion] — like, if you have any questions about the compute market and sandboxing and Daytona, like, this is the best place to start.

[But] where does this go, man? Like, you know, we're here in April, things are going 75% month-to-month — like, where are we going to be by end of year?

It's an insane number, I'm sort of scared to say it out loud. So, like, it is very big.

Just the sandbox market — and we talked about this — in general, the entire infrastructure market is growing 40%, plus or minus, month over month. Everyone is growing 40% [a] month. And that's also a hot take, [which] is: like, if you're not growing 40%-ish, it's not [you] — it is just the market. You might as well — you don't have to come to work, you'll grow that amount, basically. I'm half kidding, but, you know, that's where it's going.

And so, where does it end? We will see.

The thing that I think about — from at least a CPU perspective (GPU is even crazier) — from a CPU perspective is, like: there's a high probability that actually owning the CPUs beforehand will be a go-to-market tactic.

And it will probably [be] — 'cause, [as] you do — you probably talk to a lot of GPU providers — their growth is hindered by the amount of GPUs that you have right now.

It's, like — it's whatever Nvidia decides to bless that day.

Yeah. That's how much they're going to grow, right? And so, [for] the CPU market in general — be it, like, something like Railway, for example, or Vercel or whatnot, or deployment, or, like, the sandboxes — there's still CPUs. So, like, each is growing at the pace of the market, [plus] or minus of that market — but it's still not constrained by that.

And so my thought is, like — for all of us in this market — and databases fall into that as well, because databases also run on CPUs — it's like, we all have to grow as fast as we can, so we can get enough of, you know, CPUs tomorrow from Intel, or from Nvidia, 'cause they have now CPUs — and everyone else later on. So it'll be interesting when we get to that.

Maybe one [way] I'll phrase this is, like: is the potential — you know, is [it] the new Heroku, new AWS, or new — what's the analogy that is most appropriate?

There's interesting [analogies]. Like, the — you know, there's "new Cloudflare." But "new Cloudflare" [could be] new Cloudflare — like, they're actually doing a really good job about [it].

And Cloudflare owns networking, no one can fight — come on.

No, they're doing — no — what I [mean] is, in the sense of: their whole agent portfolio is actually really good. And I should say — there [are] some technical [limits], I think personally, around, like — everything's under-constrained under Workers — like, Workers is their thing. But, from a go-to-market vision perspective, I think they're actually really, really good. I think they actually get it, unlike some other companies.

And, to your question — like, what is going to be there — there will be an equivalent. Everyone says, like, "an AWS for AI agents." But your answer — like, it might look more like Stripe than AWS, in the sense [that] there will be a cloud built out specifically for agents. And so that cloud will have sandboxes, and it will have web search, and it'll have databases — like, SQLite, or Neon, or whatever — specifically for agents, and other things.

We are not at the end of the new infrastructure primitives for agents. There are more coming. So people think, like, "oh, there's nothing else to it" — there are more. Like, we have some ideas about the next ones — we don't have time to do them — but there are definitely more primitives that are being built out for AI agents. And there will be, I think, a cloud that runs all that.

Yeah. OpenAI has said "AI cloud," Vercel has said "AI cloud" — and you are potentially also one of the other prospective AI clouds. I think it's a very big prize to win.

Well — thanks for coming on.

Thank you for having me. It's been amazing.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最值得玩味的信号：不是"好评"，而是"被人追着要"
大多数创业者用正面反馈来验证 PMF——用户说"我喜欢"。Ivan 的判断标准恰恰相反：真正的拐点不是用户在 demo 里夸你，而是**第二天没发 API key，每一个人都打电话回来追问"我的 access 在哪"**。这是一个反直觉的观察——"我们想要 access"这句礼貌话本身毫无信息量（人人都会说），有信息量的是**用户愿意承担社交成本去催你**。这其实和医学诊断里的"疼痛迁移测试"是一回事：病人说哪里疼不可靠，但当你按一个地方、他的身体不受控制地躲开，那才是真信号。Daytona 的 PMF 不是被"听"出来的，是被"追"出来的。
## 二、隐藏的真相：Daytona 根本不是一家"算力公司"，而是一家再保险公司
这是整集里被轻描淡写、却最颠覆的事实——**平均利用率 15%，峰值 90%**。如果你把 Daytona 当成"卖 CPU 的"，这个数字是灾难；但如果你换个领域看，它立刻合理：这正是**保险公司、电网调峰电厂、航空公司超售系统**的财务结构。客户买的从来不是 CPU，而是"峰值那一刻一定有容量

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] The Ancient Tradeoff Behind Being Intelligence – David Reich
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被我们误以为永恒的价值观，其实只有几代人那么年轻

最反直觉的观察来自Reich的一个时间错位：当《旧约》被写下时，恰恰是人类历史上对"智力"自然选择压力最强的时期之一——然而这部文本通篇不歌颂智力，它歌颂力量、勇气、虔诚。 这是一个深刻的悖论。我们今天把"聪明"当作不言自明的最高德性，但写下人类最古老经典的那些社会，明明正活在智力被高度筛选的环境里，却选择不去赞美它。

这暗示了一件令人不安的事：一个性状被自然选择，和一个文化去崇拜它，是两件完全脱钩的事。 我们对智力的崇拜不是因为我们"终于看清了真相"，而是一种极其晚近的、可能不到两百年的文化突变——它和大规模识字、考试制度、学历筛选同时诞生。换句话说，IQ崇拜不是人类的觉醒，而是工业社会的一个局部时尚。我们误把自己房间里的墙纸当成了宇宙的颜色。

"鱼与哺乳动物"——藏在生育策略里的同一个开关

Reich把人类家庭决策和鱼类产卵放进同一个框架，这个跨领域连接值得停下来品味。生态学里有个经典的 r/K 选择谱系：鱼把成千上万颗卵撒进河里、几乎不投资（r策略）；哺乳动物一胎少子、重度投资（K策略）。Reich的大胆之处在于——他说冰岛过去一百年的"反智力基因选择"，本质上就是这个开关在人类身上被拨动了一下。

这里有个让人意外的推论：那个被我们浪漫化为"少生优生、精心培养"的现代中产育儿哲学，在演化的账本上并不比"多生少养"更高级，它只是 K 端的一种下注。而所谓"智力相关基因被选择掉了"，并不意味着人类在变笨——意味着环境（20世纪富足的冰岛）暂时奖励了 r 端策略。潮水会回来。 同一个基因组合，在资源紧缺时是劣势，在资源过剩时也可能是劣势，只是劣在不同的地方。这不是退化，是钟摆。

精神疾病可能是"创造力"的体检报告——以及它对AI的隐喻

最具颠覆性的一段是Reich对精神分裂和躁郁症的重构：这些"疾病"也许不是纯粹的故障，而是某个性状光谱的临床读数——焦虑、想象力过剩、神经质，在萨满传统、宗教异象、艺术创造被珍视的社会里，曾经是亚临床的优势。我们今天叫它"病"，是因为现代环境恰好惩罚这个谱系的那一端。

这里我想做一个视频之外的连接：这与当代关于人工智能的争论结构惊人地一致。 我们正在拼命优化大模型的"幻觉"——把模型偏离事实的倾向当作纯粹的缺陷来消除。但Reich的逻辑提醒我们："幻觉"和"创造力"很可能是同一个谱系的两端，一个能编造不存在引文的系统，和一个能写出前所未见隐喻的系统，底层是同一种"愿意偏离训练分布"的倾向。把谱系的一端压到零，另一端往往一起塌掉。

Reich真正的核心命题，因此可以提炼成一句他自己没明说的话：凡是被进化长期保留下来的复杂性状，几乎都不是"越多越好"，而是一根两端都有奖赏的杠杆。 任何只朝一个方向优化的人——无论是优化孩子的IQ、优化基因、还是优化一个神经网络——都在无意中砍掉了自己看不见的那一半价值。多样性不是冗余，它是把未来的不确定性提前储存起来的方式。

📝 转录文本

智力，真的在所有时代都是好东西吗？

And so you might wonder isn't intelligence good — you know, in all contexts and places of time? And I think that there's a number of ways to think about that.

First of all, I think we are speaking from the point of view of a society which intensely values this particular trait — you know, ability to score well on IQ tests or things like them, or to go to school for a long time, or whatever it is. And I think this is unprecedented in human history that we live in a time like this.

It's like, if you look at the Hebrew and Christian Bible, and you look at how much intelligence is valued, it's basically not at all.

But that — when the Bible is being written, especially the Old Testament — is exactly when selection for intelligence is the highest point it's apparently ever been.

Yeah. Exactly. But there, it's about strength, or courage, or religiosity. Right? Those are the values, right? Or if you read Homer, or the other texts of other religions, it's not intelligence. It's beauty. It's other things.

And so this value system which has a hyperfocus on smarts is not obviously a trait value that's been common in the past. You might think that in certain communities there might be valuation of things that are more approximate to years of schooling, but really, broadly, it's not been a high value in the population.

IQ 测验的智力，和"在新世界活下来"的智力

But obviously the thing we're referring to — or the thing we care about — is not direct performance in IQ tests, especially in the past.

The thing I'm trying to understand better is intelligence more broadly. And maybe just that IQ-test intelligence is not that correlated with: here is a new world environment, and go figure out how to process food there, and make shelter, and everything else — all the things which your colleagues like Joseph Henrich talked about, like how modern people underestimate the difficulty of doing this kind of thing with a small band of people.

Anyways, maybe that's not IQ-test intelligence, and that's why we don't see that strong a selection effect on this thing. But intuitively it seems like, regardless of the value system, it just seems very valuable to have this trait maxed out.

推测一：两种投资孩子的方式

So I'm being very speculative, and let me give you two examples about what this is about in my head, how I'm thinking about this — not that I'm a particularly good authority on these things.

As I mentioned, a lot of these traits which are quite disparate are highly correlated to each other — obesity, years of schooling, walking pace, performance in IQ tests, household wealth — all these crazy traits all seem to be governed to a substantial extent by a shared combination of genetic variants.

And let's just think about what this might mean. In Iceland, in the last 100 years, there's been selection against this combination of variants. And one possible interpretation is: it's basically selection for two ways of investing in your children.

Having many kids and not investing a lot in them, or having few kids and investing more in them, right? So if you defer having kids — becoming wealthier, having more resources, and putting more into each kid — you're going to have lower fertility, you're going to have fewer kids. And that's going to result in lower fertility, but those kids might survive more and do better in society.

Alternatively, you can just have as many kids as you can and invest less in them. They might have individually less good outcomes, but in a time of plenty — which is potentially Iceland in the 20th century — it might make sense to have more kids and invest less in them.

从哺乳动物到鱼：环境决定的生育开关

And so you can imagine that actually, at different times and in different places in ecology, there are different ways. Mammals often invest a lot, with a pregnancy and a small number of children, whereas fish will spawn huge numbers of offspring into the river — the great majority of whom will be eaten. But that is an effective way to produce offspring in certain conditions.

So there'll be a toggle, depending on the environmental conditions, back and forth between investing in large numbers of offspring with less investment, or smaller numbers of offspring with more investment. And maybe we're just seeing that move back and forth over different places and times.

推测二：精神疾病作为创造力光谱的"读数"

Similarly for schizophrenia and bipolar disease — how could this ever be advantageous? But maybe what we're seeing with these diseases is a kind of readout of some kind of spectrum of traits that actually, in some context, might be advantageous.

Maybe being anxious, or being imaginative, or being neurotic, might be helpful in a shamanistic tradition — you know, in a religious tradition which values people who can have visions, or values people who can be creative. And maybe these are subclinical versions of schizophrenia or bipolar disease that, in certain times, may be advantageous and in other times may be disadvantageous.

Maybe you're just seeing selection for different types of creativity or other thinking that can be valuable in different contexts.

核心观点：复杂性状是两端都有优势的光谱

I'm waving my hands here, but my sense is that these complex traits have not pushed in one direction because there's advantage. There are spectrums where there's advantages to both ends of the spectrum, and there's multi-dimensional impacts of these different traits.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Bits + Bips] Powell Is Out, Warsh Is In: What It Means for Crypto
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、比特币最大的悖论：它是做空美联储的资产，却靠美联储的"恐慌反射"而活

整集节目里最被低估的一句话，是 Noelle 对 2023 年银行业危机的复盘。当时所有人欢呼"比特币上涨是因为人们终于看清了银行系统的腐败与脆弱"，而她冷冷地纠正：不，比特币上涨是因为人们预期央行会出手放水。

请把这句话和她后面讲的 BLIS 交易（Big, Large and Lasting Stimulus）并排放在一起看，你会得到一个令人不安的结论：比特币诞生的叙事是"对抗美联储的法币滥发"，但在真实的资金流里，比特币其实是做多美联储那只随时会按下去的恐慌按钮。它不是法币的反义词，它是法币救助机制的杠杆化衍生品。换句话说——这是本期最反直觉的观察——加密世界一边在意识形态上诅咒央行,一边在仓位上押注央行永远不会让系统真正崩溃。一个真正"去中心化、抗审查、对抗印钞"的资产，它的最佳买点恰恰是"印钞机即将启动"的时刻。比特币的信徒和美联储的救市派，本质上是同一笔交易的两端。

二、"通行费"是贯穿整集的隐藏母题——从霍尔木兹海峡到 Hyperliquid 的 90% 分成

注意一个文字上的巧合：节目里中国油轮要驶出霍尔木兹海峡，需要向伊朗交一笔 "fee / toll"（通行费）；而 Ram 评价 Hyperliquid 时也用了完全相同的词——Hyperliquid 拿走 90% 的收入，是它多年苦功"exact 的那笔 toll"。两处用的是同一个英文单词，绝非偶然。

这揭示了一个跨领域的结构真相：地缘政治的咽喉要道经济学，和加密市场的基础设施经济学，是同一套逻辑。 谁卡在"必经之路"的节点上，谁就收税。伊朗卡的是物理海峡；Hyperliquid 卡的是链上交易场所；而 Coinbase 这次最精明的动作，恰恰是把自己安插成 USDC 的"抵押品管理者"——一个新的咽喉节点。它表面上是在和竞争对手"对齐合作"，实质上是花钱买下一个未来的收费站。

更妙的跨域连接在于：沃什想要的"减少点阵图、减少前瞻指引"，以及 SEC 想要的"减少财报披露频次"，同样是一种收费站的建立。当信息从"被免费喂养"变回"稀缺品"，谁能解读信息谁就重新拥有了护城河。 节目里 Noelle 半开玩笑地问"这是不是意味着分析师终于得真正干活了"——这句玩笑底下是严肃的产业重组：过去十年央行的"信息透明化"实际上摧毁了卖方分析的价值，而沃什正在悄悄把这个行业的定价权还回去。霍尔木兹、Hyperliquid、美联储——三个看似无关的场景，都在做同一件事：在必经之路上重新设卡收费。

三、美联储真正的产品不是利率，而是"信任"——它和它鄙视的加密 KOL 卖的是同一样东西

Noelle 反复强调一句话：鲍威尔的首要工作是"管理信任"（manage trust），美联储 most of what it does is 管理信任。她甚至毫不留情地拆解了"鲍威尔人格崇拜"——那个"你愿意一起去喝棉花糖拿铁的爷爷"形象。

这里藏着一个会让人愣一下的观察：美联储的核心产品，和这期节目广告时段里那些"KOL activations"、那些靠人格魅力带货的加密影响者，是同一种东西——被精心管理的、半准社交化（parasocial）的信任。 区别只在于美联储管理的是对一个机构的信任，KOL 管理的是对一个个人的信任。鲍威尔的 FOMC 发布会，本质上是一档运营了七年的、收视极高的"信任内容节目"。这也正是为什么 Steve 顺口说"SNL 第 52 季应该演一集 FOMC 会议"会显得如此自然——FOMC 早已经是一档有固定角色、固定剧本、固定观众的连续剧了。

由此引出本集最深的一处张力：当一个机构的主要资产是"信任"，它就再也无法承认自己错了。 这正是 Noelle 解释为什么美联储不能把通胀目标从 2% 改成 3% 的原因——"三是新的二"人人心知肚明，但一旦官方承认够不到 2%，被摧毁的不是某个数字，而是机构信任本身。于是美联储被锁进了一个荒诞的处境：它必须假装追求一个所有人都知道它达不到的目标。这和加密世界的某些项目方何其相似——叙事一旦立起来，就成了不能拆的承重墙。Noelle 留下的真正问题不是"独立性好不好"，而是那个更锋利的反问：当"独立"意味着"无需对任何决策负责、可以无视传票"，独立性还是优点吗？ 一个把信任当作产品来经营的机构，最终会发现"维护信任"和"承认真相"是直接冲突的——而这，恰恰也是整个加密行业还没想明白的那道题。

📝 转录文本

Powell Is Out, Warsh Is In: What It Means for Crypto

Bits + Bips — The Interview

【冷开场片段：Coinbase 与 Hyperliquid 的"对齐"】

So I was wondering what it said to you that Coinbase is, you know, through this gesture, aligning more closely with Hyperliquid. And if that, you know, kind of move there to increase its position of stake type even shed any further light on the stablecoin deal.

Yeah, Laura, I was thinking the same thing. It feels like in many ways, Hyperliquid and Coinbase are both going after this sort of everything-exchange situation — you know, spot trading, perpetuals trading, equities, prediction markets. And it's true, like, you know, they have to acquire a significant amount of HYPE and stake it to be able to offer this, you know, AQA. And so this shows significant alignment between the two businesses, even though there's so much competition.

To me, what this shows is that Coinbase is very certain in its growth as a primarily US-based compliant institution, whereas Hyperliquid, I think they see as more of this outside-of-US-jurisdiction, no-KYC platform — which I think Coinbase can't really compete as much in. But they can leverage the usage of Hyperliquid globally to grow distribution of USDC, which I think ultimately in the long term will positively affect their bottom line.

We're at a critical point. And so you're spot on in that, for the longest time, I would always say: Econ 101, Bitcoin has all the attributes of a successful currency. But the one thing it's been missing is a clear set of legal and regulatory guidelines. So if we have clarity in the mix, that's the puzzle piece. Like, that's the last piece to unlock all of the attributes of a successful currency.

Ram, let me actually throw this ball to you on the market side of things. Does this make you more or less bullish on Coinbase, on Circle in terms of the stocks, and on the deployment of stablecoins versus banks overall?

It's a win for all of them. It's an especially big win for Hyperliquid. For Coinbase and Circle, they were able to neutralize an emerging competitor. Very strategic move to get USDC involved — and with Coinbase essentially this collateral manager, installing themselves at a very key point in this new infrastructure.

For Hyperliquid, it's a major win — like 90% of the revenue here. So that's the toll that they exact for having accomplished a tremendous amount over the last several years. We talked about Hyperliquid, I think, three or four weeks ago. This is one of the assets that you wanna own this cycle. And it was very prophylactic of Coinbase to get ahead of that, because Hyperliquid is emerging as a decentralized trading venue. Obviously, Circle generates a material amount of recurring revenue, net interest income. So it's a win for everybody, but it's especially a big win for Hyperliquid.

I agree with you on distribution, but I think the other end of the barbell is energy. He who has access to unlimited energy and effectively cheap compute probably gets a win — and I think it's pretty fair to say that Elon has an edge there. I know that we were talking about this morning, the physics, the science of getting your energy pretty much for free in space ain't easy. But if anyone can do it, it's the guy who has the — I don't want to say the M word, but he has a very, very robust capability across the globe in getting things into outer space. So I think that's Elon's unfair advantage at the very back of the stack.

【广告时段】

A 3% top-off on my entire portfolio? Uh, yes. Who is saying no to that? That's free money. I don't care what's in your portfolio. That's a yes.
