item.title: [Business of Sport] Should Southampton have been Kicked Out of the Playoffs?
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、这不是史上最大罚单——这是一份被没收的"期权"

南安普顿 CEO Phil Parsons 说这是"英格兰俱乐部史上最大处罚"，主持人轻轻一句话就把它拆穿了：如果南安普顿本就会输掉决赛，那么这个"史上最大处罚"实际成本为零。这里藏着一个出人意料的真相——这个处罚的大小是不确定的，它取决于一场永远不会发生的比赛的结果。

用金融的语言说：南安普顿失去的从来不是 2 亿英镑奖金，而是一份看涨期权——一张"约 50% 概率 × 2 亿英镑"的入场券。媒体说"踢出最富有的比赛"，仿佛奖金会原封不动躺进银行账户，但主持人点破：这笔钱会立刻"从另一头流出去"，变成工资和转会费。所以这是一份"薛定谔的罚单"：在判决落下的那一刻，它同时是史上最大、也可能是史上最小，只有那场不存在的决赛才能让它坍缩成确定值。Parsons 要求 EFL 在量刑前先做财务影响评估——主持人反驳得很准：那等于"对英冠球队从轻、对英乙球队从重"，因为损失的绝对值不同。这恰恰说明，惩罚一旦与"赌注大小"挂钩，规则就不再是规则，而变成了一张可以讨价还价的价目表。

二、最反常的细节：间谍战根本没用，他却坚持不懈

这是整段对话里最容易被忽略、却最值得玩味的事实：南安普顿赛季中早就派过"间谍"——去牛津，结果 2-1 输了；去伊普斯维奇，2-2。这套谍报战从未带来过好结果，主教练却一次次坚持。

如果间谍战是一个理性的"获取战术优势"行为，那么连续失败之后理应被放弃。它没有被放弃——这说明它的真实功能不是战术性的，而是心理性的。请注意一个跨领域的连接点：对话里反复强调，对手主教练的周四训练课是"魔法发生的地方"，是他"整周做的最重要的一件事"、是他缓解赛前焦虑、获得掌控感的仪式。南安普顿主教练想偷走的，与其说是对方的战术细节，不如说是对方的"确定感"。这让间谍行为更接近赌徒的迷信仪式、运动员赛前固定的穿袜顺序——一种在不确定性面前管理焦虑的强迫性动作。它之所以"前所未有地处心积虑"（换上 Eastleigh 球衣伪装、研究共用训练场地），恰恰因为它不是冷静的成本收益计算，而是一种强迫性的安全感采购。真正高明的间谍会在失败后收手；一个停不下来的人，暴露的是恐惧，不是算计。

三、为什么"最具体的那条规则"是承重墙——以及"中立专家"自己也是当事人

主持人坚持"规则手册神圣不可侵犯"，并做了一个关键区分：EFL 不是监管者，而是赛事组织者。这是一个被低估的洞见。监管者追求"正义与比例原则"，而赛事组织者追求的是机制设计中的激励相容——惩罚的任务不是"配得上罪行"，而是让违规变成一个严格劣势策略。这正是核威慑、博弈论里"可信威胁"的逻辑：罚款 30 万、扣 2 分，对 2 亿英镑的诱惑而言不构成威慑，反而是一张"值得一试"的报价单。

而这里有一个递归式的"啊哈时刻"：第 127 条是 EFL 所有规则里最具体、最不可能被误解的一条（72 小时内不得观察对手训练）。主持人的逻辑是——如果连这条最清晰的规则被打破都只换来"手腕上轻轻一拍"，那么书里所有更模糊的条款都自动贬值了。换句话说，第 127 条是整本规则手册的承重墙：抽掉标记最清楚的那块砖，整栋楼才会真正动摇。

最后一个让人意外的角度——请留意这位"中立专家"自己的身份。他在节目中坦白，自己正作为 Walton & Hersham 的董事，对前任教练（现在约维尔）"挖角"自家球员提起申诉。也就是说，这位反复强调"规则神圣、必须严惩、必须杀鸡儆猴"的评论者，本人此刻正是一桩同类案件中的原告。这并不削弱他的论证，但它揭示了一个系统性真相：在足球这个封闭生态里，几乎没有真正的"局外人"，每个发声者都同时是潜在的当事人——他的"规则原教旨主义"既是专业判断，也是利益结构的自然产物。而这也呼应了对话里那个最锋利的观察：为决策买单的，永远是离决策最远的人——不是董事会（"董事会绝无可能知情"），而是球员（薪资随升降级剧烈浮动）和球迷（不可退的酒店、孩子第一次去温布利的梦想）。这是企业丑闻里反复上演的委托-代理悲剧：责任在理论上集中于顶端，冲击波却总是打在边缘。

📝 转录文本

Should Southampton Have Been Kicked Out of the Playoffs?

Business of Sport — Bonus Episode

开场：一期临时加更

Bonus episode alert, Charlie. Ding, ding, ding.

And a first. A first for us. I'm not — who is it a bonus for? Is it a bonus for listeners, or is a bonus for me to see my fellow Charlie and his burgeoning Norwegian ski jumper ginger beard, which is, um, emerging at the moment?

Yeah. Well, it's a bonus for all of us, isn't it? Um, I know I said a little bit — more pronounced on a video, so I may need to get that sorted out. But, uh, it's quite nice doing a show that isn't under bright lights, so that I'm not looking the most pale that I could look, uh, against someone of your nice tan complexion.

Anyway, why are we doing this? Well, we're doing this obviously because of Spygate and the, um, recent developments in the case which, Charlie, when we recorded our show this week on Tuesday, was very much underdeveloped from the information that we have now. And we didn't really, uh, feel capable of talking about this at length without that information, knowing that it was going to release on Friday. So this provides the perfect opportunity to take a look at what's been happening over the last few days, 'cause I don't know about you, but my phone has been going off, and the amount of people going "I can't wait to hear your thoughts on Spygate" has made me think we have to do this.

Yeah, absolutely. It's been a seriously hot topic of conversation amongst — like, a lot of my friends are, like you, really Premier League fans, um, who only really pay attention to what's happening in the Premier League. And I guess probably where the crossover here is, this relates to the playoff final, which determines who goes into the Premier League. But nonetheless, it is still fundamentally an EFL matter. And what's been interesting to me is I've had loads of friends of mine who are EPL club fans getting in touch, um, with their views on this particular thing. I have to say, in most cases I don't really, um, agree with them, but, um, there we are.

Look, and I want to get into that. I want to get into why you don't agree with them.

案件回顾：Spygate 到底发生了什么

But first, there were a few things that were confusing me. And it's worth, just for a minute or so probably, explaining to anyone that hasn't been fully aware what's been happening, because this is probably one of the biggest stories in the EFL's history.

The two teams in one of the Championship playoff finals — Southampton and Middlesbrough — were caught up in this when Southampton sent a data analyst intern to go and record a training session, or multiple training sessions if you believe some of the WhatsApp messages, of Middlesbrough before — I think it was the first leg, wasn't it? It was the first leg of the playoff semi-final.

Anyway, this guy was caught on camera filming, and, uh, if you want to go and see quite an entertaining cat-and-mouse chase as he made his way through the golf club, putting on a different disguise in the changing room and shooting out the back to the local town — Sky Sports have done quite a good, uh, reenactment of this.

Either way, this is an illegal act in the articles of the EFL. It has been — I think it's regulation 127 — which was put in after Leeds United were caught doing a similar thing in 2019, uh, where they sent someone to go and have a look at a Derby County training session in a January of a standard, uh, season, and they were fined £200,000 for that at the time, and then this regulation was put in place. So since then, this has now become a specific offense against the rules of the league.

Anyway, the Championship playoff semi-finals went on. Southampton, unfortunately for the EFL, beat Middlesbrough in those, and were due to play the playoff final on Saturday. And, in quite an incredible turn of events, at the independent hearing on Tuesday — which was taking a look at the case and deciding how to punish Southampton, who have not denied the allegations — um, the commission kicked Southampton out of the playoff final and reinstated Middlesbrough.

And even better, Charlie — I think the other reason we wanted to do this today in particular — last night we got the confirmation that the appeal Southampton had lodged against that decision has been rejected by the EFL, meaning Middlesbrough will play Hull City on Saturday in the playoff final.

听证会的当事方：Middlesbrough 该被纳入吗？

Couple of things I was confused about. I'd love your thoughts on, uh, when it came down to that hearing. The EFL and Southampton were the two parties involved in it — so Southampton were the defendant and the EFL were the prosecutor — but Middlesbrough were very upset that they weren't involved in it. They thought that they had a right, based on the infringements taking place against them. That confused me, firstly just because I don't understand why the team that have this act against them can't then be a part of that, and most importantly couldn't have appealed had the decision gone against them — which I think is key, because Southampton could have appealed. So that's a first point. I was just interested on your thoughts on that.

Well, that's totally basic — just following of the normal justice procedure. So, for instance, you know, if you just take it out of football, go into normal life: um, I'm walking down the street, some guy comes along, he nicks my phone. Um, I report it to the police, the police interview me, the police take all the evidence that I've got to give, and then they put it to the Crown Prosecution Service, and the Crown Prosecution Service decides whether the prosecution — then conducts the prosecution. I'm not then invited back in to give a further opinion on the matter. I mean, I might be interviewed by a barrister as a witness — maybe, could be — but yeah, I don't get the chance to sort of say "Oh, I don't think you did a very good job there, I think you need to go back and ask the same questions again," or whatever. It's not the way it works.

So it's absolutely clear. Um, as somebody who's been multiple times an EFL club director: if you have a complaint against another club, you file that complaint, you give your evidence to support that complaint, and the EFL in this case then takes that on.

And I can tell you, I'm involved in one of these right at the moment, with a non-league club that I'm a director of, Walton & Hersham. We are, um, complaining about our former manager, who's now at Yeovil Town, um, tapping up our players while they were still under contract at Walton & Hersham. Um, and these things are often quite hard to prove. In this case, it's actually a photograph of him coming to our end-of-season celebration party uninvited and then speaking to the players in question. But, you know, however open-and-shut we might feel the case is, the bottom line is we have to present the evidence that we have to the Football Association in this case. And the Football Association then has to decide, first of all, whether to charge Yeovil Town, and then how to do so and go about it. And that's just the way it goes.

This is totally and utterly normal. This is how it should be. So first of all, you know, I'm sure that Middlesbrough aren't complaining today, right? But in as much as they ever had a leg to stand on, it was a wooden leg — that they didn't have a leg to stand on. No, they should not have been involved beyond the initial complaint.

处罚本身：到底有多重？

Should we evaluate what the outcome of the actual decision is, because it is absolutely massive? So, the punishment laid out to Southampton — of being kicked out of the playoff final — means that they'll miss out on the chance to get to the Premier League, which, as we know, is, for three years really, over £200 million worth of TV money. They've also been given a four-point deduction next season. So, a significant punishment. What do you think about the punishment that's been laid out?

So, um, I'm hearing words in the media from people who should know better, like "unprecedented." Well — I, a youngster like you might not know the history, um, but those of us who've been around a lot longer certainly do. This is not the first time the EFL has kicked a club out of the playoff final. In fact, the last time they did it, they did it after a club had won the playoff final.

Right. So I'm going to come on to Southampton's words on this, uh, in a moment, but Southampton basically are being denied the opportunity — 50/50 opportunity — to access the Premier League.

When Swindon Town were found guilty of financial infringements in 1990, they had already won the playoff final against Sunderland and they were effectively already promoted, and then they were not just stripped of that, they were relegated to the division below that. Now, as it happens, on appeal, on an independent appeal, they got reinstated back up into, um, what's now the Championship. It wasn't called that at the time — it was division two, because we were still pre-Premier League, um, days. But it's worth bearing in mind that their chairman got sent to prison. Right, their chairman got sent to prison. Lombardi was up in court. You know, the whole thing was absolutely mega.

So when people talk about this being unprecedented — it's not. There was a bigger case that happened 30 years ago, which was more dramatic than this, and it all adds up to the same thing.

规则手册的神圣性：EFL 是组织者，不是监管者

Which is that when you enter a competition, you do so based on the rule book. And once you sign a document saying "I accept the rule book, I have entered this competition and I accept the rule book" — once you've accepted the rule book, that's it. From that moment on, if you break the rule book, then effectively you delegate the judgment as to what your punishment might be to the competition organizers.

People often speak of the EFL as being a regulator. It's not. It's a competition organizer. That's what it is. And to make that competition work, all the participants in the competition have to accept that that rule book is absolutely sacrosanct.

Now, I've heard from a few friends who aren't professionally involved in the industry — and, you know, even people who are sort of quite hot football fans: "Yeah, but is this really such a big offense?" Absolutely, yes, it is a massive offense.

为什么是大罪：第 127 条与 72 小时规则

Which is why the EFL changed their rule book after the Leeds United affair, 'cause they found out, to their horror, that barring a rather broad commitment to act — this is EFL regulation 3.4, which requires clubs to act towards each other in good faith, which is a rather sort of vague catch-all thing — that's what they did Leeds United under. But that's not specific enough to really land on a club with full force. And that's why they created EFL regulation 127, specifically, 'cause they found out and were then advised by their legal advisers: "Look, your rule book currently isn't specific enough. If you want to stop that behavior, you have to specifically state that that behavior is not on."

So let's just read out what that rule says. EFL regulation 127 prohibits any club from observing or attempting to observe another club's training session within 72 hours of a scheduled match between those two clubs. It could not be clearer. It could not be clearer.

Now, the reason for this 72-hour rule is because of the rhythm and, uh, sort of the way in which a club's training schedule will work. So typically, if you have a match on Saturday or Sunday, then match day minus two — or sometimes match day minus three, hence the 72 hours, etc. — is what the coach will call his tactical training session. So that is the training session when the entire intended starting eleven will line up, and either those first-team squad members who are not playing, or the club's under-21 team, will then be put into the formation that that manager expects the opposition to use, and they will then walk through the whole tactical plan for that particular match.

Somebody said to me this week, "Yeah, but surely Saints know what sort of system Boro play." Yeah, but that's just a generalization — the system. It's the tweaks. It's the little moments and nuances within the system, where they've spotted something about how, in this case, how Southampton play. And the Middlesbrough manager saying, "Right, within my formation, I now want you to position yourself five yards further right, 'cause I want to block off that inverted run that comes off that wing." "Hang on a second — I've spotted that when one of their center backs steps out, quite often the other one doesn't step out with him. That offers a chance for a run in behind. So let's rehearse that." That's what happens in this training session, and that is what was the intent in recording that training session: to try and find out the special little plans that Boro were working on to try and help them against Southampton.

So Southampton were deliberately cheating, and there's no other way around it.

蓄意性：周四训练课才是"魔法发生的地方"

You've used the word "deliberate," right? And that, to me, is where everyone who comes at it going "Is it that bad an offense?" just falls down. This was so premeditated. It was so planned, and strategically planned to the extent where the day they decided to do it was specifically the day you've told me that sometimes even directors weren't allowed to come and view training sessions. People directly working for the clubs weren't allowed to be there.

Absolutely. 100%, Charlie. I would be told — and this is not by one manager, this is by pretty much every single manager that I've ever worked with — in various of our commercial agreements there'll be, you know, "Well, in return for your sponsorship, you can come and watch six training sessions a year, with certain guests," and all this type of stuff. And I'd always speak to the football department, and the football department tell me, "That's absolutely fine, Charlie, but not on Thursdays." Right? Never on Thursdays. That training session is sacrosanct. Even people inside the club are not allowed to come and watch that particular training session. That is where the magic happens. That's where the manager makes his biggest single difference.

If you were to say to a manager, "Of all the things that you do in the entire week, what is the single most important thing you do?" — he will tell you the Thursday training session. Almost without fail. That's where the real work is done.

Sunderland 的故事：规则就是规则，即便是无心之失

So, deliberate in nature. And, you know, as you've told me before — I know you nearly fell foul of it at Sunderland — people get kicked out, teams get kicked out of competitions, for relatively, should we say, innocent mistakes, where they field an ineligible player in a cup competition and he plays for the final five minutes of a game, and then they're kicked out.

This happens every year, Charlie. So it's a fun story, because it ended up being quite dramatic in a way. So, this is going back a few years. I was at Sunderland, um, and we were playing in the EFL Trophy. And it's probably fair to say that, in the group stages of the EFL Trophy, it's not the most important match that you're going to play in any season. It's quite often a match where various young players are given opportunities — maybe some players who aren't currently getting first-team action, or whatever it might be. And that might include some players who've been brought to the club on loan relatively recently, because these group matches happen often in September and October, just after the end of the transfer window. So quite often what you'll find is a player comes in on loan, isn't getting much match time, and then the manager says, "I'm going to give him a few minutes in the EFL Trophy next week."

Okay. So we're playing — I think it was away at Rochdale — in the Pizza Trophy, 'cause it was sponsored by Papa John's, I think, at the time. And the team sheet was submitted. I wasn't there, and I had a call from our head of communications, our head PR guy, Oscar Chamberlain, said, "I've just got the team sheet. Um, I'm pretty sure that one of our players on that team sheet is cup-tied. I think he played this competition — he played one match, or a few minutes in this competition, in August or something, for his home club," or whatever it might be.

So anyway, [I] said, "Right, go and get the — go and get [it] before it gets in front of the referee, 'cause the moment your team is declared, the referee then — that is your team." So [Oscar was] scurrying down to try [to fix it] now, and we rescued the situation. I say "we" — I had nothing to do with it. Oscar rescued the situation. And the long and the short of it is that, you know, we ended up going all the way. We ended up — now, if he hadn't spotted that...

If he had — you won the trophy.

We would have forfeited that match. We would not have got through to the next stage, and we wouldn't have ended up at Wembley.

Right? This is normal. This is normal. When you sign up to a rule book, you fully accept that there are consequences that come with breaking the rule book. And that would have been a totally unintentional, you know, minor infringement, with no intent to try and gain an unfair advantage.

如果不严惩，下一步会怎样？

Because what you have to think about, Charlie — I'm just going to sort of finish on this thought and then hand back over to you — is: if breaking rules, and particularly intentionally breaking rules, is not subject to severe punishment, what happens next? Where does that go?

So let's just say, for instance, that the punishment for this rule breach — which is spying on clubs' training games — is actually a £300,000 fine, £400,000 fine, maybe even a two-point deduction in the following season, whatever it might be. What question mark does that put in other clubs' heads?

Yeah — "might be worth it."

Charlie, you know, that's why everyone says "throw the rule book at them."

And I'm not saying in this case, but — you know, that whole phrase comes about from people saying, when there's an injustice or a breaking of a rule like this, you have to make an example. And you mentioned their points difference and financial [penalties] — like, if they didn't kick them out, the two other options could have been those two things. Now, if they'd done a financial penalty of £300,000 and Southampton get promoted, you know, that's a drop in the water of the £200 million windfall they're going to get from Premier League revenue over the next three years just from the media. Secondly, a points deduction — they would have, you know, the Premier League and the EFL — that would have been a separate regulatory discussion, because the Premier League don't necessarily enforce the points deductions that an EFL would impose. So they'd have to have an agreement there. Moving into a different competition, you know, there wasn't a clean punishment other than a complete breach of that rule. Fully intentional, absolutely deliberate.

不是第一次：牛津与伊普斯维奇

And, Charlie, most importantly — not the first time they've done it this season. They sent a spy to Oxford United before the game earlier in the season, uh, to watch the training sessions. Now, Oxford really showed them how that was done, beating them 2-1 in the game. So that didn't exactly work well. And then in April, I think they did the same to Ipswich. Um, and that game ended 2-2. So it's quite interesting that he persevered with this strategy — Tonda Eckert, I'm saying here, which is the manager of Southampton — persevered with this strategy of sending analysts to go and look at training sessions, when it didn't actually throw up great results for them.

The premeditation there — I saw that, you know, in the Ipswich game, I think it involved the data analyst dressing in an Eastleigh kit as a disguise, because Eastleigh play in [a] similar ground — I think maybe even the same complex as where Ipswich trained. So you just start thinking about the amount of thought, right, that has gone into this. And his defense was "I didn't know this wasn't allowed," reportedly — that was the defense that was given. So it's the ignorance card, which, as we know, is not a defense. Now, that's unfortunate if you didn't know that, but you can't use that as a defense here.

It's absolutely not a defense at all.

Phil Parsons 的声明与"史上最大罚单"之争

Um, so I just want to read out what Phil Parsons, the chief executive of Southampton, has said — and, by the way, somebody who, you know, I absolutely respect, and I think he's actually a very good CEO.

Agreed.

So Phil's said: "We are sorry to the clubs involved, and most of all to the Southampton fans." That's fair enough — you know, that's correct. Because, yes, absolutely, the clubs have been wronged — the other clubs, that is — but it's fair also to point out that, you know, the Southampton fans have been spending a lot of money traveling home and away supporting that club, and effectively all of their hopes and dreams [have] been dashed on, effectively, something which they would have had no part in and wouldn't have supported.

He then goes on to say, more interestingly: "We believe the financial consequence of yesterday's ruling makes it, by a very considerable distance, the largest penalty ever imposed on [an] English club."

Now, I totally understand why he's trying to make that point. You have to try and, you know — as in poker, you have to try and do the best you can with the hand you're dealt. But it is — not to put too fine a point on it — first of all, I'd like to point out: they have not been promoted, as Swindon Town had been all those years ago. They are in the final, and therefore the punishment that's been given them might either be the largest or maybe the smallest. Because if they had actually lost the game this weekend and been kicked out [of] the playoffs, then they're not really giving anything up, really, in financial terms. Okay, there's a small points deduction next season — but then we've had teams getting handed points deductions this season for tiny infractions of spending rules. I mean — like, in West Brom's case, [a] tiny, tiny infraction.

So really, I don't think it's up to the EFL to assess the financial consequence of punishments it hands out.

£110 million 的真相：球员才是受影响最大的人

Because let's just think about this financial consequence as well. There's a lot of lazy thinking around what this means financially — winning the playoff final: "Oh, you get £110 million quid, it's the richest game in football." So, are we imagining that £110 million quid gets stuck in the club's bank account, that you just sit on it, and it's just like pure — it's like prize money, right? It's like prize money for a tennis player. It just sits there. Far from it. All of that money would be flowing straight out the other side into wages and transfer fees for new players.

So, in actual fact, the people who have been most significantly financially affected by this are actually — Charlie, who?

Players.

The players. The players are the ones who have been most financially punished by this. Because what you'll find in their contracts is that they've all got very significant salary differentials, [depending on] whether they're in the Championship or in the Premier League. I mean, by "significant" I mean really, really significant. So, I think, you know, I saw last night that some of the players are apparently, you know, considering their options, right, in terms of what happens here.

Well, I get that. But what Phil's saying here, I think, is specious. I get why he's saying it, and I respect his position — I do — and I think overall his statement had about the right sort of tone about it. But this particular point, I think you can't be saying to the EFL that every time they make a decision about one of their member clubs, they have to try and pause and get financial analysis on "Well, what would this actually mean for this particular club?" That then basically means that you give much milder punishments to clubs in the Championship, where the financial consequences are higher, and then much more severe punishments to clubs in League Two, where the financial consequences are lower. I mean, it's just absolute rubbish.

球迷的代价

It's massive for the players. But the fans — I mean, my God, if I was a fan of that club, I would be absolutely devastated. And you look at who's been responsible for this — you know, I think it's safe to lay this very firmly at the kind of, should we say, collective management table. The players, the fans, the ownership even — you know, we know a couple of the ownership, really good people, good owners; Phil Parsons, from by all understanding, [a] very good chief executive. Now, we can never say who did and didn't know what, but it looks firmly like this has come from the management side, and everyone else is having to deal with the fallout.

There's no chance — there's no chance the boardroom knew about this. I can't [see it] — no chance.

So, yeah. So, you know — but the fans: you've got a day like this coming up at Wembley, you've got a playoff final, everyone's bought their hotels, they've got their tickets, they've got their travel. You've probably got kids in there who, you know, [are] going to Wembley for the first time and experiencing the most incredible game — and then, in the most cruel way to the fans, you get that completely stripped away from you. You've got non-refundable hotels, I'm sure, for the majority of [them].

There might be a market for those rooms on the, uh —

You'd think so, wouldn't [you] — on the internet, from people coming from the northeast, who might be more [in need of] a hotel room than people coming from an hour and a half away in Southampton.

Hull City 的处境：他们该自动升级吗？

The point is that the impact of something like this is significant. But also, this is where I wanted to have your thoughts — because the impact on Hull City, on the other side: from having prepared, or begun to prepare, for a game of this magnitude against Southampton for a number of days, to then have the opponent also changed at late notice — I mean, really only confirmed last night, because that was when the appeal was sent through, last night being Wednesday night this week — like, that has a significant impact. There is an impact of this on Hull.

And my question really — that has been running around this morning, I've seen, but it's been the obvious one — is: surely, if Southampton are kicked out, they forfeit the final that they're in. Middlesbrough lost, right? So Southampton forfeit the final — you don't reinstate the team that was beaten, and Hull City go up automatically.

Sorry — is that something you actually believe?

I'm asking a question, because that's what I've heard this morning.

Sorry, I thought you were proffering an opinion.

No, no — I'm saying I've heard it this morning. And, look, it's another discussion to have, because Hull reportedly are exploring their legal options.

Yeah. Well, I take what that bloke says — the Hull owner — with a pinch of salt. Southampton gained an unfair advantage in a match against Middlesbrough. So, effectively, what you'd be doing — if I was regulating this — I would say: right, the match which they did that for, which was the first leg, that is then awarded 3-0 to Middlesbrough, which would be the usual practice in these situations — a 3-0 award. So, if you go back to the discussions I was talking about, fielding ineligible players: it's not forfeiting the game, [it's a] 3-0 award. So then, effectively, you say Middlesbrough won that first game 3-0, because Southampton tried to cheat in that first game, and then what happened at Southampton happened at Southampton. Right. So overall, Middlesbrough go through.

如果间谍战影响了常规赛季排名呢？Wrexham 的角度

Now, if this is solely related to matches during the main season, right, then there might have been an argument for saying, "Well, hang on a second — who was it who finished one place outside the playoffs?" Right? Because if Southampton have been getting an unfair advantage during the main season, then the club that I think has the greatest grounds for complaint is the club that finished one point — one place — outside of the [playoffs]. I think it might have been Wrexham.

Wrexham. Yeah.

Um, I would say they would be the ones to have the case. But it's an absolute try-on by Hull to say "We get an automatic go straight to go, pick up your £400," [going] to the Premier League without it actually even having to win a playoff final.

改变对手对 Hull 的影响：其实很小

What about the changing of opponent? Because, you know, in all seriousness, in football, I'd imagine that does have an impact. And this is a massive game for them. So this is far from ideal for Hull City.

Right. So, look — I think, as things have panned out, [it's] marginal. So what would actually have happened here is that it would have been clear from midway through last week that this was a possibility. So the analysts at Hull would have been looking at both Southampton and Middlesbrough, right. And then, when the decision came through — the first decision came through — it would have been "Right, our working assumption now has to be that we are going to be playing against Middlesbrough," right. And at that point, you've got four days to prepare. Now, in a normal Championship situation, you would have three days to prepare.

So they already knew that it might happen. And even after the decision was actually made, they still have four days. And even after the appeal was heard and rejected, you've still got that crucial main tactical session still to come. So I think any supposed disadvantage to Hull is pretty minor in the big scheme of things.

But I do take the point that has been made by a couple of people I've heard — that the fact that these offenses were also happening during the main season: you know, what does that mean in terms of Southampton's ultimate finishing position, and how would one treat that?

结论：他们其实已经被从轻处罚了

I've got to be honest with you, Charlie — I think, given the scale of the offenses, and given the seriousness of the intent to cheat, and the very specificity of the rule in question as well, which is so specific — I actually think they've gone off reasonably lightly. I think, you know — you've got to think about the other stuff that clubs are being done for. You know, getting their accounts a little bit the wrong way round — "We didn't quite get that amortization number quite right" — points deduction. Right?

Which can mean relegation, which can then mean massive financial hit, which can then mean —

Yeah.

100%, it can totally change everything in this situation. You know, Southampton finished fourth or fifth, I think it was — fourth.

Yeah. Well, they literally finished on the same points as Middlesbrough. I think they both finished on 80 points, I think.

Right. But, you know, Millwall finished ahead of them. Right. So we're in a situation here where I think that Southampton should go quietly on this one. I think it's such a bad offense, and so comprehensively proven, and specific and intentional, that really, you know, this is a sport — deliberate sporting cheating. In conclusion, this is an absolute shocker. And the idea that they should be allowed to do it and then carry on as if nothing's happened, with a little slap on the wrist —

From that moment on, given the specificity of this rule, you'd have to look at every other rule in the EFL book and say, "Well, if it's a few hundred grand slap on the wrist, I might as well just start breaking rules willy-nilly, just to see what I can get away with here, just to see where this goes." And the EFL can't let that happen.

And let's just remove the "precedent" chat. You know, what happened to Leeds in 2019 is very different. It led to the introduction of that new rule, and since then it has been very clear that you are not allowed to go and record an opponent's training session 72 hours before. I mean — I kind of laugh even having to say it. I find the whole thing incredibly embarrassing, from a start. But also, as I said, it's the people that are being impacted by it outside of this. My God — it's a horrible situation for the club. But, I mean, just absolutely crazy, crazy — and it's cost them.

收尾：周六的决赛

So, yeah — I mean, look, now it does all seem like everything's set to go, and with the appeal done and managed relatively quickly. Um, I think it's 3:30 on Saturday.

Who?

Middlesbrough versus Hull City.

Who'd bet against Middlesbrough now?

I know. I mean, they'll go up there — they'll go up. Mind you, Hull looked excellent against Millwall. So they had a couple of very, very good players in there, and Ollie McBurnie will be firing after being left out of the Scotland squad, incredibly. So — who knows?

Um, very good. Look, Charlie — great to do it, and I quite enjoyed the emergency pod. Thank you.

Very good. Thanks a lot.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、这不是史上最大罚单——这是一份被没收的"期权"
南安普顿 CEO Phil Parsons 说这是"英格兰俱乐部史上最大处罚"，主持人轻轻一句话就把它拆穿了：**如果南安普顿本就会输掉决赛，那么这个"史上最大处罚"实际成本为零。**这里藏着一个出人意料的真相——这个处罚的大小是**不确定的，它取决于一场永远不会发生的比赛的结果**。
用金融的语言说：南安普顿失去的从来不是 2 亿英镑奖金，而是一份**看涨期权**——一张"约 50% 概率 × 2 亿英镑"的入场券。媒体说"踢出最富有的比赛"，仿佛奖金会原封不动躺进银行账户，但主持人点破：这笔钱会立刻"从另一头流出去"，变成工资和转会费。所以这是一份"薛定谔的罚单"：在判决落下的那一刻，它同时是史上最大、也可能是史上最小，只有那场不存在的决赛才能让它坍缩成确定值。Parsons 要求 EFL 在量刑前先做财务影响评估——主持人反驳得很准：那等于"对英冠球队从轻、对英乙球队从重"，因为损失的绝对值不同。这恰恰说明，**惩罚一旦与"赌注大小"挂钩，规则就不再是规则，而变成了一张可以讨价还价的价目表。**
## 二、最反常的细

item.title: [Sequoia Capital] Notion’s Ivan Zhao: The Refounder
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Capital
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

最反直觉的一点：Notion 的两次"重founding"不是战略决策，而是身体决策

注意 Ivan 描述重大转折时用的语言系统——几乎全是身体词汇。决定裁掉团队搬去京都，是"你的身体会告诉你必须做""一种熊猫般的内在能量";第一次见到 GPT-4，是"a full body religious experience";给同行的建议是"feel the AGI";甚至 wartime 的方法论被他总结成"我必须照顾我的身体，每天健身"。这里藏着一个系统性真相：Notion 的组织战略，和京都那些做刀、做陶杯、做榻榻米匠人的认识论，是同一套——隐性知识，靠手和身体去"知道"，而不是靠计划书去推演。他说软件分两种，"造桥"（可预测、可演绎）和"酿啤酒"（不可预测、靠经验逼近）。这看似是产品比喻，其实是 Ivan 决策风格的自画像：他本人就是用酿啤酒的方式经营公司。所以他去京都不是巧合——他需要一个用身体而非用 KPI 思考的城市来给自己背书。

跨领域连接：被忽视的"钢铁悖论"

Ivan 说"语言模型 + 软件是组织的钢铁"，钢铁让大楼能盖得更高;可下一句他又说组织正变得越来越扁平。这看起来自相矛盾——钢铁让楼变高，AI 却让组织变矮?真相在建筑学里：钢框架结构让大楼变高的同时，恰恰拆掉了内部的承重墙，于是诞生了开放式楼层。组织的对应物一模一样：AI 这块钢拆掉的不是"楼层"（规模、营收量级仍在增长），而是内部的"承重墙"——那些专门在人与人之间推送信息、做微决策的中层。"更高"和"更扁"因此并不矛盾：还是高楼，只是没有了隔墙的中层。这也顺带解释了他那个全场最反潮流的决定——"杠铃式招聘"：只留顶端（提供 taste 与方向的资深架构师）和底端（年轻、便宜、高能动性的 IC），正中间被故意掏空。当别的 CEO 在抢资深工程师时，Ivan 在抢的是建筑结构本身的改变。

最深的系统真相：为什么"taste"和"cult"会同时流行

Ivan 一句话点破了全行业的困惑："taste is now in language models。"品味之所以突然成为热词，是因为能力（写作、编程、智力）被 LLM 商品化了，价值于是向唯一未被商品化的互补要素迁移。这是一条纯经济学机制：当一种生产要素被压成白菜价，租金会全部流向稀缺的互补要素——正如劳动被自动化后地租反而上涨。而 Ivan 把这条逻辑推到了尽头：他公开说公司就是宗教，全员会就是每周的礼拜，天主教会是史上最成功的公司，"耶稣是伟大的创始人，保罗是伟大的销售主管"。把"taste 崛起"和"CEO 被叫 cult leader"并置，会冒出一个 aha：AI 接管的决策越多，人类的"剩余价值"就越精确地收缩到那些无法被证伪的东西上——价值观、信仰、品味。AI 擅长的，恰恰是人类不再需要聚集的地方;人类仍需聚集的地方，恰恰是 AI 做不了的。所以"邪教感"在 AI 时代不是 bug，正在变成 feature。

一个少有人注意的镜像结构

还有一层自指值得点出：Notion 是一个"帮别人重构组织"的产品，而 Ivan 正用同样的手法重构 Notion 自己——拆掉 CMO、把营销劈成"讲故事"与"需求生成"、销售面试不看简历只看你 build 了什么。Notion 的内部重组本身就是它的 R&D：公司是自己的第一个客户，也是未来工作形态的实验室。再往外接一层——他随口说的"现代知识工作只有约 150 年历史，比火和语言年轻得多"，其实是整集最大的地基。如果知识工作只是一个 150 岁、被发明出来的临时形态，那么三角形组织图、中层、CMO、甚至"全职员工"都不是自然律，而是一段可被改写的工业惯例。Ivan 真正下注的，不是"给公司加 AI"，而是"知识工作这个发明，现在轮到我出第二版"。

Cold Open: Jazz Band, Not Marching Band

We start a mantra in the company. We want to be a jazz band, not a marching band.

I love that.

It's like a self-reflecting value thing. It's auto equilibrium with me. So, it's like, okay, I'm a jazz band person. So, we've been hiring more and more jazz band person. Also, AI happened in the past two, two and a half years. So, more jazz band people can really shine during this moment.

Okay. So, we had manager mode, we have founder mode. There's this new mode. What should we call it?

Jazz mode, maybe.

That's not bad actually.

Host Intro

Hey everybody. Today we have Ivan Zhao, the co-founder and CEO of Notion. I like to think of him as the refounder. Uh, he started the company a long time ago and in 2015 he didn't have product market fit. He and his co-founder moved to Kyoto of all places and kind of restarted the company and rebuilt it. After that, they got product market fit and it really took off.

He did the same thing in 2023 around generative AI in an offsite down in Cancun and literally restarted the company, and in my mind he's the canonical example of how a SAS company can move and become an AI company. So that's super interesting.

The second thing that's interesting is just hearing how he builds the company. He's very AI native in the way he organizes the company, the way he compensates people, the way he builds today. And it rhymes a lot with some other CEOs that I've interviewed, like Jack Dorsey a couple weeks ago. I hope you like it.

I think your outfit — it's uh, your pants could be looser.

Looser. Okay. On the bottom or just in general?

In general. Uh, I like your sneakers. I don't know what they are.

Okay. They're Italian. Premiata.

That's cool.

You're known as a very fashionable fellow. Where do you shop these days here in New York?

New York has a good store near here in like Soho, Tribeca, called L'Acassan. Um, not like a designer designer, but more like a European, easy to wear. Um —

I like the vibe.

Thank you.

Um, I gotta up my game. Ivan, just channel some nice — let's go shopping. You want to go shopping?

It's right around here. Afterwards, we can go.

Okay. Hey, I want to talk about CEO stuff, not just fashion.

Founder Mode and the New Mode

I've been doing this Sequoia thing for a couple years and a lot has changed in CEO. Like, the whole manager mode, founder mode thing happened, and now it's founder mode and some new mode seems to be happening. Let's just reflect back when Paul Graham's founder mode memo came out. My — my person —

When was that? Since 2022, right? 2021.

No, no, no. It's like — it was later than that. It's like 2024. My reaction, by the way, was relief, because I was a quirky kind of founder mode type of person. Always being — everyone's trying to talk me out of doing that. What was your reaction?

I didn't have too much of a reaction, to be honest.

Yeah. Nowadays, uh, you know, Jack Dorsey kind of redesigning his company to put AI in the middle, and Brian Armstrong doing the same thing. My sense is you're kind of heading this way as well. Um, can you talk about your journey there, your company's journey? What's new? How are you thinking about the organization? How's it changing?

Notion's Early History and Learning to Delegate

Yeah, my journey actually — I have a small detour off the founder mode, um, which is like trying to delegate, trying to grow. Notion has very been small as a company in the early days. Like, we had product market fit 2019, early 2020. Uh, we're sub-10-ish people 2019, and um, company become profitable because we're small, right? Um —

Did you raise venture in the early days, or you bootstrapped it?

We raised venture.

Okay, that must have been tough.

Um, was tough. Like, first five years just seed money dragged us for five years.

Yeah, HubSpot was like that. Took us a long time to get the product market.

It's very tough. But yeah, it's a different type of tough compared to now. It's — back then there's no product market fit. It's despair. You just black. You don't know where to go, right? Um, so we have been very small from the get-go, and we sort of taste the sweet of it, because you're small, you're profitable, easier to raise money, you're default alive. And um, we've been resisting growing team size, and during COVID a lot of companies really blew up — remember that, right?

I'm sure you did.

We relatively, yeah, blew up.

You got to product market fit at the right time.

We grow to product market around the same time. Um, there's a lot of eyes that you got to grow, so we did grow. Um, relative to other company, still resistant to grow a lot, uh, in terms of headcount. Um, but that's the moment I sort of learned, oh, you need to delegate, need to have introduced in professional manager, management.

Why? Who is telling you that?

It's a good question. Who tell us — some investors —

You didn't tell me that, I don't think so. Uh — it was conventional wisdom amongst the investor class.

I would say there's a pro and con for that. Like, we sort of, eyes, resist that for a long time, so we did not build a sales team. In retrospect, I should have started sales team earlier, because I thought I can first-principle the whole way, like create new kind of PLG, some kind of brand new motion. That's a mistake. Um, the other part is product, marketing, engineer — those functions sort of, I wouldn't call mistake, but we're upping the traditional way of building it.

Did you hire been-there-done-that people in that phase?

We did.

And do you have any of them still?

Um, many of them are still with us, yeah. Our CTO, it's last three years; before that there's another CTO during that phase. Um, but I would say some part of Notion DNA are defined by the classic SAS executive.

Building With AI: Bridges vs. Brewing Beer

Now, the thing changed in the past three years since we're building with AI. Like, we're one of the earlier um adopter of language modeling or product, and you start think things are breaking, building with this new technology — the classic boundary between roles doesn't quite work.

The analogy I like to use back then, it still is, is like building classic software is like engineering bridge: if you can design it, you usually can build it, fairly predictable. Um, like product manager talk to customers, pass to designer, pass to engineers, right?

Building with language model, back then and somewhat still is, it's like brewing beer.

You can't truly predict the things, the underlying thing — good analogy.

You cannot tell the yeast, hey, hey guys, go go go go go towards that flavor profile more — you can't do that, right? You sort of have to throw your best people in there.

See what the model, see what technology gives you. So it's not customer first, it's more experiment with this technology first.

Although the last year or so, model gets so much better now, but still the spirit is technology-first-driven development rather than customer-driven first development. Um, that really forced us to change at least how we build product. Um, designer is the most productive thing — our designer and engineer and product people all just sit in the same bucket, then work with evals, work with experiences and figure out.

Inside your company, has one of those personas kind of dominated and the other ones falling aside, or is there — they all kind of do the same thing now?

We always started by, like — our DNA in the early days always blurry. Like, we are one of the first to hire designer who can code. I'm a designer who can code, right? So we build a company based, um, my image and my company, my co-founder's image. So those profile really shines: designer who can code, engineer have strong product sense. Um, PMs not afraid to roll up sleeve and try things. Like, one of our heaviest token consumption person in the company is a PM, for example. Those people really shines, and we have quite a few of in Notion.

Org Structure: The Steel of Organizations

Okay, so you read the Dorsey stuff, you read the Armstrong stuff. My summary of it is they think of like a big triangle or chart. It's very inefficient flow of information up and down and across it. It's been around for thousands of years and it's time to rethink it. Basically stick AI in the middle and start delegating more and more over time, and letting the system make decisions and feeding it context in good ways. That's kind of my high-level summary of it. What's your take on what those two are doing, and are you doing basically the same thing?

In some sense we're creating product for other company to do that.

But yourself?

The high premise is similar, at least my interpretation of it. It's — so much knowledge work is paper pushing. In the past it's really hard, require software to push information from one person another, but so much of a knowledge work is fuzzy, so human need to be in the middle to pushing information from one to the other.

And turns out language model can do really good at two things. One thing is to write the software, to design the perfect pipes for your company to pushing information. Second is like doing this kind of mini decision making in the stop between one decision point to another decision point. Right? So a lot of — used to companies, a lot of people just in the middle to pushing information, decision around to sustain a business at scale — that could be rethink.

Okay.

The analogy I like to use, we've been using somewhat, is like before steel, buildings cannot be higher than five, six floors, and New York City are largely six-floor-tall iron and stones and bricks. After steel, there's — you, information or way structure can go much higher. Since sometime language model plus software is that — it's the steel for organizations.

What does the organization look like now that we have the steel? I know buildings got taller. What happens to orgs?

Uh, I think everybody's trying to figure out, right? Like Dorsey's structure is like three layers or something, or three type of people, right?

Yeah. Three types of people that sit around the AI system that mostly train it. Um, extremely flat. And I think Brian Armstrong said it's five layers and everyone's a player-coach, which I think is pretty interesting.

I think all the spirits are right. Yeah, I don't think — and I believe it should be a hierarchical. Like, I think people tried in the past, like flat organizational structure — I don't think that's part of human nature. Human nature are hierarchical.

And do you think that's changed now we have AI, that it can be flatter? Or you're like, that's BS?

Oh, definitely can be flatter. Like, we are getting flatter and flatter.

Okay, how many direct reports you have?

I have 7-8.

Um, in general, what is it?

The company-wise, probably around 7, 8, 9 people per person.

A lot of people are going to 15, 20, 25.

Yeah. Many of our best people, 15, 20-something.

Hiring: The Barbell Shape, Taste, and Agency

Got it. How have you changed your company, how you compensate people, how you build your org, how you hire people, just in the last year as all this stuff has developed?

Yeah. Two years ago, we start to um hire different shape of engineers.

Okay.

Um, no longer optimized for people's experience, optimized for people's um agency, energy, optimism, curiosity. A rough framework we're using, like — okay, what is the talent in the company? A talent equal to this person's capability, experience, times this person's um taste or value system, times this person's um agency, will, right?

What language model change is — like just like Google, everybody can access information, language model allow everybody to be a pretty good writer and programmer. So capability got normalized, democratized, and taste becomes still important: like what's your value system, what do you want to bring to the world, which direction do you want to — that you cannot change as easily. And your will, how hard do you work — this you cannot change. So we optimize for the latter to today.

Okay.

That means in engineer science we're hired for much younger people. Okay. Internally we're called like IC, early-out-of-school IC.

This is the opposite of the way most of the CEOs I'm interviewing are going. It's interesting.

I think more people are thrilled —

They say that, like, a junior engineer powered by the stuff's more productive, but if you got a senior engineer that understands the whole system, been there a while and they're plugged in, they're like a 100x.

Yes. So hire for super junior, super senior.

Oh, interesting.

So the shape, it's not — it's more like the barbell. Barbell shape, right? Um, because the senior provide taste. There's still a lot of out-of-distribution information in language model architecting and language models — no one, very bad with that. So they need to provide a direction. It's almost like this — a good engineer managing four to six coding agent at once, right? Um, but a good, really senior architectural engineer can managing two to three junior interns or engineer, they each manage two or three, then that's the number is higher, right? And they can train the next generation. Uh, I think that might be a more optimal shape than bunch of senior people manage coding agents.

The definition of designer, PM are different. Like, we're intentionally hired designer who work as PM. But designers want to talk to customers and get feedback.

Yeah, rare, but uh, don't stereotype designer though, they do exist.

So if I look at your website, you're hiring engineer, junior, senior. Are you hiring PMs and designers, or do you have some — I don't know how to combine those two words together.

We hire PM-designer. By the way, we interview them, we evaluate them — like, we're in talk with two to three design leaders, they're kind of — we value them as PMs basically. Are you — how — can you drive things yourself? It's not just come up good ideas. Um, how much are you interfacing with customers, besides you're good with craft, design craft.

Some of this stuff is like the fashions change. Like, for the longest time it was experience. Then the word everyone used was slope — we hire for slope. And now it's kind of taste and curiosity and agency.

That's kind of slope though. That's like the slope-ish.

How is it different?

Slope to me is just horsepower smart.

Well, intelligence is — first, intelligence multiple dimensions.

Like GPU speed, like how'd you do on your freaking SAT?

But there's a plenty smart people are lazy. That's for sure.

Do you call that high slope or low slope? I don't know. I just always bucket it into hire the people who learn fast and are very smart. And I think it's changed — the word taste comes up a lot, and you've been talking about taste for a long time, the industry talks. Why is that all of a sudden a thing? Why is that word everywhere now?

Because taste is now in language models.

Yeah.

Taste is in — if you zoom out a long, long time, maybe it is, but right now it's not. Yeah. And taste is more rooted based on your value system. Are you drinking sparkling water or still water? Are you drinking Diet Coke or iced tea? Yeah. It's to each their own. There's no right answers.

This is this year? Last year, this year?

Those are the two.

Totally.

Actually make the SAS feel like — what are we doing back then for?

Well, nothing changed for the longest time. Like, nothing, right? Like Notion, we had —

Until a couple years ago. Now it's completely changed.

Or you can't — well, come back to become operator if you like.

Oh, wartime is way more fun.

You feel more alive.

Are you more top-down versus bottoms-up?

It's like, they really can lead things, right? Um, and they're welcome me to top-down, into jump into any part I can work on, I'm interested in, where I can provide you new value, and they don't feel territorial. This is in a good place that people who didn't feel that way either no longer at Notion, or let them go. Um, so the company, me — in a pretty interesting equilibrium at the moment.

I love that. I do the orchestra versus a jazz band — I like the marching band.

Yeah. Like, I realized I cannot be a marching band person. That's just not me. I would feel like, if everybody — I delegate everything, everybody do thing, what do I do? I feel like I can't do that. Um, so it's like a self-reflecting value thing. It's auto equilibrium with me. So it's like, okay, I'm a jazz band person. So we've been hiring more and more jazz band person. Also AI happened in the past two, two and a half years, so more jazz band people can really shine during this moment.

Okay. So we had manager mode, we have founder mode. There's this new mode. What should we call it?

Jazz mode, maybe.

That's not bad actually.

Because jazz mode has some structure, right? Yeah.

You don't want to be like — you want other people to contribute. You don't want — you want people to have fun, not just you having fun or dictating everything.

Depth mode. I think that's a good idea.

Planning in the New World

One other question on this new world. Um, I work with all these startup founders. The planning's tricky, and I sat through your pitch at Sequoia and you had a plan and you had a P&L. Like, what is it actually like internally, planning-wise?

Well, group has our plan, which most company at this point — if it works, if group has your plan, right? So, um, I would say the financial planning is still — at least I find useful, to give you a like, you know you're on a treadmill, you know how fast you're running, right? That's like a financial plan in my mind. It's like, oh, am I running seven, eight, one, nine — what number that is.

But product strategy, it's — oh, there's no freaking plan. No plan. Uh, because what changes, the market, the technology, is so fast. It's not in the six month, it's not three months, it's week by week. So this you have to jazz this thing. Financial things march that, right. Um, then at least we try to be conservative, mid-to-conservative with financial things, and extra take risk. And there's a buffer — like cost becomes another dimension. Like, if you're just building software, don't think about cost.

No.

Now you have to — like, at least I have to really think about cost.

Gross Margins and Cost

Can I ask you about that? Like, a lot of these startups, gross margins are awful. And like, companies like HubSpot were like 86% gross margins — that's sort of SAS normal. What are happening to your cost and your gross margins, and where do you think it settles out for companies like yours?

I don't have an answer for you.

I'm assuming your gross margins have gotten worse, but maybe you didn't hire as much headcount, so maybe operating margins are better. And how willing are you to be like — gross margins to get radically worse?

Oh, you have to.

You're down for that.

You like — what are you doing then? You're not in the war mode, right? I think Notion is for knowledge work, which might have a different curve than coding products. Coding product, you run frontier models, usually the more, the smarter, the better. Um, and knowledge work, you can — we already start to see this — you sort of can settle with the second-tier, the Chinese models, the open-weight ones. They're not super smart. Like, a lot of this kind of paper pushing information in your company — oh, I spill coffee on the carpet, can HR team come someone to tidy this up next week — that doesn't require Opus to file tickets, right? So that's a lot of problem we are best at solving at Notion. So that will have a different gross margin profile than —

Yes.

— the biggest product market fit today, which is a coding agent.

Yes.

The Refoundings: Kyoto

Okay. You're kind of, in my mind, the king of refoundings. You've done it, I think, twice at least — Claude thinks you've done it twice. Uh, your first one, you and your co-founder moved to Kyoto, locked yourself in a very small room and rebuilt.

It's not that small, but yeah.

Okay. You know, more recently, obviously — everybody knows the story — you went to Mexico and you had early access to the models and you turned into an AI company. Talk about the different refoundings, and what was your mindset at the time. There's lots of early-stage founders who need to refound, and there's a lot of bigger SAS companies that definitely need to refound.

Yeah, I like — as an idea, as a — the best artists reinvent themselves. Um, Miles Davis has three, at least three face, right? Picasso, two to three faces. Uh, Michael Jordan, the second one probably not as good — no, but the best one, truly, Steve Jobs, the king of refounding.

Uh, the first version of Notion is like — we took us four, five years to find product market fit. In the middle of that, we sort of find the shape of the product we want to build, but we'll be running out of money building it. So my co-founder Simon and I said, let's just lay off everybody, just go by the two of us. That's the Japan story. Uh, we rebuilt Notion from Japan. Um —

How hard was that, the laying off everyone?

It's not easy, but sometimes your body just tells you you have to do it. Like, you know you're dead if you don't do it. Uh, you know, just there's like a panda energy, just like —

Why the hell did you go to Kyoto of all places?

Well, actually we never been to Japan. Cuz when you just say goodbye to your friends, all those people you work with — Notion, by all means not big, five people-ish. Um, it's — morale so low, right? So Simon and I was saying, like, let's go somewhere that we just changed the mood a little bit, and we never been to Japan. So initially I picked Tokyo on Airbnb, realized the house are so small. I don't — I love Simon, but I don't want to cram into a small apartment with him.

And Kyoto, the apartment much bigger, because they didn't get burned on the World War II.

They weren't bombed.

They weren't bombed. They spared that city — too much treasures. And the house is much bigger, so — and cheaper. So that's why we picked Kyoto. And we rented our place in SF, uh, and our office in SF, we actually become cash flow positive doing that. Um, that's the first time. But once you land, you start coding — just those kind of rep, coding, eating, coding, eating. Um, it's actually really liberating.

Japan and the Craft Ethos

I lived in Japan for a couple years. Was there something about Japan that was inspirational in your refounding or the way you built the product? There's a certain aesthetic there that sort of rhymes with Notion. Or could you have been in anywhere? Could you have been in Thailand?

Well, usually we like summer, warm — Simon likes warm climate, ideally he can swim. Um, Japan just happened to be — we're both curious about it. But once you go to Kyoto — I don't know, you probably been to Kyoto. It's a very special city.

Very special.

Yeah. Very inspirational. Those things are hundreds — everything. Oh, this shop, 150 years old, right? I'll open that door. The — the shrine. Gone to a beautiful place in Thailand, Phuket, whatever — do you think you still would have pulled it off, or was there something magical about the location that really worked?

Most of things are a story we tell ourself, right?

Of course. What story is the true story?

The story we tell ourselves is like, Kyoto is a special place. If you can pull off anywhere, you can pull off, from reborn in Kyoto. Then it's also inspiring, just look at the surrounding — the craft is probably craft capital of Asia, right? So just make you — okay, if Notion fundamentally is tool, is a tool for humans, and those people are building knives, ceramic cups, tatami seats — like, they're the tools, other tools for —

Craft, craft central.

It's a craft central. And like, how do we — how can you not be inspired by building a better software?

Okay, you still haven't answered my question, which is — if you had gone to whatever, let's pick some place in the Philippines, and it was pretty — would you have pulled this off, or was there something exceptionally important about being immersed in such a craft-centric place?

I think I pulled it off, but yeah, but the story is the better story.

Okay, fine.

The Second Refounding: Cancun and GPT-4

Okay. So your big refounding — not easy, didn't immediately hit product market fit after. Still not easy.

No, it took a while after that, because it took a year and a half to rebuild from scratch.

Why didn't you just quit and start a new company at that point?

We never start a company for starting a company. You want to build something. Like, I got obsessed about this Notion-shaped tool problem since last year college, and Simon and I were both in this kind of tools-for-thought community, which — you can trace the lineage back to Grateful — that era is the first generation computing pioneers around the Bay Area. Like, the lineage trace way back, directly back to there. I want to continue that lineage. Um, so to me it's not —

It wasn't like you had another idea kicking around. This is your life, and this is like bigger than — I can ever chew on this idea. So I wouldn't do anything else. He's in that community, he's obsessed with this idea too. So if we don't work on this, we'll work on another company doing the same thing.

Okay. I'm going to get back to the refounding, but just on the Grateful Dead — uh, you study very important computer scientists from the '60s, you're a student of Steve Jobs. I'm sure there was sort of a craft ethos about the Grateful Dead and the music back then, but certainly about the company, certainly about Jobs. Is it still here? It seems like it's a lot more commercial than craft today in Silicon Valley. Have we lost that?

I think most people don't have it. Most company don't have it.

I agree.

Like Steve Jobs — I look up to him, or people like him — is like interception of humanity with technology, versus Silicon Valley, at least tech, is largely a technology and tinker culture. And tinker culture doesn't even know the history of — it's not even science, it's tinkering, right? It's like people like to build things in their garage, and something works, and maybe has a product market fit and grow a business out of this.

To be fair, HP was tinkering in the garage.

Yeah, but you required to meet someone, to bring the human side of it, right? Um, I think tinker culture — the risk of us, like, you don't know what comes before you. Science, at least you respect the history. Like, if we ask people who um Douglas Engelbart or Alan Kay, those computing pioneers are — most people in tech have no idea.

Honestly, the only reason I know them is I work for this guy Ray Ozzie.

Ray Ozzie, like — who you would know, of course, because he's the father of Notes. But he was obsessed with those two and talked about them constantly.

Reality — uh, it's like Microsoft CTO for a while too, right? So um, that lineage is broken, by the way. The people who remember this — '70s and '80s, they were sort of — people don't even — yeah, they no longer work in tech today. Um, so tech is like industry doesn't know its past. If you don't know its past, you don't know history, which is humanity. I think your point of view is like, what's in front of you, what your competitor is doing. Versus, if you bring humanity, you have all other discipline around you, you have all the history behind you. There's way more good stuff you can steal from. Um, I wish more tech founders steal from aside or from behind.

I think they'd benefit from it too.

Advice on Refounding

Um, back to the refounding — lots of founders are going sideways. Uh, lots of companies that started in 2021 are going sideways. They've got 10 people. It's like, maybe they're cash flow break even. What's your advice to somebody — is it a founder been going sideways for a long time, got venture dollars in like you, and working on product market fit? Like, should everybody just be like, rip the band-aid off and shrink it and move to Kyoto and refound?

If their body tell them so. Like, I — for me it's like, there's — you just feel you have to do something drastic. And um, Simon during that time was — he broke his — he got his wisdom teeth taken out or something. So I'm by myself. I was like, I remember I was at my ex-girlfriend's studio, I just like, inner urge — there's no way out, I have to do this. And it's like, then then you feel liberated once you land in Japan.

I guess your advice would be — is probably more people should do it, and they should listen to their inner body and be more risk-seeking on that, because they can get stuck for a long time in a venture-backed startup. They can spend 15 years of their career on something going sideways.

I think there's no better time than now to do that, because the market dynamic is wide open.

Yeah, I agree.

The Second Refounding, Behind the Scenes

Okay. You refounded a second time when you had a thousand employees. Uh —

Back then it's probably 500.

Yeah, okay. 500 boys, different animal. Without telling the same story you've told a thousand times, because I think everyone knows the lore — what was it like, kind of behind the scenes? What did you feel? Was it the same thing? Your body felt it? Like, what was the story?

So the second time was in Mexico, Cancun, right? Happen to be out somewhere.

Right, you're always in a nice place.

We just got early access GPT-4 model. It feels like the world is stopping. Like, when you first interaction with the GPT-3, is fine. GPT-4 is a religious experience for me. It's like, holy [ __ ], it's a full body religious experience. Like, you have to do something with this. This is what change everything. Anything you do, if you don't do this, it will be meaningless.

Um, I think the company has been fairly — there is be people doubt, because remember that, around three years ago, that's the crypto era, lending, right? A lot of people — is this another crypto thing? There were a lot of doubters. Um, some of them obvious no longer at the company. But the conviction is so clear, at least for me and Simon early days, like, we just have to do this.

Did you take two steps back in the business to go 10 steps forward? Like, did revenue really slow while you're rebuilding everything?

Our first products actually has a meaningful revenue boost. We launched our uh first AI product actually two weeks before ChatGPT happened. Um, it's like AI writing product, that has a boost. But after that, we've been pretty much want to build agent product in 2022, end of 2022, and that's a slope — that took us a year and a half to really —

Didn't really work back then?

Didn't work at all. Like, Simon — I would say my problem and Simon's problem, we might be living too much into the future.

Yeah.

It bites you with new technology. Like, we try everything, built a model for us, work OpenAI fine-tuning or another — just none of them work. That was, like, a year and a half just go with no error, and morale is definitely low. Only start to work a year and a half — our revenue grow inflection, this around the same time, right, when the AI product —

The underlying got better.

Yeah. It's like, um — but that was a pretty painful period.

Was that the most painful period in Notion history for you?

Different type of pain.

What was the most painful?

Just when those two, three early days, pre-product market fit — is despair. I just like —

Despair is that the word. Okay.

You don't know where it's coming from, you don't know where to go, right? Um, when your growth rate low, like during that period of time where we were try to rebuilding our AI foundation multiple times — it's, yeah, that's not great. Um, right now, just everything on fire — it's a different type of pain.

Advice for SAS Companies Doing AI

Nice. So a lot of people, 500-person companies, a lot of SAS companies are doing the AI thing, but certainly haven't refounded. Advice?

Start with your product. We just talk about — treat it like brewing beer, not building bridges, because that's, end of the day, most tech companies is the product, right? So um, start with the products, cuz then you the founder has to be really hands-on to experience that way of building things.

Can non-founder-led companies pull it off?

I don't know. Would be tough. I think founder has the moral high ground to change things. People are more tolerant and forgiving with founders, right? Um, I think the person need to be use language modeling, in just know it. Like, you got to feel the AGI, feel the AI.

Yeah. You can't read about it, you can't watch YouTube. You got to build.

Feel — you have build it. Building is one of the best way.

For your product, or build for your internal systems?

Build for yourself. Yeah. Tinker on the weekend, tinker on the side. You have to do that, because, like, at the end of the day, business are kind of like pathfinding entities, right? Like, we're in this thing called a market, there's equilibrium for your company with the market, with your shareholder, with your company. Like, we're just finding local optimas, local maximums for different — our niches.

When this new brand new ingredient got introduced into the market — in this case language model — doors are, path that are opened up. And we are — each company at different point in the market, each you have different angle, you have different niche, you have to taking this, internalize this new ingredient, figure out which path got opened for you. The truth is, with the customer, with yourself — if you don't know how this can do, you cannot find a new path. I think you have to do something, you have to feel this — you feel the AI, feel AGI.

Decalcifying: Acquisitions and Founders

I think you're right. You use a word that I cringe at, but I think is good, called — a lot of companies calcify as they get older. Let's just say you're a company that's calcified. How do you get uncalcified?

We intentionally do a lot of acquisitions. We have 50, 60 founders at Notion.

And were they aqua-hires?

Aqua-hires, acqui-hires, acquisitions. Um, founders are kind of this kind of like little decalcified meathead machinery, just trying to break things, right? So —

Keep your regenerating.

I agree with that. Yeah, um, I find that really helpful.

So, founders found for a reason, and now all of a sudden they're in a thousand-person company. How do you keep them motivated and not leaving and starting something new?

Well, first, some of them work on a domain that they were working on before. Like, the person who leading our AMO has a startup of AMD notes before the company. The person leading our enterprise search is a founder of enterprise search product. So you give actually give them a better platform support to do this. They people feel appreciated. Like, I just had lunch with the founder who start enterprise search product before — it's like —

You give them leverage, they give them —

You give them leverage. They actually people care about the thing they're building, right? Versus fighting for product market — think really crowd the market, they don't. You can argue that it might be more difficult to start new companies today compared to even a year ago.

Okay.

Because the world's getting more noisy. Like, the shipping cadence from existing companies are just increasing. It's like the — everybody got Twitter fatigue, right? So —

And the SAS companies have got the memo. Most of them got the memo and are shipping fast now. You were ahead of everyone, but —

Yeah, people like — it's still —

Like HubSpot's cranking.

That's good. No, that — people are still kicking that — they have to kick in different gear, you know.

My thing these days is like, it could — it's never been easier to start, man, has never been harder to scale. Because, like, in every little micro segment, if there's a little traction, like there's a hundred competitors two days later. And the thing I've also noticed about the startup land is, almost in every one of these verticals, it's a duopoly. There's like a one and a two, and then 50 tied for third — they're way off.

When you look back in time — the great companies were often founded in shitty times, because people who are really grinders and people who really want to start a company and really have a vision, didn't start in like 2022, 2023, a terrible time to start. In times like 2026, you get a lot of tourist entrepreneurs. And so I've heard people say, you know, 2026 ventures is going to be crap, even though the valuations are —

Like crypto, crypto vibe energy.

Yeah, a little bit.

A little bit. Um, I don't disagree. Market will do the thing. Market is kind of beautiful in that way.

Yeah. Advice to founders of reasonable-sized companies who are a little scared to refound? You know — I'm CEO of a 500-person company, it's going pretty well, I'm doing some AI stuff, my board's kind of happy with me. Um, but there's a birdie inside of me listening to Ivan, and maybe I should refound. Advice? Does a person need to feel the AGI first?

If you feel — you realize most tech industry is support different flavor knowledge work, that we're at the beginning of how this can do to this industry.

The Future of Organizations

I agree with you. How do you think organizations look like? So, they're changing a lot now. What do you think they look like in like three, four years? It's your knowledge work — you're right at the center of this.

I think truth is probably somewhere there's a Dorsey, Jack Dorsey's thing. Uh, there is the traditional hierarchical ones. Um, I like to think, what are invariables? Like, let's look invariable. In this case, human nature are rather static. We all —

We don't change that fast.

We don't. Human don't change. Humans as a species doesn't change. That's where I think it should — it will be a hierarchical organization. If just observe bonobo, chimpanzees, right? It's like, very — if you watch nature program, there's hierarchy. That doesn't change, this everything hasn't changed of us since we left Africa.

Um, division and labor make sense. Um, so people have different interests. You look at all the different, like, personality tests, right? They're just different way to saying, like, people are different. They're not 10,000 flavor of them there, but there are probably a dozen flavor of humans. They cluster around this different dozen things.

And we'll know that — our legal system, well, at least right now, there's no autonomous companies, right? It's the CEO, CFO will have to sign up for something. So it will be a small team of human to start. So those to me are the invariables. So this invariable tells me, you need to figure out how those group of human collaborate. The group of human all have different biases, preferences, different values. Um, and it will be somewhat a small, some kind of hierarchical system there. Then, how do we use this new technology, new ingredient to passing information, decision around a small, midsize group of humans? That's how I think about it.

I kind of think about it as like, right now in companies the AI system makes very few of the decisions. Let's call it 1%. But as that system gets more context — using Notion for example — it'll make better decisions than humans. It'll have more context and more history. And then humans will be more training that system and giving it context and giving it taste. And I think over time the system makes more and more of the decisions instead of the humans, but the humans are still really necessary.

Yeah. People say AI researchers, in 15 months, will not be able to make decision to improve AI models, right? Kind of the same thing, but they're just in a bleeding edge for this.

Yes. By the way, I think you're incredibly well positioned for all this.

Oh yeah.

I worked for a while with this guy Ray Ozzie. It was a company called Groove Networks. We were in the knowledge management industry, which is a [ __ ] industry, cuz chief knowledge officers have no money and it was never urgent. In theory, it sounded great. Now, knowledge management is actually incredibly important and works incredibly well.

I know. It's like — what's that industry called in the '90s? People hire consultant to map out their org chart and information flow — that become like what AI and for deployment.

Totally. And your platform is ideally suited for it.

Yeah. Um, I think we always forget that modern knowledge work is only about 150 years old.

It's invented. It's not as old as fire or language. It's only 150 years old.

Yeah, right. So why can it be a new flavor of it?

On Ivan: Introvert, Schedule, All-Hands

Okay, I want to talk about you.

Okay.

I don't know you super well. You're soft-spoken, and I thought you were an introvert, but in getting to know you better — was I wrong about that?

I think I'm still introverted.

Okay.

Yeah, I like one-on-one conversation, one too many. I train myself to do company all-hands just by doing a lot.

A lot of founders are introverts. The world is sort of built for — I'm kind of extroverted, extroverts. Advice for founders who just don't like talking to other — you don't like the whole extrovert game.

I have to evolve a lot. Like, I hate doing —

Force yourself? Like, how — to work, a significant other push you?

No, I force myself. Okay. Um, to lead the group of human, you need to do one-to-many communications, otherwise people don't trust you. They want to hear from you. Writing is one mode, which is like, sometimes long enough. But even more so is face-to-face, to the all-hands. So for a while I try to delegate all-hands to my co-founders, to other execs — no way. I have to, now, at every all-hands, I'm open the mic. I do it. I lead all-hands. Now I'm pretty comfortable with it. Uh, I still don't love it, but — well, it's not, I don't dread it.

Okay. Advice for people doing all-hands? Everyone seems to do it differently.

Oh, get a teleprompter.

I'm the opposite. I can't read fast enough for the teleprompter.

Really?

Yes. I hate a teleprompter.

Well, that changes — me, because, like — well, English is my second language, so it's like, if I have to think and speak, it's hard. So I just prefer to spoken before, and right now all those, like, uh, speech-to-text model locals are so good.

They're so good.

So good. I literally, like, okay, the night before or then, I just like talked to myself and got what I'm roughly want to say, and the time doing all-hands, teleprompter there.

Do you do them once a week, once a month, once — like, what's your cadence?
