I mean, in fact, if that was the case, all of us would be first class politicians. If most of us went into the political arena, the people would rightly be suspicious of us, right?

And one of the elements of the success of Mr. Modi, I mean, you know, doesn't matter which side of the political spectrum you are on and And, you know, whatever changes might happen in the next four or five years. But you have to admit that just in sheer political terms, he was a once in a generation politician. Absolutely. The sense of being able to produce an identification. Actually, Trump has that in some quarters, actually significantly. And one of the questions I said, I've now puzzled over, which is, It's not obvious to me what an answer to this question is what makes for a political class that can generate this presumptive trust and then hoping that a political class that can do that can actually direct that trust to constructive purposes.

To me, I think for a democracy, that form of trust is probably the most important one, right? Because that gives the state the right kind of authority and headroom. be able to do the things that in a sense would. And I think one of the disappointments about Mr. Modi, I mean, you know, there is the kind of the deep worry about communalism. But just on his own terms is that he had this extraordinary headroom in terms of that presumptive trust. And in a number of areas has not capitalized it in a way that his supporters had hoped.

内婚制、隔离与种姓的三个机制

So, I'll tell you where I would push back on the trust question. The way we thought about market transactions and social transactions is once you have trust in the market sense and the structural transformation, some of these other identities that we rely on to foster that, you know, within network trust can start getting weaker, which means then you are less reliant on the pathologies of that particular identity. We seem to have carried on with both for the last 30 odd years. We have people from every caste participating in the structural transformation, which, as you pointed out, is the wonderful news. And yet, we don't have the kind of breakdown of marriage endogamy as one would expect coming out of that structural transformation. Even everyday dignities or indignities, rather, of, you know, where you're allowed to live, where can I get housing? You know, what kind of a family will accept me either as a paying guest or, you know, accept our child, you know, to study with them or get tutored by them or eventually to marry into a particular family. Those things have not moved much. The amount of segregation we have in our urban landscape is not that different from what we had before. So it's almost like everyone goes out to this space of a market order where everyone mingles. And then we go back to our separate corners, which are largely within network. And I don't know if that's only about social trust, but there's something there about trust, which seems to be a factor.

No, the phenomenon you're describing is, is, is, is spot on. I mean, you know, Indian cities are amongst the most segregated and, you know, My colleague Parthamukhapadhyayar has asked this question, are Indian cities urban? Right? In the sense of what we associate with urban and urbanity. We're just a collection of ghettos almost.

But there's two or three different things, I think, here, right? One, and I honestly don't, again, I'm less confident of answers on this one, which is, and not to kind of exonerate what's happening in India, but segregation has proven to be so difficult to address globally. I mean, it is actually truly astonishing in some ways. I mean, you know, lots of different hypotheses, the kind of bland social science kind of hypothesis that the threshold of collection of action has to be so high, right, for actually desegregation effects to kick in. And those are very hard to achieve through policy instruments. Through the old-fashioned ones, which is just the sheer persistence of racism or casteism in the kind of deepest sense possible. And it's probably true that there is a mix of both, right? So, yes, I mean, absolutely. I think we still don't understand the deepest dynamics of segregation, right?

The second thing I think, but I think in the Indian condition, I would argue that the basic social preconditions for the kind of social endosmosis that Ambedkar talked about, You know, that phrase from Dewey he used a lot, right? And I kind of like that phrase, endosmosis. You know, everybody in a sense kind of freely mingling with each other, right? I think those conditions, I never thought they would actually come just from market interactions. In fact, quite the contrary. I mean, what the market does is it produces two effects on society, right? One effect is actually a question of individual reciprocity. So market transaction is just that. It is an atomized act of individual reciprocity. You have something to sell. I have something to buy. We exchange. And the reciprocity impact part of it is morally important. It's a very important form of reciprocity, but that's all there is to it, right?

The second thing that we thought the deepening of market relations would do is, in a sense, weaken the relationship between occupation and caste, right? Because one of the things that had made the three mechanisms that made caste such a stranglehold, one was endogamy, right? The most persistent. The second was the association of caste and occupation, right? Which in a sense, completely fused economic and social power in deep ways. And the thought was that if these can be dissociated, even if caste does not disappear, right? It actually either mitigates its effects or it has to take, in a sense, a newer form. And the third was the monopoly over education, right? The three basic mechanisms.

Now, if you think about the three basic mechanisms, we barely progressed on all three, right? Yeah. We barely progressed on education. Again, the formal data looks good, 100% school enrollment, finally 75 years after independence. But that's different from learning. Not just different from learning. I mean, India is one of the few countries, few democracies, where the kind of experiment that most democracies have done with shared public education. I mean, American democracy was built by its public education system. That was the crucible of citizenship. We did not create that public education system. And again, this is not about just the ownership structure, public versus private, but the idea that the school is actually a public institution and a crucible of citizenship. Even as a pedagogical philosophy does not exist. And, you know, for those of us who've lived in England, in America, for all the problems of its schools, this aspect was extraordinarily powerful. renounced. So yes, we have education, but those effects have not kicked in.

And actually, those are also important for endogamy, because actually the social space is going to come through education. It's not going to come through market relations or maybe even office relations, which don't kind of exist. I mean, you know, one of the reasons endogamy, I mean, there's a joke going around in India that, you know, India will, like in everything, skip every stage. We will not go through this stage of sort of really dismantling endogamy, but marriage as an institution might disappear. I mean, at least in urban circles, that's been talked about quite, quite, quite openly. I've heard the other version, which is actually endogamy we're going to now also see in queer relationships. Apparently, like, you know, in queer dating websites and things like that, people have caste preferences.

So, but again, if you think about deeply about the conditions that would even allow endogamy to be dissolved. Again, none of this is a guarantee, right? Actually, none of those social institutions, in a sense, exist in a serious form. And the caste and occupation dissociation is also much slower. I know our common friend Chandrabahan Pasad has been assiduously documenting that dissociation. He's much more optimistic about that dissociation and its effects. But you'd say in aggregate. Yeah, the scale at which it needs to be done hasn't happened.

The introduction of the welfare state, we can talk all about the expansion of the Indian welfare state. But the fact of the matter is 40 to 45 percent of the country still lives at levels of precarity where those norms of social identification matter a lot. It's not even an option. actually say I'm going to reject kinship. Absolutely. And that's what I meant by kind of the endogenative caste which is that the basic things that Dr. Ambedkar very clearly identified. We've actually fallen short on that.

The one last thing I'd say about caste which I think is important I think in this context and particularly in relation to the question you raised about segregation which is One of the unintended consequences of the reservation debate in India, to my mind, was that the question of ethics became completely sidelined in favor of a pure politics of distribution. Now, distribution itself has an ethical dimension. But ethics meaning a very simple proposition, which is how do we relate to each other? How do we treat each other? again on a spectrum, at the very least not inflict the worst kinds of indignities on each other.

Once the debate switched to the distributive question, two consequences happened. One, I actually do strongly think that for a lot of urban Indians, the specificity of the Dalit experience has actually been eclipsed. I mean, it's all become, you know, all backward castes, Dalits, all as if this was just one big historical blob. You know, once you begin to say 90% of the country was oppressed, India is the only country where 90% was oppressed and 80% of them were oppressors in their own way, right? So that complete eclipsing of that ethical question, and it's supplanting by apologists, raw politics of distribution. And you see it reflected, unfortunately, even in institutions of higher education. You have granted access, but you've not asked the question, what are the pedagogical practices, ethical relations that should, in a sense, govern this modern campus? It frankly let the upper caste off. We've given reservation. What else is there? I mean, that's the milder version. The worst version is, and look what a disaster is. I mean, that's the kind of unacceptable, you know, version. So that whole kind of ethical debate around caste in some ways, I think got sidelined in this political articulation.

制度建设的必要条件

The last question I have, and feel free, if you want to keep this abstract, is you have a very long track record of building institutions. You know, you build CPR, you were instrumental in building Ashoka University. And your experience both shows what it means to be an institutional builder long term, but also what are the kinds of constraints that are faced, you know, in modern India and across the world, perhaps, in developing in independent institutions that need to survive over a long period of time to really fulfill their purpose. So you don't need to get into organizational details, but what are the necessary conditions for institutional building going forward, like from where we are today, not the glorious past when these things were possible? And is there a purpose for these institutions beyond truth-seeking? And if so, what is that purpose?

So, I mean, first of all, I would not take the credit for building Ashoka. I mean, I was there for a brief time and I think at a crucial time in a sense in its history. And, you know, looking back, I think, you know, my own story about CPR is actually, I think it's now looks like more of a story of how we failed to build it. But that is a kind of, you know, in a sense, a segue into, I think, the general lessons.

So, you know, One, I think, general thought, I mean, I know it's an obvious one, but sometimes the most obvious things are missing from our societies, right? Particularly true of institutions of higher education, right? I mean, there is an extraordinary conceit in an institution of higher education. I mean, the conceit is not just that we are going to, you know, shape minds and be this place where young people discover themselves and flourish, but the time horizons on which they operate. I mean, you know, many of the world's greatest universities are hundreds of years, hundreds of years old, right? And you need, and in fact, often they've survived lots of political discontinuity. I mean, that's, you know, there's been a kind of bulwark against that. Political, religious, you know, ethnic mass mobilization, so many things. And, you know, I mean, India also had amazing, you know, amazing institutions of those kinds.

And one of the interesting questions is, where do you get a sensibility that actually even has that ambition? You know, the more, you know, we often talk about it in the context of human capital, right? Where do countries get human capital from? How do societies generate entrepreneurial capital, right? Again, that's something that's produced. It's not something that's, in a sense, given. There is something, in a sense, similar here, where what are these moments in history and for what reasons do you actually get this extraordinary alignment, right, of actually people thinking of this as their professional identity? This is a thousand-year project, right? Right.

四条腿的桌子：学术共同体、国家、资本与公众

The second general principle, I think for a modern society, I think it goes without saying, is four things have to work in tandem or at least not completely at cross purposes. The professional identity of the community of academics and their vocation and mission. The state valuing these things. Capital supporting it enough. I mean, honestly, we cannot wish away capital as much as we, you know, we can rail about sort of, you know, and the influence of donors and so forth. Fourth, a kind of demanding public. Yeah. Right.

And again, there are these brief moments even in, so when I think of sort of, you know, snippets of good institutional building phase in India, I mean, I think of Ahmedabad in the 1950s and 60s, right? This is... interesting alignment of Gujarati capital, the state, independent academics. And union movements and mass movements. And a sense that this is actually an engine of empowering you, even though it looks like an elite institution. Those alignments have actually got to be actively produced by some forces of political leaders. If any one of these legs is weak, It's actually very hard to sustain, build and sustain, build and sustain those institutions.

And each one of them actually understands that one of the values of that institution is just by existing. It's a force for democratization. Not in the sense that it actually necessarily preaches democracy or is kind of brainwashing people into a progressive ideology, but because it is actually an alternative center of power. And has a different basis for legitimacy, because that's what a liberal society also requires, different principles of legitimacy across different kinds of institutions. I mean, Michael Walzer used to have this nice phrase about, you know, liberalism is the art of separation, that the only way this politics can work is if you can make distinctions between public and private. If you can recognize that you need capital, you need the state, you need the autonomy of knowledge, There are principles specific to each of these fears. They lean on others for support, but they have to do so in a way that their own essential integrity is not compromised. And so they should not be subject to a similar singular logic, right? So if you do subject, for example, I think if you do subject higher education to the logic of profit, there is no way it can actually fulfill its core mission.

YouTube、影响力者与大学的不可替代性

But... You know, I see two things happening. So one is this sort of exit from state built or state led higher educational institutions. And this has all kinds of problems, right from the way they treat personnel to leadership to, you know, campus politics and so on. But the second part simultaneously is sort of the rise of the multimedia education empire that we have. I mean, there are enormous YouTube stars who are actually teachers, you know, teachers who are teaching IIT classes or helping you prepare for civil services or something. And now they've become modern day icons or heroes almost, which I largely applaud. But it's very difficult to convert that into some kind of stable institution. So is this a threat? Is this a like what is a good way of thinking about it? So I also like you operate in the world of second best where I'm like, thank God for YouTube. You know, if everyone can't get a decent STEM education in their, you know, local school, then at least there's someone to teach them literacy and numeracy. But on the other hand, that doesn't quite seem to be the only purpose of an education institution, which is literally transferring skills and knowledge. There seems to be something bigger, which we're now not able to do on digital media or social media. So some physical building and continuity seems to be important. So how do you grapple with that?

Look, you know, as you have pointed out, I mean, we are at a moment where There will be massive disruption in higher education because of these new technologies. There's no question about it. And like all disruptions at the moment, it seems there's both a democratizing potential and possibly a destructive potential. Or at least narrowing. Yeah. As happened, I think, in the first phase of social media when we thought of its impact on politics. Initially, there was this incredible euphoria about democratization. And then, you know, it turns out it has authoritarian possibilities as well. And the honest answer is we don't know where all these chips are going to fall. But I think there's agreement that there is massive disruption.

Now, the thing about the kind of the influencers, the YouTube here, which is, of course, it's part of a general phenomenon of... kind of curious individualization of authority, right? Because the world of social media does not require intermediaries and gatekeepers. And so it's not just happening in the realm of idea. It's happening practically to every institution of society, whether it's media houses, whether it's... Any institution whose function was kind of arbitrage and mediation is increasingly under threat, right? Yeah.

I think the question to ask, and I think this is the one which is, one, obviously a university has a very different social function, particularly a residential university. It has the function of scholarship and the production of knowledge. And I think this sense of we are just trying to rest, you know, it has to convey what it means to rest snippets of intellectual order. a world that seems so unintelligible to us, what that process looks like, what that enchantment looks like, right? That to me is still the core purpose of the university. I mean, if you don't think that's the core purpose of the university, we have no business being in universities.

But of course, as a site of education, right, universities have traditionally been the most powerful agents of a certain kind of socialization. I mean, The joke in America is that, you know, you broke an endogamy basically by the university system, right? I mean, in a sense, it's socialization function. At all different levels of experience, personal relationship, the experience of this kind of transition point between leaving home and being then completely abandoned to, in a sense, the wider economy. I mean, it's this sort of, you know, still structured arena. that incredibly luxury space that a four-year education is supposed to give you, which is it's the last time you probably have in your life where your function is actually to accumulate intellectual capital, discover yourself in some ways, in a way that becomes much harder later on.

And to that extent, There will still be need for institutions. I don't think those functions will go away. And there's, I think, another market reason to think you will still need institutional universities is that a lot of what we see by way of individual influencers and so forth, they have relied upon the fact that they themselves came through these institutions. Now, you could say, look, this was the story of the past. You may not need them in the future. I suspect you still will. I mean, that this is going to be the cherry on the cake, maybe a slightly bigger cherry, maybe a plum on the cake, as it were. But the conditions of interchange through which our views evolve, through which we form identities, that will still require modes of socialization. And in fact, I could make the stronger argument that if you don't have those modes of socialization, what you will actually produce is a society of incredible anime and atomization. I mean, the theme which we began with, that if even these last bastions of structured sociability and the formation of non-religious identities disappear, we are just like, you know, from grade one, we are on our own, you and your YouTube channel and one influencer, right? And an occasional chat in a cafe with friends. Yeah. think that atomization, frankly, is going to be detrimental to society. So I suspect that functionalist logic will kick in, even if you don't buy the intellectual logic entirely.

I'm actually nervous about the you and your AI tutor, you know, having the conversation, at least even with the YouTube influencer, there is a community which is not in contact, but there is this sense of community that we all follow a particular influencer. But one-on-one you and your AI tutor could unleash amazing things, but also a terrifying level of atomization.

Yeah, no, you know, there was one essay I read recently, I think it was in New York, where they were interviewing a bunch of undergraduates about, you know, what they thought of AI. And there's this one Princeton undergraduate who says this absolutely extraordinary thing, actually, why she likes AI. So she says, look, in a sense, it solves three problems for her in learning. One, she says, with the best professor, right? may get two hours lectures a week and maybe 20 minutes office hours times. This is unlimited. Two, this is unlimited Socratic questioning. Yes, absolutely. Actually, that even when it's getting it wrong, if you approach it in a Socratic spirit, if you just approach it in as, you know, I'm taking a question and not thinking about it. And three, the more kind of interesting social thing, which is the fact that right now, at least one is doing it in a context where we are not being judged. opens up even greater Socratic possibilities. Yes, absolutely. Which maybe traditionally, you know, education won't.

But I do think, I mean, you know, the human element, I mean, you know, there used to be these studies which used to say that one of the hallmarks of a good teacher, right, indicators of a good teacher, actually had to do with one physical attribute of lecturing, which is how you make eye contact. Yeah. You know, they looked at like tons and tons of videos of how people lectured and apparently eye contact turned out to be a pretty good proxy for how much effect you actually had on students. So, you know, maybe we will need that eye contact.

不结盟 2.0 在新世界秩序中是否还成立

At the head of the conversation, we were talking about what's happening in the global order. And I just want to end with what that means for India. So, you know, in 2012, you had written Non-Alignment 2.0 and that Within that, there was the baked in assumption that the international system had enough order and coherence, despite all its flaws, you know, for India to navigate it and get a kind of place in the world and strategic autonomy. Now, that's completely changed and you've written a lot about it. Does that 2012 framework survive for what India needs to do going forward? If it doesn't, what replaces it?

I think here's what survives of that framework, because, you know, I think one of the things we underestimate about non-alignment, partly because we just confuse non-alignment with the non-aligned movement. I don't mean that. I know you don't mean that, but I think for some listeners, I think it's an easy association sometimes, is the core idea of the non-alignment was... We have to ask the question, what kind of world order do we want, which allows maximum space for our development and adheres to some basic norms, which in the long run are good for all of us, right? So it was actually an exercise in world order building. In fact, it did not even take for granted. I mean, I know the context was there is a Cold War, there's a Soviet bloc, there's an American bloc. Can you be non-aligned between them? the ambition was actually deeper.

And it was tied to, I think, a second assumption, which these days gets ridiculed left, right, and center. The deepest assumption was that, look, even if sometimes in the short run, there might be choices that are not entirely in your optimal interests, right? It is almost certainly the case that most things that will be good for the planet as a whole or humanity as a whole will end up being good for you. I think we forget actually the power of that assumption where if you gauge every little war and skirmish with what it'll do for me in the next 10 years, as opposed to saying, what does it do to institutionalize the rule of law or the next, you know, long due day, right?

So in that sense, I think, and then, of course, the third idea was a kind of the idea of kind of strategic autonomy in some ways, which is that, and strategic autonomy was the idea that actually what you need is options. Yes. We never fulfilled the material dimension of strategic autonomy, but it was that in order to be an autonomous actor, you actually need a range of options. And when you devise a strategy, you devise it in a way in which those options are actually available. You're not locked into just one choice or another choice.

I think those three principles, I think, still hold, I think, quite powerfully. Because now, right now, if you see the critique of non-alignment, so on the options view, it's, oh, we have no option but to align with X or Y because, you know, China is an immediate neighbor. We have to put all our eggs in the U.S. basket. What options you can accumulate will be a matter of context. But you have to think of those options.

I think the second point, that I do feel strongly about, maybe this is the kind of last vestige of the idealism of Indian nationalism, you know, the Tagore Gandhi Nehru legacy kind of left in us, which is it can never be a mistake to ask the question, what is good for the planet and humanity will in the long run be turned out to be good for you. If you are always playing the short term tactical game, you will undermine not just your interests, but you will actually undermine your capital in your moral capital in some ways, which I would also say it more strongly, which is I think there is a narrative in India and I can see I can sympathize with where it's coming from, which actually thinks that India was hurt a lot by actually trying to occupy these moral high grounds. Some of that critique is fair. Obviously, when you just speak and don't act, it opens you up for the right kind of criticism. Sometimes we did it in a way that was completely formulaic. It was not actually attentive to varying historical contexts. So, you know, you just trot out a standard communique on everything.

But I think what that narrative misses is how much of credibility that also gave us. I mean, there is that other side of the ledger. And that, in fact, many of the big breakthroughs we managed to produce, the U.S. nuclear deal, independently of what we think of our strategic relationship with the United States, was in part because you actually had that capital. Whatever else, the charge was never leveled against India that India is actually going to be a threat to world order. I mean, it's an extraordinary compliment to have, actually. You know, in the long run, it's a form of capital. But now there's, I think, deep skepticism about that idea in some ways. What did we get out of that? And, you know, and it's true, you know, sometimes... bullies and bad boys get more attentions than good boys in class. But, you know, ultimately it's the grade at the end of the year that's going to count. Yeah. Right.

But the core of it remains that, you know, we have to go. I think this is a moment to go back to the basics and say, what are the first principles that will allow us to accumulate these options that do not make us vulnerable in the way in which colonialism did?

So geopolitics and economic reform, again, the imperative has never been greater of their alignment.

I couldn't agree more. Thank you so much for doing this. I've been waiting to have this conversation with you for a long time. And it was wonderful to have you here. Thank you so much.

Always a great privilege to talk to you.

节目结束语

Ideas of India is produced by the Mercatus Center at George Mason University. If you enjoy this podcast, please subscribe on Apple Podcasts, Spotify or your favorite podcast app. Help us grow by giving us a rating and leaving a review. Follow us on Twitter at S. Rajagopalan and at Ideas of India. Also check out our initiative commemorating 30 years of India's market reforms at the 1991project.com.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、"真诚的崩塌"其实是一个密码学问题
Mehta 谈"sincerity collapse"时，把它归因于语境的崩塌——全球化的传播让一句话脱离了"谁在说、对谁说、为何说"的坐标系。这里有一个让人意外的角度：他描述的现象，结构上和**零信任网络安全模型**一模一样。
在一个语境完整的世界里，每条信息都带着"签名"——发言者的职业身份、关系史、场合，构成了可验证的来源凭证（provenance）。当语境崩塌，信息变成了**未签名的数据包**：你无法验证发送者意图，于是系统的理性反应只能是默认敌意。Mehta 那句"knowledge is made increasingly for cutting, not for understanding"，翻译成工程语言就是：当无法做身份认证时，每个节点都进入防御模式。这不是道德滑坡，而是**信任基础设施失效后的理性退化**。他自己也给了最好的类比——印刷术革命也曾让权威与可信度的归属机制整体崩溃，然后用了几代人重建。我们现在缺的不是善意，而是"签名协议"。
## 二、被掏空的"职业身份"与被掏空的"真诚"，是同一件事
对话里有一个

item.title: [邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain] 市場要崩了？川普想降息，但新聯準會主席可能不敢！【邦妮區塊鏈】
item.dc:creator: 邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被忽略的事实：邦妮真正卖的，不是观点，而是"焦虑的可贷款性"

表面上这是一期讲联准会换帅的财经科普。但如果你把整支影片当作一个系统来看，会发现它的结构暴露了一个令人意外的真相：影片的情绪曲线和它的商业模式是严丝合缝咬合的。

前半段拼命堆叠数字——"每分钟 20 万美元利息""加热燃料涨 54.3%""39 兆债务"——制造的是一种无处可逃的窒息感。然后，恰好在观众情绪最紧绷、最想"做点什么"的那一刻，Nexo 借贷广告插了进来。这不是巧合。这支影片真正的产品不是"看懂联准会"，而是把宏观焦虑转译成一笔可以立刻执行的抵押贷款。它和它所批评的对象，其实是同构的。

啊哈时刻：邦妮和华许在做同一件事，只是方向相反

这里有个让人意外的对称。Kevin Warsh 的核心主张是"联准会不该当市场最大的金主，要把流动性收回来"。而这支影片在教观众什么？——用加密资产抵押，借出最多 50% 的流动性，不要卖掉核心资产。

换句话说，当央行试图从系统里抽走流动性时，散户被引导着在个人资产负债表层面自己印钱。华许想缩表，观众被鼓励扩表。整个金融系统"对联准会的钱上瘾"这个病灶，并没有被治好，它只是从联准会的资产负债表下沉到了每个人的钱包里。影片一边诊断系统性成瘾，一边贩卖同款成瘾的零售版本——这是这期内容最深的讽刺，也是最值得你警醒的地方。

跨领域连接：这不是经济学难题，是一道"委托代理 + 公地悲剧"的合体题

把华许的困境从财经语境里抽出来，你会发现它其实是两个经典模型的叠加。

第一层是委托代理问题(principal-agent)：川普是委托人，华许是代理人，但联准会的"独立性"要求代理人背叛委托人的短期利益。影片点破的人性洞察——"总统给了你最重要的位子，你真能完全无视他吗"——正是代理理论里所谓"捕获"(capture)的通俗版。而华许娶了雅诗兰黛家族、当过 Druckenmiller 的顾问，意味着他还同时被第二个委托人——华尔街资本捕获。他不是夹在两股力量之间，他是夹在三股之间。

第二层更隐蔽：联准会面对的"该不该缩表"，本质是一场反向的公地悲剧。在经典公地悲剧里，每个人多放一头牛，草原最终荒芜；而在这里，每个参与者(政府、企业、房贷族、散户)都依赖"别人先承受紧缩的痛"，于是没人愿意先停。流动性不是被过度消耗的草原，而是一种没人敢第一个戒掉的集体麻醉剂。这也解释了为什么 CME 数据显示市场赌"全年不降息甚至升息"——市场早就算准了：华许喊了一年要缩表，但结构性的囚徒困境会让任何一个理性个体都选择不行动。所以真正的预测不是"华许会怎么做"，而是——他大概率什么决定性的事都做不了，而市场的悲观，恰恰是对这种"瘫痪"的精准定价。

📝 转录文本

开场：投资界的新大佬登场

Everyone, the new big boss of the investment world has taken the stage, so why has the market become so pessimistic? And it will stay pessimistic until the end of the year.

I am talking about the new Fed Chair, Kevin Warsh, who faces a super grim and tangled dilemma.

Just how hard is it? Imagine this: US federal debt is 9 trillion dollars. In 2026, interest expenses alone will be about 1.18 trillion dollars, close to the Netherlands' annual GDP. This means the US government has to pay 3.2 billion dollars in interest every day, 130 million dollars every hour, and 200 thousand dollars every single minute.

So by the time this video ends, the US government will have paid tens of millions in interest. Watch another video, and they pay another few tens of millions — and that's just interest.

回顾鲍威尔时代

You and I are familiar with the former Fed Chair Powell. As soon as he said "good morning," the market would skyrocket or crash, right? If Powell just furrowed his brow, the market would think, "oh, it's over." But if he sounded optimistic, the market would react wildly.

Anyway, in the end, many media outlets didn't care what he said. They just analyzed, "hey, did Powell smile today? Did he pause? Did he emphasize any keywords?" Quote, right? The US stock market, housing market, cryptocurrency, and bond market — your portfolio and mine have almost entirely fluctuated based on Powell's remarks.

His term as Fed chair officially ended on May 15, 2026.

川普与鲍威尔的冲突

Looking back, the relationship between Trump and Powell was really not good. Trump once even went in front of the media and was very fierce, right?

The main conflict was that Trump always wanted the Fed to cut interest rates so more money could flow into the market to stimulate the economy, especially with the US midterm elections coming up in November, which were very important to Trump. But Powell was never willing to do so because he worried that inflation would continue to worsen.

In the end, even though Trump publicly threatened to replace Powell, right? He didn't succeed, and Powell successfully completed his term.

Now, Trump has handpicked Kevin Warsh as the new Fed Chair, but the economic situation is in a state of high tension. The reason is that Kevin Warsh now faces both Trump's expectations and the Fed's mission, and these two things currently appear to be in conflict.

So this episode is going to be fantastic — we are going to analyze exactly what the market will have to face this year.

频道介绍

Hello, I am BNY. Our channel interviews the most brilliant figures in the global fintech world, and we continuously provide the latest market research and news.

Our content is released in various formats across all platforms, including Instagram, Facebook, Threads, Spotify, Apple Podcasts, Bilibili, X, and more. You can also ask questions via our official LineEd and Telegram group links, all of which are in the description box. Feel free to choose whichever way is most comfortable for you.

新主席尚未就职

As just mentioned, Powell's term officially ended on May 15th, but the new appointee, Kevin Warsh, will not be sworn in immediately.

This is because the Fed chair is one of the most powerful financial positions in the world. A single word from them can cause global assets to be repriced, right? Therefore, before officially taking office, Kevin Warsh must handle many of his investment positions to avoid future conflicts of interest where he might be setting policy and profiting from it at the same time.

For now, the Fed is letting Powell serve as acting chair. The information currently announced by the White House is that Kevin Warsh will have a swearing-in ceremony at the White House on May 22, personally hosted by Trump.

史上最富有的联准会主席

You might not know that Kevin Warsh himself is extremely wealthy. He could be considered the richest Fed chair in history. According to the latest 2026 documents, his personal net worth is roughly between $130 million and $200 million.

But more importantly, his wife, Jane Lauder, is a third-generation member of the Estée Lauder family. Based on the 2026 Forbes Billionaires list, Jane Lauder's personal net worth is around $2 billion, and the entire family's wealth is even greater, with estimated total family assets of about $25.9 billion.

Kevin Warsh himself is a typical Wall Street elite, having worked at Morgan Stanley and later serving for over 15 years as a senior advisor at the family office of legendary investor Stanley Druckenmiller. He currently holds significant private funds and investment positions.

Because of this, many people are naturally starting to question: Look, this new Fed chair has so many ties to Wall Street, capital markets, and super wealthy individuals. Now that he is set to control the most important central bank in the world, can the Fed truly maintain its independence? Or will there be too much pressure behind the scenes that he needs to appease?

This background information is essential to know, because of the discussion that follows.

2026 期中选举的关键

The US 2026 midterm elections will be held on November 3. This election is critical because it will likely determine whether Trump can continue to push through his various policies during the latter half of his second term.

Although the Republican Party currently controls both the White House and Congress, their advantage isn't particularly large, especially in the House of Representatives where they hold only a narrow lead, meaning just a few swing district shifts could cause them to lose control, and the situation in recent months may be turning against them.

Looking back at history, the party in power in the US almost always loses seats during midterms, as voters tend to use these elections to express their dissatisfaction with the ruling party.

Furthermore, a major reason for Trump's re-election was that voters believed he could improve the economy. And everyone wants the economy to thrive, right?

为什么川普执意要降息

However, inflation began to heat up again in 2026. The war in Iran drove energy prices higher, and the year-on-year CPI growth in April returned to 3.8%.

This is why Trump's persistent push for the Fed to cut rates leaves many wondering: If rate cuts fuel inflation, why does he still want them?

This doesn't mean he thinks inflation is unimportant, but based on his past public statements, he is likely more concerned about the pressure high interest rates place on economic growth, the market, and employment. In short, he wants to boost growth.

During his first term, Trump repeatedly criticized the Fed for raising rates because he believed high rates would increase borrowing costs for companies, causing both the housing and stock markets to stabilize.

高利率环境下的连锁压力

The current situation is even more sensitive. In 2026, the 30-year US mortgage rate is nearing 7%, credit card interest rates average over 20%, and the national debt has already exceeded $39 trillion.

In such an environment, if interest rates remain high for a long time, the pressure on the public to borrow money will increase, and the US government will also have to pay more in interest, right?

The entire financial system relies on lending and liquidity. The US government is borrowing, corporations are borrowing, and real estate is borrowing. Many high net worth individuals don't want to easily sell off their core assets, especially in such a volatile market. Many care more about whether they can secure liquidity first rather than selling their assets outright.

赞助商：Nexo

For instance, in the cryptocurrency market, many people choose lending platforms like Nexo to use their crypto as collateral, allowing them to borrow up to 50% of their asset value.

By joining through the link in the description below and opening the app, you'll see the credit center on the dashboard. Click in, and you can find various collateral options. The higher your membership tier, the better your interest rates.

I must remind everyone of the risks. If you don't calculate your margin carefully, encounter a sharp drop in asset prices, or face platform-related risks, you could still face forced liquidation.

Nexo is a digital asset management platform established in 2018 and is also an official partner of the Audi F1 team. Beyond lending, Nexo also allows you to earn yields. Simply click on the savings center on the app's dashboard and follow the steps to stake your assets and start earning returns.

Our channel's current promotion is that by using the exclusive link and depositing $5,000 within seven days of joining, you get 30 days of platinum membership, offering up to 15% APY. There are other perks too. Check the description for details.

政府的两难处境

Looking back at the US government, they're in a very awkward position. The Fed worries that cutting rates too soon will worsen inflation, but Trump is more worried that if high rates persist, it will crush economic growth and the job market. And then how can you win the election?

The task Kevin Warsh faces now is basically Mission: Impossible 9 — inches for Tom Cruise. He has to fight inflation while also delivering results to please his boss, Trump.

Alright, so everyone's question is: what are you going to do next, Kevin Warsh? Will you hike rates? Will you cut rates? Will you print money?

Many say, "no way, the Fed is independent, Trump can't do anything." But after all, you, Kevin, were handpicked by Trump. Think about human nature. If the president gave you a huge opportunity to sit in the most important seat in finance, would you completely disregard his thoughts? I don't think that's possible.

But the contradiction is that with inflation rising rapidly due to international conflicts, can we really cut rates right now?

检视通膨数据

Let's examine the inflation figures. No — I wonder if you remember during the pandemic, the Fed kept emphasizing its inflation target.

By February 2026, the US annual inflation rate was only 2.4%. It rose to 3.3% in March and to 3.8% in April. This marks the largest increase in the last three years, and what's even more troublesome, many costs of living are surging at the same time.

Let's look at the US Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) CPI data for April 2026. These are all year-over-year growth rates, meaning compared to the same period last year:

  • The overall US inflation rate has returned to 3.8%.
  • Gasoline prices are up 28.4% year-over-year.
  • Heating fuel is up by a staggering 54.3%.
  • Electricity bills have risen 6.1%.
  • Rents are up 3.3%.

So, rationally speaking, how could they cut rates at a time like this?

CME 数据：市场押注全年不降息

As for the Fed, they are truly stuck in a very awkward position regarding interest rate hikes and cuts. Let's take a look at the CME data. The market currently feels that the Fed won't be cutting rates at all — not just recently, but for the entire year. Boom!

Currently, the CME data shows that for the June Fed meeting, the market sees a 96.7% probability that interest rates will remain unchanged, with only a 3.3% chance of a cut.

Looking at July, the market thinks there is an 84.7% chance rates remain unchanged, about a 12.4% chance of a hike, and only a 2.9% chance of a cut.

Even looking toward the end of the year, the probability of a hike is over 50%, maintaining current rates is 40%, and the chance of a cut is currently only 1.8%.

Of course, these numbers are fluid.

华许的核心主张：联准会不该当市场最大金主

But you can see that right now, Kevin Warsh has been saying the same thing for the past year: The Fed cannot keep printing money to bail out the market.

He believes the Fed has pumped too much money into the market over the last few years. Before the pandemic, the Fed's balance sheet was about $4 trillion, but it surged to $9 trillion within a few years after. It means that in just a short time, the Fed injected trillions in liquidity by constantly buying US treasuries and mortgage-backed securities, creating massive amounts of dollars out of thin air to flood the market.

Looking at Warsh's public statements over the past year, he has consistently criticized the Fed for printing and buying too much. He argues that the Fed should gradually shrink its balance sheet and reduce its interference in the market. Simply put, he believes the Fed shouldn't be the market's biggest financier.

缩表的现实困境

What Warsh wants to do now is the opposite: gradually pull that capital back. What are the methods? For example, buying fewer bonds, reducing market liquidity, or having the market absorb more debt on its own.

But the problem is, the entire system may already be too dependent on the Fed's money. US national debt has now exceeded $39 trillion, and the 30-year US Treasury yield recently climbed above 5%.

If the Fed really starts pulling liquidity out of the market, US interest rates could continue to rise. This means mortgage rates, student loans, credit card debt, and corporate borrowing costs will all surge, causing US government interest expenses to skyrocket as well.

So what everyone is wondering is: okay, theoretically it makes sense for the Fed to want to stop printing money, but in reality, has the entire financial system become trapped in this inevitable fate? There's no way to stop. If they tighten, things might collapse — and who's going to deal with that, right?

留言互动

So, Kevin Warsh, the new Fed chair, is currently stuck on a super fragile and sensitive seesaw. I'm asking you: if you were this new Fed chair, what would you do? Let me know in the comments.

  • If you think "I would maintain interest rate hikes and balance sheet reduction to control inflation," type 1 in the comments.
  • If you think you would choose to cut rates and inject some liquidity to let asset values keep rising, even at the risk of worsening inflation, and you're okay with that, type 2 in the comments below.

结尾：频道资源与防诈咨询

Hey, you know what? You should make the most of our official line resources in the info section, as we have experts providing one-on-one answers to crypto-related questions, plus an exclusive 7-day mentorship course for beginners to teach you all the basics of the crypto space. Click "7-Day Beginner Mentorship Course" on our official line and fill out the application form. Registration is open now.

Additionally, we also collaborate on anti-scam consulting. If you or someone you know might have encountered a scam, we have specialists here to help you assess the situation and assist you in reporting it to the police.

Of course, there are also many perks to claim in the group. Detailed information is in the info section. Use it well.

Let's keep working hard in the crypto space. See you next time. Bye bye.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Mercatus Center] Pratap Bhanu Mehta on Liberalism, Nihilism, and the Collapse of Sincerity
item.dc:creator: Mercatus Center
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一切危机，其实都是「膜」的危机

Mehta 这场对话表面上跳跃于尼希主义、真诚的崩塌、专业身份、民族主义、种姓、机构建设、不结盟外交之间——看似七八个不相干的议题。但有一个他自己都没有点破的系统性真相：这整场对话从头到尾只在谈一件事——半透膜的失效。

注意一个被轻轻放过的词。Ambedkar 借用杜威讲社会融合时用的是 "endosmosis"（内渗）——这本是物理化学术语，指流体穿过半透膜的过程。这不是修辞巧合，而是整个诊断的隐藏骨架。自由主义运转所依赖的，从来不是「连接」也不是「隔离」，而是一系列半透膜：公共与私人之间的膜（崩了，于是私下一句话能被截图钉死你一生）；专业共同体把国家与资本挡在门外的膜（崩了，于是法官、资深律师、医生不再有独立的合法性来源）；大学作为「政治不连续性的防波堤」的膜；市场作为人们「外出混合、再各自退回网络角落」的第三空间的膜。Walzer 说自由主义是「分离的艺术」——用这个框架重读，分离的艺术其实就是膜的工程学：膜不能是墙（那是种姓与隔都），也不能消失（那是原子化与尼希主义），它必须是半透的。Mehta 谈的所有崩坏，本质上是同一种病理：所有的膜在同一时刻一起变得过度通透。

真正令人意外的，是「安全」才是道德怯懦的温床

大多数人会本能地假设：道德溃败来自不安全感——是焦虑的、被globalization抛下的失败者在「烧掉房子」。但 Mehta 引 Montesquieu 投下一颗逆向炸弹：「安全往往滋生胆怯、缺乏信念与勇气。」 在两个民主国家里，道德弃守表现得最充分的，恰恰是最安全的阶层。这与他另一处观察精确咬合——「孤独领袖崇拜最大的拥护者其实是精英」。把这两点连起来，一个反直觉的系统结论浮现：尼希主义不是从底层的绝望里长出来的，而是从顶层的舒适里长出来的。烧房子的人并不住在棚屋里，他们住在装了保险的豪宅里——正因为装了保险，烧起来才毫无心理负担。这彻底颠倒了通行的「民粹来自被剥夺感」叙事。

专业共同体：社会的免疫系统，而非中产阶级的装饰

Mehta 谈 Durkheim 设想的「专业作为现代性核心道德」时，给出的其实是一个被经济学和政治学共同遗忘的中间层。我想推进一步做一个跨领域连接：专业共同体是社会的分布式免疫系统。 它的合法性既不来自资本（资本生资本），也不来自国家（强制力与大众动员），而来自知识本身——这使它成为对抗两种「单一逻辑」感染的抗体。当律师、法官、医生、学者的专业身份被掏空（医生变成股东、资深律师不再接「烫手」的案子），社会就失去了分布式免疫应答。而免疫系统一旦瘫痪，机体唯一的求生方式，就是退回到单一的、集中的中央权威——这正是 vishwas（垂直信仰）的来处。于是一条惊人的因果链显形了：专业身份的空心化，与对强人的盲目信仰，是同一枚硬币。 横向信任的消亡不是被纵向信仰「取代」的——是免疫系统失能后，机体被迫接管的应急代偿。

一个隐藏的对称：AI 家教与「真诚的崩塌」是同一件事

对话开头谈「真诚的崩塌」（语境塌缩、公私界限消失），结尾谈「你和你的 AI 家教单独对话」的原子化——看似首尾两个无关话题。但它们其实是同一个现象的两端。那位普林斯顿本科生说她喜欢 AI 的第三个理由是「不被评判」——这恰恰是 Mehta 哀悼的、已经消失的「私人领域」的功能：一个可以说错话、可以自我修正、不会被钉死的缓冲空间。讽刺得近乎残酷：AI 家教之所以诱人，正是因为它人工重建了那道被社交媒体摧毁的公私之膜。 我们用一项加速原子化的技术，去补偿另一项技术造成的原子化创伤。这也解释了为何 Mehta 对大学的辩护最终落在「socialization（社会化）」而非「知识传递」上——大学真正不可替代的，不是讲座内容，而是它作为最后一道「结构化可错空间」的膜功能。当连小学一年级起就只剩「你和你的 YouTube 频道」时，社会失去的不是教育，是最后一处允许真诚存在的物理场所。

📝 转录文本

Pratap Bhanu Mehta on Liberalism, Nihilism, and the Collapse of Sincerity

Mercatus Center — A conversation between Shruti Rajagopalan and Pratap Bhanu Mehta

Opening: Reading the Arc Across the Decades

RAJAGOPALAN: Pratap, welcome back to the podcast. It's a pleasure to have you here in the studio, in person, as opposed to doing this online during COVID times. I'm thrilled that you're here.

MEHTA: It's such a privilege to be here and to think together actually, [chuckles] in a sense, more than just talk.

RAJAGOPALAN: I will think with a little bit of lag compared to you, I have a feeling. I've been reading a lot of your older columns again recently, in general, both to prepare for this conversation and also to make sense of the world, which is in so much flux right now.

Across all your writing, even if I go as far back as the financial crisis, when you talk about how we should think about repairing globalization, especially to make it a little bit more of a level playing field across different stakeholders in the world. Whether it's arguments about the war in Ukraine and Gaza, whether it's what's happening now with the entire WTO liberal order being upended, the sense I got from the arc of your columns is that you've moved away from very specific critiques of globalization, and the failures of the old liberal order.

Your deeper worry is the broader norms like the erosion of restraint, the erosion of legitimacy, of leadership, of a kind of moral authority in the international order. Is this the correct way to read you across the decades and the columns? Perhaps, you can help us make sense of where are we right now? What is this new world we're entering, and what is the frame through which we can enter it?

Nihilism and Disinhibition

MEHTA: First of all, thank you for reading things so closely. I'm not sure they merit as much close attention, but I think you have characterized the arc correctly. One way of describing that arc, which might be an entry point into the current moment, is, as you know, 15, 20 years ago, we used to discuss things like institutions, incentives, questions of distributive justice—questions of what's the basic architecture of a global economic order.

I think all of those questions and the way we framed that did not reckon with one historical possibility, which I think we are having to face up to now, and liberalism has probably had to face up [to] in its past as well. What do you do if there are, in society, forces that have a degree of ruthlessness and a willingness to, as it were, burn the house down rather than merely disagree or let the other side win?

I was going back to an old essay by Leo Strauss on German nihilism. His characterization of nihilism—and you can add it to it—nihilism for him was a condition where a political party, a political dispensation is willing to call for radical change without the foggiest idea of what that new change is. But more importantly, in the process willing to jeopardize the gains and potentialities of the current order.

If you add on to that—I think the word you used or something similar—which is this complete lack of disinhibition in doing it, right? Disinhibition in the sense that a certain valorization of violence and a propensity to engage in it both internationally and domestically. I think the construction of your opponents as existential enemies. Once you have done that, then of course everything becomes permissible.

I think [there's] deep skepticism that our institutions, as they are currently configured, can actually do the democratic job of producing regimes of truth that are widely accepted. This is not just a critique from the right. I think there are sections of the left. I think liberals have lost [the] courage of their own convictions in some ways, right? You have that deep skepticism about truth itself. Skepticism about authority would have been an absolutely wonderful thing.

I think this amalgam of dispositions is an existential condition which I don't think liberals know quite how to respond to. It's not the kind of condition that will be easily addressed just by recourse to the very comfortable, bland things we used to talk about. Tinkering of institutional design incentives. Those things do matter. I think for a world that we reconstruct after this, those things will continue to matter.

But at this moment, diagnosing why we are in this condition—historians used to say that if you want to understand the prelude to World War I, don't read the social scientist. Read Robert Musil, [chuckles] The Man Without Qualities, that depiction of nihilism. Cultures have been through this. I think that's our bigger challenge. To that extent, it's not that I've given up on those convictions or actually changed my mind about those institutions. But the battle on which we'll have to fight is of a very qualitatively different nature.

Good People, Bad People, and the Erosion of Moral Authority

RAJAGOPALAN: When I was writing my dissertation, my dissertation adviser, Peter Boettke, used to say that as economists, we never rely on saying bad people do bad things, and good people do good things. "When bad people do bad things, bad things happen. When good people do good things, good outcomes happen."

Yet, that's what the nihilistic framework seems to suggest. Either accidentally or through some emergent process, we have landed on a particular set of actors who either lack moral authority or leadership or just a long-term view of how to build the world.

I think the counterweight to that seems to me to also be that liberals and elites have also lost that moral authority. In earlier cycles of this, when terrible people came and tried to do terrible things, there was another group that was ready to absorb the burden of that. Because of their moral authority or leadership, they were willing to be heard, or people were willing to hear them. Now that seems to be lost. What explains this erosion on all sides?

MEHTA: It's wonderful you're going to begin with Peter Boettke. As you know, my dissertation was on Adam Smith. One of the great virtues of the Scottish Enlightenment was that it never fell into the simplistic trap that good people do good things and bad people do bad things.

In fact, the dynamics of history are always more complicated. Sometimes bad people actually end up producing policies that are beneficial in the long run. History is very paradoxical. They all believed in progress, but [in] a very skeptical way, right?

I think that's absolutely right. I think that sensibility is very important to understanding politics, actually. Politics is not just about drawing these lines between good people and bad people. Politics has to firmly keep its eye on consequences, I think, again, as any serious student of politics from Max Weber onward has told you.

I agree. I don't think the issue is who are the good people and who are the bad people. You can certainly map on weaknesses, mistakes, terrible moral judgments on a wide range of the political spectrum. It's very hard to argue at this point that there are political dispensations that are immune from that kind of moral abdication.

I think the question is even deeper and more troubling: Do we even have a sense of what the standards of right and wrong are, independently of who actually lives up to them and, in a sense, who betrays them? That's, I think, the nihilism I was hinting at. There is such a radical instrumentalization of almost every value.

The challenge of dealing with good people and bad people is easier if you can make the argument that, look, if you want to critique something, by all means critique it. But critique it in good faith and then live up to the principles on the basis of which you are critiquing. Don't use critique merely as a pretext to beat down on somebody else.

I think the disinhibition we are experiencing at the moment—take the issue of war, for example. It's very hard to argue that there is only a particular political dispensation, whether it's the right, the centrists. In some cases, the left's abdication has been as extraordinary. How can we in democracies not ask tough questions about our own moral complicity?

Sometimes people accuse others of acting out of self-interest. Yet, the most secure classes in both the democracies we care about exemplify traits that Montesquieu described as, "Security often breeds timidity, a lack of conviction and courage." I think that landscape is actually all over the place. My worry is that our sense of where the red lines are is eroding. That sense is much harder to reconstruct once that breaks down.

The Civilizing Process: Micro Progress, Macro Disorder

RAJAGOPALAN: Here, I have difficulty between matching what's happening in the micro sense and the macro sense. I feel like if I just look at the data on how much violence we commit against each other as individuals, against women, how we treat children, how we treat animals. Do we care about the simplest of things, like conservation of extinct species? Or the big rise in vegetarianism and against this industrial complex that's cruel.

On all of those margins, we have made progress. People are actually kinder to each other in a very literal sense of not being violent. They are kinder to each other within the household. Those norms have all changed, and they've largely stabilized. But that doesn't emerge into a macro order of where we know what is right and wrong at that level. We seem to know what's right and wrong at the lower level, and in fact, we're sticking with it quite well. What causes this?

MEHTA: That's a wonderful observation, but if you take the long arc of history, there's nothing anomalous about this, even if you look at debates in late 19th century. There's one kind of long-term story about what Norbert Elias would have called this civilizing process, where lots of different forms of violence are tamed and actually no longer found acceptable. We don't do duels anymore [chuckles], as if we are settling disputes, right? We don't do an eye for an eye.

There's a whole range of things. In many ways, a lot of the phenomenon that you describe, a certain kind of revulsion against certain forms of violence. I certainly think, for example, the discourse on animal rights, the turn toward vegetarianism. If you place it in that long arc of societies trying to curtail and eclipse violence, these are of a piece. I think the norm toward a certain kind of moral egalitarianism—you're also right—is a long-term trend that is going to be hard to reverse.

Nationalism and Feminism: The Two Triumphant Ideologies

In some senses, I think, if you look at the 20th century, there are only two ideologies that have unequivocally triumphed: One is nationalism and the other is feminism. Feminism is the frame of reference to which every other ideology has to adapt. Yes, sometimes there are pockets of resistance. It's really an extraordinary achievement if you actually think about it. Part of the answer is actually the tension between these two ideologies.

If you think of what produces or licenses a certain kind of moral disinhibition, the claim that nationalism is the one ideology that allows you to do that much more easily is a very powerful win. It's true that nationalism and the modern nation-state form have enabled liberal democracy, at least as we understand it. It created the idea of the people, popular sovereignty.

Yet, it is also the one ideology that is the biggest competitor of religion. It's the only modern ideology that can consecrate death. It's an absolutely astonishing psychological fact about nationalism. It is the one ideology that can colonize our sense of meaning. Even our sense of economic meaning comes in national categories. GDP growth rate for my country, [chuckles] as it were. It is so pervasive in some ways in its ability to actually create meaning, and through its ability to create that meaning, license instrumentality in its cause. Once you say, "This is for the nation," it's extraordinary how many barriers you can break.

If you look at the current moment, I think one of the things that is actually very striking is almost every form of authoritarianism is having to rely on nationalism to give it that cloak and cover. Not just to create an electoral majority, not just in purely instrumental terms, but also, in moral terms to say that, "Look, this is the highest god we'd worship. Once you worship this god, you are allowed to take all of these necessary—what we think are necessary—steps."

I think that tension—If you use them as two archetypes, feminism is representing progress, equality, a certain softening of public mores, dismantling of patriarchy, that modern impulse toward liberation. And nationalism has that modern ability to mobilize collective power, in all its most murderous forms. That's the tension [chuckles] I think we are experiencing.

Globalization, Community, and Capitalism

RAJAGOPALAN: If I had to connect this to how some of the other scholars have made sense of it, to me, it seems like—and I'm drawing the very simple version of the story here—we had globalization with its extraordinary success, but one of the outcomes of globalization was people were less connected to their local communities. They were less connected to their local identities. Local identities, as Ambedkar pointed out, can sometimes be very problematic because of those hierarchies and sometimes be wonderful to give you a sense of reciprocity and really who you are in the world.

If I look at, say, what Raghuram Rajan had to say about what happens to community when we are thinking about the broader global economic order, that erosion has led to people floundering for new identities.

Nationalism seems to be quite convenient. It's still rooted in localism because if you belong to a particular country, then of course that gets subsumed in it. At the same time, you get to keep all the other good things. You don't have to do this around religious lines or gender lines or caste lines or ethnic lines or state lines. You still get to pretend to be modern and liberal, while having a single point of identification, which can then unleash these consequences.

Is that just too simple a frame, or is that a good way of thinking about what's going on? Because nationalism has not always had this kind of power.

MEHTA: I am a little skeptical of the story. I actually agree with the underlying premise, that there is an experience of a social lack, in terms of community, in terms of actual relationships. In fact, that is actually being radicalized. All kinds of evidence, not just Robert Putnam's bowling alone stuff, but if you think of what's happening to family structures. There is a sense in which the sociological atomism of society is quite deep and persuasive.

I think the question is whether that has something to do with globalization or, to use that old word, capitalism. Because remember, this is a debate that begins in the late 18th century. In fact, somebody like Edmund Burke—I think it's the last moment where conservatives have this illusion that you can actually hold on to stable, fixed institutions in the social realm and yet give markets free rein. That's been the biggest tension and challenge for conservative thought.

The reason, in part, I'm skeptical about the globalization story is the best literature on this crisis of relationships, of crisis of communities, is actually not the literature that's being produced now. It was the literature that was produced in the 1970s. I think Alasdair MacIntyre, Christopher Lasch were probably the most prescient and deepest diagnosticians of this phenomenon. It's the '70s, which was, given that '50s and '60s was the high tide of what we now think of as, at least in the Western world, the nostalgia for the '50s and '60s as forms of industrial production, forms of national identity and community.

The deeper question might be, is it to do with globalization, or is it to do with the fact that capitalism requires both commodification but also such extraordinary mobility that it is just very difficult? I'm sure at personal level, at least almost everybody in the younger generation, we know just this whole idea of what it might even mean to create strong relationships in a world where everybody's constantly mobile. [chuckles]

I actually think its roots are deeper. The turn to nationalism at the beginning of the 20th century, again, was, in part, a response to that. This idea that Kultur, in the German sense—some idea of culture could be an antidote to atomization. Actually, Matthew Arnold is already talking about, in the late 19th century, where he thinks of culture as an antidote, both against mass democracy and what he thinks is the leveling down. It's also, again, a kind of revolt against atomization.

I think this inherent tension between the scale effects, the productivity effects—which are genuinely enchanting. The reason this is a difficult problem is, again, it's not as simple as this is good and that is bad—

RAJAGOPALAN: The benefits are so extraordinary, yes.

MEHTA: —It's how do we find win-win solutions that actually mitigate some of the tensions between these?

The Collapse of Sincerity

RAJAGOPALAN: Yes. Here, I just want to zoom back a little bit. I'm going to be a little clumsy in how I phrase this because I want to reference a number of different works of yours. To look at not just the economic component of what you talked about, but also the cultural component.

If I go back and look at your 2003 piece, "Passion and Constraints," you look at Rawlsian public reason against what's happening in India, Indian electoral law. You find the Rawlsian framework useful but also insufficient.

Then, if I look at your 2012 column, "Public Reason, Indian Style," and I've gone back to revisit that column multiple times. Actually, that column, I recommend all the listeners go back and read, because it's very prescient. You talk about what's going on with media and media capture and the problems of social media and the 140-character limit and what that does to us. Everything you warned us about in that column has, in some sense, come to pass, right?

In your more recent columns, you've argued that sincerity is not just an inner mental state, but it's also about social legibility, or legibility of at least how we interact socially. Or social practice. You mentioned MacIntyre. You also wrote a recent column on MacIntyre where you argue that we've lost a shared moral vocabulary.

Now, I don't know if I'm jumbling too many things together, but to me, this again seems like a very clear arc on how you've progressed. To me, it seems like you've gone from a Rawlsian framework where we look at institutions and test them and where they're sufficient, and then move closer and closer, in some sense, toward Smith, where we're now talking about "The Collapse of Sincerity," a shared moral language.

Without sincerity and a shared moral vocabulary, how can we even think of what is right or wrong, or who has moral authority or who doesn't have moral authority? Is this a good way to both think of your own thinking, or I'm jumbling too many things together? [laughter]

MEHTA: I just think you're being very, very generous in characterizing what was in those columns. I think here's how I'd put it. I have to say, I've grown even fonder of Rawls actually. Despite what I'm writing, I think I discover even more depth in him now than I probably did 10, 15 years ago. I always loved both Rawls and Smith and thought you needed to engage with both.

I think what has changed or one way of telling the story is thinking about the sociological preconditions. I think one of the columns that you referred to, "The Collapse of Sincerity," if one just takes that as a reference point—the puzzle behind that column was actually a very simple one. I'm sure any of you have engaged in public writing, public discourse, which everybody does these days. That's what social media has done. It has empowered everybody to be a public figure in some ways.

One of those very simple experiences you have is you will almost always be misinterpreted. Now, you can make a philosophical argument about, "All acts of interpretations are misinterpretations." But, you know what I mean, in a much more colloquial sense.

RAJAGOPALAN: Yes.

MEHTA: There used to be all of these arguments in the philosophical language going back to Paul Grice and so forth, which is, what are the conditions under which we even actually understand each other as engaging in acts of communication and meaning? One of the conditions used to be you actually attribute sincerity to somebody.

Now, one of the things globalization has done with communication, in particular, is that it has completely collapsed all contexts. Without any context, the basic requirements of what it means to do intelligible communication, how is this word actually being used? What is its function? Who the target is? It's much harder to make these kinds of judgments. I often say that in, at least in a sociological sense Derrida has won out, that all signs have become indeterminate. [laughs]

If you praise somebody, in a very different context, somebody is asking, "Oh, but why hasn't this column praised so-and-so?" You're reading silences. It was actually just an observation of almost a phenomenological kind of experience of why communication across contexts is actually becoming so, so much harder.

Once you find it difficult to attribute sincerity to any act of communication, and you find it difficult to reconstruct the context, which gives that communication intelligibility, then the only function left for communication is either pure expression or, in a sense, fighting words. Knowledge is made increasingly for cutting, not for understanding, right?

RAJAGOPALAN: Yes.

MEHTA: At one level, I think our challenge with this new information order is even a deeper one. I still like to think that it is much more of an institutional and sociological condition. It's maybe the kind of thing that people experienced when the printing revolution came, where structures that allowed you to attribute context, the trustworthiness of authorities and credibility—you went through a phase where all of that collapsed. There will be, hopefully, through time, some reconstitution of norms.

Another example—for liberals, this is absolutely central—is the complete collapse of the distinction between the public and private. What we implicitly ended up doing was taking norms that were appropriate to private conversation and taking them to the public sphere.

By and large, even if it was not OK, one of the advantages of a private sphere was that you could actually say things without those being your public identity. There was an opportunity for self-correction.

You observe that among students in a very trivial sense. People talk a lot about political correctness and censorship. The worry is not that there is actually an authority censoring you or that in classrooms there is a monocultural ideology or the professors won't let you speak. The worry simply is that even a private conversation can be put on Meta or X, "Oh, so-and-so said this," and you are then stamped for life. In fact, your only option then is to double down on that. [laughter]

A lot of these institutional norms that liberalism had supposed about the intelligibility of communication, context, thickening our understanding of each other, the distinction between public and private. The modern information order has made those distinctions much, much more difficult to hold on to.

I'd still like to defend the Rawlsian impulse in some ways, which is the fundamental question of society: How do we reconcile the terms of social cooperation with institutional conditions that acknowledge us as free and equal? I think that still remains the central question. But now, doing this across these extraordinarily disparate contexts all at once, I think, has made our challenge harder.

Beyond Institutional Design: Ideology and the Professions

RAJAGOPALAN: If I look at your earlier work, the way I think about it is you were really thinking about institutions from the point of view of design, capacity. And therefore, what are the reforms—sometimes major reforms, sometimes minor tinkering—that will get us to the appropriate institution design, institutional capacity, and thereby, incentives and outcomes?

This, you did at the level of the constitution for all major central institutions in India, because that was where most of your work was rooted. And also local governments and things like that. Now again, when I read your more recent work, the thread I see is that's not enough. It may be necessary, but it's nowhere close to enough because in all this institutional capacity, tinkering, reform discussion, we lost track of what are the broader ideological forces that both bind us and animate us and mobilize us.

We perhaps need to first understand that ideological sense, or what it means to be a citizen or what it means to be governed by a particular institution before we actually start fixing the institution.

One, again, is this a good way of thinking about how you're approaching these questions? Not to say you've abandoned the institutional agenda, but something else needs to precede that agenda to make it sensible again.

MEHTA: I think you're exactly right. Look, post–1989, even if you didn't subscribe to the literal thesis, I think we all acted—not all—many acted as if we were in a kind of end-of-history world, right? There is a broad consensus on evolving meta-norms of conduct, particularly around the pacification of violence, the delegitimization of large wars, the delegitimization of civil strife, a whole range of political phenomena.

The main issue for democracies was how do you make them amelioratively better over time? You took it for granted that almost all the actors in democracy were operating within certain bounds of conduct. They just did not have the will to ruthlessness that you're seeing [in] many actors now. It was a tremendous time to think about institutions. [chuckles] What are the incentives for judges? What are the rules of parliamentary procedure that might actually make deliberation more effective?

Now, two things happened since. One [is that], as you have characterized, the attack on institutional life came not from where we thought it would come, which is, "Institutions will internally get dysfunctional. Incentives will change." The answer to that will just be better institutional design. The attack came from quarters which basically said, "We actually don't care what the institutional design is. Moreover, if we can't win, we are willing to burn the house down with it."

The second thing that I think happened—and this is just a piece of intellectual biography. When we did the institutions work, and particularly some work with Devesh Kapur, one of the projects we had in mind, which never got going—partly just turned out to be difficult to do—was a project on the creation of professional identities.

Many years ago, when I used to teach modern political thought, what I was very struck by in the late 19th and early 20th centuries—there was extensive discussion of the professions. Not just in terms of service delivery norms, but for people like Durkheim—actually to some extent even Max Weber—professions were going to be the central morality of modernity. Because they would give modern subjects a very specific identity.

If I say I'm a lawyer, I'm an officer of the court. If I'm an officer of the court, how do I understand that phrase and internalize that phrase that disciplines me, commits me to certain moral norms, and commits me to defending certain kinds of institutions, right?

They were also interested in the professions because for them, professions were an interesting class. The professions could be a bulwark, both against the state and the market. Not that professions are not embedded in the market. Obviously, professionals work for a fee and they are involved in market relations. But the basic idea was that [for] the professions, the source of their legitimacy is modern forms of knowledge. With capital, it's capital creating more capital, in some senses. With [the] state, there's coercive power and the power that comes from legitimation en masse, publics, right?

RAJAGOPALAN: Yes.

MEHTA: If you could, through these sociological experiences of the professions, create a large class of middle-class professionals across a range of professions. Who made their institutions their professional identity, and therefore, were willing to protect those institutions, and through protecting them, decentralizing power.

If universities protect their autonomy. If courts of law protect their autonomy. Lawyers historically were at the forefront of thinking of a constitutional culture. You achieve a lot of things simultaneously. You achieve a decentering of power. You actually give people a source of meaning because it has become your moral identity. It's also the nice confluence of something that has become your moral and institutional identity, also allowing you to sustain a living.

I was actually very struck by the fact that maybe the post–1940s, 1950s, if you look at old journals, American Political Science Review and stuff, lots of articles on the professions—and then, that kind of drops out. Or rather, it becomes much more a narrower question of very technical questions about professional ethics.

There's some work in the '70s, I think, a wonderful book by Abbott. It was partly on the psychology profession, but the theoretical frameworks there were quite interesting.

There was this kind of missing middle, as it were, of what actually made institutions work. It came home to us. I know it's something you are concerned with. I can speak of India, but I suspect there are parallels in the United States. One of the most disappointing things, as you know, over the last 10 years has been the abdication of the judiciary.

The disappointment has not just been the abdication of judges and the absolutely scandalous performance of the Indian Supreme Court. It has been the abdication of the senior bar, which we always thought was one of the most powerful groups in Indian society. Who could possibly touch them, that they would forget rising to the cause of liberty? They have reached a point where they're not even willing to do the sort of quotidian things that lawyers used to do, which is accept cases of people who are being targeted. Ram Jethmalani used to very proudly say, "I will defend anyone."

What is it about those professional identities that have become hollowed out? I think the crisis of healthcare. One of the things I've been really struck by—I'm not a health economist and so will be agnostic on what organizational forms healthcare should take. But the number of medical professionals in this country who we increasingly meet, but at one level, we'll say, "Look, there's been a lot of progress in a number of fields." The sense of satisfaction they're deriving from their professions, because of the institutional conditions under which they work, so should doctors be shareholders?

What does that do to professional identity and that hope Durkheim had? That actually the basis of your legitimacy is your knowledge. The basis of your legitimacy is not that you're just another property holder. In fact, signaling that was extremely important. I think, again, that sociological story about what made institutions function.

Tocqueville, when he talks about lawyers in America, he says, "This is going to be the aristocracy American democracy needs."
