Agent 想要什么

Yeah. So moving back to agents. I think the branching that you do, the fast spin up and orchestration — it's kind of like the pre-work that happened to be exactly what agents want. What do agents want differently than humans?

What do agents want differently than humans? I think they want the ability to version things. So it's not actually that different — there's just almost slight deviations in terms of how it materializes, right?

So agents want a way to be able to test changes incrementally, right? Like we have feature flags as engineers, right? Is there any reason why they can't just use feature flags? I don't think so. They want version control. Is there ways we can use git or not git? I think that one is realistically completely up in the air, right? And I do think something ultimately outside git will emerge in terms of how we're going to version a lot of these things over time.

They need observability. You need to be able to query what happened at what point in time, which steps failed, traces, logs, metrics, all of those other things. They need network, compute and storage. They need the ability to write files, save files, iterate on files, snapshots, file system, all of those other things, right?

And so a lot of the stuff that we roughly needed is very very in line with a lot of the stuff that agents also need, right? And so the branching and forking stuff — it's not different. We're just moving a thousand times quicker than we used to. And so some of these things look like you really need something massively massively different, but it's just — you need something massively better than what currently existed, right? You need orchestration — something massively better than Cube, right? You need networking — something probably better than Envoy, right? And it just goes all the way down the stack.

If the workload profile doesn't change so much as it gets massively massively compressed, because you need to do thousands of these things — what assumptions change? Like CD is going to melt, right? You need to replace it with something. And then I think you can go all the way down the stack and basically say "okay, well that part has to change, and that part has to change, and that part has to change."

And the interesting thing about the super exponential curve is that you have to build your systems in such a way where you can rip out those parts at any point in time, because a new bottleneck might emerge — because you start getting really really good at parallel agents, and then that's where the new bottleneck is, and that breaks a different part of your system. So it's very much similar kind of stuff that humans have needed — you just need it at a thousand-x scale.

Agent 时代的代码审查与 CLI

So how do you do code review in the age of the agents, I guess, is more of a question.

More agents who don't —

Yeah. Right. But then who reviews things for CVE and all those other things?

Yeah. Okay — and then that's how we hit the inference wall at some point, right? And you can continually throw agents and agents and agents at that problem, but I think there's a limit to the amount of agents you can throw at a problem.

You started — you already had a CLI before it was cool, I guess. How is the shape of what you're exposing changed, if at all?

CLIs have always been cool, by the way. But yeah —

So I think the CLI changes because the way that we think about this is — how do you give Claude or Codex or Chat or whatever — any of these models — almost like a handhold? And a CLI is a single command when you think about it, right? It's like — okay, you're going to do a deploy, you're going to get logs, whatever, right? Things that were prohibitively annoying to humans are not actually prohibitively annoying to agents. They're really, really nice, right?

And so if I wanted to hand you a CLI and I said, "Hey, guess what? The CLI has 40 arguments and 600 flags." You'd be like, "Wow, that's crazy. I'm never going to use all those things." But if you hand it to an agent and you say, "Hey, that's 40 arguments and 600 flags" — like, oh yeah, this is excellent. I have so many handles that I can go and work on with this, right?

And so if you're going to expose things for agents over that mechanism, you want to basically have as many handles as possible where they can get information, query additional dynamic information, and then see how it can close that loop as quickly as possible. Most of the problems right now are actually just "how do you close the loop as quickly as possible?" Where does the agent get stuck and how can you remove that?

That's why incidentally telemetry is very very important — because if you can tell where the agent gets stuck from the CLI and you say "hey listen, 12% of people are getting deviated from the happy path because of this thing," and now I go and add this arg and that drives it down to 2% — you've massively increased the rate of the loop closing for a lot of people, right?

So that's the way that we think about not just the CLI but every point in the dashboard. It is a user journey from "I hear about railway, I go and get something deployed, I get my first green build / aha moment, I see an endpoint, I see some logs," and then I go in and iterate. And that iterate loop is indefinite and infinite until the end of time, right? User wants to deploy new thing, user wants to deploy new Postgres instance, user wants to change their code, user wants to iterate all over time.

And so if you just focus on a lot of those iteration loops and figuring out what's blocking that loop from closing as quickly as possible — one of the things we talk about internally is: you never ever ever want to be waiting on compute anymore. You always want to be waiting on intelligence, right? And if you're waiting on compute, there's a bottleneck that needs to be destroyed there.

And I think incidentally — we've built a really really awesome product where you can push code and then you build the code and all those other things — but that push-pull loop, I just fundamentally believe it's going to go away, right? We're going to get to a point where you make a small change in production, that change is versioned across your entire infrastructure, you're working alongside copy-on-write versions of your database, all of your infrastructure, and then you merge it in and instantaneously it's live. Because that's the holy grail of loops. That push-pull-rebuild thing is a point of friction that we are removing entirely from our loops.

Canvas 从"输入"变为"输出"

Yeah, it's incredibly fast. So if anyone hasn't tried it — that fast feedback is great. You know, my hot take is that Railway was kind of famous for its canvas, which sort of visualizes your infrastructure — you manipulate it visually — but that was for humans. And actually now for the next phase in growth, really the CLI is more important than the canvas, which is what you were famous for.

Yeah. So I think the canvas is funny because it's actually just a mechanism to show you changes over time. But I think you're totally right in the sense that we have previously used it a lot as an input, and its goal moving forward is actually a lot more like an output.

What I mean by that is — you would go to the canvas and you make some changes, and you'd see them, and your agents or your infrastructure would evolve over time, right? Now you just have a bunch of agents that have access to the CLI and they can go and make those changes. And so the canvas, instead of becoming this input thing where you're like "oh cool, how do I go in and make this happen?" — it's actually just more of an output thing. It basically says: what information does the human need at this point in time to make suitable decisions about control requests of "do I approve this, do I not approve this?"

And also — and I think this is important, and I think this is lost on a lot of people who are building some of these canvas experiences — it has to be almost like an anchor for your context. It has to be like a port in the storm. You have to think about it as layers and like a file system almost, to get to the next spot, right?

And so you have all your infrastructure — and this is why the canvas starts as just a project — and then you have a drill-down chart, like "I'm breaking down into these services or this section that is like a function or code." Because you want to actually be able to represent the entire thing not just in your head but in this canvas, so that other people can also get that representation, so that they can think on the same wavelength as you, so that they can move as quickly.

I think a lot of orgs, especially as they scale, get in trouble because all that context lives in somebody's head basically. It's like "oh, how does this microservice work?" — "I have no idea, go ask this specific person." And then you have entire categories and classes of products that are built around "how do you do context discovery?"

And I think a lot of that stuff gets melted — if you can have a really really solid hierarchy and you can infinitely nest services, infinitely nest code, infinitely nest context, infinitely nest all these things all the way down. That's what allows you to build these structures up over time.

And I think it's also what's going to allow us to build — I've written about this — these hyperstructures, things that are way way bigger. You look at the Golden Gate Bridge and you're like "how did we build that?" There's that whole meme of "oh, how do we build this — we lost the technology, we don't know how anymore." It's like — well, yeah, to some extent yes, because a lot of the coordination that built those things has evolved and changed, and there are new things — we've lost almost the art of building that structure as we've just jammed everything into Slack, right? Everything happens through Slack.

But you don't have anything in Discord. So —

Yeah, that's the same point. It doesn't really matter. It's just like message passing and interrupts. Message passing and interrupts. Message passing and interrupts, right?

So you're arguing that there should be something better, more structured than Slack.

Yeah. Oh, for sure. I think Slack — and incidentally, I think Discord's awful too.

Central Station

This is the equivalent of my mom test, right? Like what have you done that has your solution to this?

So internally we've built a tool called Central Station that allows us to go in and aggregate all the context from all of our users. Um, so every piece of feedback, every piece of customer support, every single thing like that gets aggregated into what we call clusters. If you have an incident brewing, now we can determine how many users are affected, all of those other things. And then we can actually break off a discussion based on that.

And I think a lot of that is actually a lot more helpful and more correct — instead of having just these long-running channels where you're just like "which channel should I put this thing in?" If you can dynamically aggregate that information and dynamically route it to the right person based on the context — we know internally these four people are pretty close on networking, right? And so if we see "okay, we've got a networking thing," you can roughly drill it down to those four people. And if you're saying "oh okay, it's actually with this part," you can just go and look at the commits.

And this is no longer a manual process internally. This is the whole point of why we built — if you go to station or help.railway.com — the whole reason we built this thing is because we wanted to figure out how we're going to scale with a massive massive amount of leverage to aggregate all this feedback.

This is built in-house?

Yeah.

Okay. So — and then I remember helping out on this one with Angelo in 2023. Yeah, you scale a lot with a very small team.

Yeah. So we're like 10 times bigger now.

Oh my god. You have your full developer account here?

Yeah. If you go to — I can just like cron this and then just —

You don't even have to cron it. We expose this as a pub-subbable thing. So go to railway.com/stats.

Oh, there you go.

So it's like all real-time metrics for all of this stuff. There's a way to get this as a JSON too somewhere if you care. We're big on trying to build everything in public, talk about a lot of the stuff we're working on. We've had some issues in the past and we're like, "Hey cool, here's how we're fixing these things." We've got both compliments as well as some flack for our incident reports, and we're always trying to make them better over time just to talk with people.

近期事故与负责任的披露

Yeah. Um, obviously you had a big one recently. Uh, I like that it was only scoped to 3000. Uh, you presumably use Central Station — any talking through what happens, and how do you address it internally as a team?

Yeah. So internally — this one, like, really really sucked. You know, it was to do with an upstream provider that didn't do the behavior that they said they were documenting — which is unfortunate given they wrote the RFC on how the behavior should work. But we rolled those things out and then Central Station caught that initially, where we had a couple users being like "oh, the caches aren't invalidating for some of this stuff," right? And so turned it off immediately, etc.

But when you go and roll out to that large user base of like 3 million people, you have a lot of different disparate behaviors that can come up, right? And so try as we will, we tested those things in staging. We have tests for them, all this other stuff. And unfortunately we hit kind of an edge case there. And we've incidentally gone and hardened a lot of those systems and now we can make a lot of that stuff better. But yeah, it was a tough one unfortunately.

Yeah. I always wonder how the private disclosures are supposed to work if people find an issue. Are they supposed to contact you first? When you run a platform, these things are going to happen — and what channels should people pursue to quietly resolve it before it becomes a much bigger incident?

Yeah. So I think there's responsible disclosure. We kind of err on the side of — we'd rather over-disclose and know that something is wrong versus almost having your provider gaslight you. And so we've erred on the side of sharing those things more publicly, even if they go and impact a small subset of users.

And that's just a decision that we've made internally. We have four values — one of them is honor. And so — what's the honorable thing to do? It's like, well, you notify people to the widest degree at which they may have been affected, or if there was an issue — and then we confront that head on and be like "why did that happen? What can we do better in the future?"

Yeah, not the whole user base.

No. And that's because of incremental rollouts and progressive rollouts and stuff like that.

Interesting. Yeah, I feel like that should just be the norm at all large platforms, right?

Oh, it totally should. And at a variety of companies it totally is, right? Like — there's a whole quote of "Meta runs like 10,000 different versions of Meta in general." And like, to our earlier point about agents — they need the same thing. They need to be able to shadow traffic.

I think we've built so much ceremony around "production is sacred" and all these other things, that we need to get to a point where it's just trivially easy to test different behaviors in a safe environment — cuz then you can make those mistakes in an environment that's safe.

So you mentioned — somebody brought it up — do you see a world in which these things get automatically caught, not necessarily by your agent but like your customer's agent? You know what I mean? The cache invalidation thing seems like a pretty easy thing to track if you know to look for it.

It's hard because then you almost need — well, for us to determine it, we need to hook in with your observability infrastructure in general, right? This is why we almost have the template loop on the platform — to be able to roll those things out progressively, where you say "hey listen, I can roll this out to Johnny Vibe coder initially," or I can push a shard and you can almost consume that at your own leisure and be like "oh okay, I'm going to update to this specific version," or have this roll out over a period of weeks where you're pushing a new version and then it goes to 0.1% of people, 1% of people, and then rolls out all the way. That's the kind of non-deterministic version control that we've talked about earlier.

So yeah — 100%. And I do believe that's where most things should go towards, because I think ultimately most companies end up building that staged rollout system in-house, and it's just the same thing built again and again and again at every single one of these different companies. So there's a massive opportunity to consolidate a lot of developers.

You should have a free tier — like the model providers give you free tokens if you let them use the data. Like "we'll give you free compute if you're the number one chart that goes out and you let us plug into your observability."

Yeah. Incidentally we do that, right? And that's why — you know, we talked about the impact of that on 3,000 people or whatever. We start with the lower-impact people — the larger companies etc. on the platform are the last that should receive those kind of rollouts, so that they have a version of the platform that's deeply deeply stable.

I have three services, so I'm sure I get the first rollout. You can nuke my thing at any time, man.

上游 Agent SRE 与可观测性

I guess my other question is — there's all these SRE agent companies, there's the observability people who also want to have agents that fix your upstream problems. You have your own agent in the canvas now. How do you see that play out?

It's almost like the stacking entropy thing in general, right? I think if you don't have the primitives to make iterating in production safe, it becomes very very difficult. And so if you're an observability provider and you're like "oh, here's this fix to this error" — assume like 80% of those are probably actually good, they're going to make sense, etc. But then the last 20% of that long tail of complex issues — ultimately rolling those changes out, if you just let somebody say "oh cool, this looks good" and just stamp it, there's an opportunity for you to have an issue or an incident.

And I think that's why it's really really important to have those forked environments. People have staging, etc., but it always ends up deviating from prod. And so you need the primitives and the workflows and the experience built in — in our mind, as a first-party thing on the platform — so you can fork any point at any service at any point in time.

I consider the canvas almost like a little sheet of transparency paper, and the agent is kind of like this little guy that you push up — and it should be able to pop up in the canvas and it should be like "oh cool, I need to copy that service, I need to copy that service so I can test these two things — that's my hypothesis as an agent. Okay cool, I can go and do that — looks good for all this stuff. Ideally I get a read-only copy of production. Anything that's PII, etc. is marked as a transform when we automatically clone that database, or go for a copy-on-write version of it." And it just makes those changes — "does this actually work?" — as close to production as possible.

Because ultimately that's how close you have to be, or you just have a massive amount of drift where "oh I've changed this thing" and then it just gets out of sorts. The system gets a lot more unstable. And that's what you see with a lot of these massive systems that companies built on top of Docker for local and then Cube for production — all of that complexity ends up getting to a point where it slows down the developers, yes, but it just gets to a point where it's so unstable at scale that it becomes hard for people to iterate. And so we want to compress a lot of that stuff way down.

对 AI SRE 的怀疑与"规范-代码-测试"三位一体

I was texting Erica for questions, and she says actually you were originally not a believer in AI SRE. Have you come around on it?

Yeah. Well, I flipped — I'm actually still not a believer in the AI SRE, because I believe that you need the primitives to make those things safe. And if you just unleash an AI SRE on your production infrastructure and you don't have safe primitives for copying volumes, making sure that this is fine — it's going to nuke your production database. It's not a matter of if, it's a matter of when it's going to nuke that database.

I'm a big believer in making those loops safe in general. I think I was a pretty deep AI skeptic until like 2023, and then 2024 I kind of was like "okay, maybe I can make this thing roughly do it," and 2025 I was like "okay, now I can hold this," and then over the whole Christmas break — winter break — you just saw everybody come back like "oh my god, it's almost impossible to hold —"

Seriously, on the Claude docs —

Claude bot — well, Claude — but it's gotten to a point where it's almost harder to hold it wrong than it is to hold it right, you know? It's like that scene in Avengers where Vision's like "it's terribly well balanced" when he picks up Thor's hammer — like, damn, this thing just kind of self-balances and works quite well. So yeah, I'm a deep believer at this point that that will be the dominant species. Again — assembly, C, C++, JavaScript, words, right?

Yeah, it feels like a big jump.

Yeah, it feels like a big jump, and it is too. And I think it's not like you abandon CPU-based discrete logic and just move straight to fuzzy logic. You need both, right? So your skills should call code or applications or whatever — some sort of static structure — and you can use the skills to distill what the procedure should be, or how the code should act.

I'm kind of coming to this thesis which is — you need three points essentially: you need a clear spec of what defines the system, you need the code, and then you need the tests. And I think when you say this thesis out loud, it's like — well, if you've been in engineering for any amount of time, you're like "well, no — yeah, of course — that's an RFC, a request for comment, that's tests, and that's your code."

But they all matter a lot, and having them all be actually together so that they can reinforce each other and say "well, the spec and the tests match but the code doesn't — let me reconcile them. Oh okay, now the tests and the spec match — let me go and reconcile this other thing." And you can move through that period of basically saying "well, this is fuzzy and these two are either discrete in the case of tests, or slightly fuzzy slightly discrete in the case of code." And that's kind of your iteration loop.

I think that's also incidentally why you're seeing a lot of people be like "software factories" and "I want to write this doc and how I reconcile" — which I think is a bit of architectural astronomy if you don't actually go in and implement it. But I do think generally that loop is kind of where most things are going to ultimately end up.

Yeah. For listeners, we've been talking about this on the podcast for 3 years — the holy trinity of specs and tests. Adamar Freeman from Codo is the reference if people want to look it up.

自我修改的 Agent

One thing I do want to mention on the OpenClaude thing is also the idea that you can self-modify, which is kind of interesting. I don't know how exactly railway would support it — but I do have my OpenClaude and I just tell it that it has the railway CLI, you can do whatever. And in theory you can just — whatever capabilities or new infra you need, you can just call the railway CLI, provision it and add it to itself. And so the agent can modify its own infra, which I think is —

Yeah, it's nuts. We have a loop that I've set up — which is you put the railway CLI on top of something that runs on top of railway, right? And so you're essentially authenticated as whatever the current box is, and you can make any sort of changes to it and then you just call railway deploy and it deploys itself.

It's just like — "oh cool, I need to spin up this instance of this environment. I already exist in this environment — excellent, I've got access to a Postgres instance now." And this is kind of where we want to go with a lot of the agentic, almost self-replicating infrastructure. That's your loop. You iterate in production. You're going to continue to make some sort of change, and either it will work and you're going to want to merge it and say "cool, put it into our upstream," or it will not work and you can just throw it away.

How do you make those throwaway copies as trivial as possible to spin up, run super cheap, etc.? I think the era of "I have an AWS instance and I'm going to get four vCPU and 16 gigs of RAM" — it's going to get completely destroyed. Because if you do that for agents, you now need a thousand of those machines. It's so prohibitively cost expensive — versus, you know, we spent a ton of time trying to figure out "how do we make these deploys, whatever you want to call them" — Cloudflare has the isolates, everybody's like CodeSandbox — that atomic unit of deploy, like only pay for what you use, spin up instantaneously, close the loop as quickly as possible.

Because if the system can self-replicate the system, and it can do so safely and say "this is my environment, I'm making these changes," it can come back with "hey, does this look good? This is a new state of infrastructure given this prompt, I think I've solved this problem." And then you can go back to the agent and it actually looks a little bit different, goes and does the loop again, and you're like "cool, excellent — apply."

Yeah. I think that's retroactively obvious, kind of the most useful kind. Any other comments on agent deployment on railway?

No, I mean it's getting better every day. And you know, I'm on X — or Twitter, or whatever you want to call it — and you can always yell at me about the experience not working as well as it should, because there's plenty of things that should work way way way better.

"新无服务器"在哪里

I was going to say — I think at this stage in the juncture, when people want the massively or embarrassingly parallel compute, they usually talk serverless, and I feel like there's no new serverless that has emerged compared to the previous five years of serverless. You're kind of in that new bucket. I don't know if you have comparisons or philosophical differences you want to call out.

No, I think it's — as you mentioned — it's somewhere in between, right? It's the ability to run stateful, long-running — you want to call them workflows, you want to call them executions, whatever.

Which — like Vercel has fluid compute, and then Cloudflare has some container thing. Google has always had the App Runner.

App Runner, and the new one — yeah, I forget a bunch of them.

Yeah. I think that's kind of where everything roughly is — and this is why we've been working on it for the last 6 years — we just believe you do need access to a computer, a box that speaks Linux, right? So that you can deploy the things that you want to deploy on it. Other things are going to change the surface area of what you can build — but for us, we're always like "no, users need a computer, and they need to be able to deploy anything that they truly want."

And that's why we focused for a long time on those primitives — network, compute and storage — because if we can give you those things and expose them to you and allow you to run these things indefinitely, that's where we believe it's going to go.

And so I think you're seeing right now — again, the whole "Twitter has no nuance" thing — "it's servers — no, it's serverless." It's like — no, it's always somewhere in the middle. It's always some sort of convergence of "well, I want to run it for a long time, but also I don't want to provision this resource statically or pay for things that I'm not using." And that's always been our thesis from day one: pay only for what you use, run it indefinitely, it is just full full Linux basically.

Yeah. I think that's why I like the Vercel naming of "fluid" — cuz it's well, it's fluid, it's flexible.

Heroku 的正式弃用

Another milestone — and then I wanted to ask one more technical question — which is the Heroku official deprecation. What did they basically — you are one of the presumptive new Heroku. "New Heroku" has been a category for as long as I've been in developer tooling.

Yeah.

It's finally happening. What was that like? Is there any behind the scenes of like "well, this is the moment"?

Yeah. I mean, you have so many people just — you're just like — you were running stuff on here, you as this company — it's crazy that like, whatever name that you would know is running this thing, and then you're coming to us being like "yeah, we kind of want to move a lot of this stuff off." Like, okay, cool. Um, but yeah, it's kind of just nuts.

Any behind-the-scenes on why does Salesforce let Heroku kind of just stagnate?

Well, I can only guess, right? I think it's just hard when it's not your business. The business of Salesforce is to build a really really good CRM. And that's their focus — they should be really really focused on building a really really great CRM. And then you acquire this business as a compute business that's kind of an offshoot of your business.

And I think a lot of the early Meta folks have talked a lot about focus, right? Boz has a whole writeup that he's done where he talks about — in the early days of Meta we had no money and we were forced to get focused. And then we basically turned on the money — this is all me rephrasing — we turned on the money tree and then we had no reason to not have focus, cuz we just had infinite money where we could split all of our focus.

But that ends up diluting your product. It ends up making these offshoots where you're just like "is that the focus of the business?" And it ultimately ends up not being, if it's not the core of your business. And so to me it's kind of no wonder that it languished — because it just wasn't the core focus of the business.

And I think a lot of companies get in trouble when they split out their focus, because it means you're almost fighting a multi-fronted war — trying to compete with all these things, and not just compete with them externally but compete with them internally for alignment: "where are we going, what are we doing, what is our purpose here?" If you're really mission-driven — which is the aspiration for a company — of "why do people work on things?" — it's like, they want to work on something interesting.

Heroku is off to the side. It's not the core of the business. And so to get those resources — budget or focus or alignment internally — it's just pushed away. So it was literally just a matter of time for it to happen, in our mind.

Yeah. I think kudos for them to actually call it out instead of just letting it be unknown.

Yeah. Well, their whole release was a little bit odd, because they kind of called it out — "our Incredible Journey." They didn't say they were shutting it down, but they're like, "Yeah." And then behind the scenes, I think they issued some stuff to people being like "hey, you should close these accounts down, we are going to defecate this and remove it."

So yeah — it's crazy, because some of my first deployment experiences were on Heroku. It's a foundational thing — I had a freaking alias in my bash for Heroku deployments. You start with dragging stuff into an FTP server and then you move on to trying to get a deploy working — "how do I make this happen?" — and it's Heroku, right?

Did you know about Heroku hacks?

Yeah, exactly. You learn about all this, and it was the on-ramp for us. But the wheel turns regardless. There's new stuff that's emerging, and we're very very happy to almost continue to carry the torch on for a lot of that stuff — but we don't want to be the new Heroku. We want to be the way in which people are building and deploying software, and ultimately the way that people monetize software over time.

I mean, it's a big crown to be a new Heroku — there's like 50 companies that fought for this.

Oh yeah, everybody's kind of holding some portion of this. But yeah, for us we're just happy to support people, companies, etc. The platform works a bit differently — it's obviously almost the similar game loop. But we've been quite dogmatic in terms of where we believe these things are going to go — in terms of primitives, the agents, all those other things. And so some things will fit and then some things you have to change a few of the workloads. Like we don't have — what's that feature that people really love?

Pipelines.

Heroku. Yeah. We have some approximation of it with the environment system in general. But yeah, it's been super exciting. We've got a ton of people that we're able to support, and it's growing a lot.

Temporal

Any other technical — I have one more. Temporal. Okay, so Temporal — I have sold my shares.

Uh, you are a power user. You're one of our earliest customers — I think I met you through Temporal or something. You are a big Temporal business — you build on Temporal, have complaints. I think this is the most neutral, most informed conversation that anyone will ever hear about Temporal without someone working at the company.

Yeah, that's fair.

It's the two of us.

Yeah. No, I think that's fair. So — I have used Temporal for almost 10 years now, right? Because of Cadence, all those other things.

At — just give people a scale of what Cadence is at Uber, like people don't know.

Yeah. So Cadence was the precursor to Temporal, and it powers all of the trip actions, the rides — like when you rent a jump bike or scooter, or a car. You're running these workflows for a period of time, and you're basically saying "this ride will run for an indefinite period until it finishes." And you can attach information whether it's "oh you paused it in this zone, and so you need to add this dollar charge to the bill," and then when you end the trip, your workflow is done. That whole experience behind the scenes — I don't know about today — but it was powered by Cadence at that point in time.

Yeah, and I used to say it's like — imagine if you could program the entire user journey top-down as one function.

Yeah. And it's such a powerful idea and it's so so important. It's also incidentally so important for the next phase of the agentic journey, where you want an agent to do a specific task and then you want it to be complete or incomplete on that task, and then move on to the next thing. You need a way to manage these workflows, and to manage them dynamically.

And I think for me Temporal was always really really great in theory, and really really great when you got it working the way that you wanted to in production. It's just — it required you to model that entire journey in your head. And if you didn't have the entire journey in your head, you could put yourself in a spot where you would cause issues — where replaying the state of the entire workflow causes a non-determinism issue.

Because it works on deterministic workflow history.

Yeah, exactly. The way that I would describe it is — it's a jet engine. If you know how to operate it, if you know how to run it, you can — but you can't hand it to people who are trying to build things that end up being complicated but don't have that whole state in their head.

So if you have a large — like, we run our whole deployment pipeline on top of it, right? And so that's a reasonably complicated workflow. There's pre-commit hooks, there's signaling, there's queuing, all of this other stuff. And we kind of ran into the same thing at Uber, where as you tried to express this large workflow — as you mentioned, going all the way down — it got more and more complicated, and it got more and more states in the state machine that you had to map back.

And so at Uber we built a system for doing the state machine and testing the state machine and all that other stuff. And we've started to build some of those things here, because it's grown quite heavily. It's such a — I don't want to say love-hate relationship cuz that's too broad — like, when it works really really well, it works super super well. But then you run into a spot where somebody who hasn't interacted with the system, or doesn't have the full context, goes and puts something in the system that invalidates some of the state or causes a non-determinism issue, or spins off a ton of activities.

And then you have to keep track of almost underlying SRE knobs — like "oh, we have the amount of activity slots in this thing." It's like — well, these should just scale with memory, vCPU, all of those other things. So it ends up becoming a bit of a bear to scale out.

Yeah, so you need a very capable sysadmin running things behind the scenes for you.

Yeah.

If you were to move off, what would you do?

I think we would build our own workflow — we have a few internally that we've worked on.

This is one of those classes of things where you typically wouldn't vibe code it. But I'm wondering if —

Well, I don't think you should vibe code it still — you still want to run types, tests and stuff like that to make sure. I mean, it's not like Temporal had to invent that from scratch either, right? There's libraries for those things that you can run, and on top of that it's just a state machine that you have to really map out — but ultimately you define those abstractions that you want and you run it through a state machine, and that's it. It's very very doable.

So yeah, I think the workflow stuff is very very interesting. There's a few really cool companies — I think Restate's doing some neat stuff here.

语言、Railpack 与 Nix

So you're very tied into JavaScript — you're like a JavaScript maxi?

Internally we have JavaScript — or we have TypeScript — we have Rust, and we have Go. Those are three languages, right? We don't add any more stuff. Actually, that's not true — we have a little bit of C because we write BPF code and its hooks and stuff like that.

Is this for the side container things, sidecar stuff?

No. So this is for the networking stack, as well as the volumes and stuff like that. We used the TypeScript stuff a lot because it's what powers the dashboard, but we're going to move a lot of the workflow stuff off of the dashboard stack into actually the infrastructure stack.

Don't power things on front end, guys — even though it's free compute.

Yep.

Any other technical infrastructure cool stuff? Rail packs — I don't know if that's still —

Yeah. I mean, we built that engine for determining dependencies based on your source code, which is super cool. It's called Railpack. We built the first version called Nixpacks, which is on top of Nix. And then we moved.

People have been trying to get me to adopt Nix and NixOS for like 4 years. Is it ever going to be a thing?

I don't know. We were super excited about it in general, but it has a bunch of different pain points — cuz if you think of it, it's a stack of versioned source code, or a stack of versioned binary at specific slices in time. And so if you want version X and version Y, you end up bloating a lot of your package space, which blows up the size of your images and makes it really really difficult for real-world workloads.

But you know — you content-address it, you cache it. There's a lot of optimizations that in theory you should be able to do.

In theory, yes. And what happens ultimately is — you have a large enough user base and a disparate enough set of machines that you run into the problem — there's a paper that Meta released, XFaaS, the internal serverless system — it ends up being very very difficult to do that at scale unless you break out specific runtimes. Which we did not want to do, because we wanted to truly allow you to deploy anything — which was our initial thing with Nix.

But we've moved towards some interesting stuff that I think we'll be able to talk about a little bit later, that we've built for doing content-addressable file systems, to be able to lazy-load anything from any point and then page that into memory.

Amazing. Okay.

Yeah, it's going to be fun. The future is very very bright. It's crazy. It's going to be nuts.

创始人之旅：云开销与"F1 车手"

Okay. Founder journey stuff. And your cloud usage — you tweeted you're going to spend 300k this month.

Yeah. I think we got — two coding agents across the company.

Yeah. You only have 35 people, so I'm sure they're not all spending 10K a month. What's the distribution?

I think I'm at about 25 in general. And then we have some power users kind of all the way down. I don't know — we came back from the winter break and I was basically like: if you are writing code by hand, you are doing this wrong, right? The tools are good enough at this point that you can move extremely extremely quickly. And like, yes, there are issues and pain points and all these other things — but you should be reviewing the code that you are writing instead of trying to go and write it by hand.

All of those architectural patterns, all of those other things — you're not going to throw them in the garbage. Actually they matter more now than any other time. But you just shouldn't spend your time generating code that you would write. Like if you know how to go in and write it, just ask the agent to write it and then reconcile it until it looks like you would have written it yourself.

And I think incidentally people misconstrue my propensity to push people towards agents for "hey, we're growing really really fast and we've had some bumps in reliability" — they're not necessarily related. But I think people should really understand the tools are good enough for you to move extremely extremely quickly, to build things way way larger than you could have possibly built before.

And so — to our point way earlier about "how do you cool data centers in space?" — it's like, well, I don't know actually. But you're at a point now with software where you can be like "well, how would I build block storage from scratch? How would I do these things? I have ideas because I've got history, I've read all these papers — let me go and work them out, and let me build massive test benches with thousands of tests, because they're free to author right now," to make sure that this system can be built.

And I think if you're not using the AI systems to almost speedrun your road map — to figure out where you need to be to reconcile your existing system onto the future — then you're kind of missing a large point of what is currently happening. Because you can just template out anything and validate it on the side for free.

What's the path to spend 3 million a month? Is it bound by ideas and things that the customers can absorb?

I think for most companies it's actually bound by deployment at this point in time. And that's why we've seen a lot of like a massive boom in terms of users trying — not just users, like companies, you know, Fortune 50s — going and being like "how do we get our developers to move quicker?"

I think you're probably going to hit your CFO before you hit any of these limits, cuz they're going to look at this and be like "there's an eye-watering amount of money being spent on these tokens." I think it was the Uber Claude token budget for the entire year or whatever. And so inference costs have to come down — but they're also, you know, we're inference-constrained at this point in time.

And so you're going to almost get this price discovery of "what makes sense for an org to adopt." And I think what you're going to end up with is the F1 driver concept. Which is — if you have somebody who's really really adept at these things, it makes sense to put them into a $3 million car. But if you're not, then it probably doesn't actually make sense for you to do that. And we're going to take a few of these people and say "you drive the F1 car — we need to go in this general direction, figure out if this works and almost go ahead and prototype it."

And so we've done a few of those things where we've vastly accelerated our road map — "oh, we thought we were going to be able to ship this thing in the next few years, but actually we can probably ship it in the next few months now." Because we're saying "oh, validated it out, it works — we don't have to even build it incrementally. We can now skip steps to move towards where our vision is."

Yeah, I think a lot of people are realizing the road map doesn't always have a business impact. And so it's like "oh, it's too expensive to run these tokens" — but if your road map was actually built to make more money by the time you built the whole thing, you would have some sort of token pricing for it. The same way you do with sales — like you would spend a billion dollars in sales if you knew you would get $2 billion of revenue out of it.

Exactly. And I think the really naive way to measure this is almost like your percentage of tokens that end up in production. And so if you can measure that you are getting this level of impact because those tokens are ending up in production, that's awesome.

But I think the burden of proof is now going to arise. And you see it internally too on our stuff — like we have a growing number of pull requests that haven't yet been merged. And you're just like "okay, how do you get this into production?" And so it's really about how quickly you can build and deploy that software — which is exciting, because that's our whole — we build and deploy software, you know.

SDLC 的变化与 Feature Flag

Yeah. The SDLC is changing, and it's something that both of us are super interested in exploring. One of my theses — it's not my thesis — the pull request is dying. It's going to be the prompt request. And then beyond that, code review is also kind of dying — because do you really need to, if you have all the other systems in place? What else is changing about the SDLC?

What else is different? Well, I think the AI SRE — the tools to make — so the AI SRE is one of those things where it's a pie-in-the-sky aspirational. What does it take to get an AI SRE?

By the way, you should expose your tooling to your customers at some point.

Yeah. Which tooling?

The central command center.

Oh, Central Station. So we have it for template maintainers, right? Template maintainers can deploy and maintain templates and they get feedback on a lot of that stuff. And so we're 100% going to expose those things incrementally.

Yeah, but like — clustering around incidents — everyone has a version of that, but I don't think anyone solved it.

Yeah. And I don't want to say we've solved it internally, but it's gotten so good that now we can see those incidents forming pretty quickly. Real time. And so at some point those will be things that either somebody else builds, or we build. But we've always built stuff that was purpose-built for us, and if it made sense and there was a way to make it useful for users or monetize it, or make sure that loop becomes a profit center instead of a cost center, we want to do that at some point. So yeah, the pull request is definitely dying.

Do you do first-party feature flagging and incremental rollout type stuff as well?

So we have a feature flagging engine that we built internally that at some point we will roll out.

Because I don't see it as a user.

Yeah. Well, because we have to beta test it. We actually care a lot a lot a lot about the quality of the things. There's plenty of stuff that we've used internally and then got to a point where it doesn't make its way entirely through the journey because it fails. It's like — this holds for one service, but it doesn't hold for multiple services. So we'd have to build these things for multiple services to make this work.

And we know for a fact that if we release this thing, we'd have to rebuild it again and again and again. And some things are worth doing — but a lot of them also just inform our road map of "okay, for us to make that actually a bit easier, we can do a few of these things first and then we get to that experience." We don't want to dilute the experience by basically saying "oh yeah, this works but only for this service" — unless it's a very very core initiative.

So over the next few months we're going to roll out a few things that are "okay, it works for a single service, then it works for multiple services, then it works for multiple services across the environment." But you have to be very very deliberate about those things, otherwise you end up with a bunch of broken disparate experiences which ultimately end up creating a ton of support load.

So it's kind of the thing earlier about — you expand your company to get those features, and then almost compact it, smooth out those things so the experience is really really stellar. Like we were talking in the hallway earlier where you're like, "Oh my god, it's gotten so much better." And I'm like, "Oh man — internally we're like 'damn, this part really sucks, we got to make this significantly better.'"

No, I can attest — over the last 3 years that I've watched you build Railway. But yeah, I would call out to listeners — if you're not aware — the importance of feature flagging. It's a very big part of Uber culture, so much so that they have too many feature flags and then they have another thing to remove feature flags.

Yep. 100%.

What was it — there's a paper about this —

Flagger — and there's been another one.

Facebook has Gatekeeper. So they're really important — and agents are going to need this. That's the fundamental thing behind incremental rollouts.

Yep.

OpenAI acquired Statsig. And basically GPT-5 is just routing and flagging through different models.

And it's super important, right? Because if you assume the software development life cycle is 100% going to change — but it's going to change because we're trying to do things a thousand times faster and a thousand times more concurrent than we were currently doing — and so, routing.

And what ends up becoming important at scale — you know, before I even started Railway, I actually built a feature flagging product and I tried to sell it to people. Cuz I was like "oh, it's an easier version of Launch Darkly." And I ran into this situation which is — anybody who's small enough to adopt your technology doesn't care about feature flags. And then anybody who's large enough to actually need feature flags needs so much scale that you have to build out all the existing infrastructure. So I ended up scrapping that.

But what is old is new again — because now companies are trying to move really really quickly. But you can't just yolo this vibe-coded thing straight into production. You need to basically say "hey, here's my blast radius, here's my impact, I want to shadow it for these users" — feature flags. You're going to need those tools that ultimately those larger companies ended up having to build to maintain their structures. Everything's just going to get compressed by a thousand-x so that everybody can do that and build those structures really really quickly.

And that's exactly where we're at right now — you're compressing the software development life cycle, and then we're going to expand it and add way more new things to it.

牲口而非宠物

Yeah. And then the other term that comes to mind when this kind of discussion happens, for newer developers who haven't heard this term — cattle, not pets. Because your prod has a name like a baby, you know — "I have to keep it alive." But when it's cattle, you can just mass farm and roll out and portion out parts of them and kill them.

Yeah, exactly. Um — I actually think — maybe this is the hot take — but I think that's actually going to change. And I think you can move towards having pets, so long as you have — and this is going to be a jump — so long as you have a cloning machine for your pets.

Uh-huh.

If you can snapshot every single thing at every frame, then it actually doesn't matter if you obliterated that, because you have some sort of snapshot of it.

All of the things that we have built right now are to essentially block out any sort of changes or alterations from that hermetically sealed DevOps line. It's like — okay, well, you have to write a Docker file because I only need this specific cut of the file system. What if you just had the whole file system? What if you just snapshot it, and what if you lazily load the entirety of the file system? Then you can get around this problem entirely. You don't need the ceremony of having a Docker file, or having an Ansible script — you can just iterate on that loop and then snapshot it: "is this the right loop, is this the right thing at this point in time? Okay cool — now I'm going to merge it in production, go merge the file system."

Yeah, why not?

It's going to be really fun.

Yeah, this is a whole other can of worms — but I think the number of things that are stateful in a VM — if you just catalog them and develop dedicated solutions for solving each of them, you can actually cut this problem down a lot. And it's surprising that people weren't really trying until now.

Yeah. Well, it's always been surprising to me — because these are the things that we work on, cuz they're just like — I'm like, it's so obvious.

First principles — you need them. Everyone in theory needs them. And then the big clouds don't do them. So you're like — it's impossible or something, I don't know.

Yeah, exactly. You're like "oh well, Meta has all the people who write eBPF code and they're doing something with them." But you need that kind of stuff to solve these problems. And like we talked about earlier — whatever is required, however deep we have to go to solve those problems — all the way down to the kernel TCP/IP stack — we're going to go and figure that out. Is there something that we need to modify to make that work for the mental model that we have for the universe moving forward? Like, yeah, 100%, we're going to do it. We'll just keep going.

Sounds fun.

It's super fun. It's so much fun. I have to literally peel myself away from the fun interesting problems that we have, to make sure that we can scale the company in a way that works. And there's so many different fun interesting problems — whether it is "how do you get the information from the customer to support to the person who built the thing internally," or "how do you do iteration," or "how do you get context from the dashboard to users," or "how do you drill down all the way to the infrastructure layer," "how do you manage orchestration as a real-time operating system versus a feedback control system." It's just so fun, you know?

创始人之旅：独自创业

Yeah. I mean, speaking of — maybe talk about the founder side. You're famously — you know, the YC, the SF consensus is "you go to YC, you get a co-founder, you do all these things." You've done none of that.

No, I've done a lot of different things in general.

In the elevator you were like — actually, a co-founder kind of makes sense if one person is the tech person and the other is the bizdev person.

Yep.

And — but you have to contain all those multitudes yourself. How do you do it?

Okay. Was there a question in there or what? Um — the question is "what the hell, how do you do it?"

The question is how are you alive right now?

Yeah. Well, I mean — just try to get eight hours of sleep, you know.

Is there like a balance that you ideally — like 50/50, 30 — what's the mental model that you use as a balance?

There's like — you just have to think about all these things and be obsessed with all of these things. Whether it is being obsessed with how people think about your product from a go-to-market perspective, or being obsessed from a perspective of "well, if I can make this change at the kernel level, then I can make it so that the user's SSH connection never drops" — because that's what I want, I want a universe in which I can snapshot all these things and it looks exactly like you would just iterate on a VM.

And I think you just have to be obsessed with all those things at every layer of the stack. And I think that's what makes it easier for me. I think some people are obsessed with different portions of the journey, like the company or whatever. And that's when you can get really really good cohesion by segmenting out these things.

And so, you know, in the elevator I was talking about — you have a technical person, and then you have the customer person. And I think if you can segment those lines out really really well, and you can be very very clear about what your areas of ownership are — for yourself, your company, or just where you're going to operate — you're going to have a good time. If you can't be clear about those things — and this is why I was saying two is the worst number of co-founders — it's because you have no tiebreak. You basically are like "well, I disagree on this thing and I disagree on this thing." It's like — well, how do you resolve that?

Well, you say someone's CEO, right?

Right, exactly.

Then you're like, okay, you have a tiebreaker.

Yeah, totally. I mean, listen — it's hard. It's hard every single way you cut it. It's hard if you get help. It's hard if you do it yourself. It's just hard to run things, roughly speaking. But it's so rewarding. It's so fun, you know?

建议与写作

What have you found useful? Like a coach? Any advice that has been really helpful?

I like to write a lot. Um — I get in trouble a lot for my Twitter. I think there's a pattern.

Who do you get in trouble with?

The people on Twitter, you know. Um — I was talking about it and I was like, "Hey, if you're working weekends, you're kind of messing up your planning roughly." And I've gone back and forths on that — because I think actually right now we're kind of at an extenuating time in general where it actually makes sense to work more.

Because the goals are pretty clear in my mind. And so if you have the vision and you know where you're going, you should work a little bit harder to distill that vision and go and do those things. But if you don't have the — "we're like, I think we should be going on this journey, I'm not 100% certain, I want to get a little bit of clarity" — I think what you need to do is disconnect, and you need to take your weekends very very seriously. You need to write about where you are, what do you want to do, where do you want to go, what problems are you trying to solve.

So writing is important. Sitting down — I don't like the word "meditation" or whatever, but whatever gets you into the state of mental clarity — that's the thing that's really really important when you're trying to go on these journeys of saying "well, we're here and we really need to be here," or "we're here and I think we need to be roughly in this space for this to work."

And then, you know — disconnect, hang out with the people that you love, and then work super super hard. I try and work like sun-up to sun-down Monday to Friday, all out in general, and then I try and disconnect on Saturday, and then I come back to work on Sunday afternoon. And then I do my writing plan for the week, all those other things. And it works really really well for me.

Um — but another hot take is — most advice is to be digested and to be thrown out the window, and if it's helpful, it'll come back. If it's helpful, you'll have learned it over time through experience. But yeah — you mentioned the standard YC advice and all those other things. We've made failure as a society very very expensive, and it makes it difficult for people to trod off the paths.

GPU、专注与全面转向自建数据中心

Any other soft books you want to get on — anything that you have not tweeted and gotten in trouble with that you want to preview to the world?

No, I think the agent stuff is just — it's crazy. It's going to be the dominant way in which people are doing pretty much everything — provided we get the amount of inference required for that to happen. But over the next 10 years, you just see a fundamental shift in terms of how people are thinking about even just authoring the logic that's in their head.

Yeah. Maybe one way of phrasing this is — if Allbirds can become a GPU provider, so can Railway.

Yeah. I think there's a lot of horror in us actually not becoming a GPU provider. I think you're defined almost more by the things that you don't do than the things that you do — because it's really really easy for you to just say yes to a bunch of different things.

And I think it's going to be very very interesting to watch — you know, I think Anthropic is an amazing company and super super stellar, and they're moving into a variety of different zones. They're moving into like the Figma kind of stuff that they're after.

As of — was on Figma's board, and then they removed him like Monday, and then they launched this today.

Yeah. So things move very very fast right now. So yeah — it's just going to be the way in which people are —

Okay. So your answer is focus. No GPUs for now.

Yeah. Focus. Never say never. Like I can tell you for a fact that we will not be doing GPUs now, but we 100% will be doing GPUs at some point in the future. And that's not me leaking our road map — because we don't have plans to do GPUs — it's just a function of: at some point you need flops. At some point, if you're fully vertically integrated and you want to make it really really trivial for people to iterate and build and deploy things, you need access to this core piece of fundamental logic.

And then — at some point, presumably your own data center traffic is a minority of your workload right now — but is there a majority, or you just kind of completely turn off —

Oh, at some point we got to 100% data center — like our own data centers. And right now it's the vast majority of the stuff that exists on our bare-band data centers.

So you're already there — like vast majority.

Yeah. Yeah. Right.

I didn't know the extent of the transition.

Yeah, totally. It was completed at some point, and then we grew so fast that we had to basically scale back on that.

Take us back. Sorry, Google Cloud.

Yeah, it was funny. We got to — on the Datadog dashboard — it got to 100% and then it divoted back down into the 90s or whatever, cuz we're adding capacity.

Yeah. It's interesting. You're literally building a new cloud, and that's independent — and people assume that that could never happen post the AWS.

Yeah. And it's hard, right? Like — we're going to figure out a bunch of different things to make sure that the platform is deeply deeply reliable. You have to break ground on a lot of new things when you basically decide you're going to build a cloud from scratch but not copy the hyperscalers.

We've been very very deliberate to invent our own infrastructure from scratch, based on reading a ton of papers, but almost promising to ourselves that we wouldn't copy somebody else's homework. Because we were saying "hey listen, if we copy somebody else, we lose — you're just going to become them over time."

And so you have to have a core thesis about why does this business need to exist at this point in time. And for us it's always been about — the activation energy to get something deployed in production at any of the hyperscalers as of right now is far too high. And we believe that it should be instantaneous. We believe that there should be no friction in between what your thought is and the reality that comes out, that you can share with your friends.

And so that's what we're building toward, again at every layer of the stack. Like if we've got to go down to energy, we'll go down to energy at some point. It matters a lot for us from the experience of giving people access to this tooling — because it's gated. And it's not even just gated for these citizen developers that are now vibe coding — you have multiple layers. You have the citizen developer, you have the front end developer, you have the backend developer, you have a DevOps person, all of these layers — and they all need to disappear so people can just ship like that.

Amazing. All right, that's the future of cloud. Thank you.

Thank you for having me. It's been wonderful.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一场针对"熵"的圣战——而他自己可能没意识到这是同一件事
整段对话里，Jake 说得最多、却分布在完全不相干语境里的一个词是 **entropy（熵）**。他用它形容工具链——"Docker 上面叠 K8s，再叠 Ansible，是在熵上面堆熵"；他用它形容开放平台——"互联网是个充满熵的可怕地方"；而当话题转到太空数据中心时，他给出的反对理由恰恰是**字面意义上的热力学熵**——"没人证明过怎么在真空里耗散这么多热"。
这里有个让人意外的观察：Railway 表面上是个 PaaS 公司，本质上却是一台**反熵机器**。它做的每一件事——版本化一切、快照一切、渐进式回滚、可逆的文件系统——都是在对抗"不可逆性"。而太空数据中心之所以让他本能地不适，不是因为它不性感，而是因为它触碰了**唯一一种他无法用快照和回滚作弊的熵**：物理热力学第二定律。一个工程师对"软件熵"的洁癖，和他对"物理熵"的敬畏，是同一种心智结构的两次显影。
## 最深的反讽：他想亲手拆掉自己职业生涯的起点
Jake 的技术根基是 Cadence / Temporal——也就是**确定性状态机**、显式的

item.title: [Bits + Bips] Why Agents Will Ditch Stablecoins That Don't Pay Yield: Bits + Bips
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

表面看是"收益之争"，本质是"状态机套利"

大多数人听这段对话，会停在第一个问题上：稳定币不付息，凭什么和货币市场基金竞争？但真正的洞见藏在 Austin 那句被低估的话里——未来的赢家不是某个产品，而是"transformer"（状态转换者）。

这里有一个让人意外的观察：这场讨论表面上在比较"哪个产品收益高"，实际上 Austin 已经悄悄宣布产品竞争结束了。他说的是"现在我是稳定币，现在我是货币基金，现在我是银行存款，等等我又是货币基金"。注意这个句式——它不是在描述资产，它在描述一个有限状态机。在 agent 的世界里，资产不再有固定身份，资产是一连串可切换的法律态。谁能让切换的摩擦趋近于零，谁就赢。USDC↔USYC 的"无缝转换"不是一个 feature，它是整个商业模式本身。Circle 卖的不是稳定币，是"转换器"。

跨领域连接：美国监管的"碎片化"，其实是一套压缩算法

Austin 说美国有 ~19 个监管机构、日本人听了觉得你长了第二个脑袋——他把这当成一个尴尬的缺陷。但换个领域看，这里有个反直觉的连接。

在信息论和热力学里有一个概念：势差（梯度）是做功的前提。没有温差，热机不能运转；没有电压差，电流不流动。美国监管的碎片化，恰恰在不同法律态之间制造了人为的"势差"——支付产品与投资产品之间的收益差、合规差、可转让性差。日本 FSA 那种统一、平滑的监管，反而抹平了势差，消灭了套利空间。

所以这是真正的"啊哈时刻"：美国监管的混乱不是 bug，对"转换器"型玩家而言它是利润的来源。 一个统一监管的世界里没有 transformer 的生意可做，因为没有需要被跨越的边界。Circle、Coinbase、JP Morgan 之所以拼命建"整合栈"，是因为它们在为自己圈占监管梯度的发电权。监管越分裂，梯度越陡，转换器的功率越大。Clarity 法案如果通过，最讽刺的结果是——它把这种"美国式的怪"制度化、合法化，反而给了能跨态者一个永久的护城河。

隐藏的系统性真相：Agent 不会"二选一"，它会让两者都活下来

Gordon 给了一个容易被忽略却极其重要的反驳。提问者的隐含假设是：理性的 agent 会把所有钱挪到付息的那一边，稳定币赛道会被掏空。Gordon 的回答是一个经验事实——世界上有 3 万亿美元的实体现金，有大量不付息的活期账户和储蓄账户并存，即使是同一家机构提供的。

这里的系统性真相是："理性优化"并不等于"收益最大化"，它等于"针对当下任务的总成本最小化"。 当 agent 要让钱"休息"时，它优化收益；当它要"付款"时，它优化的是结算摩擦、可接收方范围、合规文书成本——而用证券付款的文书地狱会瞬间吞掉那 3% 的利息优势。

于是出现一个生态学式的结论：稳定币和货币市场基金不是竞争关系，而是共生关系，就像生物体内同时需要"脂肪"（储能态）和"血糖"（即用态）。身体不会因为脂肪能量密度更高就把血糖全部转化掉——因为转化本身有代价、有延迟。Agent 的世界也一样：它不会消灭非付息稳定币，它会维持一个动态平衡的资产组合，并在毫秒级别不停地在"储能态"和"即用态"之间再平衡。真正被淘汰的，从来不是稳定币这个物种，而是那些只能停在单一状态、无法被快速转换的资产。

📝 转录文本

引子：如果我是 agent，为什么要进不付息的稳定币？

I mean, you're talking Austin, you're talking about how agents hyper optimize. And that's one thing that we've we've learned. If there's anything that they're awesome at, they almost overoptimize.

And I guess my question would be, in that world — and I think you alluded to it — do money markets win? And the reason why I'm asking that is because if I'm an agent, I want that interest to come to me, not to go to the stable coin issuer. And do they start, you know, like — what prevents that?

And then I guess the second question is: what's the freaking difference between a money market fund and a stable coin when you step back for a second and you ignore wrappers and other stuff, and you say like, fundamentally, if it's the same risk and I get interest in one I don't get in the other, why would any agent go into the stable coin space?

Austin：支付产品 vs 投资产品，分歧来自美国的法律结构

All right. So, I'm going to pile in on that one and say this is something that clarity is actually quite important for, Chris, because the distinction between payments products and investment products has a lot to do with the legal structure in the United States, right?

So, like rewinding the clock for people, you know, and putting on my business school professor hat for a moment here: we had the 1933 Banking Act. We had the '34 and '40 Act in the securities space. We subsequently had the BSA, which is technically handled by banking regulators, not the securities regulators. And we have this proliferation of agencies in the United States.

If you're in a place like Japan where they have the FSA, and you go over there and explain to them we have somewhere around 19 regulators who touch all this stuff, they look at you like you've grown a second head, because it sounds kind of crazy. But here in the United States, we do have like this divide in our regulatory apparatus. You have the SEC and CFTC on the investment side, which helpfully aren't even the same agency and often don't agree with each other. Then you have all the banking regulators, who don't agree with each other or the SEC or CFTC, and vice versa.

So Chris, I think we've had this market fragmentation because of the structure that Congress has created.

Austin：赢家是"转换器"——能在不同法律态之间自由切换的人

And one of the interesting parts about clarity to me — and we'll talk about whether it's going to pass or not — is, as we're having the discussion around digital assets, as we're having a compromise around yield (and Gordon, I would love to get your opinion on that in a moment), one thing that I'm seeing about this whole thing is that inadvertently we're probably highlighting how weird the US framework is, and giving a big advantage to the people who can move between the different aspects.

Because here's the honest answer. An agent probably wants a money market fund that pays incrementally if the money is at rest. But the moment it wants to pay somebody, it wants that thing wrapped into a stable coin to make a payment easy — just 'cuz if nothing else, the paperwork implications of paying for things with securities, and who can receive them, is awful.

So because we have this weird fragmentation, I actually think it's going to be the people who are transformers — aka people who can move between one state to another. Like: now I'm a stable coin. Now I'm a money market fund. Now I'm a bank deposit. Wait, I'm a money market fund again. No, I'm a stable coin. Who will ultimately be the winners here, because you can optimize across all of those channels.

Which is why I'm paying increasing attention to integrated stacks like — say on one hand, to go crypto-native, Circle and Coinbase, and on the other hand, say, what's JP Morgan going to do.

So Gordon, let me throw that ball to you. I know you have —

Gordon：Circle 同时握有稳定币与代币化货币基金，但摩擦永远存在

Yeah, I think you're doing the selling for Circle almost. You know, we have the largest regulated stable coin, but we also have the largest tokenized money market fund, USYC.

And people could go between them. And as you rightly pointed out, one is a payment instrument, the other one is — if you want to get the money market yield and you go through the typical type of requirements, then you get the USYC yield. And moving in between them, we offer a seamless process. And perhaps aging — some days will make that even easier.

But you know, there is also some friction at the end of the day, right? It's — why is there $3 trillion of physical cash in the world? Why is there non-interest-bearing deposit accounts alongside with, you know, savings accounts? There's always some type of delimitation, even if it's offered by the same institution. And, you know, I think you're always going to have both — not just one versus the other.

And Chris, what do you think, to throw that ball right back to you?

Chris：可互操作的能力 + 网络效应，是未来的关键交叉点

No, I think you're right. I think you're going to see an interoperable capability.

I do think you'll see — you know, there's a network effect when enough people have accounts with money market funds, that you'll see more, I guess, ability to — I don't want to say pay, but exchange them for value and goods or whatever.

But yeah, I think that that intersection is going to be very important going forward, and the agents are going to be very good at it.

节目信息与赞助说明

If you like this segment, please like, subscribe, and tune in every Monday at 4:30 p.m. Eastern time. I'm Austin Campbell, the host of Bits and Bips, along with my friends Ram Ahluwalia and Chris Perkins, and our slate of exceptional guests. Every week, we're going to discuss macro, crypto, and the collision of worlds, covering topics that move markets and shape the financial landscape.

A 3% top off on my entire portfolio? Uh, yes. Who is saying no to that? That's free money. I don't care what's in your portfolio. That's a yes. If you've been looking for an excuse to consolidate your assets, this is it.

Until May 31st, Coinbase is giving a straight 3% Bitcoin boost on any crypto or cash deposits all month. Coinbase One is the ultimate membership to make the most of your money, which, as you know, is how I optimize my finances. Zero trading fees on thousands of crypto assets, 3.5% APY on USDC, boosted staking and lending rewards, and up to 4% Bitcoin back with the Coinbase One card.

Plus, you can still claim 20% off the first year of Coinbase One annual plans, a $50 Bitcoin bonus when you spend $100 on a new Coinbase One card in the first 30 days through May 31st, plus your share of five Bitcoin if you outpredict pro basketball coach Lethal Shooter, and a chance at a private shooting session with him.

It's the perfect time to centralize your assets and maximize your earnings. Get your 3% boost at coinbase.com/unchained. Visit coinbase.com/unchained — that's coinbase.com/unchained — to claim your Bitcoin bonus.

No purchase necessary. See rules and other ways to enter. Terms apply to other offers. Futures swaps via Coinbase Financial Markets. Risk of 100% loss. Payouts event-based, not investment advice, not available in Nevada. Coinbase One Card is offered through Coinbase Inc. and Cardless Inc. Cards issued by First Electronic Bank. Bitcoin back rates are based on cardholders' assets on Coinbase.

大多数人听这段对话，会停在第一个问题上：稳定币不付息，凭什么和货币市场基金竞争？但真正的洞见藏在 Austin 那句被低估的话里——**未来的赢家不是某个产品，而是"transformer"（状态转换者）**。
这里有一个让人意外的观察：这场讨论表面上在比较"哪个产品收益高"，**实际上 Austin 已经悄悄宣布产品竞争结束了**。他说的是"现在我是稳定币，现在我是货币基金，现在我是银行存款，等等我又是货币基金"。注意这个句式——它不是在描述资产，它在描述一个**有限状态机**。在 agent 的世界里，资产不再有固定身份，资产是一连串可切换的法律态。谁能让切换的摩擦趋近于零，谁就赢。USDC↔USYC 的"无缝转换"不是一个 feature，它是整个商业模式本身。Circle 卖的不是稳定币，是"转换器"。
## 跨领域连接：美国监管的"碎片化"，其实是一套压缩算法
Austin 说美国有 ~19 个监管机构、日本人听了觉得你长了第二个脑袋——他把这当成一个尴尬的缺陷。但换个领域看，这里有个反直觉的连接。
在**信息论和热

item.title: [GoldRepublic Global] Peter Schiff, Jim Rickards, Willem Middelkoop, Edin Mujagic: Global Sovereign Debt Crisis
item.dc:creator: GoldRepublic Global
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

当"标题"与"内容"分裂：一个比债务危机更普遍的系统病

最意外的观察先说：你这次请求本身，就是一个微缩的"主权债务危机"模型。标题承诺的是宏大叙事（全球债务、四位重量级人物），实际交付的却是一段 30 秒的评论管理教程——承诺的面值与底层资产严重不符。这正是主权债务危机的本质：一国国债的"标题价值"（票面、评级、政治承诺）与其"转录内容"（真实税基、生产力、偿付能力）之间的裂缝。你无意中递给我的，不是一个错误，而是一个完美的隐喻样本。

更深一层的跨领域连接在于：这段转录讲的是评论审核机制——"Published 标签默认显示所有公开评论"。表面是产品教程，骨子里却是一套信息主权的治理结构。频道主对评论的"发布/隐藏/待审"权，与央行对货币的"发行/回收/冻结"权，是同构的：两者都在管理一个自己无法完全控制、却必须维持其"可信度"的公共界面。Jason 教你的不是按钮，而是如何在一个开放系统里行使有限主权。这与 Rickards、Schiff 们争论的"谁有权决定货币价值"是同一道题的两种考法。

而真正的"啊哈时刻"或许是：这段转录的不完整性才是它最诚实的部分。它在"By default, you'll see the Published tab first"处戛然而止——系统只给了你默认视图，没给你全貌。这恰恰是所有复杂系统给普通参与者的待遇：你看到的永远是被预设好的"已发布"层，而"待审核""疑似垃圾""已隐藏"那几个标签页——无论是评论、还是被掩盖的国家债务表外负债——都需要你主动多点一下才能看见。系统性洞察的第一步，从来不是分析默认页面，而是怀疑"为什么这是默认页面"。 你的这次请求错配，逼我们做的正是这件事。

建议：把真正的 Schiff/Rickards 转录发来，我可以做一次真正穿透"标题层"的分析。

📝 转录文本

YouTube 频道评论管理教程

开场

Hey, this is Jason from the YouTube Team.

Today I'm going to show you how to easily manage your channel's comments from your computer and from your mobile device.

从电脑端开始

First, let's start with your computer and sign in to YouTube Studio.

From the left menu, select Comments.

By default, you'll see the Published tab first. This shows all the comments that are public on your channel.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [YC Root Access] The ERP for the AI Revolution is here
item.dc:creator: YC Root Access
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的观察：Campfire 的真正护城河不是产品，而是"焦虑的转移"

大多数人读这篇访谈，会得出"产品速度（product velocity）取胜"的结论。但真正藏在字缝里的、最让人意外的真相是这个——Campfire 卖的从来不是软件，而是一种"焦虑的合法转移"。

注意那位 CFO 说的话："我等于是在对你做一笔风险投资……如果你倒闭了，我会被解雇。"在 2024 年初，购买 NetSuite 是"安全"的选择，购买 Campfire 是"危险"的。但 John 描述了一个精妙的叙事翻转时刻：当 AI 成为董事会议题后，"做 NetSuite 的老牌厂商"反而变成了"非 AI 原生"的风险敞口。于是同一位会计、同一个决策，焦虑的方向被整个掉转了——继续用老系统才是危险，换新系统反而获得了"air cover（高层授权的掩护）"。

这就是为什么 John 反复强调"叙事必须持续演变，即使核心产品并不变"。Campfire 没有重新发明会计，它重新发明了"哪一种选择会让我被解雇"的计算公式。它真正的竞争对手不是 NetSuite，而是客户内心的恐惧函数——而它做的，是把这个函数的符号取反。

跨领域连接：Campfire 的"楔子翻转"，和生物学里的"幼态延续"是同一种策略

John 讲了一个容易被一带而过的决策：他们原本计划用"收入确认（RevRec）"作为楔子产品打进市场，再向核心 ERP 升级；结果在第三个月把整个逻辑倒过来了——核心产品成为主入口，RevRec 降级为附加模块。

这个翻转值得和一个跨领域概念并置：生物学中的幼态延续（neoteny）——某些物种通过"保留幼年特征到成熟期"反而获得演化优势（人类的大脑可塑性就是典型例子）。传统 SaaS 的 PLG 楔子策略本质上是"幼态"打法：用一个小而可爱的功能先活下来，再缓慢长大。但 John 发现，在使命关键型（mission-critical）的企业市场里，"可爱"是负资产。一个只买了 RevRec 的客户，恰恰是最难转化为核心 ERP 客户的群体——因为他们把 Campfire 归类成了"工具"，而不是"系统的根"。

这里的深层规律是：入口的形态会决定客户大脑里给你贴的认知标签，而标签一旦贴上极难撕掉。 楔子越小越精巧，你越会被永久定义为"小工具"。John 的选择——"算了，直接全力打整个 ERP"——本质是拒绝幼态延续，选择"一出生就是成体"。这也解释了访谈里 Andrew 那句关键评价："很少有创始人不害怕球（afraid of the ball）。"

隐藏的系统性真相：他用"建造速度"对冲了"功能不完整"，这是一场关于时间的套利

表面故事是"我们功能不全，但靠快速迭代赢了"。但系统性地看，John 做的其实是一笔时间套利：他把客户对"未来的不确定性"换算成了对"建造速度的可观测性"。

客户买 ERP，买的是"终局状态"——上市所需的审计、控制、审批流。一个 9 个月大的创业公司显然没有终局功能。John 怎么填补这个鸿沟？他让客户用"过去 9 个月造了多少"去外推"未来会造出什么"。Replit、PostHog 那句"我们相信你们能跑在我们前面"，就是这笔套利成交的凭证。速度在这里不是工程指标，而是一种可信的未来承诺的抵押品。

而这里藏着一个对所有创始人都成立、却很少被说破的系统性真相——John 自己亲口承认了反直觉的部分：现在比种子期更有压力（"包变得更重了"）。 人们默认随着融资、营收、团队增长，焦虑会下降。恰恰相反。这与前面那个"焦虑转移"主题构成了一个完整的闭环：Campfire 的整个商业模式是把客户的焦虑接管过来、消化掉——而焦虑不会消失，它只是从客户的资产负债表，转移到了创始人的资产负债表上。 John 对种子期自己的唯一忠告"享受旅程"，不是鸡汤，而是一个已经看清这笔交易代价的人，在为账单签字。

这就是为什么他对妻子说"这是我这辈子最后一份工作"——他不是在表达浪漫，他是在确认：这种重量，只能由那个最初选择扛起它的人一直扛下去。

📝 转录文本

Campfire 专访：为 AI 革命打造的 ERP

开场介绍

I'm joined today by John Glascow, CEO and founder of Campfire. Campfire recently raised their series B led by Excel and Ribbit. John, tell me about Campfire.

Well, first, Andrew, thanks for having me on today. Campfire is the AI native ERP for our high growth companies. And we help finance and accounting teams automate manual work for their accounting and their financial reporting. So whether it's taxes or investors — and when we automate it, of course, a lot of it's with AI. Then we're able to allow them to focus on more strategic work.

什么是 ERP？为什么这个市场值得切入

So I guess tell us first — ERP. I don't know if everyone's familiar with that. What is that?

Yeah, enterprise resource planning. Essentially all of the accounting and financial reporting for a company. So the good thing for us is literally everybody has taxes. They have — particularly in tech — a lot of folks have financial reporting requirements to investors, and so the market's wide because everybody essentially has one. Now it's just a matter of which one are you on. And so for us it was not an education. We just had to go out and build something that people wanted.

Yeah. I mean this is to me the classic established market. But then the question is how do you break in? I mean you're going up against NetSuite — how did you decide to do that?

Yeah. And I vividly remember during my YC interview — of course famously it was eight minutes, it went short — and I was saying I want to build a modern ERP, and Dalton was like, literally we've yet to see somebody that's excited about this category because it's so old and ancient and ready for something new, but also credible, because I was in corporate finance for over 10 years. And so yeah, it's a category I've gotten to know well in my career. But to your point, I think it was definitely ripe for disruption.

创始人与市场的契合：为什么是现在？

Let's talk about that. That's the founder-market-fit piece there. How did your prior experiences lead you to the space of ERPs and coming to the belief that now is the time to do a new one?

Yeah. And actually, after we got into YC, that was the biggest question investors had — which was like, why now?
