Host: Firstly — okay, so when you're 14 years old and you get called up to that game and you get given the sympathy over: did you have any idea what you were doing?

Tymal: I knew I bowled and I knew I was left-handed and that was about it. I hit somebody in the first over. I was in trainers, borrowed kit, didn't even have spikes on. But yeah, as I said, I'd messed around in the park, so I knew the fundamentals of cricket. I wasn't chucking it, I was bowling it and stuff like that, but I hadn't played an organised game before. But yeah, just loved it. And just played. Just played for fun.

被Essex发现：火车、补助金与好心人

Host: For a sport that does have such a fixed structure around its selection — you referenced that private school players, massively influential historically — now I think there's signs that it's starting to move in the right direction where far more players do not have to come from that type of background. How did you get picked up by Essex?

Tymal: So I was lucky, and there were a lot of barriers. Word of mouth, really. So again, growing up in Suffolk, which is in cricketing terms known as a minor county. So it doesn't have a professional side in it. You have 18 major counties that do have a professional side. So my closest major county were Essex. So I kind of went through the ranks at Suffolk, playing under 16, 17s. And then, yeah, I essentially got passed on to Essex via my Suffolk coach.

And yeah, I went and played a second team game for Essex when I was 17 years old. Bowled well and got given an academy contract there and then.

But it was, yeah, then I had to do a winter on the academy, but I lived maybe two, two and a half hours from Chelmsford. Grew up in a single parent family. My mum couldn't take me anywhere because I had a younger sister as well at the time. And so I was getting public transport twice a week. I had to leave school early. I was at this point at sixth form. So I had to drop an A-level at school to kind of free up my schedule to get a bus from Mildenhall to Bury St Edmunds, a train to Ipswich, another train to Chelmsford to go to train in the evenings.

But I had a lot of help. I had grants from the local council who helped me pay for train fare and — cricket, as you said it, is an expensive sport, there are a lot of barriers. The Brian Johnston Memorial Trust — I got a grant from them to help with kit and equipment and things like that.

In return, all people wanted from me was that I gave it a good crack. And, you know, I got on those trains and I was on time, and I didn't miss sessions, and I was reliable. And that's one thing I say now — as long as you give it a good, good crack, people will help you and there is help there available. And yeah, I certainly received a lot of that growing up, and looking back now, I wouldn't have progressed or been able to progress without that help.

速度作为唯一武器

Host: One of the fastest bowlers that this country's produced, I think it's probably fair to say, right? You're bowling, you know, 150 km/h plus on a good day. Right, so when you're doing that, I would imagine, as standing out as a young player… that must have been kind of a real "well" moment for people that were either playing against you or watching you play.

Tymal: Yeah, it was all I had, really. I could bowl fast and I wasn't very accurate, I wasn't overly fit, I couldn't bat, and I couldn't field very well. But pretty much in every game I played in, I was the fastest bowler — probably from the age of 14 up to 20, 21 really, at the different levels. And it was my one thing that got me picked.

Looking back, what I was very lucky with is — because normally, as I was very wild, I was very erratic — I've found them on my phone and there's the scorecards of some of my figures from when I was playing as an under-16, under-17, and you'll see my figures and there'll be like 20 wides, 15 no-balls. And normally kids, if you bowl a few wides and a few no-balls, you're not bowling again. The captain or coach will go, "field at fine leg, you won't be seen again."

Whereas I was very lucky that I had captains, even at a young age, and coaches and teams that kept picking me. Because obviously I had this X factor — and when I got it right and when I pitched it on the stumps, I was a good chance of getting a wicket.

So yeah, pace alone pretty much got me from never playing to — I went on an England Lions tour when I was 19, I think. So within five years, all on the back of being able to bowl, being left-arm as well, as opposed to being a right-armer. So there's not as many left-arm bowlers around, and being able to bowl fast. That kind of carried me a long way. And then I just kind of had to pick up everything else around that.

Host: Are you ambidextrous at all?

Tymal: I'm quite ambidextrous. I write left-handed and I bowl left-handed. I don't do many other things left-handed. I throw right-handed, which is quite unique. Yeah, I'm all over the place — but yeah, throwing and bowling left-handed.

板球里的"差异化产品"

Host: Bowling at that speed — you know, you look at differentiators for players in lots of different sports, but in cricket it can be quite hard to stand out when you hit that kind of height. And people just generally talk about you. Have you found that that's helped you in different ways throughout your career, having a kind of marketability that is linked to the game that people naturally are just impressed by?

Tymal: I think with cricket there's three things — especially in short form cricket, but in Test match cricket as well — if you have high pace as a bowler, a batter that can hit sixes, big sixes, or a spinner that is either a mystery spinner or spins the ball really big (Shane Warne, Muralitharan, these type of guys) — if you have one of those three things, you have a reason to be watched and to pull people out of the bar at club cricket, whatever it might be, or turn on the TV.

Even now, I might not be watching a game of cricket, but if I know that Mark Wood's bowling a spell in a Test match, I'll want to watch it. It's exciting for that five overs he's on.

So 100% it helps. And even now, I'm 33, I still know that I need to keep bowling fast and I still need to look after my body to be able to bowl fast. Because in the world of franchise cricket now that I'm in, you need — there's so many bowlers — you need something, a point of difference. So even if I've been around a long time, a team will still want a left-arm fast bowler, at least I hope. And if my pace drops and I go back into the pack a little bit, you're then in the same bucket as a lot of other bowlers. So even though I'm 33 now, it's still the same as when I was 18 — that pace is still really important.

22岁的电击：脊髓压迫与人生分岔

Host: Yeah. So tell us about that focus. I mentioned it at the beginning. You've just touched on it there, being a franchise cricketer. How did this cricketing path get laid out for you? Why is it that short form cricket has become the thing that you've specialized in?

Tymal: Because it had to, yeah.

I played — sorry, I started at Essex on the academy, signed my first professional deal and I played four-day cricket. I played more championship cricket than I did any other cricket. I struggled to get into the Essex T20 team because they had a good team at the time — older players, more senior players ahead of me. But I did actually play a decent amount of four-day cricket, championship cricket.

And then when I was 21 at Essex, out of nowhere, running into bowl, I had a — I hit the crease and I had a massive electrical shock around my waist into my legs and just felt really strange. The gait of my walk was funny. I tried to run, I couldn't really run properly. This was a Bishop's Stortford cricket club; I still remember it very vividly.

Went off, walked off the field mid-over because I just knew I couldn't bowl. Went over to see the physio, explained what happened. He did a few tests — he asked me to do a squat. When I dropped down for a squat, I couldn't get up. My legs physically weren't working. So it was quite scary. And I had pins and needles in my legs and my feet.

By the evening, things kind of subsided and I could do a squat again, for example, but still I didn't feel right. And that period I went for a lot of scans. But in the meantime, I started to feel better. Things kind of got back to normal, got back bowling again, felt okay. And then sporadically it would keep happening.

By this time my contract at Essex was expiring, they weren't sure what to do with me. So I ended up leaving Essex, moving to Sussex who took a punt on me. Spent a winter getting fit, felt in really good space, started the championship season with Sussex — this would be 2015. Same thing happened again towards the end of the day. Bowled a lot in the day, felt really good, ran in, bang — this big electric shock, legs not working properly.

Back to the drawing board in terms of more scans. And essentially, they couldn't quite put their finger on it, but I was under the care of a neurologist who was scared I had multiple sclerosis because of the symptoms that I was showing, or early kind of stages of it — but was cleared of that, thankfully. I had a lumbar puncture done, all these nasty things, when I was 21 at the time, I think.

And yeah, essentially, my spinal cord was being compressed. But it was only happening when I'd bowled a lot. So we looked back and looked at the pattern of when it was happening, and it was always towards the end of the day or if I'd bowled a lot on day one and then bowled again on day two. So what we kind of came up with was that it was only happening playing four-day cricket. So when the load was high — so I'm bowling 15, 18 overs in a day — that irritation of the spinal cord was over and over again, until something just kind of packed in and didn't work anymore.

Host: Were you bowling at those paces?

Tymal: Yeah, I was bowling fast. My pace would drop during the day, but the intensity is still there. And I was still trying my best, but I'm a big guy. I'm not built like most cricketers, I guess. It's just that load through the spine — for whatever reason, my body just wasn't handling.

So yeah, at the age of 22 I was at Sussex and I was presented with my options. Medical grounds to retire — you get an insurance payout, et cetera. We could try again — at that time I was feeling okay again, do we try again? Or do we try, let's just try some T20 cricket because obviously the loads are much smaller. You're only bowling four overs in a game, you're not bowling 15 overs, and then we'll see where we go.

So that was scary. That was not a nice place to be. I think the year before I'd been away with England on an Ashes tour to Australia as a net bowler. I was being spoken about as potentially playing for England in Test match cricket. And then all of a sudden I'm sat in a boardroom around a table like this being told, "it's up to you, you can retire if you want to." So that was pretty scary.

But obviously I decided then the T20 route was what I was going to go down. I was never going to just stop. And touch wood, since that day, those symptoms have never happened again. And I've just played T20 cricket pretty much since 2016 now. So yeah, 10 years.

没有梦想，反而没有失落

Host: It's a really hard thing, isn't it? Because I guess as an athlete, you map out a career in your head and how you think a lot of things will go. And then if it deviates from that, coming around to a different side of it and a new reality can be quite difficult. Did you have kind of a period where you mourned a career that you couldn't have, before actually realizing the one that you could still have?

Tymal: Do you know what? I don't think so. Because I think one thing that's really helped me in good stead throughout my career was that I never played cricket growing up. So I never wanted to be a cricketer because it wasn't an option.

When I was at Essex, for example, in the academy, you could tell — I was in the academy with Ben Foakes, who's gone on to play for England and still playing for Surrey. But a lot of the other lads in the academy, you can tell they'd been doing it since they were in the under-7s. Winter in, winter out, working their way up through the system. By the time they got to the academy, they were fried, really, because they'd just been doing the same thing over and over again. Whereas I was so fresh to it, and everything was so new and exciting and I was learning and progressing quickly.

It was just a kind of a bump in the road really. It was just kind of a detour as opposed to the end of something. So whilst I wanted to play Test match cricket — because I hadn't grown up with dreaming of it, I wasn't mourning a loss, as you say, because it wasn't something that was ever really a dream. It was something that felt like I was on the course to. But I quickly pivoted onto something else: "okay, I can't play Test match cricket for England. Maybe I can play T20 cricket for England. There's still an avenue there."

2017 IPL拍卖：从7000英镑到150万英镑

Host: And there's no better time, probably, for a cricketer. I mean, if you'd done this 20, 25 years ago — massively different. You'd have been done. You'd have retired because there was no other living there. But now, cricketers literally — if anything, people are worried that they will actually start choosing to do this rather than play long-form cricket.

So if you're looking at that — you come out of it, so that would have been 2016. 2017, you're the second most expensive international player in the IPL auction for about one and a half million pounds to RCB.

How does that happen?

Tymal: So I guess maybe to rewind, I'd obviously stopped playing four-day cricket the year before. Credit to the ECB, they looked after me really well. So the winter of 2016, I pretty much spent either at Loughborough or in South Africa with the ECB. They pretty much just gave me a whole winter. A guy called Raph Brandon and Pete Atkinson, who now both work in rugby, I believe — pretty much looked at my body, how I move, any way to take the load off my back, changing how I walk, how I run.

We're at the airport and Pete's coming up to me saying, "I've been watching you walking around the airport. You need to stop moving your hips as much" — trying to retrain all these little things. And I had a brilliant winter, I went away a few times to South Africa, just to get some warm weather training.

So by the time the 2016 season came around, I'd been training for like eight months, feeling in a really good place. And again, my career has been a series of perfect storms, I think.

Our first Blast game of the summer got rained off for Sussex. But then the second one was a televised game on Sky against Somerset, who had Chris Gayle playing for them. It was a cold night at Hove. He just got off the plane from Jamaica. He had about eight jumpers on. He didn't fancy it. But by that point, I'd been building up to this game for a good eight months. And it was my first game of the season. I bowled fast and I got Chris Gayle out on Sky.

Nick Knight did a brilliant bit of commentary on it. And the difference with the T20 Blast is not every game is televised. So we have a live stream and then the odd game is televised. And luckily for me, that game was televised on Sky and that wicket then kind of went around the world and went viral — even back in 2016, when things probably didn't go as viral as what they do now. But that really kind of got my face and my name out there around the world.

Because they had the speed gun — I think the ball was maybe 93 and a half miles an hour, and Chris Gayle's stumps going. And it was a big thing and it was amazing. And people still ask me about it now.

Host: This is the height of Chris Gayle spanking 90% of bowlers around.

Tymal: Yeah, he was. As I said, that particular night he wasn't, because as I said it was a cold night in Hove, he didn't fancy it. At that point, he is Chris Gayle, the universe boss, as he calls himself.

And then that really kind of projected my name and my face out there and what I could do. And then I made my England debut maybe six weeks later. And then that winter, I went on my franchise journey that culminated in the IPL and that IPL mega auction or mini auction.

拍卖会的机制：经纪人、底价与世界级博弈

Host: How does it work with the auction? Always interested in that. Do you put yourself forward for it? How do players get into the auction in the first place?

Tymal: Your agent does it all. By that point, I'd had an agent since I was 18, 19, and he's still my agent today. He's been my agent my whole career. I actually found out I was his first ever client, which is amazing, and he's now one of the biggest cricket agents in the world.

So I got through the English summer all good. Back was going okay, and then we kind of decided — this was back in 2016. Franchise cricket is obviously happening. There's not as many tournaments as there are now, but there's still loads of tournaments — but it was still a little bit unknown. So we just decided, okay, I want to try and play as much cricket as I can. Money wasn't even a conversation.

So I got picked up. I left England in October. I went to Bangladesh to play in the Bangladesh Premier League on a low contract out there, maybe $30,000 US. Played in the BPL, flew to New Zealand from Bangladesh, to play in the Super Smash, it's called, for the Auckland Aces. Again, I think in that tournament I maybe got paid £7,000. That tournament doesn't [pay much], but it was just good to keep playing. I had a brilliant time — if you've ever been to New Zealand, it's an amazing country.

So I spent five, six weeks in New Zealand. I then picked up a short-term contract in the Big Bash because there was an injury in the Big Bash and I was in New Zealand, so I could literally hop over, play two games for the Brisbane Heat. In January that would have been.

And then England had a series in India, a T20 series. So then I flew from Australia to India in January to play three games against India. And that was my first time in India. Hadn't ever been, hadn't experienced cricket in India. And yeah — amazing. Insane. Incredible.

And we had three T20s in India, and I bowled really well. Bowled fast, took wickets. I had a good slow ball. Those three games I played then put me right in the shop window for the IPL auction that was coming up after the series.

So your agent enters you into the auctions. You decide your price, your base price — basically essentially what's the minimum value you're willing to go for. And then you either get picked up via a draft system or an auction system.

By that point, I'd already been picked in the Pakistan Super League. So I flew from India to Dubai, where that was being held. So I hadn't been home for about four months at this point. So I've gone to Dubai, started the PSL — and then the IPL auction was happening early in February 2017. And yeah, it kind of changed things forever.

Host: What's the minimum price you put down at that auction?

Tymal: I think it was the lowest you can put it at, which was, I think, $50,000 — or lakh, 50 lakh.

Host: And you went for 1.5 million?

Tymal: Yeah, around that. It was 12 crore at the time, yeah, exchange rates.

在迪拜酒店里看着自己的脸出现在屏幕上

Host: When you actually hear players going for that money — is that the value of the contract? That all goes to the player?

Tymal: Pre-tax, yeah.

Host: Wow.

Tymal: Yeah, so again, I watched that on TV in my room in Dubai. So basically, my agent texted me the night before a list of the teams — "okay, this team are interested, this team have already got a bowler, they don't need…" So I knew that there were a few teams interested. We were confident I was going to go. He didn't put a figure on it. I spoke to him afterwards — he knew I had a lot of interest, but he didn't expect it to go as high as what it did.

But I woke up early and I watched it. I had my best mate in England. He couldn't watch it in the UK, so he's FaceTiming me in Dubai whilst I watch my face pop up on screen. And yeah, the number just keeps climbing, climbing, climbing, climbing. At which point you're like, "oh, I don't know how much it is because it's in lakh and then crore, and I don't know what it means, but I know…"

So Ben Stokes also went for a lot of money. He was the most expensive. He went maybe half an hour before me, and I'd seen on Twitter or whatever. So I knew I was close to his number, which was, I think, maybe he went 1.6, 1.7 that year. So I'm like — well, I was numb. You know, you kind of just…

Host: Fresh off the back of 7K from New Zealand.

Tymal: Literally. And at that point, I had no money. We grew up with no money as a family, and I had no savings. I didn't own a property. I was just enjoying life and kind of just bumbling through.

IPL "水货"标签与残酷文化

Host: But that is an incredible part of what the IPL and then franchise cricket has afforded cricketers, right? Because previously, cricketers just didn't have the same opportunities to make money that we see in other sports. And now, if you have a good year, you can really set yourself up — especially the opportunity it gives guys from poorer countries.

Tymal: Yeah. So basically the money — I got injured during the tournament, so you lose some money there. And it's pre-tax. You get taxed in the country you're in, you get taxed back in the UK, you pay your agent and things like that on top of that.

But yeah, the actual IPL experience itself didn't quite go as well as what I would have liked it to. So I pulled my hamstring after about four games, five games. I got back fit for the end of the tournament, but wasn't quite able to capitalize on it. And actually it damaged me reputationally, even — labelled kind of a "bust", because you go for so much money, the expectation is so high. And the games I played, I did okay. I didn't do great, but I didn't do terribly. I didn't, in my opinion, bottom out and just get whacked around everywhere. I did okay. It's just that I got injured, and I certainly didn't repay the investment that RCB put in me for that year, and the fans.

And even now, the IPLs — I'll get tagged in the "biggest bust 11 of the IPL" and things like that. Every year when the IPL's [on], without exception. Honestly. Brutal. If you talked to my name on Twitter now, you'd see just countless people saying, "I remember Tymal Mills and how terrible he was" and things like that.

Host: But it's a brutal kind of cricketing culture out there, isn't it?

Tymal: It is. I mean, it's literally — I was speaking to someone the other day who's also played in it and it was like, one minute you'd be Orange Cap, top run scorer; next minute, a couple of bad innings, "get him out of the team." Literally — it's cutthroat. And that's franchise cricket, and it's hard, and dealing with the uncertainty and the lack of security. It is ruthless. But it is what it is. You've got to learn to just roll with it and keep going.

与Kohli、ABD、Gayle同处更衣室

Host: But as an experience, you must have had a hell of a time bowling — because I was having a look at the team that you came into. You had Virat Kohli, you had AB de Villiers, you had Chris Gayle, you had Shane Watson, you had Travis Head…

Tymal: We finished bottom of the IPL that year.

Host: How?

Tymal: I don't know. I don't know. We somehow — well, probably my fault. That's why. Too much money. I've done two years of the IPL, one with RCB and one with Mumbai Indians, and finished bottom both years. So I must be a bad luck charm.

Host: That must have been amazing, though — again, coming from what, in the space of just a few years, you go and then start rocking up on the pitch with players like that.

Tymal: Yeah, I think it was cool. But again, I think my naivety and my lack of cricketing kind of aspirations really benefited me. Because I didn't go over there and was like, "oh my God, it's Virat Kohli and whoever." Because again, I hadn't grown up idolizing cricketers, and I was very naive. And looking back, I was only 24 at the time. So that definitely helped me, because I think the pressure of going to the IPL for a lot of money can engulf a lot of people, and that pressure is real. But I was just — again, it was just kind of another rolling stone, I guess, of my career, just kind of going down this hill and just enjoying it.

But still disappointed not being able to [perform], but I didn't realize that at the time. I thought it'd come again. And then all of a sudden — okay, you go in the auction next year: unsold. Nobody wants you. And the year after: unsold. And the year after: unsold. So you look back on it now…

But in between, I've been able to do some really cool things.

一年合约与"被锁定的低薪期权"

Host: So they're one-year contracts?

Tymal: They are. Essentially, in American sports terms, there's a team option. The team have the right to retain you. And it's done in three-year cycles. So the year that RCB bought me was the last of a three-year cycle. So at the end of that IPL — or the start of the next one — each team is normally only allowed to retain maybe four or five players, and then they reset it all.

But if you get bought in the first year of a cycle, you can get retained for up to three seasons, but it's on the same money that you were bought for originally. So it can work really well. If you get bought for big money in year one of an auction and do well, you'll get retained on that for hopefully a couple of years. But if you get bought for a really low contract in year one in the auction and do really well and outperform your contract, you're then stuck — because the team will retain you because you're cheap, and you're on the same salary. Whereas if you went back in the auction, you'd go for way, way, way more.

So yeah, timing is massive. And that's why you've started to see players pull out of the IPL auction or set their base price really high — because they know they don't maybe want to get stuck, if you want to put it like that, on a salary that they're not happy with.

短期组队的艺术：Mickey Arthur 的智慧

Host: How was the gelling of that? I mean, I mentioned all those players and then you said you came last — and the talent in there is absolutely insane. When you're putting all these players together, you know, you use a football analogy of "you can't just spend a billion quid and win trophies." And I'd imagine it's the same when you're putting dressing rooms together in cricket.

Tymal: Yeah, it's hard. So franchise cricket is — it's not like no other kind of vehicle in sport, really. I could play for eight different teams in a year, in a calendar year.

Host: Promiscuous.

Tymal: Yeah, literally. You fly in, you maybe get together three days, a week at most, before your first game, you play for three to five weeks and you leave. But if you do it enough, you learn, you come across people you've played with before or you've played against. So it does become a bit incestuous really. So you know guys.

But that is one of the challenges. One of the hardest things to do in franchise cricket is to create a team within that short period of time. Mickey Arthur — he's now Derbyshire coach — he was one of the best at that. I played for his team. He was the Pakistan head coach in the PSL and he was the head coach of the Karachi Kings in the tournament. So he had a vested interest in Pakistan cricket because he was the head coach at the time. So I was one of the overseas players for the Karachi Kings.

And I remember he brought all the overseas players together before the start of the tournament and said, "Guys, look, glad to have you all. I know you're going to give it a good crack. What will be will be. But for me, one thing I want you to do is try and help some of the younger Pakistani players in the squad. Take a bowler, take a batter — for the four weeks you're here, take him under your wing. Try and help them in training. Because for you, you'll go off and do your next thing. But for these guys, this is their big moment. And then they'll go back into Pakistani domestic cricket and hopefully take what they've learned into that."

And that's a little thing there — because franchise cricket can be incredibly selfish. You're there, you've got, most of the time, no loyalty to the team. They've got no loyalty to you outside of this four-week block. And then it's onto the next thing. You want to try and secure a contract in the next tournament or for the next season of the same tournament, hopefully with the same team. You want continuity, really.

I've been lucky, I've had a little bit of it — I've had a lot of it in the UK and a little bit overseas. But if you can go back to the same thing, it's nice. You don't want to be starting again, over and over again. Unfortunately, you get good at it. But the nature of the beast is that these are short-term deals. So if you can find something as a coach or the technical directors — a way of bringing these players together in a short period of time — those teams generally tend to do better on the pitch from my experience.

球队"联邦化"：Trent Boult 模式

Host: To me, it looks like to combat some of that, there's a move between syndication of teams now. You see the expansion of IPL teams out into South Africa and the West Indies and the UAE, and now we have the investment into The Hundred. And I wouldn't be surprised if in not too long, everything aligns so that you have the "ex-Super Kings", as we've seen in some of these tournaments. People have kind of talked about that in a relatively negative way quite often in a cricketing sense.

Tymal: Well, it sounds a bit — maybe it helps. It helps the players 100%. Because if you can have — so Trent Boult, for example. He's employed by Mumbai Indians to play for their different franchises around the world.

It's hard to line those up as the franchise — it's hard because they're all individual competitions with their own drafts, their own auctions, their own signing systems. It is hard for a team to own you 12 months of the year, really. But you can get close.

For him — I don't know him really well, but I know him a little bit — Mumbai Indians will send an S&C and a physio and a coach to New Zealand to help him train for three weeks ahead of a tournament. Or they might fly him out somewhere to help him train and prepare to get ready for a tournament. If they invest in you — because your investment really, because they'll be looking at the IPL or the MLC or whatever is coming up — they'll think, "okay, we need Player X to be ready. What does he need?" And these people have a lot of money and a lot of resources in cricket.

So if you can get that relationship and you can build that, and if you can repay that on the pitch, that's the ultimate thing. If you do well, you'll get it back in return. It's hard. There isn't a lot of it, and there is a lot of pressure on these short tournaments to do well, because your rope does run out eventually.

IPL的氛围与板球的部落主义

Host: The IPL — I mean, it's transformed cricket in many ways. The thing that you watch every single time. And I went out to India for the 2023 One Day World Cup. And firstly, it's just incredible. But it's incredible because of the fandom. And in cricket, you don't see that type of tribalism, that type of fandom every day — it's associated with other sports. It's so important that the sport actually has that.

Do you feel it from the players?

Tymal: I think so. I think it depends on the individual. Some players, you get numb to it and you just play, and it doesn't matter if you're playing in Mumbai or Trinidad or Lord's, it all kind of becomes the same. For T20 cricket anyway, it does roll into one a little bit sometimes, and you've got to find that intrinsic motivation. What motivates you? Is it the atmosphere? Do you actually feed off that? Is it money? Is it acclaim? Everyone will be motivated by different things.

But Test cricket still does have a massive place for a lot of players. Most players still — if they got the opportunity to play Test cricket, they would take it. They wouldn't turn it down. That still certainly does live true, especially in the bigger countries that pay well as well.

Host: We had Pat Cummins on a couple of weeks ago. He said the players literally this year were turning down half a million quid to play the Test match series in Bangladesh.

Tymal: Yeah, but those guys will still be getting paid very well by Cricket Australia, the ECB, and the BCCI. It's the countries like the West Indies, those Caribbean players, where the discrepancy is just so huge between what they would earn to play for the West Indies versus what they can earn to play franchise cricket. That's where the huge disconnect is with cricket at the moment, which is forcing players to make unpopular decisions. Well, for the most part, anyway.

在巴基斯坦打球：总统级安保与久违的板球饥渴

Host: What was it like going to Pakistan and playing? Was it cool?

Tymal: It was. I was one of the first batches of players that went when they had cricket reinstated. So they lost cricket for a long time when there was a terrorist attack on the Sri Lankan cricket team. So there wasn't any international or domestic cricket played in Pakistan. So they had their T20 tournament held in Dubai for quite a long time. And then slowly they started to phase it in. So one year, the final was held in Pakistan. Then the year after, the knockout stages were held in Pakistan.

And yeah, we were nervous because you'd seen things, you'd heard things, and a lot of us were still quite young. But the security was huge. It was present. We got presidential-level security: streets, roads closed, helicopters, army on the roofs of buildings. And even now the security level is so high in Pakistan.

But the one thing that stuck with me was — I've been to Pakistan now maybe five, six times. Especially in those early years, they were so grateful for the foreign [players], because that helped them bring international cricket back, and eventually Champions Trophies played there. So they can now essentially be uninhibited in terms of what they can host. But they're so grateful — the amount of messages, even from the players, some big-name players, really appreciative of the foreign guys for coming and trusting the government and the system and everything.

And as I said, I've been there many times now and never had a single problem. I was there during COVID, when COVID started. So that was — but that's not a security threat. That was just more, "okay, we need to probably go home now because the world seems to be ending."

But Pakistan is a cool country and they love cricket and they were starved of it for so long. They were so hungry for it when it came back, and the appetite for it there is huge.

最爱的赛季：南非SA20

Host: Do you have a favorite country to play these tournaments?

Tymal: Yeah, I think the SA20.

Host: Really?

Tymal: So, I played SA20 for the first time this year for Pretoria Capitals, and it was amazing. It was the most fun I've had playing cricket in my career.

Host: Why?

Tymal: South Africa in December, January, is just an amazing time of year, being in an amazing country. For whatever reason, they've got really good buy-in from the public. South Africa haven't had a major tournament until the SA20 came about. And yes, it's all IPL-owned — all six teams are subsidiaries of IPL franchises. But they've just adopted it. Every game was sold out, essentially. The atmosphere, you got the bands. It was really authentic. They're cricket grounds as well. It's not like you go to Australia and they're multi-use grounds. Every ground is unique. They're proper cricket stadiums. And it just works.

So I've watched it on TV and heard from other guys. Everyone says, yeah, the SA20 is awesome. But this year I actually got the opportunity to go and play. And honestly, it was amazing. And the cricket was a really good standard. It was hard. It was tough cricket. But the weather's amazing. The food's amazing in South Africa. You get to travel around the country, see all the different parts of it.

So yeah, at the moment the SA20 is four weeks long, five weeks long. The culture — South Africa's just a cool place. I don't know if you've been, it's a cool place. To play cricket. It's just a cool place to be. And especially at that time of year, I think right now it's number one for me. It's amazing.

圣诞节的家庭交易：从迪拜飞开普敦

Host: That was quite a late opportunity for you, wasn't it? When you were already in the Middle East.

Tymal: Yeah, so I was playing — well, last winter, 2025. I didn't have a great English summer last summer. Had a few months off at the end of the summer just to get the body right, see where I'm at, work on a couple of things. And then I played the Abu Dhabi T10, which is a tournament that happens every year. So 10-over tournament, bowled really well there, felt really good. My team lost in the final there, and I had a contract for the ILT20, which is in Dubai. So I went straight from Abu Dhabi over to Dubai.

But because my team made the final in Abu Dhabi, I literally arrived at my team the day before the first game in Dubai — which meant I didn't get picked for the first game because everybody else had been there for a week and they'd had practice matches, whereas I arrived late. So I didn't get picked for the first game, which was fine. I didn't get picked for the second game. Didn't get picked for the third game. I was kind of struggling — "I'm not getting a game here."

By this time, my family had flown over for Christmas, so we had Christmas planned in Dubai. The ILT20 was running literally over Christmas. So my wife flew out with our two kids, but I wasn't playing, so essentially I'm not having a holiday because I was trying to go into training. I was 12th man for the games and that was it. I just wasn't getting a game.

In the meantime, my agent calls me and says, "Oh, Pretoria Capitals have had some injuries. They want to know if you can go and play in the SA20." I'm like, "Yeah, of course. I'd love to. They tell me I'm going to play there. I'm not playing here." But they said that they only want you if they need you there for the first game of the season. They don't want you to come [later]. So normally there was an overlap of the tournaments. The ILT20 would finish in the new year. SA20 started on Boxing Day. So there was a little bit of an overlap, but they said they wanted me for the first game.

So I had to, A, ask for my release from the Dubai contract — which they were happy to do because they could save half of my salary to let me go. I wasn't playing. No hard feelings, just one of those things. They had other bowlers, team was doing okay.

But then I had to send my family home back to the UK. My wife flew home with the kids on the 23rd of December, I think it was. And I had to fly to South Africa on Christmas Eve. So all of a sudden we went from having Christmas planned in Dubai — presents I'd bought in Dubai for the kids — to all of a sudden telling my wife, "sorry, I've got a contract now in South Africa where I'm going to play. The money was good. You're going to have to go home now and spend Christmas back in the UK with your parents." That was a tough decision.

And those situations crop up within cricket and franchise cricket — the short-term nature of things. In September, for example, my agent calls me on a Monday afternoon. "St. Lucia Kings have had an injury in the CPL. Can you fly out there for the knockouts?" I'm like, "Yeah, okay. When do they need me?" "Tomorrow morning." My wife's at work in London, we haven't got childcare sorted, we've got two young kids. So again you're trying to scramble around — okay, my wife now has to cancel her meetings later in the week because she has to be at home because I'm all of a sudden now in Guyana.

These things crop up in franchise cricket a lot, and it puts strain on your family life as well. But hopefully it's one of those things that, looking back, when we're old and grey, we'll look back and be happy that we took these opportunities when they came. But in the moment, it's tough.

体育的隐藏代价与"自私的运动员"

Host: It's a hidden side of professional sport, isn't it? Everything we see as fans and you enjoy watching on TV — you take that for granted. Or not even take it for granted, you don't even think about it.

Tymal: It is great. I can't sit here and ask people to cry for me, but as I say, it has an adverse effect on your family. Then I went to South Africa. We made the final of the SA20, which extends your trip. So I was in South Africa for five weeks. So that's five weeks extra that my wife's on her own at home. We don't have grandparents that live near us. So our childcare is a bit tricky at times. And my wife's working full-time and she's trying to juggle all that. In the meantime, I'm in Cape Town having an absolute, you know, having a belter, and it's January in England.

We had one time we were doing a loft conversion in the house. The whole roof's off, the house is freezing, it's December. And my wife, bless her, she's doing her best, but she's not happy with me. Because in the same time, I'm in Barbados, literally on the beach. The Instagram's just pulled up — posting on social media: "great evening, sat by the beach with the boys." And she's in the depths of winter in the UK with no roof on the house. Two young children, trying to entertain and keep warm.

Host: Did that take time? You know, from a relationship perspective — how long have you been with the wife?

Tymal: Married six years, together nine years maybe, yeah. So I met her in Dubai playing in that PSL where I got bought in the IPL auction. Yeah, so she was living and working in Dubai at the time. So she works in foreign currency then in Dubai, now in the UK. And yeah, I met her then.

Host: I'd imagine it's such a key part of you being able to do what you do, having that kind of partnership away from the game that allows you to actually do this — because it's a massive sacrifice on her side if she's got a career and a job as well, being at the front end of childcare when you are off to the Caribbean for four weeks on a jolly.

Tymal: Literally. It's not easy and it's hard, and it puts a strain on you at times. But she's amazing, and she puts up with a lot. And I'm sure you've spoken to so many sports people, and I'm sure every one of them says, "we're selfish and we have to be, and we can always be more selfish." I could always do more if I really went 100% in on sport and recovery and everything else. I could literally never see my family if that's how I wanted to be. But yeah, we're selfish, but you justify it because it's a short career. You think, okay, I can pay now and get the rewards later. And also we love what we do. Like, I love playing cricket.

And I know when I retire, I won't be going to Barbados and Cape Town and Australia and getting the opportunity to do these amazing things. Well, hopefully I will — but chances are…

Host: You'll just have to take them with you.

Tymal: Exactly. And you've got to pay for a full holiday for everybody.

女儿中风：流动人生的脆弱断点

But yeah, I'm very lucky to have a wife who's supported me through that time. She met me, I guess, at the height of it. She knew what I was doing and stuff. It didn't come out of the blue, I guess. But as soon as you chuck kids into it…

And yeah, our daughter got sick. She had a stroke a couple of years ago. We were at the airport about to fly to Australia to play in the Big Bash. We were all going as a family to Perth, and she collapsed. And obviously I had to cancel that contract and we were in hospital for a few weeks. And that puts a whole other strain on things. You've got health [issues] — now, am I going away and leaving a child who, luckily she's recovered brilliantly, and she's amazing? But at the time you don't know what the future is going to look like. And that's more of a strain on, "okay, can I leave, or should I now be at home, and how do you juggle these things?"

So yeah, we've been through a lot, but we'll look back on this chapter of our lives as a really fun time and be better off for it.

Sussex合约：流动人生里的"国债"

Host: And the realities of that, I guess — it's a pressure on also the fragility of the contracts that you're working with and everything from a financial perspective.

Tymal: I've been lucky in terms of — I've had security in the UK in terms of my Sussex contract. And then now that we have The Hundred — even though The Hundred is a one-year contract — so I've done well in The Hundred and I've always had a good contract in The Hundred. So from April through to August, I know where I'm going to be. I know who I'm playing for. I'm at home and I'm playing cricket.

And then in the winter, that's the Wild West, I guess. Who knows what's going to happen. Hopefully I can get a repeat contract somewhere, but probably it's just going to be a case of seeing what I can get. And hopefully I get a contract with enough notice that my wife knows where I'm going to be — but it might be a case of, "can you fly tomorrow and go and play?"

But my county contract with Sussex has been pretty much the foundation of everything I've been able to achieve. Because it gives you just a security. It gives me a base. Financially, it's one of my lower contracts — or probably is my lowest contract that I'll earn throughout the year — but you get 12 months of the year coverage. So if I get injured in South Africa in January, I'm coming home to Sussex and I'll get rehabbed there. And I've got a gym and a physio and the support and everything I need, which as a player has been invaluable.

And if I need to go training, like before here — we're about to start the season for Sussex — but for example, last September, before I'm about to go off and play franchise cricket, I'm in at Sussex every day, in the gym, in the indoor school, in the nets, just practicing, training. I have access to the ground. So I'm lucky — a lot of players don't have that. If they go full franchise, full freelance, they don't necessarily have that. And it's 100% on them. Whereas what Sussex have given me has been paramount to everything that I've been able to achieve in cricket.

Host: And amongst all of the movement around the world, when you have that stability — what are the obligations from you to Sussex for that contract?

Tymal: So I have to get, for every contract that I apply for, I have to get an NOC — a no objection certificate — from Sussex, essentially, from your home board. One from the ECB and one from your county who you're employed by. So if I get picked up in the SA20, I need an NOC from Sussex to say "yeah, no problem." So they have to sign that.

And then now — they've only actually introduced it a couple of years ago — we have to take insurance out against our county contracts. So if I was to get injured playing in the SA20 and missed games for Sussex, Sussex wouldn't lose out financially on that. So they'd be able to claim on the insurance against my contract. You have to take insurance personally — we have to pay that.

And to be honest, it's PCA — the Personal Cricket Association — handle all that, and it's a pretty good deal. It's not hurting you too much, really, financially. And it makes sense. Fair play. We can go and earn whatever we want in the winter, but you have to give that security to your county. That's a trade-off right there.

Host: They're giving you the solidity. But also for you, as someone who is a specialist, to have that with a county where so much of county cricket has been in four-day games — that's great loyalty.

Tymal: Yeah, and as I said, I'm into my 12th year now with Sussex. I had a testimonial year last year. And the biggest message I had to give was: "I'm not retiring." Like, it was just — okay, they've given me a testimonial year, it's great, but every event I'm at: "okay, I'm still playing. I've still got two years left of my contract. I feel great."

But yeah, the club just have given me an awful lot, and I like to think I give back as well — with the foundation at Sussex and doing bits around. Because we live in Hove as well — I live five minutes from the ground, so I'm always around, I'm always available to do bits. Like tomorrow, I'm filming something for one of the club sponsors and those little things — they use me as and when they need.

英格兰国家队生涯：16场国际赛与两次世界杯

Host: Can we talk about England? We've moved all around the franchises, and international cricket is a really important part of that discussion because congested calendars and trying to align everything seems like one of the hardest jobs to do. You've also had a significant England career. You've been part of World Cup winning squads in probably the most successful short-form era of cricket that England's seen. But at the same time, when I was looking at the stats, I was like, "he's only played 16 games." But in my head, you played way more than that.

Tymal: Yeah, I don't really see myself as an England cricketer, if I'm being honest. So I've barely played.

Basically, I got brought in for two World Cups, but aside from that, haven't really played an awful lot.

I made my debut, as I mentioned earlier. Basically, when I made my debut, there wasn't an awful lot of international T20 cricket played. It was still of the era of 50-over cricket. So if I remember correctly, the summer I made my England debut, there was a one-off T20 and five ODIs. So I played the T20 and then I wasn't playing 50-over cricket. Again, then I think later in the summer they played Pakistan — I think, again, a one-off T20 and maybe three ODIs. So I'd kind of dip in for a squad, play a game and then be gone again.

And then to be fair, during my mid-twenties, I had a lot of injuries. I was injured quite a lot and not selected. So that was no real kind of gripe there.

And then the first year of The Hundred came about and I bowled really well. I was 28 maybe then, and I was one of the best bowlers in the competition. Jofra Archer got injured that year, unfortunately, so there was a space in the England team ahead of the World Cup, and I got picked for the World Cup. So that was a really nice moment, because that was about five years apart from my last England appearance to the next one.

And it was a World Cup in the UAE, it was just after COVID, and it was amazing. So I felt like an England player then. I played the first game against the West Indies in Dubai, played against Australia, was a leading wicket-taker for us. And then I pulled my quad in the last group stage game, I think it was against Sri Lanka, and had to sit and watch when we lost in the semifinal to New Zealand in Abu Dhabi. And it was terrible. It was only a minor injury, but I was out for a few weeks and that was that.

In the meantime, in the subsequent years, I had some other really good summers for England — particularly in The Hundred, performed really well — and then didn't get picked for England on the back of it. Kind of messaging was always around, "okay, we're going to have a look at other guys, younger guys, we know what you can do," that type of thing — which I personally pushed back on, because that doesn't help me at all. I want to play, I haven't played a lot.

And then I got picked for another World Cup in Australia that we won, but I didn't play. I was 12th man for the whole tournament. Myself and David Willey didn't play a game. So whilst you can say you're a World Cup winner, you're not really. In my eyes, you weren't on the pitch.

Host: No kid dreams about being on the bench for a World Cup final, do you?

Tymal: You dream about scoring the winning goal or taking the winning catch, bowling the winning wicket, whatever it might be. But you really don't consider yourself a World Cup winner because — you're a part of the squad, and the squad wins a tournament. It does, yeah. But as a selfish sports person, you want to — still, for that month, it was an amazing month or however long it was in Australia, an amazing country, the World Cup. I loved it, it was great. But yeah, you just — it's not the same.

It's not the same when you're not — especially when you play a single [game]. It's not like I played one of the group stage games. It's not like in football, where you can even say, "okay, I came off the bench three times." I literally just ran drinks for five weeks and trained. That was really my contribution to the squad. But I was ready if the opportunity would have come.

I was still proud to have been part of the squad. I'm not saying I didn't want to be there, but you'd rather have been there in that 11. It hits a little bit differently.

And then since then, I haven't featured for England.

Host: Eoin Morgan said he'd only consider you in a World Cup year, right?

Tymal: Yeah, again, it's frustrating. That was when I was a bit — yes, that was during the time where I was injured quite a lot. And for right or for wrong, I back myself. Even now, I feel like I'm still good enough to play for England. I don't know whether — I haven't had any contact with England for a few years now. But honestly, I'm okay with it. I just want to play as much cricket as I can, stay healthy, play at a high level, perform well.

Host: But you'd want to — you keep yourself available?

Tymal: Yeah, I haven't retired. I'm available if anybody wanted to pick me. But also, if that ship sailed, then that's fine. You can only control what you can control. But I still feel really good. My pace feels good. I'm confident. Like, look, we've got another busy summer coming up now — the Blast about to start, straight into The Hundred. That's all I'm focusing on right now, and what will be will be after that.

板球能承受多少板球？三种格式的角力

Host: We heard so much about the demands on players, and you mentioned previously about how many months of the year you're away. Beforehand when we were downstairs talking, we were talking about just the amount of cricket that some of these guys are having to play now. Do you think something has to give? Are we sustainable in what we're really asking for? And what I really want the response on — if sport (and you're a big NFL fan, right) — if sport is about the quality of a product, having more and more of it may be great for some means because you can sell more TV rights and you get more fans in the stadium. But if the product suffers and the cricket isn't as good because the players can't perform at the highest level, eventually that's going to have a negative impact on the whole cricket product.

Tymal: Yeah, I think the problem cricket has is that there's three formats of cricket. It's not just one game. There's T20 cricket, 50-over cricket and Test match cricket. So there's essentially three different sports within a sport. So naturally, you're going to saturate it, aren't you? Because it's not just one product. So that's the main issue, I think.

And players — there's so much choice now. But as I said, within most of the players you speak to in England, if you offer them a Test cap, they'd bite your hand off. And that still holds a lot of value, without a doubt. When I was younger, I wanted to play Test cricket for England. My decision was made for me due to injury. Whether I would have achieved it or not, who knows. But most players still want to play Test match cricket.

But the challenge is now that you can earn a lot of money not doing so and going off and doing T20s. 50-over cricket is obviously the format that's probably the most under pressure. The format players enjoy the least. There's less reward there. There's no domestic 50-over cricket really being played at all. Certainly none that pays as well as T20 cricket.

Host: It does just feel — I'm again a huge fan. Now I watch — I used to love it — and now I watch it and the first 30 overs, the way we're playing our Test match cricket, we're sometimes scoring quicker in Test matches than we are in the first 30 overs of ODIs. And then the last 20, I'm just watching a T20 game. So you kind of look at it and go, it's just really now become a combination of both of those.

Tymal: Yeah. And as you said, because now in England, obviously, the schedule is the most talked about thing in English cricket — and The Hundred taking primetime. And now no 50-over cricket is played at the highest level because it's played at the same time as The Hundred. So it's been neglected. And it just had to [give], because you can't fit all the cricket into the English summer. The English summer isn't long enough that you can fit, with enough space, a good product. So 50-over cricket was what had to kind of get pushed by the wayside. And unfortunately, we can't fit everything, and you can't do something that just fits everything and pleases everybody. And I think rightly they've made the decision to prioritize short-form cricket and Test match cricket.

灰飞烟灭的Ashes与新一代英格兰投手

Host: Observer's, fan's hat on. Pretty depressing winter for England cricket fans. There's been lots of talk of change. Now continuity's been confirmed. We look at different types of players coming in. The bowling department's obviously one that's got a significant impact shift and kind of change coming to it. You have Broad and Anderson holding down so much of that for so many years. It's inevitable, right, sport, when it happens. What do you think about the kind of depth and the quality of the bowlers that we have at the moment to try and fill some of that excitement within the Test squad?

Tymal: I think the irony is, I think Jimmy Anderson's the leading wicket-taker in the County Championship this year. Still bowling all the overs and taking loads of wickets for Lancashire.

But yeah, going back to the winter, I think with the Ashes — it was a shame because this Ashes was really built up to be: "okay, England have got a really good chance here. And if England are at full throttle here, they should win the Ashes." And I think the thing that probably I found, straight out — so I was in the UAE for most of it, and then South Africa — because the games were so short, there wasn't anything to watch. It got built up to this massive occasion. And then the first Test was over in two days. And then there was two weeks or whatever it was until the next Test. It loses momentum. And then the next Test doesn't go very long. And then another two-and-a-half-day Test. So it just felt really disjointed, which was a shame because I thought England were going to go there and do really well. I think everybody did. And it wasn't misplaced judgment at the time, I don't think.

But looking to where they're at now — yeah, it's a tough… It's always tough because the English Test summer now comes straight after the IPL. So some of these players are going to come in not ready, in terms of having not played and practiced four-day cricket. The bowlers — Jofra, for example — is going to have a pure diet of T20 cricket. Well, they put him straight into a Test match. They talk about bowling loads and red zones and spikes.

So then you're looking at county cricket and who's performing well. Ollie Robinson, my teammate down at Sussex, he's taking wickets pretty regularly down now at Sussex. He's just had a good game whilst we're recording now.

Host: Wants to come back as well, doesn't he?

Tymal: Yeah, of course he does. He's had a taste of it. He's done well. He just hasn't stayed fit when he has played. So from what I understand, he's been told that the door's open to him. We'll find out if he gets selected ahead of this summer. But he's saying all the right things and doing all the right things. He's captain now for the four-day stuff at Sussex and he's bowling his overs. He's taken wickets. He scored 100 at the Oval last week, so that helps.

But there's a lot of exciting players. Sonny Baker is bowling fast at Hampshire. Josh Tongue, who was probably one of the bowlers to come out of the Ashes with a bit of credit — he's now doing really well. Unfortunately… I said it. So Tongue's probably your one that he's hanging a hat on. He's going to play because he looks [the part] and he's doing well this summer.

But there's spots up for grabs, which as a player is exciting. And the England hierarchy, I guess, are making the right noises that they're going to start looking at county cricket a bit more and taking those performances more seriously now when picking the international side.

200分时代：当击球员把比赛搬到新维度

Host: Style in cricket has changed. I don't want to use "Bazball" because now we know what it means, but the style of our Test match cricket has changed dramatically. The style of even short-form cricket has changed dramatically. I had a stat here that I was looking at, which was ridiculous. I saw a video — it said in, I think it was 2009, there was one score over 200 in the IPL. Last year, there were 52. There are 46 already this year of scores over 200. So you've got England scoring 400 in a day in Test match cricket, you've got scores of 260 being chased in T20 cricket. This kind of shift is incredible on one side from a viewer's perspective. What does it do for a bowler?

Tymal: Yeah, it's hard, because it's about scoring runs now. The mindset you need to try and have with it is that it does create an even bigger opportunity — because as a bowler, if you can be the one bowler to do well in a game where all the bowlers have done poorly, it makes you stand out even more.

And it also — it makes the game, you stop looking at the game from 20 overs and you start looking at it in two-over blocks. Because say my team bats first and we score 250. Really, if you can bowl two good overs — if you can have two overs that go for five in that 20 overs — you're probably going to win. Because the run rate for the rest of those overs has to be so high when it's already high. It has to be astronomical. Does that make sense?

So there's opportunity there. It's just managing — I guess you have to focus on the small details so much. Your margin for error becomes so small, because in this situation, the conditions are clearly so against you. If a team is able to score 240, 260, that clearly the batters are on top here. And then also it won't be like that every game. You will have games where it's your day, or it should be your day — but it's making sure you capitalize on them, because you need that credit in the bank for when you do have a bad day, which is coming.

You need those good memories from your coaches, your captains, the selectors, whoever it might be. Because if you're constantly just going around the park, then you're not going to find yourself in the team.

Host: Do you think it's changed Test match bowling? If I look at the aggressive format that England have played now, do you think that changes in the requirements for bowling in a longer-form game when batters are being far more aggressive?

Tymal: I think it tests your tactics and your relationship with your captain. Travis Head was brilliant with it in Australia. He put the bowlers under pressure and forced you to change — change the field. Because in the Test — I've never played Test match cricket, so it's difficult for me to say — but you want to have four slips or three slips and a gully, a couple of men around the back. That's for most of the day. That's what you want. That's traditional Test match cricket, isn't it? And then, okay, they might hit you for a cover drive through the covers, they might hit you for a pull shot, but okay, we'll live with it.

Whereas now the ball's flying early and suddenly, okay, well, we can't just keep all the men in because then it's going to keep scoring boundaries. So then you have to spread the field. Then it gives them easy singles everywhere. Then you can't build any pressure because they're either able to hit a boundary or just get off strike. Because what a bowler wants to do — you want to bowl dot balls. You want to think, "I want to bowl a good ball, and it's either going to get them out or they're not going to score." That's the fundamentals of bowling, of Test cricket really.

Whereas now you get thrown off your length, or the field has to spread, you bowl your really good ball and they're just getting an easy single, and then all of a sudden they're going at five an over without even having to take a risk. So that's where the successful aggressive batters in the modern game, I think, have challenged the bowlers most.

数据与背景：经济率 vs 平均数

Host: I mean, I was looking at — you played 259 T20s. You got an average of 23.36. That was according to Wiki, so maybe — I mean, give or take. That's outrageously good for T20 cricket, right?

Tymal: Yeah, but you take more Wickis. I think for me, anything under 25, I think, is a good average in T20 cricket. Anything under 20 is excellent. I'm sure Bumrah and these type of guys — I don't have his numbers to hand — but I'm sure he'd be under 20. So that's, yeah, I'm happy with that. You're happy with 20. I'm happy with 20.

Host: It's your economy rate now, though, to be honest. I'd rather have a better economy rate [than a] better average.

Tymal: That's so hot. Yeah, again, as we were talking about with economies now, I mean, surely isn't it a wicket-taking — you'd maybe give 20 runs in an over if you can get Travis Head out.

Yeah, so that's where context is massive. The game, who you're bowling at — so that's where stats can be tough. Because you can look at a bowler — could bowl four overs and take one for 50, which is a bad day by anybody's metric. But if that one wicket was the batter that was on 100 or 90 at the time and he still had four overs left, that one wicket is really valuable. And even though you've had a bad day there — or even if you're bowling second, you've had a bad day, you've bowled three overs for 40, but you've got to defend 11 off the last over. If that last over goes for 10 and you win, you've bowled four overs for 50 — but you won the game because of that last over. And that won't come up on the scorecard or in the Wikipedia. So context is really important within cricket to take stats away a little bit.

死亡之球：最后一个over的剖析

Host: Okay, so you've been doing some really interesting stuff alongside the game and I want to use that as a segue to talk about it. Because I saw — I'm going to hijack your really good content and have you explain it for everyone, because I loved it, it was great. You're bowling the last over in a T20 game. How are you approaching those six balls?

Tymal: Again, ball one — it doesn't even start there. So because by the time you've bowled the first ball of your last over, you've already bowled three overs in the game. So you're already in a position where either you're going well, you're going badly, or you're in the middle. So your confidence — that affects your confidence. If I bowled three overs and I've already been hit for 40 runs and then I've got to go bowl that last over, you need to be able to push those three overs away. That's really important. If you're coming into it with your head down, your shoulders a little bit down, that's going to translate into your performance.

That's really important because T20 is such a short game. You've got to be able to identify who you're bowling at. So I'm quite big on doing my homework before the game. I go out onto the pitch with a piece of paper that I've written down with the analyst — the names of the batters, whether pace-on or pace-off is a good option, and whether they ramp (so whether they play a scoop shot, a tickle across).

So I've got that, so I know that — because once you're in the game, you might forget it. We'll have an analysis meeting the day of the game or the night before the game, but when you're out in the middle, you might forget it. So I actually write it down on a bit of paper. So: pace-on or pace-off? Are their strike rates higher against pace-off or pace-on bowling? Because that gives you an inclination of whether to bowl slower balls or whether to bowl faster balls.

So those are little things that I do on the pitch to help me. And then gut instinct's a big part of it as well. You need to analyze the wicket. Because by the time it gets to the end of the over, end of the innings, some of the game has already happened, so you'll know — okay, slow balls are working on the wicket today, or slow balls aren't working on the wicket today.

The boundary sizes play a big [role]. If you've got a short leg-side boundary, you need to go wide. So that's the hardest thing about T20 cricket at the moment — you don't always play in the middle of the pitch. So you often have a short boundary on one side. So the batters will target that short boundary wherever they can. So there's a lot of times in cricket where you know what's going to happen. I'm bowling to a short boundary on the leg side. The batter's going to try and hit it there. I'm going to try and bowl it wide to try and get him to hit to the long boundary. And it's just a case of who can execute the best.

And those are the tough situations as a bowler because you don't really have anywhere to go. You can't really bowl a bouncer to the short boundary because you're probably going to get hit for six. Unless you can bluff. And then it's just a roll of the dice.

So sorry, to answer it — we've got all of that and you haven't even bowled a ball yet.

Host: Haven't bowled a ball yet.

假设场景：在RCB群星里选一个对手

Host: So you've got to assess all of that. Who are you bowling at? Are they in good touch? Are they going well? Who's at the other end? Firstly, okay — on the other end of this, you're back to your RCB team. You have to choose one of the following to face: Head, Kohli, AB, Gayle.

Tymal: Oh, Gayle and De Villiers.

Host: As in to be on my team?

Tymal: No, they're who you're bowling at right now.

Host: Okay, it's a tough day out. Because obviously by the time you're bowling at them, they're probably in. De Villiers was probably the worst because he was known as Mr. 360 — he could hit you, he could manipulate the ball to anywhere he wanted to on his day. If you typed in his highlight reel on YouTube, it'd be amazing — just the entertainment value. And I was lucky enough to play with him and taste a little bit of it first-hand and see it and experience it. And yeah, he was special.

Host: Okay, so you're bowling this last over — who do you want to bowl at in this situation? I'm giving you, you've got the best chance of winning a game. I know there's not many positives in there.

Tymal: Yeah, it's tough because you'd probably say Travis, but at the moment he smokes it everywhere. But Chris Gayle in his pomp could literally hit every ball out of the ground. He's such a big man and hit the biggest [sixes], because those guys don't need to get the middle of the bat. They just need to get a bit of it. You know, as long as it's not the edge, it's probably going to go for six.

That's one thing we say in analysis — is conditions dictate your plan. Because it doesn't matter who you're bowling at. If you've got a short leg-side boundary, you have to bowl wide most of the time. You can throw the odd ball in straight, but if you keep feeding it in straight, they're just going to hit you to the leg side. So you try as a bowler — you try to not bowl to the man and you've got to focus on yourself and execute. Because ultimately we are the ones in control because we are the ones delivering the ball. The batter has to then react to what we do. So in theory, if I nail my skill, I should win. It doesn't always work like that, but at least we're the ones in control of what we do as a bowler.

Host: And the skill you need now for that — let's just use again, like a last over — you're looking at bowling a mixture of back-of-the-hand slow balls, you're looking at cutters, you're looking at like Yorkers. I mean, there's a lot of skill, a lot of options to this, right?

Tymal: A lot of options as well. You need a lot of options. So it's choosing the right option at the right time. You need luck as well. Like, T20 cricket is a massive game of luck. I could bowl the same ball back-to-back. One could take a wicket and one could go for six. Because then it comes down to the batter's execution.

I could bowl you a ball on the stumps and you could just miss it and I look great because I've taken a wicket. Or I could bowl that exact same ball and you can hit it for six. So cricket's a funny game. I've had some games where I bowled rubbish and not executed where I wanted to execute, but you end up with four wickets because the batter hasn't quite timed it and you've got them caught out on the boundary somewhere. And then you'll have other games where you've bowled really well, or feel you've bowled really well, and the batter's just beaten you and you've been out-executed.

自我分析：在变量风暴中保持诚实

Host: In all seriousness, the mentality of that is quite challenging, right? Because you probably can't be too self-critical to some extent. There's an element of the controllables you can control, and then the things that are just part of the game. How do you compartmentalize that as a player?

Tymal: That's really important. I think self-analysis, being honest with yourself in cricket, in T20 cricket, is really important. Because whether I have a good game or whether I have a bad game, I'll watch it back after. I'll watch my bowling back, because in your mind's eye, it's often different to how it actually looks. You might think, "I executed that really well," but then you watch it back and you think, "actually, no, I missed by half a foot and that's too much."

So that's a really important part of it. Being honest with yourself — okay, did I have a good performance there because I bowled well, or was it because I got a bit lucky and the batter on another day probably could have hit me for six? Or likewise, my figures are really bad today, but did I actually execute really well? Were my plans the right plans?

That's one thing we speak about a lot in cricket is: you can't always control the outcome, but you can control the process. Okay, have you set the right field? Is that the right plan to these conditions? And have you just lost out to the batter?

That's really important in cricket because it's such a variable sport. A batter can — big swing of the bat and it's an edge and it goes over the keeper. That's not — you haven't done anything bad, but that's just bad luck. You've executed well, the batter got the luck on that occasion, but it's gone down as four runs or six runs against you. And that's why I say context is so important within cricket. But yeah, self-analysis and honest self-reflection in cricket is massive.

OnlyFans之约：把内容当成第二事业

Host: So the reason I asked you that question about the last over was because you put up something online not long ago about breaking down the anatomy of bowling a final over. And you've been doing a lot of work — or should we say, what looks like an increasing amount of work away from the pitch, doing things with some really cool content. You've done some interesting partnerships that I'm sure you can bring up now. Why is it so important for you to be doing work alongside your cricket, away from the pitch, while you're still playing?

Tymal: I think it's important in all sport. I think you speak to people from all forms of sport and it's the way the world is at the moment. I want to be successful. I want to be a successful cricketer for as long as I can. And then whatever I do after cricket, I want to be successful — or as successful as I can be. I don't know exactly what that looks like yet, but I know I want to do cool things, go to cool places, meet cool people, be able to provide a good life for my family. Those are things I know I want to do. And social media and the internet, it's a huge part of it.

And I've always kept one eye on it. So I've done commentary and broadcast work alongside playing pretty much my whole career. So since I retired from four-day cricket back in 2015, I've been doing commentary. I work with the BBC a lot during The Hundred on TV, on the radio. I've done work with Sky, talkSPORT, those companies. Because I've got time. I'm not playing four-day cricket. I'm only playing T20, so I physically have the hours to go and do it. It's not like I'm playing a four-day game and that takes up your whole week and then you're knackered and you don't want to do anything else. I'm playing a T20. I might have three weeks off where there's no T20. I can go do a bit of broadcast work.

But social media — I've just been a bit vanilla. Just done the bare minimum. Accepted the posts, just the basic stuff. My wife's always saying, "you should do more, your social media could be a lot better." Because I've never been one of the big names in cricket. I've never had an England contract — well, I have, but not been an IPL regular. You know, these guys that have millions of followers on Instagram — it's achievable. I've never been in that realm. So for me to do something on social media needs to be impactful and worthwhile.

So, yeah — to what you alluded to — obviously an opportunity came about with OnlyFans last year, which, yeah, I ummed and ahhed about for a while. But that kind of triggered and gave me no excuse really to push what I'm doing on socials and just be brave really and try some new stuff.

Host: And this was a different type of opportunity? OnlyFans content strategy to the one that everyone I'm sure is thinking about?

Tymal: Very much so, yes. The deals I had — the contracts expired now. But yeah, it was all obviously just safe-for-work content. It came about — my brother-in-law runs an influencer marketing company, mainly in football, was working with OnlyFans, who wanted footballers on the platform, were struggling to do so. And then he put me forward and eventually got on a meeting with the OnlyFans people, told them about cricket, what I do, where I go, kind of what the life looks like. They thought, "that sounds quite interesting." And yeah, so I kind of eventually signed this deal with OnlyFans to be their first cricketer endorsed athlete. Yeah — sponsored by OnlyFans.

体育内容的下一站：旅行、分析、自我曝光

Host: But there's so much right behind the scenes that people will want to see now. And that's, if you're looking at where that type of partnership can go — you can create content around your life that people don't see yet, it's probably actually now some of the most interesting parts of being a professional sports person. How do you train? How do you eat? What's the travel like? Where do you stay? Like, especially when you're on the move for so much of the year and you're going to some really cool places. But then also, it's like we just talked about — again, the anatomy of bowling a death over — you can go and explain it. I've seen you do stuff highlights and you'll talk people through that over. So an OnlyFans deal opening up the industry to the work that you're doing and kind of giving a lens into that world, right? That's the next evolution of athletes really taking control of their own content.

Tymal: Yeah, and I think it's value added, isn't it? And cricket's a bit behind — similar to I'm sure other sports — in terms of it's quite a safe, you know, a bit backwards, in terms of we're not pushing the envelope really. Or teams maybe are playing catch-up now. But again, if I can try and be ahead of it and get ahead of the game in terms of what I'm producing, and if people find it interesting, that's the main thing.

And I think the low-hanging fruit is the travel side of things. Because traveling isn't that exotic really, but it's just — okay, it's tricky because you can't… You've got to be careful because there's always other people at stake.
