item.description:
## 一、USDH 之死：一场关于"货币摩擦力"的精妙战争
表面上，Coinbase + Circle 吞并 USDH 是一笔流动性整合交易。但更深的真相是：**这是一场关于"摩擦力归属权"的争夺**。
传统金融中，最赚钱的从来不是交易本身，而是**资金在两种状态之间转换时被收的过路费**——这就是 NIM（净息差）。Chris 点破了一个被加密行业长期忽视的事实：Hyperliquid 此前把这块"无摩擦的浮存金收益"白白让给了第三方。这本质上是一种**协议级别的"租金外溢"**。
而更让人意外的是：**90% 的储备收入回流给 Hyperliquid**——这意味着 Circle 主动放弃了大部分息差收益，换取的是"USDC 成为新兴霸权链的官方语言"。这不是一笔金融交易，**这是一场殖民地的语言征服**——就像英语之所以是商业通用语，不是因为它最好，而是因为它在足够多的关键节点上被默认安装。Circle 在买"默认安装权"，代价是放弃短期 NIM。
## 二、一个隐藏的跨领域连接：稳定币正在悄悄重塑美债收益率曲线
Gordon 抛出了一个被严重低估的洞察——**稳定币正

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Finding 3,800 Evolving Sites in Human DNA – David Reich
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个让人不安的反向规律：David Reich 的"创伤性认知"

最让我意外的不是 3,800 个进化位点的发现，而是 Reich 自我陈述中的一个反复出现的认知结构——他把这种被数据反复打脸的经验称为"几乎是创伤性的"(almost traumatized)。这是一个值得停下来凝视的措辞。一个顶尖科学家用"创伤"这个临床心理学词汇来描述自己的研究历程，意味着什么？

注意他叙事的三幕结构： 1. 先是建立一个共识性的信念（非洲人变异完整包含非非洲人；自然选择在人类近期已沉寂） 2. 数据出现强烈反向信号 3. 花数年时间试图让这个结果消失，但它"只是越来越强"

这个结构在 Neanderthal 项目和 selection 项目中几乎是同构的。换句话说，Reich 不是一次被数据打脸——他建立了一种与数据对抗并失败的科研身份。这本身就是一种罕见的"被驯化的科学家心智"。

跨领域连接：他无意中复刻了金融业的"反向交易者悖论"

这里有一个非常奇特的跨学科对应。Reich 描述的认知模式——坚信主流共识 → 发现反向信号 → 长时间试图否定它 → 最终被迫接受 ——和顶级量化对冲基金（如 Renaissance、Two Sigma）面对"过强的 alpha 信号"时的标准流程几乎一模一样。在金融中，当一个信号好得不真实时，专业的反应不是兴奋而是怀疑——你会假设是数据泄漏、look-ahead bias、survivorship bias，然后花数月去 kill it。

更有意思的是：Reich 团队所用的新方法本身就来自医学 GWAS（全基因组关联研究），而 GWAS 的统计哲学又深受计量经济学影响。所以这个发现链条是：经济学的统计推断 → 医学风险因子识别 → 古 DNA 自然选择检测。三个领域用同一把"识别微弱但持续信号"的数学锤子，敲出了三个完全不同领域的真理。

这暗示了一个反直觉的洞见："如何在噪音海洋中识别微小但持续的方向性信号" 可能是 21 世纪所有数据密集学科共享的元方法论。古 DNA、对冲基金、医学遗传学——它们本质上在解同一个数学问题。

隐藏的系统性真相：12 → 21 → 3,800 不是渐进，而是相变

表面上看，这是一个"样本量更大、方法更好、发现更多"的常规进步故事。但仔细看数字会发现一个非线性的诡异跳跃： - 2015 年：12 个位点 - 2024 年（哥本哈根组）：21 个位点 - Akbar/Reich 2025 年：3,800 个位点

从 12 到 21 用了 9 年，从 21 到 3,800 是180 倍的跃迁。这不是渐进改进——这是相变(phase transition)。

而触发相变的关键不是数据量（数据只增加了 14 倍），而是问题表述的转换：从"频率在不同时间点之间显著不同吗"变成"整个相关性矩阵能否被一个恒定方向的选择系数更好地解释"。

这里藏着一个非常深刻的方法论真相：当某个领域长期停滞在某个数字附近时，问题往往不在于数据不够，而在于提问的方式锁死了答案的上限。12 和 21 之间的小幅增长其实是一个警报——它在告诉研究者"你正在用错误的方式逼近一个真实存在的丰富现象"。哥本哈根那项研究的"令人失望"，其实是旧范式触底的声音。

更出人意料的是：这个故事还暗示了一个关于"沉寂叙事"的普遍危险。每当某个领域的数据反复显示"什么都没发生"（自然选择沉寂、市场有效、基因决定论失败、暗物质找不到）时，真正的可能性往往是——我们的探测器被自己的理论预期校准成了对真相不敏感的仪器。Reich 团队所做的，本质上是把探测器从"寻找突变"重新校准为"寻找方向性的持续微小偏置"。这一调整使隐藏的进化海洋瞬间浮现。

人类基因组从未停止进化。只是我们之前一直在用错误的频率扫描它。

📝 转录文本

一段几乎"创伤性"的科研经历

The work that I've been involved in is consistently shown that I was wrong in my biases coming into the work, and I've really been almost traumatized by this. Like, again and again I've come into a project with some kind of guess about what the data was showing, and then the data doesn't show that.

案例一：尼安德特人基因组的反转

So, for example, when I got involved in the Neanderthal genome project and helping to analyze data looking at how archaic Neanderthals were related to modern humans, I was part of a group of scientists who had established that non-Africans were a simple subset of African variation, and that there was no evidence at all of Neanderthal interbreeding into the ancestors of modern humans or other archaic interbreeding.

Different analyses that I and very much more other people had done made it look like non-Africans variation was just a subset, a small sample of that in Africa, and that could have fully explained the data.

And so, that when I was involved in analyzing the Neanderthal DNA sequences, what happened was I found this very strong evidence of Neanderthals being more closely related to non-Africans than to Africans, and it was very surprising and I thought it must be a mistake. I thought it was — I was quite incredulous. I thought it was unlikely to be true because other evidence that had been found before seemed to point in the other direction.

And so, I spent several years trying to make these results go away, as did my colleagues, and we just couldn't make the results go away. They just kept getting stronger.

案例二：关于自然选择"沉寂"的旧共识

And this experience working on natural selection was the same. So, what we had felt here was that what we were convinced of was that natural selection had been pretty quiescent in our species over the last several hundred thousand years. Therefore, if we look at patterns of variation in non-African people today, or in any people today, we should see not a lot of selection going on.

And indeed, the first ancient DNA studies beginning in 2015 with this paper that we were involved in with Ian Mathieson and colleagues — indeed, these papers seem to show relatively small numbers of genetic positions associated with natural selection.

2015 年的初步发现：12 个位点

So, in 2015, we analyzed data from about 200 Europeans and Middle Easterners to try to understand frequency changes over time. And we compared those ancient people who were the sources of modern Europeans to people in Europe today. And we looked at frequency differences that were at two extreme to be due to chance.

We were very excited to find 12 positions that we were convinced were highly different in frequency between Europeans today and what we would expect based on the history that we and others had identified was the history relating modern to ancient Europeans. And so, some of these were known and some of these were not known and this was very exciting.

And we hoped that as the numbers of samples would increase and we would get higher resolution to be able to appreciate differences in frequencies over time, we hoped that this would make it possible to detect far more.

失望的十年：2024 年依然只有 21 个

And what was quite disappointing over the subsequent decade is that that didn't happen. So, for example, the largest study of that type in 2024 by a group in Copenhagen analyzed the data, much better data than we had in 2015, and found only 21 positions that were highly different in frequency across time.

And while that was exciting, it was almost twice as many as we had found in 2015, in a lot of ways it was disappointing cuz the sample size and data quality had gone up so much and yet this is all that was found.

And so, what that suggested is that we might be hitting an asymptote and we might not be able to get beyond where we currently were and that this approach to learning about biology, sort of which would be was very promising in theory, might actually not produce a high yield. That maybe in fact natural selection was quiescent and in fact, what the reason we're seeing so few changes is that actually there's not been a lot of adaptive directional selection.

突破：Ali Akbar 领导的新研究

So, that was the situation we found ourselves in until just a few years ago when we carried out this study in our research group led by Ali Akbar. So, [snorts] so what we did is we deployed a few innovations or change to try to improve our power to detect natural selection.

创新一：14 倍数据量扩展

One of them is we just pumped a lot of data into the system. And so, we increased the amount of data by about 14-fold. And the main thing that we do in this study is we report data in this study from about 10,000 individuals with new data. So, this is like a very big increase in the amount of data in the literature.

And the total data set size of ancient individuals distributed over the last 18,000 years is about 16,000 people. So, this is a large data set. It's much larger than was previously possible. And when you have more data, you can estimate frequency changes with much more subtlety.

And the data comes from only one part of the world, which is Europe and the Middle East. It's not a more important part of the world than other places, but it's the place where maybe 70 or 80% of the data in the ancient DNA literature so far comes from due to historical reasons. And it provides us with a natural laboratory where we can see what happens over one place over time as environments change to the genome. It's really interesting to imagine doing this type of analysis in other parts of the world. And the comparative analyses are super important and interesting, but this study right now is about this one place in the world where we have particularly fantastic data.

创新二：借用医学研究的新方法论

The other thing we did is we developed an entirely new methodology that hadn't been used in this area before. And the methodology is based on a technique that had been developed for finding risk factors for disease in medical studies.

And a simple way to explain it is we ask how to predict the genetic type a person has based on its pattern of relatedness to other people. So, we'll have a data set of about 16,000 ancient people and 22,000 people if we include the ancient and modern people. And then we look at how closely related each of these 22,000 people are to each other. And we predict the genetic type at each position in the DNA at 10 million positions based on the pattern of relatedness to all of the other 22,000 people.

And then we ask if natural selection blowing the frequency of the mutation in the same direction in all geographic places and at all times predicts the data a little bit better than just knowing the relatedness to all the other samples in the database. So, we're simply asking the alternative hypothesis is that selection has been blowing in the same direction at all times and we simply ask if that explains the data better.

And that's a dumb assumption uh because of course the truth is that natural selection is going to have changed in frequency over time. But, we're just asking the simplest of questions whether assuming a constant rate of selection explains the data more than not doing so.

主持人的复述确认

And just to summarize to make sure I've understood, you're trying to make a model that predicts allele frequency changes over time.

Right.

have two different parts.

Right.

One part is this uh genetic relatedness matrix which captures um how similar different genomes are to each other and that should capture um the impact of different bottlenecks and of drift and of population admixtures and all those things which affect the entire genome.

Correct.

And uh then you have the separate thing which is like, okay, if we look at specific locations, can we just say that oh, this location has been selected at whatever coefficient over time um and if we add some coefficient, it does it become easier to predict the allele frequency changes than you would have just seen from this other artifact which is only predicted which is just looking at like, oh, if you look at the whole genome, are these guys in the same you know, are they going through the same bottlenecks, have they they've through the same drift etc.

That's precisely right.

结果：数百到数千个位点

Okay. Um okay, so what have you learned?

So, when we analyze the data this way, we looked at 10 million positions in the DNA uh that uh in these 22,000 people, 16,000 of them were ancient, and we looked to see if there was more change in this consistent direction over time than you would expect by chance.

And when we analyze the data, we found many many hundreds of places in the DNA that were changing too much over time in too consistent a way to be explained by chance.

Now, there's a bit of a statistical problem in figuring out how many there are because they're so densely packed that they're close to each other and they're interfering with each other. But, when you try to piece them out and say, "Let's look at — Let's count them only one in each place in the DNA and blank out the others," we find at least about 479 positions that are all independently pushing in the same way. Those positions are 99% confident that they're real. By another criteria of more than 50% confident that they're real, we think that about 3,800 positions are all pushing in the same direction.

So, this is like a crazy number of results given that in our work previously and other people's work, there were at most a couple of dozen discoveries coming from a single scan.

再一次的"试图否定却失败"

So, when we got this result, we were very surprised. We were thought it must be wrong, and we spent the next couple of years trying to make the results go away, but they just got kept getting stronger.

And so, what we were trying to do is to look for some kind of independent type of evidence to tell us whether these positions were real. And we stumbled on something really powerful for this purpose that had not been used in this way before, and it relied on the fact that we had very large numbers of discoveries, like many hundreds of discoveries or even thousands.

独立验证：与 UK Biobank 的交叉对照

And so, what we did is we took a completely independent data set, which was the corpus of genome-wide association studies. So, these are studies that people have carried out in hundreds of thousands of people looking for whether particular genetic mutations are more common in people with high blood pressure with than with low blood pressure or something like this.

So, we took the UK Biobank, which is about 500,000 people from Great Britain who have been measured for hundreds and hundreds of traits. The whole genomes of all these people have been sequenced and for each of these traits, we could look whether each of these 10 million positions are connected to this trait in some way in a convincing way.

So, in 10 million positions about 15% — about 1.5 million positions in the DNA — are predictive of at least one of these several hundred traits.

So, then we could ask a question: is our natural selection signal or statistic — is it related to whether a mutation causes high blood pressure or some other trait? So, we slid our statistic for natural selection from upward, you know, to a value of one, a value of two, a value of three, a value of four, a value of five. And as we did that, the enrichment for genetic mutations that affect traits got higher and higher.

So, whereas it was only 15% when we didn't use our selection statistic, when we required the selection statistic to be above about five, there was about a fivefold enrichment for mutations that cause traits.

什么是"选择统计量"？

What is a selection statistic?

This is the statistic we use to measure whether a mutation is changing over time significantly in a non-zero way. So, it can be approximately thought of as a normally distributed statistic, a Gaussian statistic, which is the number of standard deviations the statistical value is away from zero, where zero is no natural selection. It's not exactly that, but it's close to that.

And so, if this statistic is above five, we see about a fivefold enrichment in mutations that affect a trait. And so, instead of 15% of the mutations that are random affecting the trait, it's like 60 or 70 that are affecting the trait when we slide our statistic upward — and this is providing completely independent evidence that these sites are real. And as you slide above five there's no more enrichment.

结论：信号是真实的

So our interpretation of these results that we were able to validate and show that these interpretations made sense using computer simulations for our process — our interpretation of this result is that once you slide the statistic above five, essentially all the signals of natural selection are real.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个让人不安的反向规律：David Reich 的"创伤性认知"
最让我意外的不是 3,800 个进化位点的发现，而是 Reich 自我陈述中的一个**反复出现的认知结构**——他把这种被数据反复打脸的经验称为"几乎是创伤性的"(almost traumatized)。这是一个值得停下来凝视的措辞。一个顶尖科学家用"创伤"这个临床心理学词汇来描述自己的研究历程，意味着什么？
注意他叙事的**三幕结构**： 1. 先是建立一个共识性的信念（非洲人变异完整包含非非洲人；自然选择在人类近期已沉寂） 2. 数据出现强烈反向信号 3. **花数年时间试图让这个结果消失，但它"只是越来越强"**
这个结构在 Neanderthal 项目和 selection 项目中**几乎是同构的**。换句话说，Reich 不是一次被数据打脸——他建立了一种**与数据对抗并失败的科研身份**。这本身就是一种罕见的"被驯化的科学家心智"。
## 跨领域连接：他无意中复刻了金融业的"反向交易者悖论"
这里有一个非常奇特的跨学科对应。Reich 描述的认知模式——**坚信主流共识 → 发现反向信号 →

item.title: [a16z] How Founders Can Build for Law Enforcement and First Responders | The a16z Show
item.dc:creator: a16z
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：警察的"两难悖论"暴露了一种制度性的"时间病"

视频里那句调侃——"警察讨厌两件事：变化，和保持不变"——表面是段子，本质却是一个深刻的组织心理学诊断。这种"既怕变又怕不变"的状态，在精神病学里有个对应概念叫"矛盾固着（ambivalent fixation）"，常出现在创伤后人群身上。

把这个视角套回来，你会发现一个令人不安的洞见：美国执法系统作为一个集体，可能正处于一种制度化的 PTSD 状态。这就是为什么 Colonel Glover 反复强调"心理健康监测""15 年和 25 年的强制休假""脑部扫描早班签到"——这些听起来像是福利，但实际上是在用机器外骨骼支撑一个已经在心理上过载的系统。技术（无人机、AI、body cam analytics）的真正角色不是"提升效率"，而是让一个濒临崩溃的人类系统延迟崩溃的时间。

二、跨领域连接：警务正在变成"医疗放射科"，而不是"特种部队"

Glover 最后那句话被很多人忽略了——"未来的警察不再是踹门的，而是看视频、查 AI 欺诈的"。这其实预示了一种职业形态的相变：

旧范式 新范式 对应行业类比
巡逻 = 体力 + 直觉 巡逻 = 传感器读取 + 模式识别 放射科医生看 CT
现场 = 唯一战场 现场 = 多模态数据流的一个节点 空管员调度航班
抓捕 = 终点 抓捕 = 长链条调查的一个事件 反洗钱合规官

换句话说，警察这个职业正在从"运动员"转型为"分析师"，而这种转型在历史上只发生过一次——20 世纪初的医生从"手艺人"变成"科学家"。这意味着未来 10 年警校的课程结构、招聘画像、甚至体能测试都要被推倒重来。今天投资警务科技的人，本质上是在投资一个新职业的诞生，而不是优化一个旧职业。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：Flock + Skyo 正在构建"城市级反向广告网络"

最让我意外的隐藏结构是这个——视频里 Rahul 说"AI、无人机、车牌识别、枪声检测，这些都在汇聚"。注意这个"汇聚"的拓扑结构：多模态传感器 → 实时事件触发 → 自动派遣物理执行单元（无人机）。

这套架构和谁一模一样？Google 和 Meta 的广告系统： - 多模态信号（搜索、点击、位置）→ 实时竞价 → 自动投放广告

只不过执法版的"广告"是一架无人机，"转化"是一次抓捕。Flock Safety 正在以 Google 之于互联网的方式，成为物理世界的"事件路由层"。

这里有个尖锐的伦理含义几乎没人提：广告系统的核心病灶——黑箱排序、误判难以申诉、规模化偏见——会以 1:1 的方式迁移到执法领域。当一个无人机因为"模式匹配"飞向你家阳台，你能像点击"为什么我看到这个广告"那样追溯它吗？

创业者真正应该思考的不是"我能不能做下一个 Flock"，而是——谁来做这个新基础设施的"GDPR"和"申诉机制"层？这可能是比传感器本身更大、更被忽视的机会。

附赠：一个 a16z 自己都没意识到的元观察

Ben Horowitz 把"FIFA 世界杯+奥运会"和"边境情报共享"放在同一段里讲——这暴露了一个更大的叙事：美国正在把"大型赛事安保"作为执法 AI 的合法化温床。这是经典的"奥运效应"——伦敦 2012 之后英国的 CCTV 密度永久性翻倍。Arizona 现在做的 TRX，本质上是在为 2026 世界杯后不会撤掉的监控基建做政治准备。这件事一旦你看见，就再也无法 unsee。

📝 转录文本

开场：警察的悖论与技术的不可避免

"There's two things cops hate: for things to change and for things to stay the same."

Colonel Glover: Most of the cops in the field are going to have to change the way their skill set is shaped, because it's going to be a little bit more investigative. It's going to be a little bit more nuanced. It's not going to look the same anymore.

主持人: Are people just going to start see drones flying around? Is that where we are? You hear a gunshot go off and the drone finds a shooter getting into a car and driving off, and then pursuing the vehicle. It's kind of almost hard to see that it isn't inevitable. We can't do that with a helicopter today, unless you just kept five helicopters up 24/7 — and that's just not sustainable.

主持人: What advice would you give to founders who are less interested in optimizing ad clicks and more interested in actually building something that helps first responders and save lives?

一、无人机降级危机：一个"持扫帚的清洁工"的故事

主持人: My advice for them is — I'm on the board of Flock Safety. There's other companies like Skyo that make drones. I'm the recipient of constant notifications from you guys: "Hey, we found a kidnapped child." "We used the technology in this way." "We were able to de-escalate a situation."

I remember one story where somebody called 911 and said, "Hey, there's a guy in the alleyway with a shotgun." Well, you can imagine how a police officer would respond to that call. But it turned out the drone provided situational awareness and said, "Wait, there's just a janitor with a broom. That's not a guy with a shotgun." And it totally de-escalates the situation.

So it's really incredible. Anything else we should know, Rahul, about where are we in the sort of nationwide state of drone deployment? Are people just going to start seeing drones flying around — is that where we are?

二、无人机的全国部署蓝图：传感器汇聚与"必然性"

Rahul: Yeah, look — drones are flying robots. And as we see AI and robotics sweep every industry, all these industries are looking for ways to either automate things, make things more efficient, make things more effective. Drones are a similar piece of that.

And in public safety, the short answer is: we are going to see more drones. We're going to see also potentially more hostile drones that we have to be prepared for. You're going to see more police and fire department drones responding to fire calls across the country.

And Flock Safety — it's not just about drones for us. We have multitudes of sensors in the community: - License plate reading cameras - Gunshot detection capabilities

All of this is coming together. All this data — where you're able to determine, "Oh, that's an Amber Alert vehicle," and the drone takes off and chases the Amber Alert vehicle. Or you hear a gunshot go off and the drone finds a shooter getting into a car and driving off, and then pursuing the vehicle, even on the highways with DPS.

That's something where it's kind of almost hard to see that it isn't inevitable. We can't do that with a helicopter today unless you just kept five helicopters up 24/7 — and that's just not sustainable. This is the only way to actually achieve that level of safety.

三、警察的"幸福感"工程：body cam 之外的心理健康闭环

主持人: I joked earlier that it's a bad time to be an enemy of America — from Maduro to Aleno to the Ayatollah — but it sounds like it's going to be a bad time to be a criminal here in America. Colonel Glover, talk about some of the other technologies and capabilities that you've been able to deploy that really changes the equation for law enforcement, either helping the officers or helping the general public — or, in most cases, both.

Colonel Glover: Yeah. So there's a couple of different things that we're doing — sort of like an ecosystem or platform within the agency.

1) 队员的心理健康与状态监测

One of the pieces is making sure that we're giving the officers what they need to be successful, but it's also about their own mental health and well-being. We utilize Vitania — Heal the Heroes: basically a brain scan to start off the day, to figure out exactly how you're doing — a temperature check.

2) Body cam 之后的行为分析层

Then they go out and they can do their shift behind the body-worn camera. We have analytics — that's Truleo — running behind the body-worn camera, just to see the behavior and interaction. Part of that was looking at: - A scorecard for how the trooper is interacting with the public - Information on how the public is interacting with that trooper, to see if they're calm or not - Flagging the situation as well for the supervisor

3) 防止职业倦怠的制度化设计

Outside of that, it helps them have that feeling of "I have a little bit of a layer of protection," because this is going to flag for burnout as well. That was one of the things the associations and I were speaking about: from the advent of body-worn cameras and being able to run those analytics behind it to check for burnout — if we need to pull them from the field, if we need to do something a little bit different — we started instituting wellness checks and sabbaticals for those at the 15 and 25 year marks, to help optimize their performance, so that when they're out in the field, they feel good about what they're doing and they have job satisfaction.

四、Body cam 与 Taser：技术的炒作周期与公众接受

主持人: Absolutely. It's been amazing to see the public sentiment shift over things like body cams. At first, people were very against body cams. Even some of the officer unions were against body cams. Then they realized, "Wait a minute, you see all the dumb things that the criminals do." So that's pretty good. It protects our officers. But also it provides you game tape, gives you footage. It could be coaching, mental health services, making sure that somebody who needs a break gets a break.

And, really incredible technology. I remember the same discussion happened with Taser. So we see this over and over again with technology — that we sort of have to go through a hype cycle of reaction. But it's great to see those things being deployed.

五、情报融合中心与国际化：从 ACTIC 到 FIFA、奥运会

主持人: Do you want to talk a little bit real quickly about the intelligence gathering apparatus? You've mentioned a couple times that we shifted law enforcement to really making sure that we have all the information we need. Talk about at the Department of Public Safety — how you're working to really make sure that officers have the best information available.

Colonel Glover: Yeah. So one of the things that we're leaning into as well is — we have the Arizona Counterterrorism and Information Center, the ACTIC. With fusion centers around the US, they're collaborating and working together in tandem to make sure that they're sharing information.

You have the TRX program, which a lot of us are doing for FIFA right now, because that's the big event that's coming up — as well as the Olympics.

But one of the things in Arizona that I'm doing is leaning heavily into making sure that we have sort of an international presence as well. So we have a lot of partnerships with different groups. Of course, we are a border state, so we have the state of Sonora, Mexico that's right next to us. We're looking at being able to have intelligence officers from: - Mexico - The UAE - Liberia - Different parts of the world

Because the world is getting a lot smaller. We all know that crime is happening everywhere. Being able to condense that down and distill it — to where we can have good information sharing that's unclassified, just to share with one another on trends, and being able to backstop that with some of the technology and AI — helps us actually stop a lot of the trends before they start.

主持人: Absolutely. We saw, very sadly in Austin, that global events have local repercussions. So I think we've seen in New York City that their counterterrorism work at the NYPD has been very successful. I'm excited to hear that you're bringing that kind of a program into Arizona.

六、给创业者的建议：别被"警察"两个字吓退

主持人: Rahul, you started out as a policeman, as a paramedic. Now that you've seen all this technology — some of which you've brought to bear in public safety — do you think there's still great opportunities in public safety? What advice would you give to founders who are less interested in optimizing ad clicks and more interested in actually building something that helps first responders and saves lives?

Rahul: I already know great founders that are working in public safety. It's funny — the Colonel was talking about tracking the performance of the members of his department in burnout. There's a founder here, Ben Curley, in the audience, who is the CEO of Sharp Performance that does exactly that. You should meet after this if you haven't already.

主持人: We're already talking to —

Rahul: All right. Good. Good.

There's plenty of great founders out there that are tackling public safety. My advice for them is: look, it comes off as very intimidating. I'm sure the Colonel's heard this saying, but — there's two things cops hate: for things to change, and for things to stay the same. And so it feels like a very narrow thread that you have to hit.

But the reality is that if you can picture something that feels like an inevitability — in the same way that we talk about drones, AI, some of these things — recognize that that change will come no matter what. It'll come because it's the best thing for them, the best thing for the communities, the best thing for the country. And just take it upon yourself to be the person that brings the change. Don't be intimidated by it just because they're cops.

And then if you do do it, I would say spend a lot of time with the cops, too. Just like Dr. Karp said previously: it is hard if you don't know what they've been through, what it feels like to be on the beat. Spend time, do some ride-alongs. Be a reserve cop, if you want to really get into it. That'll really help you not only speak the language, but know what to build for them.

七、给合作方的最后一句话：跳进来

主持人: Colonel Glover, any final words that you want to share for the people that want to work with law enforcement, work with public safety officials? What's the best way to partner and work with you?

Colonel Glover: Well, back to R's point — really, jump in. Jump in, really engage, connect. You're going to find there's a lot of law enforcement leaders that are looking to figure out the best ways of implementation and trying to get ahead of the curve with technology.

We know that it's going to be something that's going to really dominate this profession over the next 10 years. We're going to see such a shift and change that most of the cops out in the field are going to have to change the way their skill set is shaped — because it's going to be a little bit more investigative, a little bit more nuanced. You're going to have to have a different framework in terms of your mindset, because it's not going to look the same anymore 10 years from now.

It may not be about kicking in doors and other things. It's going to be looking at the technical aspects of "this video you just received," and looking at AI and certain things that are going to come up from a fraud standpoint as well. So it's going to be very nuanced in that way. Every leader is looking at: how do we make this adjustment? How do we become a little bit more flexible and adaptable to what's going to be the need?

主持人: Awesome. Well, thank you both, gentlemen. Appreciate the work you do out in the field, as technologists, and thanks for being with us here today.

嘉宾们: Thank you. Thank you.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Latent Space] The Next War Is Already Here — Yaroslav Azhnyuk, The Fourth Law & Noah Smith, Noahpinion
item.dc:creator: Latent Space
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最意外的观察：你的 AI 数据中心，正在和顿巴斯前线抢同一卷光纤

整段对话里最容易被忽略、却最震撼的一句话是——光纤涨价的原因不是战争，而是 AI。Azhnyuk 说乌克兰和俄罗斯的采购员去中国工厂买无人机光纤，被告知"卖光了，都卖给美国人了"，价格三个月内从 4 美元/公里飙到 32 美元/公里。

请停下来感受这个连接：OpenAI、Nvidia 的资本开支，与一架 FPV 无人机能否飞到 30 公里外击中目标，被同一种物理商品耦合在了一起。 我们习惯把"AI 革命"和"无人机战争"当成两条平行的新闻线，但它们其实在争抢同一根缠在线轴上的玻璃丝。这意味着一件反直觉的事：硅谷每多建一座数据中心，乌克兰前线的有效打击半径就缩短一点。战争与算力不是隐喻意义上的"军备竞赛",而是字面意义上的同一条供应链。和平时期的繁荣与战时的杀伤力，第一次在一根光纤上短路了。

二、隐藏的系统性真相：他从未"转行"——Pet Cube 和杀伤性武器是同一个产品

Azhnyuk 把自己的人生概括为"从制造朝宠物抛零食的摄像头，到制造朝占领者抛炸药的摄像头"。这句话被当作一个黑色幽默的金句，但它其实是整集播客里最深的技术真相，而且没人点破。

宠物摄像头的核心功能是什么？目标识别 + 持续追踪 + 一个远程触发的执行机构。FPV 无人机的"一级自治/末端制导"的核心功能是什么？一模一样。所以当他说"乌克兰有 200 家公司在做末端制导，似乎只有我们真正做成了"——能做成的为什么是他？因为他根本不是在学一项新技术，而是在把 Pet Cube 的"自动跟随宠物"功能换了个目标类别。军事化没有要求新科技，它只要求一个新靶子。 这就是为什么这场战争演化得如此之快：它不是在等国防工业发明东西，而是在征用消费电子早已成熟的能力栈。真正可怕的不是无人机有多先进，而是它有多平庸——平庸到一个做宠物玩具的人,凭旧技能就能登顶全球战场。

三、跨领域连接：自动驾驶的"长尾诅咒"原封不动地搬到了战场——连伦理结论都搬过来了

Azhnyuk 明说他的"五级自治"是抄自动驾驶汽车的 SAE 分级。但连接比这更深。自动驾驶十五年没能解决的"长尾边缘案例"问题——雾天、罕见路况、分布外场景——他承认在无人机上是同一个瓶颈："原型到量产百万台之间，隔着一条非常宽的鸿沟。"Waymo 和一架神风无人机，共享同一个工程学命门。

但真正的"啊哈"在伦理推论上：他说"5 到 10 年后，使用不带 AI 的武器将是不道德的，就像在公共道路上手动开车将是不道德的"。请注意这个反转——主流叙事里 AI 武器是伦理问题的来源，而他把因果完全调转：AI 是更精确、更少附带损伤的那一方，拒绝自动化才成了不道德。 这与"人类司机比自动驾驶更危险"是字面上同一个论证结构。

而最后我想留给你一个未被言明的系统性模式。Azhnyuk 在全程反复做一件事：拒绝说俄军"愚蠢"，拒绝断言中国军力、提醒主持人"你活在西方宣传泡泡里、我也是"、戳破"好莱坞英雄美国"的神话。表面看这只是谦逊。但把它和他对欧洲的诊断并置——欧洲的弱点不是预算，而是民众说"打仗我就搬走"——你会看到一个隐藏结论：在不对称、高速演化的战争里，真正的约束变量不是技术，而是一个社会愿不愿意放弃令自己舒服的故事。 安慰性叙事本身就是一种战略脆弱性。他对认知诚实的执着不是性格，是武器系统的一部分。这也解释了为什么他用《星际争霸》的 Zerg/Protoss 来讲地缘政治——对成长于即时战略游戏的一代人，战争早已是一道高维搜索题，而你能不能赢，取决于你敢不敢如实看清搜索空间。

item.description:
## 一、最意外的观察：你的 AI 数据中心，正在和顿巴斯前线抢同一卷光纤
整段对话里最容易被忽略、却最震撼的一句话是——光纤涨价的原因不是战争，而是 AI。Azhnyuk 说乌克兰和俄罗斯的采购员去中国工厂买无人机光纤，被告知"卖光了，都卖给美国人了"，价格三个月内从 4 美元/公里飙到 32 美元/公里。
请停下来感受这个连接：**OpenAI、Nvidia 的资本开支，与一架 FPV 无人机能否飞到 30 公里外击中目标，被同一种物理商品耦合在了一起。** 我们习惯把"AI 革命"和"无人机战争"当成两条平行的新闻线，但它们其实在争抢同一根缠在线轴上的玻璃丝。这意味着一件反直觉的事：硅谷每多建一座数据中心，乌克兰前线的有效打击半径就缩短一点。战争与算力不是隐喻意义上的"军备竞赛",而是字面意义上的同一条供应链。和平时期的繁荣与战时的杀伤力，第一次在一根光纤上短路了。
## 二、隐藏的系统性真相：他从未"转行"——Pet Cube 和杀伤性武器是同一个产品
Azhnyuk 把自己的人生概括为"从制造朝宠物抛零食的摄像头，到制造朝占领者抛炸药的摄像头"。这句话被当作一个黑色幽

item.title: [投资TALK君] ✨【投资TALK君1433期】美债风险回归？短期有做多机会，中期换仓准备✨20260517#CPI #nvda #美股 #投资 #英伟达 #ai #特斯拉
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽略的"叙事补全机制"：市场不是被收益率打跌的，而是被自己的强势打跌的

视频里有一段话值得反复咀嚼——"过去一个多月市场太强了，硬件太强了，芯片太强了。所以一旦回调，人们立刻去找原因，恰好赶上收益率上行，逻辑就被绑在一起了。"

这其实揭示了一个让人意外的因果倒置现象：财经媒体并不是在解释市场，而是在为市场的情绪波动事后配字幕。30年期美债收益率在2023年下半年就突破过5%，但那并没有终结牛市。所以"收益率破5% = 熊市"并不是规律，而是一种叙事工件（narrative artifact）——它在市场需要解释时才会被激活。

跨领域连接：这和神经科学里的"裂脑实验"惊人地相似。当大脑某半球做出一个决定后，另一半球（语言中枢）会立刻编造一个看似合理的理由。市场的"日报评论体系"就是金融世界的语言中枢——价格先动，理由后补。真正的投资者训练，是学会识别哪些是因果，哪些是字幕。

二、隐藏的系统性真相：60/40组合的"复活"，意味着我们正在告别一个时代

讲者顺带提了一句被大多数听众忽略的话：2008年之前股债是对冲关系，2008年之后是同涨同跌。他没有展开，但这里藏着对未来5–10年配置最关键的一个判断：

如果通胀来自经济过热（而非央行放水），股债负相关性会回归——这意味着"60/40已死"的论调本身正在死去。

这是一个出人意料的反转：过去三年所有人都在唱衰传统配置，押注另类资产、加密、私募信贷。但讲者悄悄暗示的是——正是因为利率不再回到零，传统的对冲机制才重新生效。低利率时代股债同涨同跌是异常，不是常态。

跨领域连接：这很像生态学中的"消防员悖论"——长期压制小火（QE抑制波动），最终导致生态系统失去自我调节能力。当美联储退出"消防员"角色，市场反而会恢复古老的、健康的负反馈机制。所以对长期配置者，今天利率上行不是坏消息，而是"市场免疫系统的恢复"。

三、最反直觉的洞察：软件板块的"投降式抛售"恰恰是结构性机会的指纹

讲者描述了一个非常具体的现象——连他认识的、坚持五年价值投资纪律的机构，也在Q1清仓软件股。这句话的信息密度远超表面。

通常我们寻找底部信号，看的是散户恐慌指数、看跌期权比、VIX。但真正的大底信号往往不是散户投降，而是"纪律者投降"——那些有体系、有信仰的玩家被迫放弃信仰的那一刻。这是一种极其罕见的指纹。

更深的模式联系：这与库恩《科学革命的结构》里描述的"范式崩溃"瞬间结构完全一致。当连最坚定的旧范式守护者都开始用新范式的语言说话时（"我们也要加芯片仓位"），旧范式其实已经过度延伸到了反转点。投降的不是异端，而是教士——这才是底部。

由此推出一个让人意外的操作含义： - 多数人看到"机构都在追芯片"，会觉得芯片还能涨； - 但更深的解读是：当价值派也屈服于动量叙事，动量叙事的燃料就快烧完了。 - 讲者用"RRSP（等权S&P）将跑赢S&P"作为下注方式，本质上是在押注"叙事疲劳"会先于"基本面疲劳"出现。

彩蛋：一个被遮蔽的元模式

把三件事并排放在一起会出现一个隐藏图案： - 麦当劳跌因为油价（战争代理变量） - 国防股涨因为油价（同一个战争代理变量） - 软件股跌因为机构追硬件（AI叙事的虹吸效应）

表面看是三件无关的事，底层是同一件事：流动性和注意力的再分配。战争吸走了消费股的估值，AI叙事吸走了软件股的估值。当前美股不是一个市场，而是两台抽水机在同时工作。 真正的alpha来自识别"被抽干的池子"——而不是去抢已经满溢的那一个。

📝 转录文本

投资TALK君 1433期 · 美债风险与中期换仓准备

开场

Hello everyone, welcome to a professional US stock channel that goes beyond just reporting news. I am Market Analyst School. Welcome to the Sunday Update.

I haven't updated in a while because I've been extremely busy. Since there was a drop on Friday and futures are down now, I'm making this video to analyze the market trends over the past week, especially since the market seems to be refocusing on macro factors and US Treasury yields.

So, what is actually happening behind the scenes? Should we also be focusing our attention on US Treasury yields? I will delve into this, take a look at US stock valuations, and finally discuss McDonald's as a target. McDonald's has seen a significant decline over the past month or so. Is this an opportunity or a value trap?

Shall we? With these questions in mind, let's get started. Before we begin today's content, please remember to like, comment, and share.

A quick heads up: NVIDIA's earnings report comes out on Wednesday, and I will be doing a live stream on the app after the release. It is for app members, so if you have time, remember to log into the app and join us in the live room on Wednesday.

一、上周资产价格回顾

Let's first review the asset price trends from last week.

The US dollar index rose significantly by 1.4%, and US Treasury yields also saw a major increase, with the 2-year and 10-year yields rising by 18 and 24 basis points, respectively. This is because last week's macro data, such as CPI and PPI, were not as good as the market expected.

However, when looking at these two sets of data together, the Federal Reserve's main focus is on PCE and core PCE. So, although PPI wasn't great, the components of PPI related to PCE actually weren't that bad. Therefore, overall, the April PCE data, which will be released in about two weeks, might not be too bad, but the bond market has already reacted to it.

The US dollar index rose and bond yields went up, but US stocks actually held up okay, with the main impact seen in Friday's market decline. That drop was likely the largest in over a month, but generally speaking, the U.S. stock market was fine last week. Basically, the three major indices were moving sideways, while gold fell by 3.7%, mainly due to its negative correlation with the U.S. dollar index. Crude oil rose by 10.5%, the VIX increased, and IGV also saw movement.

I will briefly talk about the Russell 2000, which fell by 2.4%. So, where is the strength of the S&P 500 or the three major indices coming from? It mainly comes from the leading big tech stocks. Let's look at the Equal Weighted Index, which fell by 1.2%. Looking at the Equal Weighted Index, it matches the decline seen in the Russell 2000. It's mainly that big tech is relatively stronger, even with the chip sector being weak. So last week was driven by big tech strength, keeping the three major indices nearly flat. The divergence in trends across sectors will be slightly larger.

二、组合再平衡：医疗与国防

Finally, I've picked two sectors: healthcare and defense. I might select some underlying assets and ETFs in these areas to add to my positions, making my overall portfolio relatively more balanced.

This is mainly targeting the second half of this year, especially the IPOs of some major tech companies early next year, and the impact on market liquidity, as well as some macroeconomic headwinds.

三、6月宏观日程：三大央行的可能动向

In June, there might actually be some macroeconomic news, such as central bank interest rate hikes.

  • First, regarding the European Central Bank, the market expects a 90% probability of a 25 basis point hike on June 11. Barring any unforeseen events, it is highly likely that the ECB will begin its rate hiking cycle.
  • Then, on June 16, there is a 78% probability of the Bank of Japan raising rates. So, by June, the ECB and the BOJ might both restart their rate hiking cycles.
  • Looking at the US side, there will also be a Federal Reserve meeting in June. Personally, since the new Fed chair has taken office, I believe that in this Fed meeting, as I mentioned last time, the Fed will undergo a transition, which will be reflected in their statement. The Fed will shift its stance from a bias toward interest rate cuts to a neutral one. This action itself signifies that the Fed has moved from its previous rate cutting cycle into a cycle of maintaining interest rates. Whether they will restart a rate hiking cycle remains to be verified by data.

四、市场参与度：指数高，情绪低

Let's provide an update on market participation. Although the index has only fallen perhaps 2% from its high, if we look at the NASDAQ market participation, it is actually at a very low level. 25 is the 100-day moving average, while 34, 46, 41, and 34 are nearing oversold levels. Anything below 30% is in oversold territory.

The Russell 2000 is the same at 33%, and the S&P is at 35%. So the three major indices are basically at a relatively pessimistic level now. If they open lower on Monday, the participation of all three major indices will drop below 30%.

My personal expectation is that if we see a decline of around 3% in the NASDAQ within the first two days of this week, that will be a short-term entry point for increasing positions. Many stocks that performed well a while ago, such as my largest holding, Enphase, may have corrected by more than 25% from their highs. So, many underlying assets have actually seen quite significant corrections.

From the perspective of indices and participation, because other indices, sectors, and assets have actually been relatively weak, if the chip and hardware sectors start to decline as well, the overall market sentiment will sink to a relatively low point once again. Even though the index is at a high level, don't be misled by the index. Since participation is at a relatively low level, it might be a buying point in the short term.

Then, if there is a rebound in the next wave and participation returns to 60% or 70%, combined with some macroeconomic data and news in June, as well as the interest rate hike rhythms of various central banks, we might enter a period of sideways consolidation. To put it simply, the easiest part of the market this year is already behind us. The road ahead might be slightly more difficult.

五、美债收益率：是真正的风险，还是事后的叙事？

Let's take a look at Treasury Yields. Market chatter is basically overwhelming, with the 30-year US Treasury yield hitting 5.16%, and all kinds of news reports are emerging.

If the 30-year US Treasury yield rises above 5%, what kind of situation might emerge? In the previous chart, we actually saw that in the second half of 2023, the 30-year Treasury yield had already climbed above 5%. So, for 2024 and 2025, will the market be a bear or a bull? So, it's not necessarily that Treasury yields have reached an extreme high. Of course, that coincides with the correction in yields.

Actually, in recent times, and especially on Friday, Treasury yields have returned to the media spotlight. But why? To put it plainly, it's mainly because over the past month or so, the market has performed too well, hardware has done too well, and chips have done too well. So, whenever the market pulls back, people immediately scramble to find reasons why. It just so happened to coincide with a rise in treasury yields, so the logic was tied together.

But in reality, if we take a step back, and I mean taking a step back now, or back in March, it's actually the same principle. If we take a step back, we'll realize the core of the US stock market is that fundamentals outweigh the macro. In other words, EPS growth is more important than the P-E ratio. Treasury yields could potentially affect EPS growth or the macro side, so both areas of the US stock market valuation can be influenced.

Bottom-up 与 Top-down 的双重影响

The impact is simple. It's either bottom-up or top-down.

  • Bottom-up: Mainly, if treasury yields rise, the financing costs for companies with debt will increase, as will borrowing costs for consumers, which will slow down consumption. Consumers will slow their spending, and companies will slow their investments, which could have a negative impact on corporate returns.
  • Top-down: When treasury yields rise, the overall PE valuation of the S&P 500 may contract.

Just like in 2022, despite the rise in treasury yields and the Fed's rate hiking cycle, not every company's earnings were negatively affected. Ultimately, it comes down to the profitability of individual companies or the S&P 500 as a whole. It's not a given that whenever treasury yields rise, the S&P 500 must fall, even if a rise in yields might cause a short-term valuation contraction.

长周期视角：60/40 组合的逻辑

Moreover, if we look back to before 2008, since the era after 2008 was basically one of low interest rates, the period following this pandemic might have brought the old era of low interest rates to an end.

What does it mean now that we've moved past that? If we look at the market before 2008, the reason behind the 60-40 allocation for pension funds, 60% stocks and 40% bonds, was based on the core idea of hedging between the stock market and the bond market.

What do we mean by hedging? It means that when the economy is strong, interest rates rise. As the economy improves and rates go up, U.S. bond prices fall, while U.S. stocks typically rise because of the strong economy. Right? So, by pairing US stocks with bonds, the bonds serve as a hedge for the stocks. The reverse is also true. When stocks fall due to a poor economy, bond prices rise and interest rates fall.

Therefore, if inflation in the market is caused by an overheating economy, the resulting rise in US bond yields is, to some extent, intended to curb that overheating. So, taking a step back, we are looking at this within a much longer cycle. If the economy continues to improve and overheats in the short term, interest rates rise to try to slow things down, but the underlying reason is that the economy is in an upward cycle, which may periodically be accompanied by rising U.S. bond yields to dampen that overheating.

So, what is more important is, what kind of cycle are we currently in? As long as the companies in the S&P 500, including big tech and hardware, continue to see their EPS growth expand, U.S. bonds are just a small part of a larger cycle.

六、美股估值现状：纳指偏贵，标普合理

If you follow me on X, the pinned post there was made when the NASDAQ valuation was nearing 30. I pinned it saying that if the NASDAQ's valuation goes above 30, we should start to gradually reduce our beta. Why? Because last year, when I started saying we should begin to lower our beta, it was exactly when the NASDAQ's valuation had climbed above 30 times. At over 30 times, we know that the valuation is already in a relatively expensive range, so if it continues to rise, you can proactively reduce your positions.

Now, if it keeps hovering at this level, for some of the assets that performed well recently, I suggested passive profit taking. What is passive profit taking? If you use a trailing stop, it's a passive take profit. If the price drops to a certain level, you exit a certain percentage of your position.

After the decline in the US stock market last Friday, the NASDAQ's valuation is now at 29.27x, which is still at a relatively high level. If it continues to rise, or if it falls — if it drops 2% or 3% over a couple of days this week, that could be a short-term point to reduce or add to positions, but if it rebounds back above 30, I think you can proactively exit.

Then looking at the S&P 500's forward P.E., it's at 20.9x. What does 20.9x imply? It's about the median of the past two years, so the S&P 500 isn't that expensive right now, while the NASDAQ is at a relatively expensive level. Why isn't the S&P 500 that expensive? Market participation is low. Many assets in the S&P 500 haven't rebounded. In fact, they haven't risen at all in this wave. So the S&P is currently at a relatively reasonable level, while the NASDAQ is at a relatively expensive one.

If you need to trim positions, there's no need to sell those that haven't grown recently because they're already at the bottom. Instead, you should trim the ones that have performed very well and trended upward recently. From an index perspective, I posted on X last week that I believe the RRSP, the S&P 500 equal weighted index, will outperform the S&P 500.

七、个股研究：麦当劳（MCD）

Okay, okay, lastly, let's talk about McDonald's. In fact, McDonald's has been trending downward almost continuously for the past two months or so, ever since the conflict began in March.

Actually, it performed very well in January and February, aligning with the market trend in December and January where value stocks performed exceptionally well regardless of valuation. Walmart, McDonald's, any value stock did very well in December. Then a reversal occurred starting from late February and the conflict events in March. McDonald's, represented by the white line, has been falling for over two months, and you can see the blue line is oil prices. Basically for McDonald's, especially since late April when oil prices rose, McDonald's has continued to decline.

So basically, as I've been saying lately, many stocks are still affected by the war and are currently lying flat on the ground. McDonald's is one example, and the stocks I own are another. Even the entire defense sector is highly positively correlated with oil prices, being completely influenced by the war and energy costs.

基本面与估值

From this perspective, McDonald's just released its earnings, and looking at their EPS growth for the next three years — 6%, 7%, 9%, and 8% — that is essentially McDonald's EPS growth.

Then, looking at the valuation, it was 22.31 times, and the forward P.E. is now around 20.83 times. So, their fundamentals are fine. It's just that they have been affected by sentiment in the short term, which has dragged their valuation down. Because their EPS growth hasn't been revised downward, and the quarterly earnings they just released were actually okay.

Let's take a look at their valuation. You can see there's a slight discrepancy from before. The current forward P.E. — this should be Friday's data — is 20.6 times. What does 20.6 times mean? I have data here from the past 7 years, from 2019 to now, and there haven't been many times when it was lower than 20.6 times. If we set aside the drop during the pandemic, it hit 20.6 times once in 2023 and again in mid-2024.

Therefore, I believe McDonald's valuation is at a relatively low point, so you could definitely choose to add to your position or initiate a new one here, and if it drops another 5%, that could be a point to add more.

风险收益比

For stocks like these, your potential return won't be huge. For instance, from the current 20.6 times, even if it rises 10%, that would bring it to 23.2 times, which is basically back into a relatively reasonable valuation range. So from this current level, your upside potential is 10%, and your downside, after you add to the position, is 5%. Once you've added, the upside potential might open up further, but at the current price, the upside is 10%. As for whether there is much room for it to fall further, it's actually not that large. So these types of assets inherently have relatively lower volatility, and it all depends. Whether you choose to invest in value stocks or hold cash, the difference might not be that significant.

技术面

Regarding the technical aspect, what I am looking at here is its weekly chart. On a weekly basis, it is now extremely close to an oversold position. Although it's not very clear from my view, if you expand it, last week's weekly candle formed a doji, but this doji didn't show any significant increase in volume. But I believe that from a technical perspective, McDonald's is also approaching a relative cyclical bottom. The last time I saw such an oversold condition on the weekly K line was mid 2024, and the time before that was in 2020, during the pandemic.

So, is it currently doing very, very poorly? No, but it is actually already at a relatively low position. Therefore, both the technicals and fundamentals are fine, the technicals are in an oversold position, and the valuation is also at a relatively low level. So I think McDonald's has basically reached a point of resonance, and it is a suitable time to start building a position.

However, I must point out one thing. For this type of target, you shouldn't expect a huge potential return. The upside is likely limited to around 10% to 15%.

八、机构动向观察

1）微软：Bill Gates 的换仓

Finally, let's talk about the recent rebalancing moves by some major players. First, Microsoft. I think this chart for Microsoft is quite good. It shows some sellers and some buyers. The buyers include Bill, who sold Google and bought Microsoft in the first quarter.

Looking at it from today's perspective, that move was quite poor, but I reminded a viewer on social media last time not to look at a company's fundamentals with a short-term perspective. You shouldn't conclude that the first quarter adjustment was wrong just because Google's gains in April were significantly better than Microsoft's.

Because, first of all, just like with the McDonald's stock I mentioned earlier, for many targets, not all of them, each one carries different risks, and with different risks come different potential returns. It doesn't mean that when we buy, we are only thinking about — it's not that we are always pursuing the stock with the best gains. That's not how it works.

So, to some extent, if you compare Google and Microsoft, Google's beta is relatively higher, while Microsoft's is relatively lower. I feel Microsoft's volatility is smaller, and Google is currently at a hot spot. So, looking at the results, if you look at the trend over the last two months, this portfolio shift is definitely not a correct position, but it depends on what risks their overall portfolio wants to bear.

However, he bought Microsoft and also posted an article about it on X. He mainly wanted to explain why he bought Microsoft, as he believes it was oversold, and he started building his position in February. The price at which I built my position was actually a bit earlier than his, and my entry price is still relatively high.

2）软件板块：从 All-In Podcast 到机构投降

This was discussed in the latest On Podcast last Friday, and they actually talked about it, including Chamath. Because they started shorting this sector a year and a half ago, but in last week's On Podcast, they started becoming bullish on the software sector, believing that for top tier B2B companies, such as WDAY or CRM, their valuations will return, and the market might see a rotation.

I believe this possibility exists, but since every company is different, it requires each company's fundamentals to emerge, along with market sentiment and the endorsement from these investment gurus to boost market sentiment.

And more importantly, as I shared on X last week, after finishing a review with an institution on Friday, I found that many institutions in the first quarter were still chasing, completely chasing the index, chasing the chip sector, and chasing the hardware sector, while for the software sector, was basically a very obvious capitulation.

Even for a company I've considered relatively disciplined in value investing over the past five years, which I think is a very standard value firm that has its own convictions for many underlying assets, they also saw capitulation and sold off a lot of their software holdings.

So, looking at it from the perspective of position allocation, are there currently any bulls in the software sector? Of course there are bulls, but these bulls are basically all a crowded trade. These bulls are essentially operating against the trend. They must be very bullish on these targets to choose to accept software companies at the current price and valuation multiples. For this type of investor, another 4% drop doesn't have much of an impact because they already believed these targets were undervalued. On the contrary, I think they might even increase their positions.

Those mainly offloading their positions are funds that refuse to admit their mistakes over the past half year or year, failing to see that the software sector could face valuation compression. These funds have performance pressures, forcing them to adjust their holdings. A month and a half into the first quarter, after reviewing with three funds, it's very clear they are chasing the chip sector and selling off software stocks. Another fund is performing well, but they have also slightly scaled back their AI exposure.

3）巴菲特卖出 UNH

Also, another point is that Berkshire sold UNH. I didn't expect this, but everyone has noticed. Didn't you say Buffett is a long-term value investor? Berkshire? How could you sell it less than half a year after buying it?

Actually, they have performed such operations before. I have a deep impression of the TSMC case from late 2022 to early 2023. They completely sold off their TSMC position, but why did they sell it later? It's not that Buffett retired and now Greg is just selling off what Buffett previously bought. I don't think that's the case at all. It's simply that they likely saw some risks, which is very normal. Buffett was still around in 2023, right? The reason they sold TSMC at that time was because they believed it carried geopolitical risks. They bought TSMC in late 2022 and sold it in early 2023 citing geopolitical risk. Did geopolitical risk really escalate within just half a year?

So often I think we shouldn't overanalyze. While Buffett certainly has things worth learning, there's no need to blindly copy his homework, as most of the time, people can't really replicate his trades anyway.

I think it is completely unreasonable for the market to drop 5% just because Berkshire sold UNH. But the main reason is that it performed very well a while ago, and if you look at the RSI now, it's at 88.584%, which is very overbought. A 5% drop is fine, reasonable, and acceptable. But that doesn't mean Buffett doesn't like the company just because he sold it. To be honest, I think the move on UNH was quite poor, so this trade might have even lost money from buy to sell, because he sold it too early and didn't benefit from the rally over the past month or so.

九、Seeking Alpha 工具推介

Since we can't copy Buffett's homework, is there any other homework we can copy?

There is Seeking Alpha, which everyone knows is a very large investment platform for foreigners, with tens of millions of writers and authors on it. They have a feature under them called AlphaPix, a quantitative stock selection tool. The strategy itself has a YTD S&P 500 increase of 8.39%, while the AlphaPix quantitative stock selection feature has risen even more.

Basically, every two weeks, they will recommend a stock, and there will be alerts for both buying and selling the stock. In the past few months, the returns on some of the stocks bought have actually been quite good, as you can see. During the same period, they have basically outperformed the index. I can't reveal the names here, primarily for compliance reasons.

So if you are interested, the AlphaPix quantitative stock selection feature currently has a 10% discount. It was originally $499 a year, and now it is $449 a year. Then they have another subscription called Seeking Alpha Premium, which allows you to see all their various articles and a lot of other very useful content. For the Seeking Alpha Premium subscription, there is also a 10% discount right now, and there is a 7-day free trial period. If you are very interested in both Premium and AlphaPicks, there is also a combo package, and the discount price for that will be even higher.

Alright, so I will put all three links in the pinned comment. If you are interested in any of Seeking Alpha's products, you can find the links in the pinned comment.

That's all for today.

item.description:
## 一、被忽略的"叙事补全机制"：市场不是被收益率打跌的，而是被自己的强势打跌的
视频里有一段话值得反复咀嚼——**"过去一个多月市场太强了，硬件太强了，芯片太强了。所以一旦回调，人们立刻去找原因，恰好赶上收益率上行，逻辑就被绑在一起了。"**
这其实揭示了一个让人意外的**因果倒置现象**：财经媒体并不是在解释市场，而是在为市场的情绪波动**事后配字幕**。30年期美债收益率在2023年下半年就突破过5%，但那并没有终结牛市。所以"收益率破5% = 熊市"并不是规律，而是一种**叙事工件（narrative artifact）**——它在市场需要解释时才会被激活。
跨领域连接：这和**神经科学里的"裂脑实验"**惊人地相似。当大脑某半球做出一个决定后，另一半球（语言中枢）会立刻编造一个看似合理的理由。市场的"日报评论体系"就是金融世界的语言中枢——价格先动，理由后补。**真正的投资者训练，是学会识别哪些是因果，哪些是字幕。**
---
## 二、隐藏的系统性真相：60/40组合的"复活"，意味着我们正在告别一个时代
讲者顺带提了一句被大多数听众忽略的话：**2008年之

item.title: [Zhang Xiaojun Podcast] 141. Freda的投资札记第2集：Tokenmaxxing、把电机塞进蒸汽机、接力赛变篮球赛、孤独、人的连接
item.dc:creator: Zhang Xiaojun Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、Token 是 AI 时代的"千瓦时"，但它正在重演工业革命最大的认知陷阱

Freda 把 dollar per token 类比为工业时代的 dollar per kWh，这个比喻精彩，但她自己揭示的反讽更深：今天投资人以"烧了多少 token"作为景气度指标，恰恰等同于一百年前有人炫耀"我家用电量很大"——这是把成本当成价值的典型误读。

让我意外的不是这个观察本身，而是它与 Jevons 悖论的反向呼应：当蒸汽机效率提升时，煤炭总消耗反而暴涨。Freda 给出的预言是"右下两项（token/task 和 $/token）都会大幅下降，但前两项（用户数、任务数）的爆炸会盖过一切"——这意味着 AI 不会出现 Jevons 悖论的简单复刻，而是一个"效率提升 + 单位价格暴跌 + 任务定义本身被重新发明"的三重叠加。这是工业革命没有出现过的结构。

二、"把电机塞进蒸汽机的位置"——这一集最值钱的隐喻其实指向投资本身

电力发明后 40 年生产率没提升，因为工厂只是把电机塞进蒸汽机原来的位置，没有重新设计流水线。Freda 用这个史实解释为什么 AI 现在还没真正提升企业生产率。

但她没有说破的是：这个隐喻同样适用于投资行业自己。 她讲到给 agent 喂足数据就能跑赢人类、event-driven 交易将"在几秒内"完成、卖方分析师的财务对比工作将被 AI 吞掉——可整个投资行业的组织结构（基金经理、研究员、合规、销售）仍然是前 AI 时代为人类信息瓶颈而设计的金字塔。换句话说，VC 们正在投 Neolabs，但 VC 自己就是那个"把电机塞进蒸汽机位置"的工厂。能否预测哪些基金会先死，本质上不是看他们投了什么，而是看他们敢不敢拆掉自己的中层翻译层。

三、最反常识的跨领域连接：孤独感、API key 焦虑、与"信息式关系"的崩塌

节目尾声那段关于"装不上 OpenCode"的崩溃，看起来是花絮，其实是全集最锋利的系统性洞察——只是 Freda 自己也没完全意识到。

她在前面用了大量篇幅论证：SaaS 的订阅模型不再适配智能层，因为智能是按效果按使用付费的。然后在结尾她说：以后见人不再为了交换信息，因为 95% 的信息 AI 都能更快给。

把这两件事放在一起看，会出现一个让人脊背一凉的推论：人类关系本身长期也是一种 SaaS 订阅模型——你花固定的时间成本（"维系"），换取一个对方"随时可能产出有用信息"的接入权。AI 之于人际关系，等同于按效果付费之于 SaaS：信息型友谊（"行业打听一下"）会迅速贬值并坍塌，留下的只有无法被 token 化的部分——情绪、勇气、遗憾、火花。

这解释了为什么硅谷集体焦虑伴随着集体孤独：不是 AI 让人不想社交，而是 AI 让"以信息为媒介的社交契约"第一次出现了便宜得多的替代品。就像电报普及后，写信问候的人际语法整整消失了一代人才重建。所以 Freda 在 Palo Alto 那次"没准备就坐下来聊的"对话之所以是几年来最高密度的——因为那是她第一次进入"后信息时代"的人类关系范式。

这也是为什么我倾向于反驳她对"长期乐观"的乐观本身：真正的痛苦不会来自失业，而来自一整代人需要重新学习"什么才是值得交换的东西"。这件事，比 capex 转负、比 Mag7 被稀释、比软件公司估值腰斩，都要慢，也都要深。

📝 转录文本

Freda 投资札记第 2 集

Tokenmaxxing、把电机塞进蒸汽机、接力赛变篮球赛、孤独、人的连接

Hello everyone, I'm Xiaojun. Today is our second episode of the series, "Freda's Investing Diary".

Maybe some of you are listening to our show for the first time. I'd like to introduce you to the program. Freda Duan is a partner of Altimetry Capital. This is a shareholder of a stock technology fund that invests in both the primary and secondary markets. The investment cases in the primary market include OpenAI and Anthropic. 二级市场的投资案例有英伟达、存储芯片、Snowflake、Robinhood。

In the first episode of the Freda's Investing Diary, which is our 125th episode, she thoroughly analyzed the current American star companies, calculated their huge income, and explored the new order of American capital, and the bubble topic that everyone was very concerned about at the time.

What can be foretold is that in this episode, we will talk about very dry content such as AI and investment, such as token maxing, 人和人之间啊……

Today we have our 125th episode guest, Freda, return to the studio. So, Freda, let's say hello to our audience first.

Hello, everyone. Thank you for inviting me, Xiaojun.

一、Token：AI 时代的新计量单位

Let's start with the model companies that we are familiar with, the three-party model companies. Let's talk about a new marketplace in the AI era, which is tokens. Now everyone is talking about the income and consumption of token companies, but how should we understand the essence of tokens?

I think because of AI, there are several such units that have been released in recent years. Including what we often call dollar per flop, dollar per watt, and dollar per token. Now, more and more are dollar per token. It is a comparison of the return on actual business.

I think a more obvious trend is that when we go to a company in the future, we will compare some companies. I think we will all go to see dollar per token. I will imagine it as the industrial era. Everyone looks at this dollar per kilowatt hour, which is a concept of a thousand-watt stone. The comparison is the energy consumption output ratio.

But at the same time, I've written some articles and I really want to say that I think tokens are actually a very easy to mislead unit. Because the most important thing in it is token per task. That is, you have the same task, different models or different applications, and the token consumed may be tens or even hundreds of times less. I think this understanding is very important.

为什么 token 容易误导

Why is that? There are many reasons.

  • One is that the output length can be very different. For example, if you have a task to do coding, a better model may have a hundred lines of code, and the very sophisticated code is finished. A cheaper model may need to write thousands of lines of very sloppy code. On the surface, the tasks on both sides are completed, but the token you consume is not a quantity at all.
  • There are some more hidden reasoning tokens in this. Many models will do a lot of intermediate reasoning before answering. You can't see this part of the user, but in fact, different models will consume a very different number of compute tokens.
  • Third, as we've mentioned, the Agented workflow will increase the token consumption like an amplifier.

So it's really the same task. The token consumption of different models can be very, very different.

Does this explain why some tokens are very large and the more tokens consumed, the better?

This is definitely not the case. And I think it's the other way around. Because in the end, everyone will realize that what you want is actually your effect. and you pursue the highest efficiency possible.

For example, I'll give you an example of feedback from the developers around me. Like the Cursor coding model. If you ask it the same question, it will consume more tokens. Then I will infer that the employees like it very much. and we can also see some improvements in the labor productivity. As a CFO, my conclusion is that I want to continue to add orders.

I think this is a phenomenon that the industry is in a relatively early stage. I think we still don't really understand that tokens and tokens are not the same thing. That's the fact.

Token 爆炸会自然停下来吗？

So will the so-called token explosion naturally stop? Because after all, the model will become more and more efficient.

I don't think it's that easy to answer directly whether it is or not. If we compare this with academic, there may be such a more academic formula.

The total investment of AI can actually be divided into several inputs: - The first is how many users you have, - then you use this to set up how many tasks each user has completed. - Then you set up the token per task, - and then you set up the dollar per token concept.

The first two concepts, which are the number of users and the number of tasks each user has completed, will definitely continue to grow. From programmers to ordinary white-collar workers, using AI is actually a very big wave of user rights. For example, Anthropic has a product called Cowork. So the first two items, which are the number of users and the number of tasks for each user, I don't think there's anything to say about it. It will definitely continue to grow and greatly grow.

But the latter two, I think, will have an advantage. Because now the whole situation, you see, everyone goes to say how many tokens I used and how much money I spent. I think this state itself is actually not very normal.

How do you think this will change in the future?

I think it's better to look at it this way. For example, tokens per task, just like what we just talked about, I think it's the most efficient model. In fact, overtime will be recognized by everyone.

There are some changes and specific understandings in the $/Token model. For example, the price of Anthropic is $5 per million input tokens and $25 per million output tokens. But according to the third party analysis of this industry, it actually only costs one dollar. Because it's very high in the rate of return on cash. Maybe 98% to 99% of the time. Of course, there are other reasons. For example, its identity workflow input is much longer than output.

So there are actually many articles in the "Dollar Per Token" section. I think in a while, people will realize that the cost of the model is the most important thing in this.

So how to evolve? I think it's definitely a very wasted state now. But it doesn't mean that after we finished these few, the total investment of AI will fall.

My own thoughts or the way I think is that I think the current era is very close. A lot of things are actually right and wrong. For example, you say that now everyone says that there is a lot of waste in token marketing. I think this answer is certain. But you say that the use of tokens will continue to rise significantly in the future. I think this is also right. Listening to contradictions is actually not contradictory.

I see some investors use the consumption of tokens as a basis for the judgment of the project. What do you think?

I think it's a stage product. It's like when you were in the industrial era, you wouldn't really show off that I have a light and I have a lot of power consumption. Instead, you would buy an expensive LED. I think this thing will change over time.

AI spend 真的在创造价值吗？

I remember you are good at accounting. Now many companies spend money on AI. Do you think it's really creating value or is it spending less money?

What everyone is talking about in this is actually Meta. There are rumors that it has several billion in the year of cloud code. It's a very scary number.

And then in this, the interesting thing is that Meta recently said it would pay 10% of its employees. I calculated it and said that the 10% of its employees are probably equivalent to the cost of its cloud code. But I'm not saying this is a direct cause-and-effect relationship.

For example, last month, Uber's CFO also jumped out and said that their company spent the entire year of the AI budget on coding within three months. So it's about tens of millions of this volume.

I calculated the first few companies, especially those who are doing token maxing, and their AI spend is about 1% to 2% of EBITDA. So it's still a controllable number. To be honest, the number calculated is better than I thought. I thought it was actually a bigger number.

按 token 付费的模式合理吗？

Do you think it's reasonable to use the token payment model?

I think this is a strong judgment. I think the industry is gradually becoming rational. So the payment model must change. I think it will gradually change from the payment according to the token to the payment according to the effect. Because as we just said, tokens are not comparable.

There is a company called Sierra, which is an AI customer service. And then he is completely based on the effect of the fee. And then we talked about it. In fact, his customers also very much agree with his kind of fee model. He said, if you, uh, I can help you solve this customer service, I didn't transfer the labor, I was paid. If I transfer it, I won't charge.

And there are many more problems in it. This complexity, this resolution, and then his unit price will be different. So as a client, Sierra and I are very interest-aligned. We both want to solve this problem, and we both want to burn less tokens.

So I think it's the best solution. But this is very okay in the customer service, because you can optimize. But it's not every industry. So I think it may be a little bit like the resolution of customer service, from your sales transformation, including what to charge loans or insurance compensation. I think some scenes will start to become cost-effective.

And then there are some more creative ones, such as writing. I think it may be right. For a long time, it may still be the effect of buying tokens and receiving fees. Because the writing effect is not easy to polish.

二、模型公司：Anthropic、OpenAI、XAI、Meta

Next, we will talk about the key model companies.

Anthropic 的领先优势能持续多久？

Let's start with the company that everyone is most concerned about in the past few months, Anthropic. It should be one of the most outstanding model companies this year. In your opinion, how long do you think the leading advantage of this wave of Anthropic can last? Is its range high?

I've been thinking about this question too. In the past two years, everyone's last wish is that SOTA's model will change hands every few months. Last year, everyone was talking about Gemini. And before that, it was OpenAI. Now it's Anthropic. Will it continue to change in a few months?

So the word "focusing" has been a rolling basis for the past year or two. But I think it's probably the first time in the past two months that I've questioned whether this hypothesis will continue.

The core reason is that after the coding agent was mature, a very important loop actually appeared. is that you need better AI to train the next generation of better AI. So once this cycle runs, there's a little bit of what the researchers call recursive self-improvement.

If it really passes a certain threshold, this curve will become very shaky. Then you have a little bit of singularity behind you, or how do you define it? The person chasing after you may really be meaningless.

When do you think this turning point will happen? It's been two years since AI training. Why is it different this time?

I think it's just a time period. Maybe it starts from January this year. The turning point is the maturity of coding agents. Before June, the model of coding may only have a 5% chance of improving the total factor productivity of an engineer. and then at the end of last year, it could have reached 15% or 20%. Now it's definitely higher.

So you see, the speed of new models coming out of the model factory is not like before, which is to spend a few months or even a year, but a month to release a new version. I think this is actually the real reason why a company as big as Meta is willing to spend hundreds of billions on a model from another company. Because if you don't have this thing, the speed you stack up is really slower than others.

So do you think today we are at a point where we can't chase after it anymore?

I personally think that it's already very, very fast. It's not that appropriate, but if you compare it to a car, it's a bit like, I don't know, like the feeling of changing a car in a carriage. I guess the first car that was created was also often a horse. So the front speedcars might still be able to chase after you. At that time, the gap between the two may not be so clear. But once the car can run normally and steadily, then you don't have the meaning of the horse of the engine of the car.

Codex vs. Cloud Code

So, back to coding, OpenAI has been doing a lot of regrouping in the last two months, according to GBT and coding. And they pushed coding to a very high position. Google is personally managing coding. Meta is also pushing its own coding model. And they hope to do SOTA at the end of the year. XAI is publicly acquiring Cursor. The logic behind it is actually similar.

How do you see the competition between Codex and Cloud Code?

Yes, there is a lot of comparison and discussion. I personally think that if there is a lot of debate between two products or even two people, it usually shows that their actual effect is not much different.

Codex has been obviously fighting for price recently. They made a huge supplement. And then everything, for example, Cloud Code is not allowed to be used. And then Codex is just welcome for you to use it. So I think this price is still a pretty good strategy for him. Because he is a later person after all.

This also speaks to my own reflection. It's just that these two years have taken too much time to compare open and private. But I think the more important logic is that we know that both of them will be very big.

And one of the recent thoughts is that if you look at the business model, the whole model layer, or called intelligence, it is not a SaaS concept. SaaS is subscription. It is paid by the head. When you have a subscription, the price is actually the peak. And the more you use it, the cheaper it is. So as a customer, you tend to use only one. Because you spent all your money on this per se.

But the model—Yes, I think there are many reflections in the past two years, but this is actually a very big reflection.

Coding 市场规模的误判

To be honest, when I first came out of the coding scene a year or two ago, the investors were calculating the market size of TAM, which is the price-to-value ratio. At that time, I thought that the quantity, that is, who will use this coding model, everyone said that there were many developers in the US, and there were 4-5 million people. In fact, it is a very small group of people.

When people were considering the price, I remember we had some internal discussions. Some people said that 20 knife is enough. Some people said that it should be 200 knife. Because a whole software is so expensive. Because a company spends the most money, maybe one or two thousand yuan on all software subscriptions. So 200 knife, right? Give it to coding, everyone thinks it's okay. Right?

You use the price to calculate. In fact, it is a hundred billion dollars. Such a market size. But obviously, this is completely wrong. Because until today, the income of your entire family is much more than this amount. So if you think about it, I think the price and price of the stock at that time were very wrong.

So how should this be thought about?

Yes, I think... Maybe it's really back to what Dario said, the global white collar. He has been in his PPT, the first page of the investment PPT is the white collar. He said it was a market size of about 3-40 trillion, which is about 3-40 trillion. He said this is the size of the market he wants to eat.

I understand this logic because we do a lot of things every day. There is a little bit, that is, if I will write the code, I should let the computer do the task automatically. So this idea is that my real time coding is actually anything that can be operated by the computer. And as the world is getting more and more digitalized, this range is expanding. Including now, we use AI to operate Excel, or I think we will use AI to do transactions. So this is its real time.

So every time I think about the product of coworking, it's really amazing to see two people doing it.

Back to coding, I think, or to reflect on investment, I think investment is often wrong. As an investor, I think what I can do is to realize my original investment frame and time when I quickly scored $1 billion when I found out about the coding income. Then I can make a timely change.

When did you realize it?

This is probably the earliest thing to look at. At first it was Cursor. Cursor's coding income soon reached $1 billion. Then there was CloudCo and then Clouco. At that time, because you had earned 10% of the $1 billion according to our time, it was definitely not that normal. So at that time, there should be a timely reflection.

反向滚雪球与训练-推理的约束

I remember you mentioned a concept called "reversed snowball". You used the "reversed snowball" to explain the business model of the model company. Have you made any changes to this framework?

Oh, this concept is actually mentioned by Dario. I just translated it into a more investment-oriented language. I still think this is a very classic concept.

To put it simply, if you trained a model last year and the cost was 1, then this model can bring about 3-4% of the income this year. Then the profit is 50%, which is less than 2 yuan.

So the company didn't think about the demand being so strong.

Yes, yes, yes. Dario has actually made the prediction very extreme and very beautiful. It's a very first-class principle.

But there is a very simple formula here. There are two things. - One is that if the cost of your income is lower than the profit you push, the company can make money. - The second is a constraint. The total cost of training plus reasoning is actually pre-ordered.

I don't know if I made myself clear. For example, the company can control the profit margin of your reasoning. But the proportion of training and reasoning is ultimately determined by your needs. So your needs are not really controllable. But for example, the needs of the beginning of the year suddenly burst. So the company will be forced to put more constraints on reasoning. So suddenly there was violence.

怎么看 OpenAI 和 Anthropic 的收入对比

Now the media often report the income of companies like OpenAI and Anthropic. How do you compare these two?

It's hard to compare. First of all, everyone calls it ARR, but it's not ARR at all. Because ARR is called Annual Recurring Revenue. In the model, it's not annual, it's not recurring. And the scope of use is really 7 or 8, everything has. Some use the income of the past four weeks to multiply by 13. And then the more radical use the past week to multiply by 53. So in fact, it can be a lot of difference.

And these two companies didn't make it easy to compare. Some used gross, some used net. For example, Anthropic reported gross revenue. And you have to make some adjustments. For example, if 40% is the third party API, then you need net. You have to deduct this and reduce it by 20%. And OpenAI is the opposite. It reports a net.

How do you see OpenAI and Anthropic's demand for compute?

I think the biggest point I learned from this is that the market, including myself, actually underestimated how much model income each gigawatt power could generate.

The third party reported that OpenAI and Anthropic now have a few gigawatt power. And at the end of the year, it should be up to 5, and next year, for example, up to 10. Before, everyone thought that each gigawatt could almost correspond to, for example, a 100 billion income is not bad, but these few months have proven that this number has been greatly underestimated.

The cost of reasoning and training should be corresponding to each gigawatt. It's okay to have a 100 billion, but the income side can actually be marked very high. So the "in-pride" of this thing, the "in-pride" of model companies' revenue, I think it's actually underestimated by the system.

How much income can a gigawatt generate? No one can see the upper limit today. You said one billion, you said five billion. In fact, it seems quite possible now.

On topic, what time do most stock investors see this company?

I think the real inflation point of Anthropic was last year. It was when its ARR was from 1 billion to 5 billion. At that time, the head was because it used less than half a year from 10 to 50. And it's much faster than the same period of OpenAI.

Actually, when you see that, you should already know, right? Like we just mentioned, for example, coding is bigger and faster than we thought. But at that time, it was not out of the question. At that time, there were two things that were out of the question. One is that the profit margin was very ugly at that time.
